Question: Scenario-based: A legal document uses the word 'he' throughout. The defense argues that the law should only apply to men because of this wording. Does the law recognize the word 'he' as applying to women as well?

Possible Sections.Full Text: The word “man” denotes a male human being of any age: the word “woman” denotes a female human being of any age.
The word 'person' includes any Company or Association, or body of persons, whether incorporated or not.
The pronoun 'he' and its derivatives are used of any person, whether male or female.
[Omitted by the Adaptation of Central Acts and Ordinances Order, 1949].
Every person shall be liable to punishment under this Code and not otherwise for every act or omission contrary to the provisions thereof, of which he shall be guilty within Bangladesh.

Section Text: The pronoun 'he' and its derivatives are used of any person, whether male or female.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Does the legal pronoun 'he' encompass both genders?

Rule.Section 10: Defines the terms 'man' and 'woman' based on biological sex regardless of age.
Rule.Section 11: Explains that 'person' includes corporate entities and groups of people.
Rule.Section 8: Clarifies that the pronoun 'he' is gender-neutral within the legislative text.
Rule.Section 13: Relates to an omitted provision that is no longer in force.
Rule.Section 2: Establishes the general territorial jurisdiction and liability for crimes committed in Bangladesh.

Application.Section 10: Fails because it defines specific nouns for gender rather than the usage of pronouns.
Application.Section 11: Fails because it addresses corporate personality rather than grammatical gender rules for natural persons.
Application.Section 8: Governs directly because it explicitly mandates that 'he' refers to both males and females.
Application.Section 13: Fails as an omitted section cannot be used to interpret active legislative language.
Application.Section 2: Fails because it defines who is liable for crimes rather than the meaning of pronouns.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 8, the word 'he' applies to women because the law explicitly states that the pronoun 'he' and its derivatives are used for any person, whether male or female.

Question: Scenario-based: I stole a bag of grain from a neighbor's building. Does the law consider this a 'bailable' crime where I have a right to be released while waiting for trial?

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 496
Possible Sections.Full Text: A person with fraudulent intention going through the ceremony of being married, knowing that he is not thereby lawfully married.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Schedule II, Section 379
Possible Sections.Full Text: Theft

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Schedule II, Section 380
Possible Sections.Full Text: Theft in a building, tent or vessel.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 497
Possible Sections.Full Text: Adultery

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Schedule II, Section 323
Possible Sections.Full Text: Voluntarily causing hurt.

Relevant Section: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Schedule II, Section 380
Section Text: Theft in a building, tent or vessel. Arrest: May arrest without warrant. Warrant or Summons: Warrant. Compoundable: Not compoundable. Punishment: Imprisonment of either description for 7 years and fine. Bailable: Not bailable. Court_Triable: Any Judicial Magistrate.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is house theft (theft in a building) a bailable offense?

Rule.Section 496: Confers an absolute right to bail for individuals accused of offenses that are 'bailable' in the schedule.
Rule.Schedule II, Section 379: Classifies simple theft as a non-bailable crime in the procedural schedule.
Rule.Schedule II, Section 380: Explicitly designates theft in a building as 'Not bailable' in the second schedule.
Rule.Section 497: Authorizes a judge to grant bail in serious cases as a matter of discretion rather than right.
Rule.Schedule II, Section 323: Lists simple physical assault as a crime where bail is an absolute legal right.

Application.Section 496: Fails because it only applies to 'bailable' crimes, and theft from a building is not on that list.
Application.Schedule II, Section 379: Fails because it covers 'simple' theft rather than the more serious category of theft inside a 'building.'
Application.Schedule II, Section 380: Governs directly because the 'Bailable' column for this specific crime states it is 'Not bailable,' removing the automatic right to release.
Application.Section 497: Fails because it describes the judge's 'power' to consider bail rather than the 'classification' of the crime itself.
Application.Schedule II, Section 323: Fails because the crime is related to property theft rather than the physical injury of a person.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Schedule II, Section 380, you do not have a right to be released; the law classifies theft in a building as a non-bailable offense.

Cited Acts and Sections: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Schedule II, Section 380

Question: Scenario-based: Does the law about court deadlines apply just to big cities like Dhaka, or does it also cover villages and small towns across the entire country?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Suits instituted in Bangladesh on contracts entered into in a foreign country are subject to the rules of limitation contained in this Act.
All instruments shall, for the purposes of this Act, be deemed to be made with reference to the Gregorian calendar.
It extends to the whole of Bangladesh.
Nothing in this Act shall apply to suits under the Divorce Act.

Subject to the provisions contained in sections 4 to 25 (inclusive), every suit instituted, appeal preferred, and application made, after the period of limitation prescribed therefor by the first schedule shall be dismissed, although limitation has not been set up as a defence.

Section Text: It extends to the whole of Bangladesh.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: What is the territorial extent of the Limitation Act, 1908?

Rule.Section 11: Requires any suit in Bangladesh regarding a contract signed abroad to follow local limitation rules.
Rule.Section 25: Mandates that all legal time limits in documents must be calculated using the Gregorian calendar system.
Rule.Section 1: States the name of the law and declares that its geographic jurisdiction covers the entire country of Bangladesh.
Rule.Section 29: Exempts specific types of legal cases, such as those under the Divorce Act, from the Act's scope.
Rule.Section 3: Mandates that any case filed after its legal deadline must be dismissed by the judge automatically.

Application.Section 11: Fails because it explains which rules to use for foreign deals rather than defining the Act's domestic geographic reach.
Application.Section 25: Fails as it defines the calendar system used rather than the physical territory where the rules apply.
Application.Section 1: Governs directly as Subsection (2) explicitly states that the Act 'extends to the whole of Bangladesh', covering all cities and villages.
Application.Section 29: Fails because it defines the legal subjects (like divorce) that are excluded rather than defining the geographic zones of the law.
Application.Section 3: Fails as it explains the penalty for being late rather than the map of where the law's power exists.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Limitation Act, 1908, section 1, Subsection (2), the law applies to the entire country, meaning it covers every village and city across the whole of Bangladesh.

Question: Scenario-based: A teacher was targeted by a colleague who sent a letter full of lies to the school board to get him fired. From the day the board received that letter, how long does the teacher have to sue for libel?

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I Article 25
Possible Sections.Full Text: For compensation for slander.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I Article 22
Possible Sections.Full Text: For compensation for any other injury to the person. One year.. When the injury is committed.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I Article 19
Possible Sections.Full Text: For compensation for false imprisonment. One year.. When the imprisonment ends.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I Article 36
Possible Sections.Full Text:
For compensation for any malfeasance, misfeasance or non-feasance independent of contract and not herein specially provided for.
*Part VI.—Three years.*

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I Article 24
Possible Sections.Full Text: For compensation for libel. One year.. When the libel is published.

Relevant Section: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I Article 24
Section Text: For compensation for libel. One year.. When the libel is published.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: What is the limitation period for a libel suit and when does the clock begin?

Rule.Schedule I Article 25: Applies to 'slander' (spoken lies), starting the one-year limit the moment the words are physically uttered.
Rule.Schedule I Article 22: Governs a one-year limit for general physical injuries like assault, counting from the day of the attack.
Rule.Schedule I Article 19: Sets a one-year limit for being locked up against your will, starting on the day you are freed.
Rule.Schedule I Article 36: Provides a general two-year limit for civil wrongs not covered by a more specific category in the schedule.
Rule.Schedule I Article 24: Requires a suit for 'libel' (written lies) to be filed within one year of the date the material was published.

Application.Schedule I Article 25: Fails because the lies were written in a letter, which is defined as libel, not slander (which only covers spoken words).
Application.Schedule I Article 22: Fails as the teacher's injury is to his reputation and career, not a physical injury to his body.
Application.Schedule I Article 19: Fails because the teacher wasn't locked in a room; his professional reputation was attacked by the colleague's letter.
Application.Schedule I Article 36: Fails as Article 24 provides a specific rule for libel which takes precedence over any general category.
Application.Schedule I Article 24: Governs directly because the letter is a written defamatory statement (libel) and the clock started when it was sent/published to the board.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I, Article 24, the teacher has exactly one year to file his lawsuit, starting from the day the letter was published.

Cited Acts and Sections: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I, Article 24

Question: Scenario-based: Salma acted as a guarantor for a friend's loan. Her friend disappeared, so Salma had to pay the bank the full amount today. How long does she have to sue her friend?

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I Article 57
Possible Sections.Full Text: For money payable for money lent. Three years.. When the loan is made.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I Article 62
Possible Sections.Full Text: For money payable by the defendant to the plaintiff for money received by the defendant for the plaintiff’s use.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I Article 81
Possible Sections.Full Text: By a surety against the principal debtor. Three years.. When the surety pays the creditor.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I Article 82
Possible Sections.Full Text: By a surety against a co-surety. Three years. When the surety pays anything in excess of his own share.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I Article 115
Possible Sections.Full Text: For compensation for the breach of any contract, express or implied, not in writing registered and not herein specially provided for.

Relevant Section: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I Article 81
Section Text: By a surety against the principal debtor. Three years.. When the surety pays the creditor.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: What is the limitation period for a surety to sue a principal debtor for reimbursement?

Rule.Schedule I Article 57: Starts a three-year clock on the day a cash loan was directly given to a borrower.
Rule.Schedule I Article 62: Provides three years to sue someone who receives money that technically belongs to the plaintiff.
Rule.Schedule I Article 81: Mandates a three-year limit for a guarantor (surety) to sue the actual borrower, starting on the day the guarantor pays the bank.
Rule.Schedule I Article 82: Gives three years for one guarantor to sue another person who was also a guarantor for their share of the debt.
Rule.Schedule I Article 115: Allows three years to sue for money if a general verbal or written agreement is broken.

Application.Schedule I Article 57: Fails because Salma didn't lend money to her friend; she paid his debt to the bank as a guarantor.
Application.Schedule I Article 62: Fails as this is a specific 'surety' relationship which has more precise articles in the schedule that override general money rules.
Application.Schedule I Article 81: Governs directly because Salma is a 'surety' suing a 'principal debtor' (the friend) after paying the 'creditor' (the bank) today.
Application.Schedule I Article 82: Fails as Salma is suing the person who borrowed the money rather than another co-guarantor.
Application.Schedule I Article 115: Fails because the specific Article 81 covers guarantor-debtor relationships and takes precedence over general contract rules.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I, Article 81, Salma has exactly three years to sue her friend, starting from today when she paid the bank.

Cited Acts and Sections: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I, Article 81

Question: Scenario-based: A woman discovered today that the house contract she signed last year was based on a lie. She wants to cancel the agreement entirely. How much time does she have from today?

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I Article 113
Possible Sections.Full Text: For specific performance of a contract.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I Article 114
Possible Sections.Full Text: For the rescission of a contract. One year.. When the facts entitling the plaintiff to have the contract rescinded first become known to him.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I Article 91
Possible Sections.Full Text: To cancel or set aside an instrument not otherwise provided for.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I Article 95
Possible Sections.Full Text: To set aside a decree obtained by fraud, or for other relief on the ground of fraud.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I Article 115
Possible Sections.Full Text: For compensation for the breach of any contract, express or implied, not in writing registered and not herein specially provided for.

Relevant Section: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I Article 114
Section Text: For the rescission of a contract. One year.. When the facts entitling the plaintiff to have the contract rescinded first become known to him.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: What is the limitation period for the rescission of a contract from the date the grounds are discovered?

Rule.Schedule I Article 113: Provides one year to force someone to finish a contract (specific performance) from the agreed date or refusal.
Rule.Schedule I Article 114: Sets a one-year deadline to cancel or end a contract (rescission) after finding out about a lie or mistake.
Rule.Schedule I Article 91: Gives three years to cancel any signed document (instrument) that does not fit into a more specific category.
Rule.Schedule I Article 95: Allows three years to cancel a final court judgment (a decree) that was won through fraud.
Rule.Schedule I Article 115: Allows three years to sue for money if a verbal or simple written contract is broken.

Application.Schedule I Article 113: Fails because the woman wants to end the deal (rescind it) rather than force the other person to complete the sale.
Application.Schedule I Article 114: Governs directly because she wants the 'rescission of a contract' and the clock started today when the facts 'first became known' to her.
Application.Schedule I Article 91: Fails as 'rescission of a contract' is specifically provided for in Article 114, which provides a shorter, more precise deadline.
Application.Schedule I Article 95: Fails as the woman is challenging a contract agreement rather than a formal judgment issued by a court.
Application.Schedule I Article 115: Fails because she is seeking to cancel the whole deal rather than just asking for money damages for a breach.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I, Article 114, the woman has exactly one year to sue to cancel the contract, starting from today when she learned about the lie.

Cited Acts and Sections: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I, Article 114

Question: Scenario-based: A Muslim woman asked her husband for her 'exigible' dower today, but he flatly refused to pay. How much time does she have to sue him?

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I Article 103
Possible Sections.Full Text: By a Muslim for exigible dower (*mu’ajjal*).

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I Article 104
Possible Sections.Full Text: By a Muslim for deferred dower (*mu’wajjal*). Three years.. When the marriage is dissolved by death or divorce.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I Article 115
Possible Sections.Full Text: For compensation for the breach of any contract, express or implied, not in writing registered and not herein specially provided for.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I Article 120
Possible Sections.Full Text: Suit for which no period of limitation is provided elsewhere in this schedule. Six years.. When the right to sue accrues.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I Article 7
Possible Sections.Full Text: For the wages of a household servant, artisan or labourer. One year.. When the wages accrue due.

Relevant Section: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I Article 103
Section Text: By a Muslim for exigible dower (*mu’ajjal*). Three years.. When the dower is demanded and refused or (where, during the continuance of the marriage no such demand has been made) when the marriage is dissolved by death or divorce.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: When does the limitation period begin for a suit for exigible dower after a demand is made?

Rule.Schedule I Article 103: Sets a three-year limit for exigible dower, starting exactly when a demand for payment is refused.
Rule.Schedule I Article 104: Provides three years for deferred dower, but the clock only starts after death or a divorce happens.
Rule.Schedule I Article 115: Gives three years for breaking general verbal or simple written contracts not listed elsewhere.
Rule.Schedule I Article 120: A six-year catch-all deadline for any case that is not specifically named in the other articles.
Rule.Schedule I Article 7: Requires household workers or laborers to sue for their unpaid salary within only one year.

Application.Schedule I Article 103: Governs directly because the dower is 'exigible' (on-demand) and the woman made a demand today that was refused.
Application.Schedule I Article 104: Fails because it only applies to 'deferred' dower, which doesn't start counting until the marriage ends.
Application.Schedule I Article 115: Fails as dower is a specific legal category that has its own articles (103 and 104) which override general contract rules.
Application.Schedule I Article 120: Fails because there is a specific provision for this type of dower, so the catch-all category is not needed.
Application.Schedule I Article 7: Fails as dower is a marital right under Muslim law, not a wage for labor or domestic service.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I, Article 103, the woman has exactly three years to sue for her dower, starting from the day her husband refused her demand.

Cited Acts and Sections: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I, Article 103

Rule.Schedule I Article 62: Starts a three-year clock the moment someone receives money that technically belongs to the plaintiff.
Rule.Schedule I Article 89: Governs suits where an agent collects money for a boss but refuses to show the records or pay it back.
Rule.Schedule I Article 96: Provides three years to sue for help based on a legal mistake, starting from the day the mistake is found.
Rule.Schedule I Article 120: A six-year catch-all deadline for any case that doesn't fit into a specific article in the schedule.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I Article 96
Possible Sections.Full Text: For relief on the ground of mistake. Three years.. When the mistake becomes known to the plaintiff.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I Article 166
Possible Sections.Full Text: Under the same Code to set aside a sale in execution of a decree including any such application by a Judgment-debtor.

Relevant Section: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I Article 95
Section Text: To set aside a decree obtained by fraud, or for other relief on the ground of fraud. Three years.. When the fraud becomes known to the party wronged.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: What is the limitation period for challenging a court decree obtained through fraud?

Rule.Schedule I Article 91: Provides three years to cancel a general document (instrument) after finding out about a lie or threat used to sign it.
Rule.Schedule I Article 95: Allows three years to cancel a court's final judgment (decree) if it was won through fraud or lies.
Rule.Schedule I Article 92: Provides three years specifically for declaring that a registered document is a fake forgery.
Rule.Schedule I Article 96: Provides three years to seek legal help if a document or contract was made by an honest mistake.
Rule.Schedule I Article 166: Gives only thirty days to ask a court to cancel an auction sale of property that was done incorrectly.

Application.Schedule I Article 91: Fails because the man is challenging the whole court judgment (decree), not just a single paper contract or deed.
Application.Schedule I Article 95: Governs directly as it is the specific rule for setting aside a 'decree obtained by fraud' after the discovery of the fake receipt.
Application.Schedule I Article 92: Fails because the focus is on the fraudulent court result itself, rather than just the registration of the fake receipt.
Application.Schedule I Article 96: Fails because using a fake receipt in court is an intentional act of 'fraud', not an accidental 'mistake'.
Application.Schedule I Article 166: Fails because there was no mention of an auction or property sale; the issue is a simple fraudulent judgment in a trial.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I, Article 95, the man has three years to sue to cancel the judgment, starting from the day he discovered that the receipt was fake.

Cited Acts and Sections: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I, Article 95

Question: Scenario-based: A woman discovered today that a contract she signed last month was based on a lie. She wants to cancel the agreement entirely. How much time does she have from today?

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I Article 113
Possible Sections.Full Text: For specific performance of a contract.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I Article 114
Possible Sections.Full Text: For the rescission of a contract. One year.. When the facts entitling the plaintiff to have the contract rescinded first become known to him.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I Article 91
Possible Sections.Full Text: To cancel or set aside an instrument not otherwise provided for.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I Article 95
Possible Sections.Full Text: To set aside a decree obtained by fraud, or for other relief on the ground of fraud.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I Article 115
Possible Sections.Full Text: For compensation for the breach of any contract, express or implied, not in writing registered and not herein specially provided for.

Relevant Section: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I Article 114
Section Text: For the rescission of a contract. One year.. When the facts entitling the plaintiff to have the contract rescinded first become known to him.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: What is the limitation period for the rescission of a contract from the date the grounds are discovered?

Rule.Schedule I Article 113: Provides one year to force someone to finish a contract they agreed to, starting from the agreed date or refusal.
Rule.Schedule I Article 114: Sets a one-year deadline to cancel or end a contract (rescission) after finding out about a lie or mistake used in the deal.
Rule.Schedule I Article 91: Gives three years to cancel any signed document (instrument) that does not fit into a more specific professional or topic category.
Rule.Schedule I Article 95: Allows three years to cancel a final court judgment (a decree) that was won through lies and fraud.
Rule.Schedule I Article 115: Allows three years to sue for money if a verbal or simple written contract is broken by the other party.

Application.Schedule I Article 113: Fails because the woman wants to end the deal (rescind it) rather than force the other person to complete the sale.
Application.Schedule I Article 114: Governs directly because she wants the 'rescission of a contract' and the clock started today when the facts 'first became known' to her.
Application.Schedule I Article 91: Fails as 'rescission of a contract' is specifically provided for in Article 114, which provides a shorter, more precise deadline than general documents.
Application.Schedule I Article 95: Fails as the woman is challenging a contract agreement rather than a formal decree or order issued by a court.
Application.Schedule I Article 115: Fails because she is seeking to cancel the whole deal rather than just asking for money damages for a breach of its terms.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I, Article 114, the woman has exactly one year to sue to cancel the contract, starting from today when she learned about the lie.

Cited Acts and Sections: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I, Article 114

Question: Scenario-based: A family mortgaged their land sixty years ago. They now want to pay the debt and get the house back, but the lender refuses. Is it too late for them to sue for redemption?

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I Article 132
Possible Sections.Full Text: To enforce payment of money charged upon immoveable property. Twelve years.. When the money sued for becomes due.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I Article 142
Possible Sections.Full Text: Against a mortgagee to redeem or to recover possession of immoveable property mortgaged.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I Article 148
Possible Sections.Full Text: Against a mortgagee to redeem or to recover possession of immoveable property mortgaged.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I Article 144
Possible Sections.Full Text: Against a mortgagee to redeem or to recover possession of immoveable property mortgaged.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I Article 120
Possible Sections.Full Text: Suit for which no period of limitation is provided elsewhere in this schedule. Six years.. When the right to sue accrues.

Relevant Section: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I Article 148
Section Text: Against a mortgagee to redeem or to recover possession of immoveable property mortgaged. Sixty years.. When the right to redeem or to recover possession accrues.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: What is the limitation period for a borrower to redeem mortgaged property from a lender?

Rule.Schedule I Article 132: Allows a lender twelve years to sue to recover money that is secured or charged against a property.
Rule.Schedule I Article 142: Provides a twelve-year limit for an owner to sue to get their land back after being forcibly evicted.
Rule.Schedule I Article 148: Mandates a sixty-year window for a mortgagor to pay off their debt and recover possession of their mortgaged land.
Rule.Schedule I Article 144: Sets a twelve-year limit for general land recovery cases based on an intruder's hostile or 'adverse' stay.
Rule.Schedule I Article 120: A six-year general catch-all deadline used only when no other article in the schedule fits the situation.

Application.Schedule I Article 132: Fails because it governs the lender's right to the money, not the borrower's right to get the land back.
Application.Schedule I Article 142: Fails because the family isn't suing because they were 'dispossessed' by force; they gave the land as security for a loan.
Application.Schedule I Article 148: Governs directly as it covers suits 'against a mortgagee to redeem' and provides the specific sixty-year period required for this scenario.
Application.Schedule I Article 144: Fails as Article 148 is a more specific provision for mortgages, taking priority over general adverse possession rules.
Application.Schedule I Article 120: Fails since mortgage redemption is explicitly provided for in Article 148, removing it from the catch-all category.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I, Article 148, the family has sixty years to redeem their mortgage — if sixty years have already passed since they could pay, they are too late.

Cited Acts and Sections: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I, Article 148

Question: Scenario-based: Babul was falsely accused of a crime by a jealous rival. After a long trial, the court declared him innocent last week. How much time does Babul have to sue his rival for this malicious act?

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I Article 22
Possible Sections.Full Text: For compensation for any other injury to the person. One year.. When the injury is committed.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I Article 23
Possible Sections.Full Text: For compensation for a malicious prosecution. One year.. When the plaintiff is acquitted, or the prosecution is otherwise terminated.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I Article 24
Possible Sections.Full Text: For compensation for libel. One year.. When the libel is published.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I Article 19
Possible Sections.Full Text: For compensation for false imprisonment. One year.. When the imprisonment ends.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I Article 36
Possible Sections.Full Text:
For compensation for any malfeasance, misfeasance or non-feasance independent of contract and not herein specially provided for.
*Part VI.—Three years.*

Relevant Section: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I Article 23
Section Text: For compensation for a malicious prosecution. One year.. When the plaintiff is acquitted, or the prosecution is otherwise terminated.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: What is the limitation period and starting trigger for a malicious prosecution lawsuit?

Rule.Schedule I Article 22: Starts a one-year limit on the very day a physical injury like an assault or a beating is committed.
Rule.Schedule I Article 23: Gives one year to sue for malicious prosecution, starting only when the court case ends or the person is acquitted.
Rule.Schedule I Article 24: Sets a one-year limit for suing over written lies, counting from the day the defamatory words were first published.
Rule.Schedule I Article 19: Starts a one-year clock on the day a person is set free from being locked up against their will.
Rule.Schedule I Article 36: Acts as a two-year general limit for civil wrongs that don't have their own specific category in the schedule.

Application.Schedule I Article 22: Fails because being falsely accused in court is a legal wrong, not a physical injury to the body like a broken bone.
Application.Schedule I Article 23: Governs directly as the core issue is 'malicious prosecution' and the clock started last week when Babul was officially acquitted.
Application.Schedule I Article 24: Fails because Babul is suing over the whole court process used against him, not just a specific lie printed in a newspaper.
Application.Schedule I Article 19: Fails as Babul was facing a trial, not necessarily being held in a private room or jail without any trial at all.
Application.Schedule I Article 36: Fails because malicious prosecution has its own specific article (23) which provides a more precise rule than this general category.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I, Article 23, Babul has exactly one year to sue for compensation, starting from the day he was acquitted by the court.

Cited Acts and Sections: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I, Article 23

Question: Scenario-based: A tenant has a legal right to use a backyard for gardening, but he instead started a small cement-making factory there yesterday. How long does the landlord have to sue?

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I Article 39
Possible Sections.Full Text: For compensation for trespass upon immoveable property. Three years.. The date of the trespass.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I Article 32
Possible Sections.Full Text: Against one who, having a right to use property for specific purposes, perverts it to other purposes.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I Article 142
Possible Sections.Full Text: Against one who, having a right to use property for specific purposes, perverts it to other purposes.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I Article 115
Possible Sections.Full Text: For compensation for the breach of any contract, express or implied, not in writing registered and not herein specially provided for.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I Article 41
Possible Sections.Full Text: To restrain waste. Three years. When the waste begins.

Relevant Section: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I Article 32
Section Text: Against one who, having a right to use property for specific purposes, perverts it to other purposes. Two years.. When the perversion first becomes known to the person injured thereby.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: What is the limitation period for a suit against a person who misuses property for an unauthorized purpose?

Rule.Schedule I Article 39: Gives three years to sue for trespass when someone enters land without any legal right at all.
Rule.Schedule I Article 32: Mandates a two-year limit for suits when someone with a legal right to use property uses it for the wrong purpose (perversion).
Rule.Schedule I Article 142: Provides twelve years for an owner to sue to get their land back after being kicked off entirely.
Rule.Schedule I Article 115: Allows three years to sue for money if a verbal or simple written contract is broken.
Rule.Schedule I Article 41: Sets a three-year deadline for a suit to stop someone from destroying land or allowing it to fall into ruin (waste).

Application.Schedule I Article 39: Fails because the tenant is on the land legally as a renter; he is not a 'trespasser' without rights.
Application.Schedule I Article 32: Governs directly because the tenant has a right to use the yard (gardening) but has 'perverted' it to a different purpose (factory).
Application.Schedule I Article 142: Fails as the landlord is not trying to evict the tenant for having no title, but is suing over the specific misuse of the land.
Application.Schedule I Article 115: Fails as property perversion is a specific legal category with its own shorter deadline in Article 32, which overrides general contract rules.
Application.Schedule I Article 41: Fails as the claim is about the 'purpose' of the usage rather than the physical destruction or 'waste' of the soil or building.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I, Article 32, the landlord has exactly two years to sue, starting from today when he found out about the cement factory.

Cited Acts and Sections: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I, Article 32

Question: Scenario-based: A domestic worker left her job at a family home last month but was never paid her final wages. How long does she have to sue for the money?

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I Article 7
Possible Sections.Full Text: For the wages of a household servant, artisan or labourer. One year.. When the wages accrue due.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I Article 102
Possible Sections.Full Text: For wages not otherwise expressly provided for by this schedule. Three years.. When the wages accrue due.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I Article 115
Possible Sections.Full Text: For compensation for the breach of any contract, express or implied, not in writing registered and not herein specially provided for.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I Article 56
Possible Sections.Full Text: For the price of work done by the plaintiff for the defendant at his request, where no time has been fixed for payment. Three years.. When the work is done.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I Article 120
Possible Sections.Full Text: Suit for which no period of limitation is provided elsewhere in this schedule. Six years.. When the right to sue accrues.

Relevant Section: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I Article 7
Section Text: For the wages of a household servant, artisan or labourer. One year.. When the wages accrue due.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: What is the limitation period for a domestic worker to recover unpaid wages?

Rule.Schedule I Article 7: Requires household servants and laborers to file suits for unpaid wages within a strict one-year period.
Rule.Schedule I Article 102: Provides a three-year deadline for wage claims that do not involve domestic help or manual laborers.
Rule.Schedule I Article 115: Allows for three years to sue for money if a verbal or simple written employment contract is breached.
Rule.Schedule I Article 56: Sets a three-year limit for general work performed at a request when no payment date was agreed upon.
Rule.Schedule I Article 120: Functions as a six-year catch-all deadline for any lawsuits not specifically categorized elsewhere in the schedule.

Application.Schedule I Article 7: Governs directly because the worker is a 'household servant', which triggers the specific one-year limitation period from the date pay was due.
Application.Schedule I Article 102: Fails because it only applies if no other 'express provision' exists, and Article 7 explicitly covers household servants.
Application.Schedule I Article 115: Fails as wage claims for specific professions have their own dedicated articles that override general contract breach rules.
Application.Schedule I Article 56: Fails because domestic labor is a specific legal category (Article 7) that provides a much shorter deadline than general 'work done'.
Application.Schedule I Article 120: Fails since household wages are specifically provided for in Article 7, making the general catch-all category inapplicable.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I, Article 7, the domestic worker has exactly one year to sue for her unpaid wages, starting from the day the salary was due.

Cited Acts and Sections: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I, Article 7

Question: Scenario-based: Salman is accused of murder. To prove he had a reason to kill, the prosecutor wants to show that Salman knew the victim was having an affair with Salman's sister. Is this reason allowed in court?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Facts showing the existence of any state of mind, such as intention, knowledge, good faith, negligence, rashness, ill-will, or good-will towards any particular person, or showing the existence of any state of body or bodily feeling, are relevant, when the existence of any such state of mind or body or bodily feeling is in issue or relevant.
Any fact is relevant which shows or constitutes a motive or preparation for any fact in issue or relevant fact.

Facts which are the occasions, cause or effect, immediate or otherwise, of relevant facts, or facts in issue, or which constitute the state of things under which they happened, or which afforded an opportunity for their occurrence or transaction, are relevant.
Facts not otherwise relevant are relevant–

Facts which, though not in issue, are so connected with a fact in issue as to form part of the same transaction, are relevant, whether they occurred at the same time and place or at different times and places.

Section Text: Any fact is relevant which shows or constitutes a motive or preparation for any fact in issue or relevant fact.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is a personal grievance relevant as a motive for committing a crime?

Rule.Section 14: Governs the relevancy of internal states of mind like intent or ill-will.
Rule.Section 8: States that any fact which constitutes a motive for a fact in issue is relevant.
Rule.Section 7: Outlines relevancy for the cause, effect, or occasion of a crime.
Rule.Section 11: Determines relevancy based on inconsistency or the probability of a fact.
Rule.Section 6: Makes facts that form part of the same transaction as a fact in issue relevant.

Application.Section 14: Fails because the affair is the 'reason' (motive) for the crime, rather than the internal mental state Salman had during the murder.
Application.Section 8: Governs directly because the victim's affair provides the 'motive' for Salman to commit the murder, satisfying the first sentence of the section.
Application.Section 7: Fails since the affair is a long-term grievance rather than the immediate physical cause or occasion of the death.
Application.Section 11: Fails as the affair does not contradict the crime but rather provides a psychological reason for its occurrence.
Application.Section 6: Fails because the affair happened long before the murder rather than being part of the immediate physical transaction of the crime.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 8, the affair is relevant because it shows or constitutes a motive for the murder Salman is accused of committing.

Question: Scenario-based: A woman lost her property case six months ago because her opponent presented a deed that she just discovered was a total forgery. How long does she have to sue to cancel it?

Possible Sections.Full Text: To cancel or set aside an instrument not otherwise provided for.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I Article 92
Possible Sections.Full Text: To declare the forgery of an instrument issued or registered. Three years.. When the issue or registration becomes known to the plaintiff.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I Article 93
Possible Sections.Full Text: To declare the forgery of an instrument attempted to be enforced against the plaintiff. Three years.. The date of the attempt.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I Article 95
Possible Sections.Full Text: To set aside a decree obtained by fraud, or for other relief on the ground of fraud.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I Article 96
Possible Sections.Full Text: For relief on the ground of mistake. Three years.. When the mistake becomes known to the plaintiff.

Relevant Section: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I Article 92
Section Text: To declare the forgery of an instrument issued or registered. Three years.. When the issue or registration becomes known to the plaintiff.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: What is the limitation period for declaring a registered document to be a forgery and when does it start?

Rule.Schedule I Article 91: Gives three years to cancel a general document after finding out about a lie or threat used to sign it.
Rule.Schedule I Article 92: Provides three years for declaring that a registered document is a fake forgery, starting when the person learns of the registration.
Rule.Schedule I Article 93: Starts the three-year clock specifically on the day someone tries to use a forged document in court against you.
Rule.Schedule I Article 95: Allows three years to cancel a court judgment (a decree) if it was won through fraud or lies.
Rule.Schedule I Article 96: Provides three years to seek legal help if a document or contract was signed based on an honest mistake.

Application.Schedule I Article 91: Fails as 'forgery' of a registered document is a specific legal category with its own article (92) which overrides general cancellation rules.
Application.Schedule I Article 92: Governs directly because the deed is a registered 'instrument' and the woman seeks to declare it a 'forgery' after discovery.
Application.Schedule I Article 93: Fails because the woman is challenging the registered document itself rather than responding to a new attempt to use it in a trial.
Application.Schedule I Article 95: Fails because she is challenging the forged deed rather than asking to cancel the whole judgment (decree) of the court.
Application.Schedule I Article 96: Fails because using a fake deed is an intentional act of 'forgery' rather than an accidental or honest 'mistake'.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I, Article 92, the woman has three years to sue to cancel the forged deed, starting from today when she first learned about it.

Question: Scenario-based: I was arrested for a minor crime. I am ready to pay bail right now, but the police officer says he can only let me go if a judge orders it. Is he allowed to wait for the judge?

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 63
Possible Sections.Full Text: No person who has been arrested by a police-officer shall be discharged except on his own bond, or on bail, or under the special order of a Magistrate.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 496
Possible Sections.Full Text: A person with fraudulent intention going through the ceremony of being married, knowing that he is not thereby lawfully married.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 497
Possible Sections.Full Text: Adultery

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 61
Possible Sections.Full Text: No police-officer shall detain in custody a person arrested without warrant for a longer period than under all the circumstances of the case is reasonable, and such period shall not, in the absence of a special order of a Magistrate under section 167, exceed twenty-four hours exclusive of the time necessary for the journey from the place of arrest to the Magistrate's Court.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 498
Possible Sections.Full Text: Enticing or taking away or detaining with a criminal intent a married woman.

Relevant Section: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 496
Section Text: A person with fraudulent intention going through the ceremony of being married, knowing that he is not thereby lawfully married.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is a station officer required to release a bailable suspect who provides security before seeing a judge?

Rule.Section 63: Lists the general categories of release, including bonds, bail, and magistrate orders.
Rule.Section 496: Directs that suspects in bailable cases 'shall' be released by the officer in charge if they provide bail.
Rule.Section 497: Gives judges the choice to grant bail for serious non-bailable crimes.
Rule.Section 61: Caps warrantless police detention at exactly twenty-four hours.
Rule.Section 498: Confers the authority to grant or reduce bail to high-level courts like the Sessions Court.

Application.Section 63: Fails because it describes the 'method' of release without establishing the suspect's 'right' to it at the station level.
Application.Section 496: Governs directly because it explicitly mandates the 'officer in charge' to release the person 'shall be released on bail' if they are ready.
Application.Section 497: Fails because it relates to cases where bail is a 'favor' rather than an absolute 'right' for minor crimes.
Application.Section 61: Fails because it relates to the 'length' of detention rather than the 'duty' to release once security is offered.
Application.Section 498: Fails because it concerns the powers of 'higher' courts rather than the immediate duty of the local station officer.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, section 496, the officer is legally required to release you immediately if you are prepared to give bail for a bailable offense.

Cited Acts and Sections: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 496

Question: Scenario-based: An 8-year-old child takes an expensive watch from a neighbor's house and hides it in a flower pot. Can the child be charged with theft in a court of law?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Nothing is an offence which is done by a child above [nine] years of age and under twelve, who has not attained sufficient maturity of understanding to judge of the nature and consequences of his conduct on that occasion.
Nothing is an offence which is done by a child under nine years of age.

Nothing is an offence which is done by accident or misfortune, and without any criminal intention or knowledge in the doing of a lawful act in a lawful manner by lawful means and with proper care and caution.

Nothing is an offence which is done by a person who, at the time of doing it, by reason of unsoundness of mind, is incapable of knowing the nature of the act, or that he is doing what is either wrong or contrary to law.

Nothing is an offence by reason that it causes, or that it is intended to cause, or that it is known to be likely to cause, any harm, if that harm is so slight that no person of ordinary sense and temper would complain of such harm.

Section Text: Nothing is an offence which is done by a child under [nine] years of age.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is a child who has not reached age nine legally capable of committing a criminal offense?

Rule.Section 83: Exempts children between nine and twelve from liability if they lack sufficient mental maturity to understand their acts.
Rule.Section 82: Grants absolute immunity from criminal prosecution to any child who is under nine years of age.
Rule.Section 80: Governs accidental outcomes of lawful acts performed with due care and no criminal intent.
Rule.Section 84: Exempts individuals from liability due to clinical mental illness (insanity) rather than chronological age.
Rule.Section 95: Exempts trivial or minor harms that an ordinary person would not take to court.

Application.Section 83: Fails because the child is only eight, which falls into the absolute category rather than the conditional 'maturity' category.
Application.Section 82: Governs directly because the child's age (eight) is below the mandatory threshold of nine for criminal responsibility.
Application.Section 80: Fails because the taking of the watch was a deliberate act by the child rather than an unforeseen mechanical accident.
Application.Section 84: Fails because the child's immunity is based on their developmental stage (age) rather than a medical state of mental unsoundness.
Application.Section 95: Fails because an expensive watch is a valuable item, making its loss a significant harm worthy of complaint.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 82, the child cannot be charged with theft because the law states that nothing done by a child under nine years of age is an offense.

Section Text: For an account and a share of the profits of a dissolved partnership. Three years.. The date of the dissolution.

Rule.Schedule I Article 115: Allows for three years to sue for money if a general verbal or simple written business contract is broken.

Rule.Schedule I Article 89: Governs suits where an agent collects money for a boss but refuses to show the records.
Rule.Schedule I Article 106: Mandates a three-year limit for suing for a share of profits, starting on the day a partnership is officially dissolved.

Application.Schedule I Article 115: Fails as partnership profits are a specific legal category that has its own article (106) which overrides general contract rules.
Application.Schedule I Article 62: Fails as the claim is about a complex business accounting rather than a simple case of one person receiving money for another.
Application.Schedule I Article 89: Fails because partners are not typically in a principal-agent relationship for the purposes of dissolving a firm.
Application.Schedule I Article 106: Governs directly because the partnership ended yesterday (dissolution), triggering the three-year limit to sue for the share of profits.

Question: Scenario-based: A business partnership ended last month. One of the partners wants to sue to get his share of the final profits. How long does the law give him from the day the business ended?

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I Article 115
Possible Sections.Full Text: For compensation for the breach of any contract, express or implied, not in writing registered and not herein specially provided for.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I Article 62
Possible Sections.Full Text: For money payable by the defendant to the plaintiff for money received by the defendant for the plaintiff’s use.

Rule.Schedule I Article 115: Allows for three years to sue for money if a general verbal or simple written business contract is broken.
Rule.Schedule I Article 62: Provides three years to sue someone who receives money that technically belongs to the plaintiff.
Rule.Schedule I Article 89: Governs suits where an agent collects money for a boss but refuses to show the records.
Rule.Schedule I Article 106: Mandates a three-year limit for suing for a share of profits, starting on the day a partnership is officially dissolved.
Rule.Schedule I Article 120: A six-year general catch-all deadline used only when no other article in the schedule fits the situation.

Application.Schedule I Article 115: Fails as partnership profits are a specific legal category that has its own article (106) which overrides general contract rules.
Application.Schedule I Article 62: Fails as the claim is about a complex business accounting rather than a simple case of one person receiving money for another.
Application.Schedule I Article 89: Fails because partners are not typically in a principal-agent relationship for the purposes of dissolving a firm.
Application.Schedule I Article 106: Governs directly because the partnership ended last month (dissolution), triggering the three-year limit to sue for the share of profits.
Application.Schedule I Article 120: Fails since partnership profits are already specifically provided for in Article 106, removing it from the catch-all category.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I, Article 106, the partner has exactly three years to sue for his share of the profits, starting from the day the partnership was dissolved.

Rule.Schedule I Article 22: Applies a one-year limit for physical injuries to a person's body, starting on the day the injury happened.
Rule.Schedule I Article 19: Governs a one-year filing clock for illegal confinement, starting only after the victim is set free.
Rule.Schedule I Article 24: Sets a one-year deadline for suing over written lies, counting from the day the words were published.
Rule.Schedule I Article 36: Establishes a two-year general deadline for civil wrongs that don't involve contracts and aren't listed in other articles.

Application.Schedule I Article 22: Fails because breaking equipment is property damage rather than a physical strike or bodily harm to the neighbor himself.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I, Article 36, the neighbor has exactly two years to sue for compensation, starting from the day the equipment was actually broken.

Rule.Schedule I Article 115: Allows three years to sue for money if a verbal or simple written contract is broken by the other party.

Question: Scenario-based: A judge decided to release an accused person on bail for a very serious crime. The victim thinks the judge was wrong and wants a higher court to cancel the bail and put the person back in jail. Can a higher court do this?

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 498
Possible Sections.Full Text: Enticing or taking away or detaining with a criminal intent a married woman.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 497
Possible Sections.Full Text: Adultery

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 502
Possible Sections.Full Text: Sale of printed or engraved substance containing defamatory matter, knowing it to contain such matter.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 63
Possible Sections.Full Text: No person who has been arrested by a police-officer shall be discharged except on his own bond, or on bail, or under the special order of a Magistrate.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 491
Possible Sections.Full Text: Being bound to attend on or supply the wants of a person who is helpless from youth, unsoundness of mind or disease, and voluntarily omitting to do so.

Relevant Section: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 497
Section Text: (5) The High Court Division or Court of Session and, in the case of a person released by itself, any other Court may cause any person who has been released under this section to be arrested and commit him to custody.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Does a superior court have the power to cancel a person's bail and order their re-arrest?

Rule.Section 498: Grants high-level courts the power to reduce excessive bail or grant bail during an appeal.
Rule.Section 497: Authorizes specific higher courts (High Court or Session) to cancel a person's bail and commit them back to custody.
Rule.Section 502: Allows the people who provided the money (sureties) to ask the judge to end their financial responsibility.
Rule.Section 63: Sets out the formal rules for how a suspect can be legally released from police station cells.
Rule.Section 491: Governs the rescue of people who are being held illegally through the power of Habeas Corpus.

Application.Section 498: Fails because it focuses on 'granting' or 'reducing' bail rather than 'canceling' it and ordering a re-arrest.
Application.Section 497: Governs directly because Subsection (5) explicitly empowers the High Court and Court of Session to 'cause any person who has been released... to be arrested and commit him to custody.'
Application.Section 502: Fails because it deals with the rights of the 'guarantors' rather than the court's power to punish a suspect by revoking bail.
Application.Section 63: Fails because it defines the 'method' of release rather than the 'power' of a higher court to reverse that release.
Application.Section 491: Fails because the person is 'free' on bail and the victim wants them 'detained,' which is the opposite of a Habeas Corpus petition.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, section 497, Subsection (5), the High Court Division or Court of Session has the legal power to cancel the person's bail and order them to be arrested and put back in custody.

Cited Acts and Sections: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 497(5)

Question: Scenario-based: A 7-year-old child picks up a gold ring from a shop and hides it. Can the child be charged with the crime of theft?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Nothing is an offence which is done by a child above [nine] years of age and under twelve, who has not attained sufficient maturity of understanding to judge of the nature and consequences of his conduct on that occasion.

Nothing is an offence which is done by accident or misfortune, and without any criminal intention or knowledge in the doing of a lawful act in a lawful manner by lawful means and with proper care and caution.

Nothing is an offence which is done by a person who, at the time of doing it, by reason of unsoundness of mind, is incapable of knowing the nature of the act, or that he is doing what is either wrong or contrary to law.
Nothing is an offence which is done by a child under [nine] years of age.

Nothing is an offence which is done by a person who, at the time of doing it, is, by reason of intoxication, incapable of knowing the nature of the act, or that he is doing what is either wrong, or contrary to law: provided that the thing which intoxicated him was administered to him without his knowledge or against his will.

Section Text: Nothing is an offence which is done by a child under [nine] years of age.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is a seven-year-old child legally capable of committing a criminal offense?

Rule.Section 80: Exempts acts resulting from accidents or misfortune when performed lawfully and with proper care.
Rule.Section 82: Provides total immunity from criminal liability for any child who has not yet reached the age of nine.
Rule.Section 83: Grants conditional immunity to children aged nine to twelve based on their individual maturity and understanding.
Rule.Section 84: Exempts individuals from liability if mental illness prevents them from understanding the nature or wrongfulness of their act.
Rule.Section 85: Protects individuals from punishment if they are involuntarily intoxicated and unable to judge their conduct.

Application.Section 80: Fails because stealing is an intentional act, not a lawful act performed with care that resulted in an accident.
Application.Section 82: Governs directly because the child is seven years old, which falls below the mandatory minimum age of nine for criminal responsibility.
Application.Section 83: Fails because it only applies to children who have reached at least nine years of age, which this child has not.
Application.Section 84: Fails because the child's immunity is based on their chronological age rather than a medical state of mental unsoundness.
Application.Section 85: Fails because there is no evidence that the child's actions were driven by involuntary intoxication.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 82, the child cannot be charged because the law states that nothing done by a child under nine years of age is considered an offense.

Question: Scenario-based: I committed a very serious crime (a non-bailable one), but I am only fifteen years old. Does the judge have any power to let me go on bail because of my age?

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 496

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 498
Possible Sections.Full Text: Enticing or taking away or detaining with a criminal intent a married woman.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 63
Possible Sections.Full Text: No person who has been arrested by a police-officer shall be discharged except on his own bond, or on bail, or under the special order of a Magistrate.

Relevant Section: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 497
Section Text: Adultery

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Can a court grant bail for a non-bailable offense based on the suspect's age?

Rule.Section 496: Confers a mandatory right to bail for suspects in minor cases, but specifically excludes non-bailable offenses.
Rule.Section 497: Authorizes judges to grant bail in non-bailable cases specifically for persons under sixteen years old.
Rule.Section 29B: Defines the 'jurisdiction' (which court hears the case) for children under fifteen rather than their 'right to bail.'
Rule.Section 498: Gives higher courts the general authority to adjust bail conditions or order release.
Rule.Section 63: Sets out the formal documents required to release someone from police custody.

Application.Section 496: Fails because the crime is explicitly 'non-bailable,' making the automatic bail rule inapplicable.
Application.Section 497: Governs directly because the fifteen-year-old suspect falls under the 'under sixteen' exception in the proviso, allowing for discretionary bail.
Application.Section 29B: Fails because it explains who will 'be the judge' for the child rather than whether the child can 'go home' on bail.
Application.Section 498: Fails because it relates to general high court powers rather than the specific age-based exception required by the scenario.
Application.Section 63: Fails because it defines the 'method' of release rather than the 'legal reason' for granting bail to a minor.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, section 497, Subsection (1), the judge has the power to release you on bail because you are under the age of sixteen, even for a serious crime.

Question: Scenario-based: A woman wants to file a case to get back 10,000 Taka she lent to a friend. Can she file this case directly in the High Court to ensure a faster decision?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Where payment on account of a debt or of interest on a legacy is made before the expiration of the prescribed period by the person liable to pay the debt or legacy, or by his duly authorized agent, a fresh period of limitation shall be computed from the time when the payment was made: Provided that, save in the case of a payment of interest made before the 1st day of January, 1928, an acknowledgment of the payment appears in the handwriting of, or in a writing signed by the person making the payment.

In computing the period of limitation prescribed for a suit for possession by a purchaser at a sale in execution of a decree, the time during which a proceeding to set aside the sale has been prosecuted shall be excluded.
Against a carrier for compensation for losing or injuring goods.
Every suit shall be instituted in the Court of the lowest grade competent to try it.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Which rank of court is the mandatory starting point for initiating a civil lawsuit?

Rule.Section 20: Explains the territorial rules — which city to sue in based on residence or where the dispute happened.
Rule.Section 16: Fixes the filing location for land-related suits at the court that covers the land's physical territory.
Rule.Section 30: Grants judges the power to order people to bring papers or testimony to court for a trial.
Rule.Section 15: Mandates that every lawsuit must start in the lowest possible court level that has the legal power to hear it.
Rule.Section 32: Lists ways a court can punish people who ignore an order to come and testify, such as arrest or fines.

Application.Section 20: Fails because the woman is asking about the 'rank' or 'grade' of the court, not about which city she should go to.
Application.Section 16: Fails as the dispute is about a cash loan of 10,000 Taka, not about a house, land, or other real estate.
Application.Section 30: Fails because she is trying to start her case, not trying to get a witness to bring documents to an existing trial.
Application.Section 15: Governs directly because it forces every 'suit' (including her debt case) to begin at the 'lowest grade competent' court level.
Application.Section 32: Fails as it lists powers for forcing attendance, but doesn't define which level of court she must walk into first.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Limitation Act, 1908, section 15, the woman cannot go to the High Court first — she must start her case in the lowest rank of court authorized to hear it.

Rule.Section 34: Allows specifically empowered magistrates to deliver sentences up to seven years.

Question: Scenario-based: I am in a lawsuit about a debt. Can I just testify in court about what was written in the loan agreement, or will the judge refuse to hear me until I show the actual paper?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Oral admissions as to the contents of digital records are not relevant, unless the genuineness of the digital record produced is in question.

An admission is a statement, oral or documentary [or contained in digital record], which suggests any inference as to any fact in issue or relevant fact, and which is made by any of the persons, and under the circumstances, hereinafter mentioned.

Oral admissions as to the contents of a document are not relevant, unless and until that the party proposing to prove them shows that he is entitled to give secondary evidence of the contents of such document

Admissions are relevant and may be proved as against the person who makes them, or his representative in interest; but they can not be proved by or on behalf of the person who makes them or by his representative in interest, except in the following cases:–
Evidence may be given in any suit or proceeding of the existence or non-existence of every fact in issue and of such other fact as are hereinafter declared to be relevant, and of no others.

Rule.Section 17: Provides the general definition for what constitutes an admission.
Rule.Section 22: States that oral admissions about document contents are irrelevant unless the party shows they are entitled to use secondary evidence.

Rule.Section 5: Permits evidence of facts in issue and relevant facts.

Application.Section 21: Fails because the issue is the quality of evidence (oral vs documentary) rather than which person is speaking.
Application.Section 5: Fails as it is an enabling rule that yields to the specific exclusionary rule for proving documents.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 22, you cannot testify about what the paper said unless you first prove to the court that you are legally allowed to use secondary evidence.

Question: Scenario-based: I want to prove what was written in a digital text message. Can I just repeat the words I saw on the screen in court, or must I produce the actual digital file?

In this Act the following words and expressions are used in the following senses, unless a contrary intention appears from the context:- "Court" includes all Judges and Magistrates and all persons, except arbitrators, legally authorized to take evidence. "Fact" means and includes-

Entries in books of account [or digital record], regularly kept in the course of business, are relevant whenever they refer to a matter into which the Court has to inquire, but such statements shall not alone be sufficient evidence to charge any person with liability.

Question: Scenario-based: I am suing for damages after a car crash. I have already proved the accident happened. Can I now bring evidence of the cost of my car repairs to show the judge exactly how much money I should get?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Facts which are the occasions, cause or effect, immediate or otherwise, of relevant facts, or facts in issue, or which constitute the state of things under which they happened, or which afforded an opportunity for their occurrence or transaction, are relevant.
In suits in which damages are claimed, any fact which will enable the Court to determine the amount of damages which ought to be awarded, is relevant.
Facts not otherwise relevant are relevant–
Evidence may be given in any suit or proceeding of the existence or non-existence of every fact in issue and of such other fact as are hereinafter declared to be relevant, and of no others.

Any fact is relevant which shows or constitutes a motive or preparation for any fact in issue or relevant fact. The conduct of any party, or of any agent to any party, to any suit or proceeding, in reference to such suit or proceeding, or in reference to any fact in issue therein or relevant thereto, and the conduct of any person an offence against whom is the subject of any proceeding, is relevant, if such conduct influences or is influenced by any fact, in issue or relevant fact, and whether it was previous subsequent thereto.

Section Text: In suits in which damages are claimed, any fact which will enable the Court to determine the amount of damages which ought to be awarded, is relevant.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is evidence of repair costs relevant to determining the amount of damages in a civil suit?

Rule.Section 7: Dictates that facts showing the cause or effect of a fact in issue are relevant.
Rule.Section 12: States that any fact helping the court calculate the amount of damages is relevant in damage claims.
Rule.Section 11: Outlines relevancy based on inconsistency or the probability of a fact.
Rule.Section 5: Authorizes evidence for facts in issue and specifically declared relevant facts.
Rule.Section 8: Relates to facts showing motive, preparation, or conduct.

Application.Section 7: Fails because repair costs are a measure of financial loss rather than a physical 'effect' like a dent on the car.
Application.Section 12: Governs directly because the repair bills help the court 'determine the amount of damages' to be awarded.
Application.Section 11: Fails as the issue isn't logical contradiction but rather the quantification of a proven loss.
Application.Section 5: Fails because it is a general enabling section that doesn't target the specific logic of awarding damages.
Application.Section 8: Fails since repair costs do not show Salman's motive for hitting the car or his preparation for the act.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 12, your repair costs are relevant because they help the court decide the correct amount of money to award you for damages.

Question: Scenario-based: Salim locks Rahim in a small garden shed and throws away the key. Rahim cannot leave the shed at all. What is the correct legal term for this act of trapping someone in a limited space?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Whoever voluntarily obstructs any person so as to prevent that person from proceeding in any direction in which that person has a right to proceed, is said wrongfully to restrain that person. Exception. The obstruction of a private way over land or water which a person in good faith believes himself to have a lawful right to obstruct, is not an offence within the meaning of this section.
Whoever wrongfully restrains any person in such a manner as to prevent that person from proceeding beyond certain circumscribing limits, is said "wrongfully to confine" that person.

Whoever wrongfully confines any person, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to one year, or with fine which may extend to one thousand taka, or with both.
Kidnapping is of two kinds: kidnapping from Bangladesh, and kidnapping from lawful guardianship.
Whoever by force compels, or by any deceitful means induces, any person to go from any place, is said to abduct that person.

Section Text: Whoever wrongfully restrains any person in such a manner as to prevent that person from proceeding beyond certain circumscribing limits, is said "wrongfully to confine" that person.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: How is the total restriction of movement within a specific boundary legally classified?

Rule.Section 339: Governs the partial restriction of movement in one or more directions (restraint).
Rule.Section 340: Defines wrongful confinement as preventing a person from moving 'beyond certain circumscribing limits' (total restriction).
Rule.Section 342: Sets out the one-year jail sentence for anyone convicted of the offense of confinement.
Rule.Section 359: Governs the types of kidnapping involving the removal of persons from the country or guardianship.
Rule.Section 362: Governs abduction, which focuses on the forced movement to a new location rather than entrapment.

Application.Section 339: Fails because Rahim was totally blocked from all directions rather than just one specific path.
Application.Section 340: Governs directly because locking Rahim in a shed constitutes preventing him from going 'beyond circumscribing limits', meeting the definition of confinement.
Application.Section 342: Fails because it is the penalty provision rather than the section that defines the act as 'wrongful confinement'.
Application.Section 359: Fails because Salim did not take Rahim to a different country or out of a guardian's care.
Application.Section 362: Fails because the core of the offense was the trapping of Rahim rather than forcing him to travel to a new place.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 340, Salim's act is legally defined as 'wrongful confinement' because he prevented Rahim from moving beyond the walls of the shed.

Question: Scenario-based: Rahim is accused of stealing a car. Can the prosecutor show the judge that Rahim has been convicted of three other car thefts in the last five years?

Possible Sections.Full Text: When there is a question whether an act was accidental or intentional, or done with a particular knowledge or intention, the fact that such act formed part of a series of similar occurrences, in each of which the person doing the act was concerned, is relevant.

Facts showing the existence of any state of mind, such as intention, knowledge, good faith, negligence, rashness, ill-will, or good-will towards any particular person, or showing the existence of any state of body or bodily feeling, are relevant, when the existence of any such state of mind or body or bodily feeling is in issue or relevant.
Any fact is relevant which shows or constitutes a motive or preparation for any fact in issue or relevant fact.
Facts not otherwise relevant are relevant–
Evidence may be given in any suit or proceeding of the existence or non-existence of every fact in issue and of such other fact as are hereinafter declared to be relevant, and of no others.

Section Text: Explanation 2 –But where, upon the trial of a person accused of an offence, the previous commission by the accused of an offence is relevant within the meaning of this section, the previous conviction of such person shall also be a relevant fact.
