Rule.Section 14: Outlines relevancy for state of mind and explicitly includes previous convictions as relevant facts if the prior offense is relevant.
Rule.Section 8: Relates to facts showing motive, preparation, or conduct influenced by an issue.
Rule.Section 11: Determines relevancy based on logical inconsistency or the probability of a fact.
Rule.Section 5: Authorizes evidence for facts in issue and specifically declared relevant facts.

Application.Section 15: Fails because theft is an act of dishonest intent rather than a question of whether the car was taken 'by accident.'
Application.Section 14: Governs directly because the previous car thefts are relevant to proving intent, and Explanation 2 specifically admits the formal 'conviction' as a relevant fact.
Application.Section 8: Fails since past crimes are used to prove current intent rather than being preparation for the latest theft.
Application.Section 11: Fails as the prior convictions do not contradict the current crime but rather suggest a pattern of behavior.
Application.Section 5: Fails as it is a general enabling section that does not provide the specific rule for criminal history relevancy.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 14, Explanation 2, the previous convictions are relevant facts because they show your past commission of similar offences used to prove your state of mind.

Question: Scenario-based: A buyer and seller agreed on a specific date, March 1st, for the transfer of a house, but the seller now refuses to sign the deed. How long does the buyer have to sue?

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I Article 114
Possible Sections.Full Text: For the rescission of a contract. One year.. When the facts entitling the plaintiff to have the contract rescinded first become known to him.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I Article 115
Possible Sections.Full Text: For compensation for the breach of any contract, express or implied, not in writing registered and not herein specially provided for.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I Article 113
Possible Sections.Full Text: For specific performance of a contract. One year.. The date fixed for the performance, or, if no such date is fixed, when the plaintiff has notice that performance is refused.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I Article 116
Possible Sections.Full Text: For compensation for the breach of any contract in writing registered. Six years.. When the period of limitation would begin to run against a suit brought on a similar contract not registered.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I Article 132
Possible Sections.Full Text: To enforce payment of money charged upon immoveable property. Twelve years. When the money sued for becomes due.

Relevant Section: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I Article 113
Section Text: For specific performance of a contract. One year.. The date fixed for the performance, or, if no such date is fixed, when the plaintiff has notice that performance is refused.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: What is the limitation period for a suit for specific performance when a fixed date was set in the contract?

Rule.Schedule I Article 114: Provides one year to sue to cancel or end a contract (rescission) after finding out about a lie or mistake.
Rule.Schedule I Article 115: Gives three years to sue for money (compensation) if a verbal or simple written contract is broken.
Rule.Schedule I Article 113: Mandates a one-year limit to force the other person to fulfill the contract (specific performance), starting from the agreed date.
Rule.Schedule I Article 116: Allows six years to sue for money if the contract was officially registered with the government.
Rule.Schedule I Article 132: Provides twelve years to get money back if the loan was specifically backed by a house or land (a charge).

Application.Schedule I Article 114: Fails because the buyer wants to finish the sale and get the house, not cancel the deal entirely.
Application.Schedule I Article 115: Fails because specific performance has its own specific article (113), which takes priority over this general contract breach rule.
Application.Schedule I Article 113: Governs directly because the buyer wants the house itself (specific performance) and the clock started on the fixed date of March 1st.
Application.Schedule I Article 116: Fails as the buyer isn't just seeking money damages; they are seeking the physical transfer of the property.
Application.Schedule I Article 132: Fails because this isn't a debt collection case where land was used as security; it's a simple house sale dispute.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I, Article 113, the buyer has exactly one year to sue for the house, starting from the fixed performance date of March 1st.

Cited Acts and Sections: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I, Article 113

Question: Scenario-based: I want to challenge my conviction from the Sessions Court. How many days from the date of the order do I have to file my appeal in the High Court Division?

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 408
Possible Sections.Full Text: Any person convicted on a trial held by a Metropolitan Magistrate or a Magistrate of the first class may appeal to the Court of Session.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 410
Possible Sections.Full Text: Any person convicted on a trial held by a Sessions Judge or an Additional Sessions Judge may appeal to the High Court Division.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 417
Possible Sections.Full Text: (3) No appeal to the High Court Division under sub-section (1) or sub-section (2) shall be entertained after the expiry of sixty days from the date of the order of acquittal.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 412
Possible Sections.Full Text: Notwithstanding anything hereinbefore contained where an accused person has pleaded guilty and has been convicted by a Court of Session [or any Metropolitan Magistrate] or Magistrate of the first class on such plea, there shall be no appeal except as to the extent or legality of the sentence.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 418
Possible Sections.Full Text: (1) An appeal may lie on a matter of fact as well as a matter of law, except where the trial was by jury, in which case the appeal shall lie on a matter of law only.

Relevant Section: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 417
Section Text: No appeal to the High Court Division under sub-section (1) or sub-section (2) shall be entertained after the expiry of sixty days from the date of the order of acquittal.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: What is the statutory time limit for filing a criminal appeal to the High Court Division?

Rule.Section 408: Governs the authority of the Court of Session to hear appeals from first-class magistrates.
Rule.Section 410: Provides the convicted individual with the legal right to file an appeal to the High Court Division.
Rule.Section 417: Establishes a strict sixty-day deadline for filing appeals specifically against an order of acquittal.
Rule.Section 412: Restricts the right of appeal when the conviction is based on a voluntary plea of guilty by the accused.
Rule.Section 418: Governs the permissible grounds for an appeal, distinguishing between matters of fact and matters of law.

Application.Section 408: Fails because it defines the hierarchy of lower courts rather than the deadline for filing papers in the High Court.
Application.Section 410: Fails because it establishes the 'right' to appeal without setting any specific 'time limit' for the action.
Application.Section 417: Governs directly because Subsection (3) provides the explicit sixty-day quantitative threshold for the High Court to accept the appeal.
Application.Section 412: Fails because it relates to the 'nature' of the plea rather than the 'timing' of the appellate process.
Application.Section 418: Fails because it describes 'what' can be argued in an appeal rather than 'when' the appeal must be started.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, section 417, Subsection (3), you have exactly sixty days from the date of the court order to submit your appeal papers to the High Court Division.

Cited Acts and Sections: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 417(3)

Question: Scenario-based: A woman finds her 10-year-old son has accidentally broken a valuable vase in a shop. The shop owner tries to charge the child with 'mischief.' Can the child be charged?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Nothing is an offence which is done by a child under nine years of age.

Nothing is an offence which is done by a child above [nine] years of age and under twelve, who has not attained sufficient maturity of understanding to judge of the nature and consequences of his conduct on that occasion.

Nothing is an offence which is done by accident or misfortune, and without any criminal intention or knowledge in the doing of a lawful act in a lawful manner by lawful means and with proper care and caution.

Whoever, with intent to cause, or knowing that he is likely to cause, wrongful loss or damage to the public or to any person, causes the destruction of any property, or any such change in any property or in the situation thereof as destroys or diminishes its value or utility, or affects it injuriously, commits "mischief".

Nothing is an offence by reason that it causes, or that it is intended to cause, or that it is known to be likely to cause, any harm, if that harm is so slight that no person of ordinary sense and temper would complain of such harm.

Section Text: Nothing is an offence which is done by a child above nine years of age and under twelve, who has not attained sufficient maturity of understanding to judge of the nature and consequences of his conduct on that occasion.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is a 10-year-old child liable for damage caused by accident?

Rule.Section 80: Governs acts resulting from accident during a lawful act performed with due care and no criminal intent.
Rule.Section 82: Grants absolute immunity from criminal charges to any child who is under nine years old.
Rule.Section 83: Grants conditional immunity to children aged nine to twelve based on their maturity and understanding of their acts.
Rule.Section 425: Defines mischief as the intentional destruction of property to cause loss to another person.
Rule.Section 95: Governs acts that cause only trivial or slight harm that an ordinary person would ignore.

Application.Section 80: Governs partly as the act was an accident, but the law's specific rules on age provide a more direct threshold for children.
Application.Section 82: Fails because the child is ten years old, making him too old for the absolute immunity provided by this section.
Application.Section 83: Governs directly because the child is ten and the court must determine if he was mature enough to understand the 'nature and consequences' of his act.
Application.Section 425: Fails because mischief requires an 'intent' to cause loss, which is absent in a case of accidental breakage.
Application.Section 95: Fails because a valuable vase represents a significant loss that an ordinary shop owner would reasonably complain about.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 83, the child cannot be charged if he lacked the maturity to understand the consequences of his accidental act.

Question: Scenario-based: A farmer was evicted from his own land by a powerful neighbor five years ago. He wants to sue now to get his land back. How much time does he have left to sue?

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I Article 3
Possible Sections.Full Text: Under the Specific Relief Act, 1877, section 9, to recover possession of immoveable property. Six months . . When the dispossession occurs.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I Article 144
Possible Sections.Full Text: For possession of immoveable property or any interest therein not hereby otherwise specially provided for. Twelve years. When the possession of the defendant becomes adverse to the plaintiff.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I Article 148
Possible Sections.Full Text: Against a mortgagee to redeem or to recover possession of immoveable property mortgaged. Sixty years. When the right to redeem or to recover possession accrues.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I Article 142
Possible Sections.Full Text: For possession of immoveable property when the plaintiff, while in possession of the property, has been dispossessed or has discontinued the possession.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I Article 120
Possible Sections.Full Text: Suit for which no period of limitation is provided elsewhere in this schedule. Six years.. When the right to sue accrues.

Relevant Section: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I Article 142
Section Text: For possession of immoveable property when the plaintiff, while in possession of the property, has been dispossessed or has discontinued the possession. Twelve years. When the dispossession or discontinuance takes place.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: What is the standard limitation period for a suit for possession of land after a plaintiff is dispossessed?

Rule.Schedule I Article 3: Sets a very short six-month limit for a quick summary case to get land back without proving ownership.
Rule.Schedule I Article 144: Provides twelve years for land suits but only when no other more specific rule in the schedule applies.
Rule.Schedule I Article 148: Gives a very long sixty-year period for a borrower to pay back a mortgage and get their house back from a lender.
Rule.Schedule I Article 142: Provides twelve years to sue for land possession specifically after being kicked off or abandoning the property.
Rule.Schedule I Article 120: A six-year general deadline used only if no other part of the schedule covers the specific situation.

Application.Schedule I Article 3: Fails because five years have already passed, so the farmer is too late to use the six-month summary remedy.
Application.Schedule I Article 144: Fails because Article 142 is a more specific rule for 'dispossession', making 144 a secondary choice that isn't needed.
Application.Schedule I Article 148: Fails because the farmer didn't mortgage his land; he was forcibly evicted by a neighbor.
Application.Schedule I Article 142: Governs directly because the farmer was 'dispossessed' of his land, granting him a total of twelve years from that event to sue.
Application.Schedule I Article 120: Fails as land possession is already covered by Article 142, so the general six-year rule doesn't apply.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I, Article 142, the farmer has a total of twelve years from the eviction — since five years have passed, he has seven years left to sue.

Cited Acts and Sections: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I, Article 142

Question: Scenario-based: Salim gets into a minor argument with Jamil. Salim slaps Jamil across the face. Jamil experiences pain and has a red mark for an hour, but no bones are broken and he doesn't need to see a doctor. What is the maximum sentence for this slap?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Whoever, except in the case provided for by section 335, voluntarily causes grievous hurt, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine.

Whoever, except in the case provided for by section 334, voluntarily causes hurt, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to one year, or with fine which may extend to one thousand taka, or with both.

Whoever assaults or uses criminal force to any person otherwise than on grave and sudden provocation given by that person, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three months, or with fine which may extend to five hundred taka, or with both.
Whoever causes bodily pain, disease or infirmity to any person is said to cause hurt.

Nothing is an offence by reason that it causes, or that it is intended to cause, or that it is known to be likely to cause, any harm, if that harm is so slight that no person of ordinary sense and temper would complain of such harm.

Section Text: Whoever, except in the case provided for by section 334, voluntarily causes hurt, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to one year, or with fine which may extend to one thousand taka, or with both.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: What is the penalty for causing simple bodily pain without serious injury?

Rule.Section 325: Governs the punishment for 'grievous hurt' such as fractures or permanent disability.
Rule.Section 323: Governs the punishment for 'simple hurt', carrying a maximum one-year sentence.
Rule.Section 352: Governs the punishment for 'assault' where no actual physical pain or hurt is proven.
Rule.Section 319: Provides the legal definition of what constitutes the act of causing 'hurt'.
Rule.Section 95: Governs trivial harms that fall below the legal threshold for criminal prosecution.

Application.Section 325: Fails because a slap and a red mark do not meet the legal threshold for 'grievous' injury like a bone fracture.
Application.Section 323: Governs directly because the slap caused actual bodily pain (hurt), triggering the specific one-year penalty tier.
Application.Section 352: Fails because physical contact causing pain (hurt) occurred, which elevates the charge from simple assault to hurt.
Application.Section 319: Fails because it is a definition section rather than the substantive penalty section required by the question.
Application.Section 95: Fails because an unprovoked slap causing visible marks is generally considered a punishable offense rather than a triviality.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 323, Salim can be punished with a maximum of one year in prison for causing simple bodily pain to Jamil.

Question: Scenario-based: Salim and Rahim plan to rob a house together. During the robbery, Salim unexpectedly hits the owner with a heavy stick, while Rahim just watches. Is Rahim also responsible for the assault?

Possible Sections.Full Text: When a criminal act is done by several persons, in furtherance of the common intention of all, each of such persons is liable for that act in the same manner as if it were done by him alone.

Whenever an act, which is criminal only by reason of its being done with a criminal knowledge or intention, is done by several persons, each of such persons who joins in the act with such knowledge or intention is liable for the act in the same manner as if the act were done by him alone with that knowledge or intention.
The word "act" denotes as well a series of acts as a single act: the word "omission" denotes as well a series of omissions as a single omission.

When an offence is committed by means of several acts, whoever intentionally co-operates in the commission of that offence by doing any one of those acts, either singly or jointly with any other person, commits that offence.
Where several persons are engaged or concerned in the commission of a criminal act, they may be guilty of different offences by means of that act.

Section Text: When a criminal act is done by several persons, in furtherance of the common intention of all, each of such persons is liable for that act in the same manner as if it were done by him alone.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is a person liable for a crime committed by their partner during a shared criminal act?

Rule.Section 33: Clarifies that the legal definition of an act includes both single and multiple sequential actions.
Rule.Section 34: Establishes joint liability for acts done by multiple persons sharing a common criminal goal.
Rule.Section 35: Governs cases where the crime's nature depends specifically on a unique individual state of mind.
Rule.Section 37: Determines liability when a crime is achieved through the intentional cooperation of several distinct acts.
Rule.Section 38: Provides that persons acting together may be held guilty of different offenses based on their participation.

Application.Section 33: Fails because it defines what constitutes a physical act rather than assigning liability for joint criminal conduct.
Application.Section 34: Governs directly because Rahim and Salim shared a common intention to rob, making Rahim liable for Salim's associated acts.
Application.Section 35: Fails because it focuses on crimes where specific knowledge is the primary trigger, not general joint intention.
Application.Section 37: Fails because it applies to offenses made of distinct parts rather than a single act committed during a joint venture.
Application.Section 38: Fails because it addresses when members of a group should be punished differently, not the core rule of joint liability.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 34, Rahim is liable for the assault in the same manner as if he did it alone because the act was done in furtherance of their common intention.

Question: Scenario-based: Two business partners sat down and signed a document yesterday agreeing exactly how much money one owes the other after checking all their accounts. When does the deadline to sue for that balance start?

Relevant Section: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I Article 64
Section Text: For money payable to the plaintiff for money found to be due from the defendant to the plaintiff on accounts stated between them. Three years.. When the accounts are stated in writing signed by the defendant, unless where the debt is, by a simultaneous agreement in writing signed as aforesaid, made payable at a future time, and in such case, when that time arrives.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: What is the limitation period for a suit for a balance due on 'accounts stated' in writing?

Application.Schedule I Article 64: Governs directly because the partners signed a written document agreeing on a final balance, which is the exact definition of 'accounts stated'.
Application.Schedule I Article 89: Fails as the partners have already agreed on the numbers in writing; they aren't suing for a refusal to show the account.
Application.Schedule I Article 115: Fails because 'accounts stated' is a specific legal category with its own article (64) that takes priority over general contract breach rules.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I, Article 64, the deadline is three years — the time starts counting from yesterday when the signed account statement was created.

Question: Scenario-based: Salim, a foreigner visiting Dhaka, steals a camera from a shop. He argues that since he is not a citizen of Bangladesh, he cannot be punished under the national Penal Code. Is he correct?

Possible Sections.Full Text: This Act shall be called the Penal Code, and shall take effect throughout Bangladesh.
Every person shall be liable to punishment under this Code and not otherwise for every act or omission contrary to the provisions thereof, of which he shall be guilty within Bangladesh.

The provisions of this Code apply also to any offence committed by- (1) any citizen of Bangladesh in any place without and beyond Bangladesh; (2) [Omitted by Article 2 and Schedule of the Central Laws (Adaptation) Order, 1961.] (3) [Omitted by section 3 and 2nd Schedule of the Bangladesh Laws (Revision And Declaration) Act, 1973 (Act No. VIII of 1973).] (4) any person on any ship or aircraft registered in Bangladesh wherever it may be.
The word 'person' includes any Company or Association, or body of persons, whether incorporated or not.

Any person liable, by any Bangladesh Law, to be tried for an offence committed beyond Bangladesh shall be dealt with according to the provisions of this Code for any act committed beyond Bangladesh in the same manner as if such act had been committed within Bangladesh.

Section Text: Every person shall be liable to punishment under this Code and not otherwise for every act or omission contrary to the provisions thereof, of which he shall be guilty within Bangladesh.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Does the Penal Code apply to non-citizens who commit crimes while physically inside the borders of the country?

Rule.Section 1: Establishes the name and geographical scope of the Act (throughout Bangladesh).
Rule.Section 2: Establishes territorial jurisdiction, making 'every person' liable for acts committed inside the country.
Rule.Section 4: Establishes extra-territorial jurisdiction specifically for 'citizens' who commit crimes outside the country.
Rule.Section 11: Defines the word 'person' to include both natural humans and corporate entities.
Rule.Section 3: Governs the procedure for trying offenses that occurred 'beyond' the country's limits.

Application.Section 1: Fails because it establishes the law's presence but does not explicitly define the scope of personal liability for foreigners.
Application.Section 2: Governs directly because the term 'every person' is all-inclusive, and since the act happened 'within Bangladesh', Salim's nationality is irrelevant.
Application.Section 4: Fails because it applies to citizens in foreign lands, whereas Salim is a foreigner in Bangladesh.
Application.Section 11: Fails because it confirms Salim is a 'person' but does not address the jurisdictional rules for his crime.
Application.Section 3: Fails because the crime occurred in Dhaka (locally) rather than outside (beyond) the country.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 2, Salim is liable because the law applies to 'every person' who commits a crime while inside the borders of Bangladesh, regardless of their nationality.

Question: Scenario-based: A prisoner is sentenced to death for a crime. The government wants to change this to life imprisonment. Do they need the prisoner's permission to do so?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Nothing in section fifty-four or section fifty-five shall derogate from the right of the President to grant pardons, reprieves, respites or remissions of punishment.
In calculating fractions of terms of punishment, imprisonment for life shall be reckoned as equivalent to rigorous imprisonment for thirty years.

The punishments to which offenders are liable under the provisions of this Code are,- Firstly,- Death; Secondly,- [Imprisonment for life]; Thirdly,-[Omitted by the Criminal Law (Extinction of Discriminatory Privileges) Act 1949 (Act No. II of 1950]. Fourthly,-Imprisonment, which is of two descriptions, namely:-
In every case in which sentence of death shall have been passed, the Government may, without the consent of the offender, commute the punishment for any other punishment provided by this Code.

In every case in which sentence of imprisonment for life shall have been passed, the Government may, without the consent of the offender, commute the punishment for imprisonment of either description for a term not exceeding twenty years.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Does the government require an offender's consent to reduce a death sentence to a lesser penalty?

Rule.Section 53: Lists the various types of legal punishments that can be imposed on offenders by the court.

Rule.Section 55: Permits the government to reduce a life sentence to a term of up to twenty years without consent.
Rule.Section 55A: Saves the supreme power of the President to grant pardons and remissions of punishment.
Rule.Section 57: Provides a numerical value for life imprisonment solely for the purpose of calculating fractional terms.

Application.Section 53: Fails because it merely lists available punishments rather than governing the process of changing a sentence after it is passed.
Application.Section 54: Governs directly as it specifically addresses the commutation of death sentences and explicitly removes the requirement for the offender's consent.
Application.Section 55: Fails because it applies specifically to life imprisonment sentences, not the death penalty mentioned in the scenario.
Application.Section 55A: Fails because it protects the President's constitutional powers rather than regulating the general government's power of commutation.
Application.Section 57: Fails because it is a mathematical rule for calculating time and does not confer authority to change a sentence.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 54, the government may change the death sentence to any other punishment provided by the Code without the prisoner's consent.

Question: Scenario-based: A man gets into a physical fight with another person in a public market. The fight is brief, and while the other person is slightly annoyed, they are not actually hurt. Is this considered a crime under the law?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Nothing is an offence by reason that it causes, or that it is intended to cause, or that it is known to be likely to cause, any harm, if that harm is so slight that no person of ordinary sense and temper would complain of such harm.

Nothing is an offence which is done by accident or misfortune, and without any criminal intention or knowledge in the doing of a lawful act in a lawful manner by lawful means and with proper care and caution.
Nothing is an offence which is done in the exercise of the right of private defence.
When two or more persons, by fighting in a public place, disturb the public peace, they are said to "commit an affray.”

Whoever makes any gesture, or any preparation intending or knowing it to be likely that such gesture or preparation will cause any person present to apprehend that he who makes that gesture or preparation is about to use criminal force to that person, is said to commit an assault.

Section Text: Nothing is an offence by reason that it causes, or that it is intended to cause, or that it is known to be likely to cause, any harm, if that harm is so slight that no person of ordinary sense and temper would complain of such harm.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Does an act causing extremely minor harm constitute a criminal offense?

Rule.Section 95: Governs acts that cause harm so trivial that an ordinary person would not find it worthy of complaint.
Rule.Section 80: Governs acts resulting from accident or misfortune during the performance of a lawful act with due care.
Rule.Section 96: Governs acts performed specifically while exercising the legal right of private defense against an attacker.
Rule.Section 159: Governs the specific offense of affray where a public fight actually disturbs the general public peace.
Rule.Section 351: Governs the definition of assault, focusing on gestures that create an apprehension of the use of force.

Application.Section 95: Governs directly because the harm from the brief scuffle was so minor that it falls under the 'slight harm' exception.
Application.Section 80: Fails because a physical fight is an intentional and usually unlawful act rather than a lawful act resulting in an accident.
Application.Section 96: Fails because the scenario describes a mutual fight rather than a situation where one person is purely defending themselves from an unprovoked attack.
Application.Section 159: Fails because an affray requires a disturbance of the public peace; the scenario specifies only slight annoyance rather than public disorder.
Application.Section 351: Fails because actual contact occurred, whereas assault focuses on the threat or apprehension of force before it is used.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 95, the man's act is not an offense because the law ignores harm that is so slight that an ordinary person would not complain about it.

Question: Scenario-based: I was sued for a debt. I wrote an email to the creditor saying 'I admit I owe you the money, but I won't pay until next year.' Can the creditor use this to stop me from changing my story?

Possible Sections.Full Text: An admission is a statement, oral or documentary [or contained in digital record], which suggests any inference as to any fact in issue or relevant fact, and which is made by any of the persons, and under the circumstances, hereinafter mentioned.
Admissions are not conclusive proof of the matters admitted but they may operate as estoppels under the provisions hereinafter contained.

Admissions are relevant and may be proved as against the person who makes them, or his representative in interest; but they can not be proved by or on behalf of the person who makes them or by his representative in interest, except in the following cases:–

**"May presume"** Whenever it is provided by this Act that the Court may presume a fact, it may either regard such fact as proved, unless and until it is disproved, or may call for proof of it: **“Shall presume”** Whenever it is directed by this Act that the Court shall presume a fact, it shall regard such fact as proved, unless and until it is disproved: **“Conclusive proof”** When one fact is declared by this Act to be conclusive proof of another, the Court shall, on proof of the one fact, regard the other as proved, and shall not allow evidence to be given for the purpose of disproving it.
Facts not otherwise relevant are relevant–

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 31, your email is not final proof, but it can act as an 'estoppel,' which may prevent you from legally taking back what you admitted.

Question: Scenario-based: A police officer is ordered by a Magistrate to arrest a man named Kamal. After making reasonable inquiries and acting in good faith, the officer arrests a man named Suman, truly believing he is Kamal. Is the officer guilty of a crime?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Nothing is an offence which is done by any person who is justified by law, or who by reason of a mistake of fact and not by reason of a mistake of law in good faith, believes himself to be justified by law, in doing it.
Nothing is an offence which is done by a Judge when acting judicially in the exercise of any power which is, or which in good faith he believes to be, given to him by law.
Nothing is an offence which is done by a person who is, or who by reason of a mistake of fact and not by reason of a mistake of law in good faith believes himself to be, bound by law to do it.

Nothing is an offence which is done by accident or misfortune, and without any criminal intention or knowledge in the doing of a lawful act in a lawful manner by lawful means and with proper care and caution.

Nothing is an offence by reason that it causes, or that it is intended to cause, or that it is known to be likely to cause, any harm, if that harm is so slight that no person of ordinary sense and temper would complain of such harm.

Rule.Section 76: Governs mistakes of fact where a person believes they are legally 'bound' or obligated to perform an act.
Rule.Section 80: Governs acts resulting from accident or misfortune during the performance of a lawful task with due care.
Rule.Section 95: Governs trivial harms so minor that an ordinary person would not find them worthy of a legal complaint.

Application.Section 79: Fails because the officer was acting under a direct command (bound) rather than exercising a general legal right (justified).
Application.Section 77: Fails because the person in the scenario is a police officer executing an order, not a Judge delivering a verdict.
Application.Section 76: Governs directly because Illustration (b) specifically describes an officer arresting the wrong person under a court order due to a good-faith mistake.
Application.Section 80: Fails because an arrest is an intentional physical act, not an unforeseen accident or mechanical failure.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 76, Illustration (b), the officer is not guilty of a crime because he acted in good faith and made a mistake of fact while believing he was bound by law to make the arrest.

Question: Scenario-based: An army officer is accused of deserting his post during a time of conflict. He argues that he should be tried under the general Penal Code. Does the Penal Code take away the power of military laws to punish him?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Every person shall be liable to punishment under this Code and not otherwise for every act or omission contrary to the provisions thereof, of which he shall be guilty within Bangladesh.

Whoever abets the committing of mutiny by an officer, soldier, sailor or airman, in the Army, Navy or Air Force of Bangladesh, or attempts to seduce any such officer, soldier, sailor or airman from his allegiance or his duty, shall be punished with imprisonment for life, or with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine.

Whoever abets the desertion of any officer, soldier, sailor or airman, in the Army, Navy or Air Force of Bangladesh, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both.
A "special law" is a law applicable to a particular subject.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 5, the officer's argument is invalid because the Code specifically states it does not affect any Act for punishing military desertion or mutiny.

Possible Sections.Full Text: [Omitted by section 2 and Schedule of the Law Reforms Ordinance, 1978 (Ordinance No. XLIX of 1978).]

Relevant Section: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Schedule II, Section 380
Section Text: Theft in a building, tent or vessel. Arrest: May arrest without warrant. Warrant or Summons: Warrant. Compoundable: Not compoundable. Punishment: Imprisonment of either description for 7 years and fine. Bailable: Not bailable. Court_Triable: Any Judicial Magistrate.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: What is the maximum legal prison sentence for theft in a building?

Rule.Schedule II, Section 379: Lists the standard maximum punishment for simple theft as three years imprisonment.
Rule.Schedule II, Section 380: Establishes the maximum punishment for house theft specifically as seven years imprisonment.
Rule.Schedule II, Section 382: Increases the penalty to ten years if the theft involves preparation to cause death or injury.
Rule.Section 32: Limits the sentencing power of a first-class magistrate to five years, even if the crime allows more.
Rule.Section 34: Allows specially empowered magistrates to give up to seven years, matching the maximum for house theft.

Application.Section 379: Fails because it relates to simple theft, whereas the crime here is specifically theft in a 'building.'
Application.Schedule II, Section 380: Governs directly because its 'Punishment' column sets the absolute legal limit for this crime at '7 years and fine.'
Application.Section 382: Fails because the scenario doesn't mention any weapons or threats of death that would trigger the ten-year limit.
Application.Section 32: Fails because it defines the judge's 'rank' limit rather than the 'crime's' own legal maximum penalty.
Application.Section 34: Fails because it discusses magistrate powers rather than the underlying legal maximum defined for the offense itself.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Schedule II, Section 380, your lawyer is correct; an eight-year sentence is illegal because the maximum prison time for house theft is seven years.

Cited Acts and Sections: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Schedule II, Section 380

Question: Scenario-based: A business owner wants to sue a debtor for five lakh Taka. Both the Assistant Judge and the Senior Assistant Judge can hear cases of this value. Can the owner file directly with the Senior Assistant Judge?

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 15
Possible Sections.Full Text: Every suit shall be instituted in the Court of the lowest grade competent to try it.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 6
Possible Sections.Full Text: Save in so far as is otherwise expressly provided, nothing herein contained shall operate to give any Court jurisdiction over suits the amount or value of the subject-matter of which exceeds the pecuniary limits (if any) of its ordinary jurisdiction.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 9
Possible Sections.Full Text: The Courts shall (subject to the provisions herein contained) have jurisdiction to try all suits of a civil nature excepting suits of which their cognizance is either expressly or impliedly barred. Explanation.-A suit in which the right to property or to an office is contested is a suit of a civil nature, notwithstanding that such right may depend entirely on the decision of questions as to religious rites or ceremonies.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 20
Possible Sections.Full Text: Subject to the limitations aforesaid, every suit shall be instituted in a Court within the local limits of whose jurisdiction—

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 24
Possible Sections.Full Text: 24.(1) On the application of any of the parties and after notice to the parties and after hearing such of them as desire to be heard, or of its own motion without such notice, the High Court Division or the District Court may at any stage-

Relevant Section: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 15
Section Text: Every suit shall be instituted in the Court of the lowest grade competent to try it.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Must a plaintiff file in the lowest competent court when multiple grades have jurisdiction?

Rule.Section 15: Requires that every lawsuit be initiated in the court of the most junior rank capable of trying the matter.
Rule.Section 6: Restricts courts from hearing any case where the financial value of the dispute exceeds their authorized monetary limits.
Rule.Section 9: Empowers civil courts to hear all matters of a civil nature unless a specific law bars their involvement.
Rule.Section 20: Sets the territorial rules for where a suit must be filed based on residence or where the wrong occurred.
Rule.Section 24: Grants higher courts the administrative power to move a case from one lower court to another.

Application.Section 15: Governs directly because it forces the plaintiff to select the Assistant Judge as the lowest competent grade available.
Application.Section 6: Fails because it defines the upper limits of power rather than the mandatory starting point among competent options.
Application.Section 9: Does not apply as it concerns the general nature of civil cases rather than the hierarchy of court levels.
Application.Section 20: Is irrelevant because the inquiry concerns the grade of the court, not its geographic or territorial location.
Application.Section 24: Fails to govern because the scenario involves the initial filing choice, not a request for a later transfer.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, section 15, the owner must file with the Assistant Judge — the law mandates that all lawsuits be started in the court of the lowest grade that is competent to try it.

Question: Scenario-based: A man won a court case, and the final judgment was delivered today. He wants to enforce it immediately. How many years does the law give him for general enforcement?

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I Article 182
Possible Sections.Full Text: For the execution of a decree or order of any Civil Court other than,— (1) applications to which section 48 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, applied; and (2) applications provided for by Article 183.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I Article 180
Possible Sections.Full Text: By a purchaser of immoveable property at a sale in execution of a decree for delivery of possession.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I Article 166
Possible Sections.Full Text: Under the same Code to set aside a sale in execution of a decree including any such application by a Judgment-debtor.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I Article 181
Possible Sections.Full Text: Applications for which no period of limitation is provided elsewhere in this schedule or by section 48 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908.

Where the period of limitation prescribed for any suit, appeal or application expires on a day when the Court is closed, the suit, appeal or application may be instituted, preferred or made on the day that the Court re-opens.

Relevant Section: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I Article 182
Section Text: For the execution of a decree or order of any Civil Court other than,— (1) applications to which section 48 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, applied; and (2) applications provided for by Article 183. Three years; or, where a certified copy of the decree or order has been registered, six years.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: What is the standard limitation period for executing a civil court decree?

Rule.Schedule I Article 182: Mandates a three-year limit for enforcing court orders, which can be extended to six years if the decree is officially registered.
Rule.Schedule I Article 180: Sets a very strict three-day limit for an auction buyer to ask for the keys to a house they just bought at a court sale.
Rule.Schedule I Article 166: Allows an owner thirty days to ask a judge to cancel a property sale they believe was done incorrectly.
Rule.Schedule I Article 181: Establishes a three-year general deadline for any applications in court that are not specifically listed in other articles.
Rule.Section 4: Extends the deadline to the next working day if the original final day to file falls on a holiday or a day the court is shut.

Application.Schedule I Article 182: Governs directly as it is the specific rule for 'execution of a decree' and provides the standard three-year limitation period requested.
Application.Schedule I Article 180: Fails because the man is a general winner of a case, not a specifically a purchaser at a court-ordered auction sale.
Application.Schedule I Article 166: Fails as the man wants to enforce his victory (execution), rather than asking the court to cancel a sale that has already happened.
Application.Schedule I Article 181: Fails because decree execution is explicitly covered by Article 182, meaning this general catch-all category does not apply.
Application.Section 4: Fails as there is no mention of a holiday or court closure; the question is about the basic length of the time limit for enforcement.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I, Article 182, the man has exactly three years to start the execution of the decree, counting from the day the judgment was delivered.

Cited Acts and Sections: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I, Article 182

Question: Scenario-based: I cheated someone using a fake document and they lost 10,000 Taka. The police caught me and want to try me. Can a junior (third-class) magistrate handle my trial for this cheating case?

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 32
Possible Sections.Full Text: In every enactment passed before this Code comes into force the expressions "Officer exercising (or 'having') the powers (or 'the full powers') of a Magistrate," "Subordinate Magistrate, first class," and "Subordinate Magistrate, second class," shall respectively be deemed to mean "Magistrate of the first class," "Magistrate of the second class" and "Magistrate of the third class," <sup>2</sup>[***] the expression "Magistrate of the district" shall be deemed to mean "District Magistrate," <sup>3</sup>[***].

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Schedule II, Section 417
Possible Sections.Full Text: Cheating

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Schedule II, Section 420
Possible Sections.Full Text: Cheating and thereby dishonestly inducing delivery of property, or the making, alteration or destruction of a valuable security.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 28
Possible Sections.Full Text: Subject to the other provisions of this Code any offence under the Penal Code may be tried-

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 30
Possible Sections.Full Text: [Omitted by section 2 and Schedule of the Law Reforms Ordinance, 1978 (Ordinance No. XLIX of 1978).]

Relevant Section: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Schedule II, Section 417
Section Text: Cheating. Arrest: Shall not arrest without warrant. Warrant or Summons: Summons. Compoundable: compoundable. Punishment: Imprisonment of either description for 1 year, or fine, or both. Bailable: Bailable. Court_Triable: Any Judicial Magistrate.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Which magistrates are authorized to try the offense of simple cheating?

Rule.Section 32: Lists the maximum sentences a third-class magistrate can give but does not assign them specific crimes.
Rule.Schedule II, Section 417: Assigns trials for simple cheating to 'Any Judicial Magistrate,' which includes third-class magistrates.
Rule.Schedule II, Section 420: Designates more serious cheating as being triable by any judicial magistrate level as well.
Rule.Section 28: Identifies the high courts for trials but defers to Schedule II for the assignment of magistrate-level cases.
Rule.Section 30: Provides for the government to expand the trial authority of specific senior magistrates.

Application.Section 32: Fails because it defines 'sentencing power' rather than the 'jurisdiction' to hear the cheating case.
Application.Schedule II, Section 417: Governs directly because 'Any Judicial Magistrate' includes those of the third class, authorizing them to hear the cheating trial.
Application.Schedule II, Section 420: Fails because the scenario involves simple cheating for a small amount rather than the serious property inducement defined here.
Application.Section 28: Fails because its general list is superseded by the specific assignment to 'Any Judicial Magistrate' in the eighth column of Schedule II.
Application.Section 30: Fails because it concerns senior judge expansions rather than the basic statutory authority granted to all magistrates.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Schedule II, Section 417, any judicial magistrate, including a third-class magistrate, is legally authorized to handle your trial for cheating.

Cited Acts and Sections: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Schedule II, Section 417

Question: Scenario-based: A man tells a lie about his neighbor to a group of friends, calling him a 'thief' and a 'liar' just to ruin his reputation. The neighbor loses his job because of these words. Is this considered a crime?

Possible Sections.Full Text: The word "injury" denotes any harm whatever illegally caused to any person, in body, mind, reputation or property.

Whoever by words either spoken or intended to be read, or by signs or by visible representations, makes or published any imputation concerning any person intending to harm, or knowing or having reason to believe that such imputation will harm, the reputation or such person, is said, except in the cases hereinafter excepted, to defame that person.
Whoever defames another shall be punished with simple imprisonment for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both.

Whoever intentionally insults, and thereby gives provocation to any person, intending or knowing it to be likely that such provocation will cause him to break the public peace, or to commit any other offence, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both.
Whoever gives to any public servant any information which he knows or believes to be false, intending thereby to cause, or knowing it to be likely that he will thereby cause, such public servant—

Section Text: Whoever by words either spoken or intended to be read, or by signs or by visible representations, makes or published any imputation concerning any person intending to harm, or knowing or having reason to believe that such imputation will harm, the reputation or such person, is said, except in the cases hereinafter excepted, to defame that person.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is spreading false statements to harm a person's reputation a criminal offense?

Rule.Section 44: Defines the general legal term 'injury' to include harm done to a person's reputation.
Rule.Section 182: Governs the act of giving false information to a public official to cause someone trouble.
Rule.Section 499: Defines defamation as publishing any imputation with the intent to harm another person's reputation.
Rule.Section 500: Prescribes the two-year maximum prison sentence for the offense of defamation.
Rule.Section 504: Governs intentional insults meant to provoke someone into breaking the public peace.

Application.Section 44: Fails because it is a general definition of the word 'injury' and does not define the crime of defamation itself.
Application.Section 182: Fails because the man lied to his friends (private citizens) rather than providing false information to a public servant like the police.
Application.Section 499: Governs directly because the man spoke words intended to harm his neighbor's reputation, which is the exact definition of defamation.
Application.Section 500: Fails because it is the penalty section rather than the section that defines whether the man's words legally count as defamation.
Application.Section 504: Fails because the intent was to ruin the neighbor's job and name, not to provoke him into a fight or riot.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 499, the man is guilty of defamation because he spoke words intended to harm his neighbor's reputation.

Question: Scenario-based: A neighbor has a legal right to buy a nearby house before anyone else. A stranger bought it and moved in today. How long does the neighbor have to sue to buy the house instead?

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I Article 113
Possible Sections.Full Text: For specific performance of a contract. One year.. The date fixed for the performance, or, if no such date is fixed, when the plaintiff has notice that performance is refused.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I Article 12
Possible Sections.Full Text: To set aside any of the following sales:— (a) sale in execution of a decree of a Civil Court; (b) sale in pursuance of a decree or order of a Collector or other officer of revenue; (c) sale for arrears of Government revenue, or for any demand recoverable as such arrears;

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I Article 142
Possible Sections.Full Text: To enforce a right of pre-emption whether the right is founded on law, or general usage, or on special contract.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I Article 3
Possible Sections.Full Text: Under the Specific Relief Act, 1877, section 9, to recover possession of immoveable property. Six months . . When the dispossession occurs.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I Article 10
Possible Sections.Full Text: To enforce a right of pre-emption whether the right is founded on law, or general usage, or on special contract.

Relevant Section: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I Article 10
Section Text: To enforce a right of pre-emption whether the right is founded on law, or general usage, or on special contract. One year.. When the purchaser takes, under the sale sought to be impeached, physical possession of the whole of the property sold, or, where the subject of the sale does not admit of physical possession, when the instrument of sale is registered.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: What is the limitation period to enforce a right of pre-emption from the date physical possession is taken?

Rule.Schedule I Article 113: Sets a one-year limit for forcing someone to finish a contract they signed but are now ignoring.
Rule.Schedule I Article 12: Limits the time to one year for asking a court to cancel a sale that was done through a court auction.
Rule.Schedule I Article 142: Provides twelve years for a person to sue to get their own land back after being kicked off.
Rule.Schedule I Article 3: Allows a six-month window for a quick summary case to get back into property after illegal eviction.
Rule.Schedule I Article 10: Mandates a one-year limit for enforcing pre-emption rights starting when the new buyer physically takes over the property.

Application.Schedule I Article 113: Fails because the neighbor isn't enforcing their own contract, but is using a legal right to stop someone else's sale.
Application.Schedule I Article 12: Fails as this was a private sale to a stranger, not a sale forced by a court decree or a government officer.
Application.Schedule I Article 142: Fails because the neighbor was never in possession of the house and isn't claiming it was stolen from them.
Application.Schedule I Article 3: Fails because the neighbor wasn't evicted; they are simply trying to exercise a first-right-to-buy that they already had.
Application.Schedule I Article 10: Governs directly because pre-emption is the specific right at issue, and the clock starts because the buyer took physical possession today.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I, Article 10, the neighbor has exactly one year to file a suit to enforce their right to buy the house instead of the stranger.

Cited Acts and Sections: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I, Article 10

Question: Scenario-based: Salman is on trial for killing his partner. Can the prosecutor show that the partner knew Salman had committed a robbery years ago and was using that secret to blackmail him?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Facts showing the existence of any state of mind, such as intention, knowledge, good faith, negligence, rashness, ill-will, or good-will towards any particular person, or showing the existence of any state of body or bodily feeling, are relevant, when the existence of any such state of mind or body or bodily feeling is in issue or relevant.
Any fact is relevant which shows or constitutes a motive or preparation for any fact in issue or relevant fact.

Facts which are the occasions, cause or effect, immediate or otherwise, of relevant facts, or facts in issue, or which constitute the state of things under which they happened, or which afforded an opportunity for their occurrence or transaction, are relevant.

Facts which, though not in issue, are so connected with a fact in issue as to form part of the same transaction, are relevant, whether they occurred at the same time and place or at different times and places.
Facts not otherwise relevant are relevant–

Section Text: Any fact is relevant which shows or constitutes a motive or preparation for any fact in issue or relevant fact. The conduct of any party, or of any agent to any party, to any suit or proceeding, in reference to such suit or proceeding, or in reference to any fact in issue therein or relevant thereto, and the conduct of any person an offence against whom is the subject of any proceeding, is relevant, if such conduct influences or is influenced by any fact, in issue or relevant fact, and whether it was previous subsequent thereto.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Does a victim's knowledge of a suspect's prior crimes serve as a relevant motive for murder?

Rule.Section 14: Governs the relevancy of internal states of mind like intent or ill-will.
Rule.Section 8: Specifically makes any fact that constitutes a motive for a fact in issue relevant.
Rule.Section 7: Outlines relevancy for the cause, effect, or occasion of a crime.
Rule.Section 6: Makes facts that form part of the same transaction as a fact in issue relevant.
Rule.Section 11: Determines relevancy based on inconsistency or the probability of a fact's occurrence.

Application.Section 14: Fails because the blackmail situation is the 'reason' (motive) for the crime, rather than Salman's internal mental state alone.
Application.Section 8: Governs directly because the partner's blackmail attempt provides a clear 'motive' for Salman to commit the murder, as seen in Illustration (a).
Application.Section 7: Fails since the blackmail is a long-term psychological pressure rather than the immediate physical cause or occasion of the death.
Application.Section 6: Fails because the secret knowledge existed long before the murder rather than being part of the immediate physical transaction of the crime.
Application.Section 11: Fails as the blackmail does not contradict the crime but rather provides a compelling reason why Salman would commit it.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 8, Illustration (a), the secret and the blackmail are relevant because they provide a motive for the alleged crime of murder.

Question: Scenario-based: I am a judge and someone has asked me to help them. I see that the court can give help by taking property, by ordering someone to do something, or by appointing a manager. Where can I find the list of all the ways a court can give specific relief?

Possible Sections.Full Text: This Act may be called the Specific Relief Act, 1877. It extends to the whole of Bangladesh. And it shall come into force on the first day of May, 1877.

In this Act, unless there be something repugnant in the subject or context,- "obligation" includes every duty enforceable by law: "trust" includes every species of express, implied, or constructive fiduciary ownership: "trustee" includes every person holding, expressly, by implication, or constructively, a fiduciary character:
A person entitled to the possession of specific immoveable property may recover it in the manner prescribed by the Code of Civil Procedure.
Specific relief granted under clause (c) of section 5 is called preventive relief.

Specific relief is given- (a) by taking possession of certain property and delivering it to a claimant; (b) by ordering a party to do the very act which he is under an obligation to do; (c) by preventing a party from doing that which he is under an obligation not to do; (d) by determining and declaring the rights of parties otherwise than by an award of compensation; or (e) by appointing a receiver.

Section Text: Specific relief is given- (a) by taking possession of certain property and delivering it to a claimant; (b) by ordering a party to do the very act which he is under an obligation to do; (c) by preventing a party from doing that which he is under an obligation not to do; (d) by determining and declaring the rights of parties otherwise than by an award of compensation; or (e) by appointing a receiver.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Which provision of the Act enumerates the different modes of granting specific relief?

Rule.Section 1: States the name, geographic extent, and the date the law came into force.
Rule.Section 3: Provides the definitions of legal terms used throughout the Act for consistency.
Rule.Section 8: Governs the specific procedure for recovering land according to the civil procedure code.
Rule.Section 6: Defines the term 'preventive relief' as a sub-category of the relief methods.
Rule.Section 5: Lists the five specific methods through which the court provides relief to a claimant.

Application.Section 1: Fails as it deals with the statute's administration and territory rather than judicial powers.
Application.Section 3: Fails because it defines words like 'trust' rather than listing the actions a court can take.
Application.Section 8: Fails because it addresses only one specific type of property recovery rather than all court powers.
Application.Section 6: Fails as it only names one category of relief and refers back to Section 5 for the substance.
Application.Section 5: Governs directly because it is the primary source listing all five ways the court can grant specific relief.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Specific Relief Act, 1877, section 5, you will find the five methods of relief — this section lists everything from taking possession of property to appointing a receiver.

Question: Scenario-based: A man was arrested for a minor bailable crime. He has his bail money ready and is offering it to the officer at the police station. Is the officer allowed to refuse and keep him in jail until court?

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 497
Possible Sections.Full Text: Adultery

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 496
Possible Sections.Full Text: When any person other than a person accused of a non-bailable offence is arrested or detained without warrant by an officer in charge of a police-station, or appears or is brought before a Court, and is prepared at any time while in the custody of such officer or at any stage of the proceedings before such Court to give bail, such person shall be released on bail.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 63
Possible Sections.Full Text: No person who has been arrested by a police-officer shall be discharged except on his own bond, or on bail, or under the special order of a Magistrate.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 498
Possible Sections.Full Text: Enticing or taking away or detaining with a criminal intent a married woman.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 491
Possible Sections.Full Text: (1) The High Court Division may, whenever it thinks fit, direct- (b) that a person illegally or improperly detained in public or private custody within such limits be set at liberty;

Relevant Section: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 496
Section Text: When any person other than a person accused of a non-bailable offence is arrested or detained without warrant by an officer in charge of a police-station, or appears or is brought before a Court, and is prepared at any time while in the custody of such officer or at any stage of the proceedings before such Court to give bail, such person shall be released on bail.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Must a police officer release an accused person who is ready to provide bail for a bailable offense?

Rule.Section 497: Outlines the discretionary power of the court to grant bail in serious, non-bailable criminal cases.
Rule.Section 496: Establishes a mandatory right to bail for individuals accused of bailable offenses who are ready to provide security.
Rule.Section 63: Prohibits police from releasing any arrested person unless specific legal conditions like bail or a bond are met.
Rule.Section 498: Confers power on superior courts to set bail amounts or order release on bail during appeals or trials.
Rule.Section 491: Grants the High Court Division the authority of Habeas Corpus to release individuals from illegal or improper detention.

Application.Section 497: Fails because it applies to non-bailable crimes where release is a judicial choice rather than a suspect's right.
Application.Section 496: Governs directly because it uses the mandatory word 'shall,' forcing the officer to release the man once he offers valid bail for a bailable offense.
Application.Section 63: Fails because it defines the general methods of discharge rather than the specific mandatory right linked to the offense type.
Application.Section 498: Fails because it concerns the broad powers of higher courts rather than the immediate duty of a station officer.
Application.Section 491: Fails because the detention is currently for a legal charge, even if the refusal of bail is a procedural error.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, section 496, the officer cannot refuse; the law states that any person accused of a bailable offense must be released on bail if they are prepared to provide it.

Cited Acts and Sections: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 496
