Question: Scenario-based: Salman told a coworker, 'If you want to know about the factory's safety protocols, go talk to our safety officer, Karim.' Karim tells you the protocols were ignored. Is Karim's statement an admission by Salman?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Statements made by a party to the proceeding, or by an agent to any such party, whom the Court regards, under the circumstances of the case, as expressly or impliedly authorized by him to make them, are admissions. Statements made by parties to suits suing or sued in a representative character, are not admissions, unless they were made while the party making them held that character. Statements made by–

Statements made by persons whose position or liability it is necessary to prove as against any party to the suit, are admissions, if such statements would be relevant as against such persons in relation to such position or liability in a suit brought by or against them, and if they are made whilst the person making them occupies such position or is subject to such liability.
Statements made by persons to whom a party to the suit has expressly referred for information in reference to a matter in dispute are admissions.

Admissions are relevant and may be proved as against the person who makes them, or his representative in interest; but they can not be proved by or on behalf of the person who makes them or by his representative in interest, except in the following cases:–

An admission is a statement, oral or documentary [or contained in digital record], which suggests any inference as to any fact in issue or relevant fact, and which is made by any of the persons, and under the circumstances, hereinafter mentioned.

Section Text: Statements made by persons to whom a party to the suit has expressly referred for information in reference to a matter in dispute are admissions.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is a statement by a third-party referee admissible as an admission by the party who referred the inquiry?

Rule.Section 18: Governs admissions made by parties to a suit or their authorized agents.
Rule.Section 19: Governs admissions from third parties whose liability is a necessary component of the case.
Rule.Section 20: Relates to admissions from persons to whom a party expressly directed an inquiry for information.
Rule.Section 21: Governs which parties are allowed to prove admissions in court.
Rule.Section 17: Provides the general definition for what constitutes a legal admission.

Application.Section 18: Fails because it addresses general agency rather than the specific act of referring an inquirer to a third person.
Application.Section 19: Fails since the safety officer is not a third party whose independent liability must be established.
Application.Section 20: Governs directly because Salman expressly referred the coworker to Karim for information on the safety protocols.
Application.Section 21: Fails as it governs the process of proof rather than identifying what counts as a valid admission through referral.
Application.Section 17: Fails because defining a statement as an admission does not establish the referral rule.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 20, Karim's statement is an admission by Salman because Salman expressly referred the inquirer to Karim for that information.

Question: Scenario-based: I have a property dispute in a village near the border. Does the Specific Relief Act, 1877, cover my village, or is it only for the major cities of Bangladesh?

Possible Sections.Full Text: In this Act, unless there be something repugnant in the subject or context,- "obligation" includes every duty enforceable by law: "trust" includes every species of express, implied, or constructive fiduciary ownership: "trustee" includes every person holding, expressly, by implication, or constructively, a fiduciary character:
This Act may be called the Specific Relief Act, 1877. It extends to the whole of Bangladesh. And it shall come into force on the first day of May, 1877.

Except where it is herein otherwise expressly enacted, nothing in this Act shall be deemed- (a) to give any right to relief in respect of any agreement which is not a contract; (b) to deprive any person of any right to relief, other than specific performance, which he may have under any contract; or (c) to affect the operation of the [Registration Act, 1908] on documents.

Specific relief is given- (a) by taking possession of certain property and delivering it to a claimant; (b) by ordering a party to do the very act which he is under an obligation to do; (c) by preventing a party from doing that which he is under an obligation not to do; (d) by determining and declaring the rights of parties otherwise than by an award of compensation; or (e) by appointing a receiver.
Specific relief cannot be granted for the mere purpose of enforcing a penal law.

Section Text: This Act may be called the Specific Relief Act, 1877. It extends to the whole of Bangladesh. And it shall come into force on the first day of May, 1877.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Does the Specific Relief Act, 1877, apply to all territorial regions within the nation?

Rule.Section 3: Provides definitions for interpreting terms like obligation and trust throughout the entire Act.
Rule.Section 1: States the official name of the law and explicitly defines its geographic extent to the whole country.
Rule.Section 4: Protects existing legal rights and other laws from being accidentally deleted or overridden by this Act.
Rule.Section 5: Lists the five general ways the court can provide specific relief to a person in a case.
Rule.Section 7: Bars the court from using civil specific relief powers solely to carry out a criminal or penal law.

Application.Section 3: Fails because providing definitions doesn't answer where the law itself can be applied physically.
Application.Section 1: Governs directly because it explicitly declares that the Act 'extends to the whole of Bangladesh,' which includes border villages.
Application.Section 4: Fails as it addresses the relationship between different laws rather than the geographic reach of the Act itself.
Application.Section 5: Fails because it describes 'how' relief is given rather than 'where' the law is valid and active.
Application.Section 7: Fails as this is a question about geographic jurisdiction rather than the prohibition on criminal law enforcement.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Specific Relief Act, 1877, section 1, the law covers your village — the Act explicitly states that it extends to the whole of Bangladesh.

Question: Scenario-based: I have a video from a doorbell camera showing someone throwing a rock at my window. Does this recording count as a 'document' under the evidence rules in Bangladesh?

Possible Sections.Full Text: An admission is a statement, oral or documentary [or contained in digital record], which suggests any inference as to any fact in issue or relevant fact, and which is made by any of the persons, and under the circumstances, hereinafter mentioned.
Oral admissions as to the contents of digital records are not relevant, unless the genuineness of the digital record produced is in question.

In this Act the following words and expressions are used in the following senses, unless a contrary intention appears from the context:- "Court" includes all Judges and Magistrates and all persons, except arbitrators, legally authorized to take evidence. "Fact" means and includes-

Entries in books of account [or digital record], regularly kept in the course of business, are relevant whenever they refer to a matter into which the Court has to inquire, but such statements shall not alone be sufficient evidence to charge any person with liability.

An entry in any public or other official book, register or record [,or digital record], stating a fact in issue or relevant fact, and made by a public servant in the discharge of his official duty, or by any other person in performance of a duty specially enjoined by the law of the country in which such book, register [, record or digital record] is kept, is itself a relevant fact.

Section Text: In this Act the following words and expressions are used in the following senses, unless a contrary intention appears from the context:- "Court" includes all Judges and Magistrates and all persons, except arbitrators, legally authorized to take evidence. "Fact" means and includes-

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Does a video recording meet the statutory definition of a 'document'?

Rule.Section 17: Defines what an admission is but doesn't define the physical category of the item.
Rule.Section 22A: Bars oral testimony about digital records unless their genuineness is challenged.
Rule.Section 3: Defines documents to include all digital records, specifically mentioning CCTV and video data.
Rule.Section 34: Governs the relevancy of business records, whether physical or digital.
Rule.Section 35: Governs official government records, whether physical or digital.

Application.Section 17: Fails as it addresses the content of the statement rather than the fundamental classification of the recording.
Application.Section 22A: Fails because it regulates testimony 'about' a record rather than defining the record itself as a document.
Application.Section 3: Governs directly because digital video from a camera falls under the specific definitions of 'digital record' and 'document.'
Application.Section 34: Fails since a doorbell video is a piece of evidence from a scene rather than a commercial accounting book.
Application.Section 35: Fails because a private doorbell recording is not an entry in an official book made by a public servant.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 3, your doorbell video is considered a 'document' because the law specifically includes digital records and video recordings in that definition.

Question: Scenario-based: Salman is being tried for a crime in Dhaka. He has a letter he wrote and posted from Chittagong on the same day as the crime. Can he use this letter and the postmark to prove he wasn't in Dhaka?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Facts not otherwise relevant are relevant–
When there is a question whether a particular act was done, the existence of any course of business, according to which it naturally would have been done, is a relevant fact.

Admissions are relevant and may be proved as against the person who makes them, or his representative in interest; but they can not be proved by or on behalf of the person who makes them or by his representative in interest, except in the following cases:–

Admissions are relevant and may be proved as against the person who makes them, or his representative in interest; but they can not be proved by or on behalf of the person who makes them or by his representative in interest, except in the following cases:–

Any fact is relevant which shows or constitutes a motive or preparation for any fact in issue or relevant fact. The conduct of any party, or of any agent to any party, to any suit or proceeding, in reference to such suit or proceeding, or in reference to any fact in issue therein or relevant thereto, and the conduct of any person an offence against whom is the subject of any proceeding, is relevant, if such conduct influences or is influenced by any fact, in issue or relevant fact, and whether it was previous subsequent thereto.

Section Text: Admissions are relevant and may be proved as against the person who makes them, or his representative in interest; but they can not be proved by or on behalf of the person who makes them or by his representative in interest, except in the following cases:–

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Can a person prove their own admission if it satisfies a hearsay exception for the deceased?

Rule.Section 11: Governs logical inconsistency or the probability of a fact's existence.
Rule.Section 16: Governs the existence of a course of business to prove an act happened.
Rule.Section 21: Bars self-proof of admissions except when the statement would be admissible under Section 32 if the maker were dead.
Rule.Section 32(2): Governs out-of-court business records when the maker is deceased.
Rule.Section 8: Governs relevancy of motives, preparation, and party conduct.

Application.Section 11: Fails because while the alibi is inconsistent, Section 21 provides the specific hurdle for proving one's own written statement.
Application.Section 16: Fails as the goal is to prove the date Salman was in Chittagong rather than just the routine of the post office.
Application.Section 21: Governs directly because the postmark is a business record (Sec 32) that allows Salman to bypass the bar on self-serving admissions.
Application.Section 32(2): Fails because Section 32 only applies to deceased persons; Section 21 is needed for a live person to use that logic.
Application.Section 8: Fails since writing a letter is a communicative statement rather than physical conduct influenced by the crime.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 21, Illustration (c), Salman can prove his own letter because the postmark is a record that would be admissible under Section 32 if he were deceased.

Question: Scenario-based: I was part of a loud group pushing people in the street. Now I am being charged with rioting. Can a local junior (third-class) magistrate handle my trial?

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 32
Possible Sections.Full Text: In every enactment passed before this Code comes into force the expressions "Officer exercising (or 'having') the powers (or 'the full powers') of a Magistrate," "Subordinate Magistrate, first class," and "Subordinate Magistrate, second class," shall respectively be deemed to mean "Magistrate of the first class," "Magistrate of the second class" and "Magistrate of the third class," <sup>2</sup>[***] the expression "Magistrate of the district" shall be deemed to mean "District Magistrate," <sup>3</sup>[***].

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Schedule II, Section 147
Possible Sections.Full Text: Rioting

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 121
Possible Sections.Full Text: The bond to be executed by any such person shall bind him to keep the peace or to be of good behaviour, as the case may be, and in the latter case the commission or attempt to commit, or the abetment of, any offence punishable with imprisonment, wherever it may be committed, is a breach of the bond.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Schedule II, Section 160
Possible Sections.Full Text: Committing affray.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 28
Possible Sections.Full Text: Subject to the other provisions of this Code any offence under the Penal Code may be tried-

Relevant Section: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Schedule II, Section 147
Section Text: Rioting. Arrest: May arrest without warrant. Warrant or Summons: Warrant. Compoundable: Not compoundable. Punishment: Imprisonment of either description for 2 years, or fine, or both. Bailable: Bailable. Court_Triable: Any Judicial Magistrate.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Which magistrates have the jurisdiction to try the offense of rioting?

Rule.Section 32: Defines the sentencing limits for third-class magistrates but does not establish subject matter jurisdiction for specific crimes.
Rule.Schedule II, Section 147: Classifies rioting as an offense triable by 'Any Judicial Magistrate,' which includes all three classes.
Rule.Section 121: Reserved serious state offenses exclusively for the Court of Session tier of the judiciary.
Rule.Schedule II, Section 160: Assigns trials for public fights (affray) to any judicial magistrate, similar to rioting.
Rule.Section 28: Lists high courts for trials but defers to the eighth column of the second schedule for magistrate assignments.

Application.Section 32: Fails because sentencing power alone doesn't grant the right to hear a specific type of case if the schedule forbids it.
Application.Schedule II, Section 147: Governs directly because 'Any Judicial Magistrate' includes those of the third class, authorizing them to hear the rioting trial.
Application.Section 121: Fails because it relates to waging war, a much more severe crime than a public riot.
Application.Section 160: Fails because it defines the jurisdiction for 'affray,' which is a different crime than 'rioting' despite the similar court assignment.
Application.Section 28: Fails because it provides a general guideline that is superseded by the specific magistrate-level assignment in Schedule II.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Schedule II, Section 147, any judicial magistrate, including a third-class magistrate, has the legal authority to handle your trial for rioting.

Cited Acts and Sections: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Schedule II, Section 147

Question: Scenario-based: A man failed to sue for his stolen land within the legal time limit of twelve years. Does the law still consider him the 'owner' of the land title?

Possible Sections.Full Text: At the determination of the period hereby limited to any person for instituting a suit for possession of any property, his right to such property shall be extinguished.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I Article 142
Possible Sections.Full Text: At the determination of the period hereby limited to any person for instituting a suit for possession of any property, his right to such property shall be extinguished.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I Article 144
Possible Sections.Full Text: At the determination of the period hereby limited to any person for instituting a suit for possession of any property, his right to such property shall be extinguished.

Subject to the provisions contained in sections 4 to 25 (inclusive), every suit instituted, appeal preferred, and application made, after the period of limitation prescribed therefor by the first schedule shall be dismissed, although limitation has not been set up as a defence.

Where once time has begun to run, no subsequent disability or inability to sue stops it: Provided that where letters of administration to the estate of a creditor have been granted to his debtor, the running of the time prescribed for a suit to recover the debt shall be suspended while the administration continues.

Section Text: At the determination of the period hereby limited to any person for instituting a suit for possession of any property, his right to such property shall be extinguished.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Does the legal title to property end once the limitation period for suing for its recovery expires?

Rule.Section 28: Terminates the actual legal ownership (right) to property once the time limit to sue for possession has finished.
Rule.Schedule I Article 142: Sets the twelve-year deadline for a person to file a lawsuit to get their land back after being evicted.
Rule.Schedule I Article 144: Sets the twelve-year deadline for land suits based on when an intruder's stay becomes hostile to the owner.
Rule.Section 3: Mandates that judges must dismiss any lawsuit that is filed even one day after the legal deadline has passed.
Rule.Section 9: Explains that once a deadline starts counting, later illnesses or problems cannot pause the clock.

Application.Section 28: Governs directly because it states that after the time limit (twelve years) ends, the man's 'right' to the land is 'extinguished', meaning he is no longer the owner.
Application.Schedule I Article 142: Fails because it only sets the 12-year time limit for filing but doesn't explain what happens to the underlying title.
Application.Schedule I Article 144: Fails for the same reason — it provides a filing deadline but doesn't say that ownership itself is lost after that deadline.
Application.Section 3: Fails as it only requires the 'dismissal' of the late lawsuit, whereas the man is asking about his technical 'ownership' status.
Application.Section 9: Fails because the question is about the final result of the deadline, not about whether the clock could have been paused.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Limitation Act, 1908, section 28, the man is no longer the owner — his legal right to the land is completely extinguished because the twelve-year time limit has passed.

Question: Scenario-based: I have a handwritten grocery ledger showing that my neighbor owes me Taka 5,000 for rice and sugar. Is this ledger enough to win my case if I have no other proof?

Possible Sections.Full Text: When there is a question whether a particular act was done, the existence of any course of business, according to which it naturally would have been done, is a relevant fact.

Entries in books of account [or digital record], regularly kept in the course of business, are relevant whenever they refer to a matter into which the Court has to inquire, but such statements shall not alone be sufficient evidence to charge any person with liability.

An entry in any public or other official book, register or record [,or digital record], stating a fact in issue or relevant fact, and made by a public servant in the discharge of his official duty, or by any other person in performance of a duty specially enjoined by the law of the country in which such book, register [, record or digital record] is kept, is itself a relevant fact.

Entries in books of account [or digital record], regularly kept in the course of business, are relevant whenever they refer to a matter into which the Court has to inquire, but such statements shall not alone be sufficient evidence to charge any person with liability.

An admission is a statement, oral or documentary [or contained in digital record], which suggests any inference as to any fact in issue or relevant fact, and which is made by any of the persons, and under the circumstances, hereinafter mentioned.

Section Text: Entries in books of account [or digital record], regularly kept in the course of business, are relevant whenever they refer to a matter into which the Court has to inquire, but such statements shall not alone be sufficient evidence to charge any person with liability.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Are business account entries sufficient by themselves to prove a debt liability?

Rule.Section 16: Governs the existence of a course of business to prove an act happened.
Rule.Section 32(2): Governs business records as hearsay exceptions when the person is deceased.
Rule.Section 35: Governs official entries made by public servants in public records.
Rule.Section 34: Determines that account books are relevant but insufficient on their own to charge someone with liability.
Rule.Section 17: Provides the general definition for what constitutes an admission.

Application.Section 16: Fails because the goal is to prove a specific debt via a record rather than a general routine of business.
Application.Section 32(2): Fails since the shopkeeper is presumably alive and the ledger is being offered as his own record.
Application.Section 35: Fails as the grocery ledger is a private commercial record rather than an official government register.
Application.Section 34: Governs directly as it explicitly states that account books 'shall not alone be sufficient evidence' to prove someone owes money.
Application.Section 17: Fails since it only defines the statement without providing the mandatory rule for its evidentiary weight or sufficiency.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 34, the ledger is relevant, but it is not sufficient on its own to prove your neighbor's debt liability in court.

Question: Scenario-based: Salman told a neighbor, 'I owe Rahim Tk. 10,000, but don't tell him I said that.' Rahim found out and wants to use the neighbor's testimony to prove the debt. Is this allowed?

Possible Sections.Full Text: An admission is a statement, oral or documentary [or contained in digital record], which suggests any inference as to any fact in issue or relevant fact, and which is made by any of the persons, and under the circumstances, hereinafter mentioned.

Statements made by a party to the proceeding, or by an agent to any such party, whom the Court regards, under the circumstances of the case, as expressly or impliedly authorized by him to make them, are admissions. Statements made by parties to suits suing or sued in a representative character, are not admissions, unless they were made while the party making them held that character. Statements made by–

Admissions are relevant and may be proved as against the person who makes them, or his representative in interest; but they can not be proved by or on behalf of the person who makes them or by his representative in interest, except in the following cases:–

In civil cases no admission is relevant, if it is made either upon an express condition that evidence of it is not to be given, or under circumstances from which the Court can infer that the parties agreed together that evidence of it should not be given.
Admissions are not conclusive proof of the matters admitted but they may operate as estoppels under the provisions hereinafter contained.

Section Text: Admissions are relevant and may be proved as against the person who makes them, or his representative in interest; but they can not be proved by or on behalf of the person who makes them or by his representative in interest, except in the following cases:–

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Can an admission be proved against a party who made it to a third person?

Rule.Section 17: Statutorily defines what qualifies as a legal admission in a trial.
Rule.Section 18: Outlines whose statements count as admissions, focusing on parties and their agents.
Rule.Section 21: Governs the general rule that admissions are admissible when proved against the person who made them.
Rule.Section 23: Excludes admissions in civil cases made under a condition of secrecy between the parties.
Rule.Section 31: Specifies that admissions are relevant evidence but not final or conclusive proof.

Application.Section 17: Fails because identifying the statement as an admission does not provide the rule for its admissibility in a trial.
Application.Section 18: Fails since it identifies the speaker rather than the procedural permission for the opponent to offer the evidence.
Application.Section 21: Governs directly as it explicitly permits a party (Rahim) to prove an admission against their opponent (Salman).
Application.Section 23: Fails because the secret was shared with a third-party neighbor rather than being a mutual confidentiality agreement between the litigating parties.
Application.Section 31: Fails as it describes the weight of the evidence rather than the threshold rule allowing it to be presented.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 21, the statement can be used. Admissions are relevant and may be proved against the person who made them by the opposing party.

Question: Scenario-based: In a murder trial, the prosecutor wants to show that the victim knew the accused had committed a previous robbery and was using that secret to extort money. Is this extortion attempt relevant?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Facts showing the existence of any state of mind, such as intention, knowledge, good faith, negligence, rashness, ill-will, or good-will towards any particular person, or showing the existence of any state of body or bodily feeling, are relevant, when the existence of any such state of mind or body or bodily feeling is in issue or relevant.

When there is a question whether an act was accidental or intentional, or done with a particular knowledge or intention, the fact that such act formed part of a series of similar occurrences, in each of which the person doing the act was concerned, is relevant.
Any fact is relevant which shows or constitutes a motive or preparation for any fact in issue or relevant fact.

Facts which are the occasions, cause or effect, immediate or otherwise, of relevant facts, or facts in issue, or which constitute the state of things under which they happened, or which afforded an opportunity for their occurrence or transaction, are relevant.
Facts not otherwise relevant are relevant–

Section Text: Any fact is relevant which shows or constitutes a motive or preparation for any fact in issue or relevant fact. The conduct of any party, or of any agent to any party, to any suit or proceeding, in reference to such suit or proceeding, or in reference to any fact in issue therein or relevant thereto, and the conduct of any person an offence against whom is the subject of any proceeding, is relevant, if such conduct influences or is influenced by any fact, in issue or relevant fact, and whether it was previous subsequent thereto.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is a victim's extortion attempt a relevant motive for their murder?

Rule.Section 14: Determines the relevancy of facts that show an internal state of mind like intent or ill-will.
Rule.Section 15: Governs the use of patterns and similar acts to prove an act was not accidental.
Rule.Section 8: Specifically declares that any fact constituting a motive for a fact in issue is legally relevant.
Rule.Section 7: Outlines relevancy for the cause, effect, or opportunity for an event to occur.
Rule.Section 11: Determines relevancy based on logical inconsistency or high probability of a fact's existence.

Application.Section 14: Fails because the extortion is an external 'reason' for the crime rather than the accused's internal emotional state.
Application.Section 15: Fails as the murder is a single event rather than a part of a repeated series of similar acts.
Application.Section 8: Governs directly because the victim's attempt to extort the accused provides the motive for the murder, as established in Illustration (a).
Application.Section 7: Fails since the extortion is a psychological driver (motive) rather than the immediate physical cause or occasion of death.
Application.Section 11: Fails as the blackmail does not contradict the crime but rather provides a narrative reason why the crime occurred.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 8, Illustration (a), the extortion attempt is relevant because it provides a motive for the accused to commit the murder to keep their secret safe.

Question: Scenario-based: A man was arrested for theft. While at the police station, he told the officer in charge, 'I stole the money from the safe.' Can the prosecutor use this spoken statement to prove his guilt in court?

Possible Sections.Full Text: A confession made by an accused person is irrelevant in a criminal proceeding, if the making of the confession appears to the Court to have been caused by any inducement, threat or promise having reference to the charge against the accused person, proceeding from a person in authority and sufficient, in the opinion of the Court, to give the accused person grounds which would appear to him reasonable for supposing that by making it he would gain any advantage or avoid any evil of a temporal nature in reference to the proceedings against him.
No confession made to a police-officer shall be proved as against a person accused of any offence.
No confession made by any person whilst he is in the custody of a police-officer, unless it be made in the immediate presence of a Magistrate, shall be proved as against such person.

Provided that, when any fact is deposed to as discovered in consequence of information received from a person accused of any offence, in the custody of a police officer, so much of such information, whether it amounts to a confession or not, as relates distinctly to the fact thereby discovered, may be proved.

If such a confession as is referred to in section 24 is made after the impression caused by any such inducement, threat or promise has, in the opinion of the Court, been fully removed, it is relevant.

Section Text: No confession made to a police-officer shall be proved as against a person accused of any offence.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is a confession made directly to a police officer admissible as evidence against the accused?

Rule.Section 24: Excludes confessions obtained through inducements, threats, or promises from persons in authority.
Rule.Section 25: Prohibits the use of any confession made to a police officer as evidence against an accused person.
Rule.Section 26: Bars confessions made in police custody unless they occur in the immediate presence of a Magistrate.
Rule.Section 27: Allows only specific parts of information from an accused that lead to the discovery of physical facts.
Rule.Section 28: Permits confessions previously barred by inducements if the court finds the initial harmful impression was removed.

Application.Section 24: Fails because the scenario doesn't mention any threat or promise, focusing instead on the recipient of the statement.
Application.Section 25: Governs directly because the confession was made to a police officer, triggering the absolute statutory bar against its use.
Application.Section 26: Fails because Section 25 provides a more specific and absolute bar for statements made directly to police officers.
Application.Section 27: Fails since the scenario involves a simple verbal admission without the discovery of any physical evidence or objects.
Application.Section 28: Fails because there was no prior inducement or threat mentioned that required removal for the statement to be relevant.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 25, the prosecutor cannot use the statement because no confession made to a police officer is allowed to be proved against an accused person.

Question: Scenario-based: A man dug a well on his land. One year later, his neighbor's wall suddenly collapsed because of the well's depth. When does the neighbor's time to sue for damages start?

Possible Sections.Full Text: In the case of a suit for compensation for an act which does not give rise to a cause of action unless some specific injury actually results therefrom, the period of limitation shall be computed from the time when the injury results.

In the case of a continuing breach of contract and in the case of a continuing wrong independent of contract, a fresh period of limitation begins to run at every moment of the time during which the breach or the wrong, as the case may be, continues.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I Article 36
Possible Sections.Full Text:
For compensation for any malfeasance, misfeasance or non-feasance independent of contract and not herein specially provided for.
*Part VI.—Three years.*

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I Article 39
Possible Sections.Full Text: For compensation for trespass upon immoveable property. Three years.. The date of the trespass.

Where once time has begun to run, no subsequent disability or inability to sue stops it: Provided that where letters of administration to the estate of a creditor have been granted to his debtor, the running of the time prescribed for a suit to recover the debt shall be suspended while the administration continues.

Section Text: In the case of a suit for compensation for an act which does not give rise to a cause of action unless some specific injury actually results therefrom, the period of limitation shall be computed from the time when the injury results.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: When does the limitation period begin for an act that only becomes illegal once physical damage occurs?

Rule.Section 24: Starts the filing period only when the actual physical damage or 'injury' manifests, for acts that were not initially actionable.
Rule.Section 23: States that for wrongs that keep happening, a new limitation period resets at every single moment the harm persists.
Rule.Schedule I Article 36: Sets a two-year standard limit for general civil wrongs that occur on a single specific date.
Rule.Schedule I Article 39: Gives three years to sue for trespass, counting from the day someone illegally entered or placed items on another's land.
Rule.Section 9: Mandates that once the clock officially starts, later problems or illnesses cannot pause the countdown.

Application.Section 24: Governs directly because digging a well on one's own land isn't a 'wrong' until it actually causes the neighbor's wall to collapse.
Application.Section 23: Fails because the collapse is a single event resulting from the digging, rather than a 'continuing' wrong like daily pollution.
Application.Schedule I Article 36: Fails as it provides the base length of the time limit but doesn't define the special 'delayed trigger' rule provided by Section 24.
Application.Schedule I Article 39: Fails because the man dug on his own land; there was no physical 'trespass' onto the neighbor's property until the damage occurred.
Application.Section 9: Fails because the question is about the start of the clock (the trigger) rather than whether it can be paused later.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Limitation Act, 1908, section 24, the time to sue starts only when the neighbor's wall actually collapses — it does not start from the day the well was dug.

Question: Scenario-based: Salman is being sued for money he owes. During a conversation at a wedding, Salman told a friend that he still owes Abul Taka 50,000. Is this statement allowed in court to prove the debt?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Admissions are relevant and may be proved as against the person who makes them, or his representative in interest; but they can not be proved by or on behalf of the person who makes them or by his representative in interest, except in the following cases:–

An admission is a statement, oral or documentary [or contained in digital record], which suggests any inference as to any fact in issue or relevant fact, and which is made by any of the persons, and under the circumstances, hereinafter mentioned.
Admissions are not conclusive proof of the matters admitted but they may operate as estoppels under the provisions hereinafter contained.

Entries in books of account [or digital record], regularly kept in the course of business, are relevant whenever they refer to a matter into which the Court has to inquire, but such statements shall not alone be sufficient evidence to charge any person with liability.

A confession made by an accused person is irrelevant in a criminal proceeding, if the making of the confession appears to the Court to have been caused by any inducement, threat or promise having reference to the charge against the accused person, proceeding from a person in authority and sufficient, in the opinion of the Court, to give the accused person grounds which would appear to him reasonable for supposing that by making it he would gain any advantage or avoid any evil of a temporal nature in reference to the proceedings against him.

Section Text: An admission is a statement, oral or documentary [or contained in digital record], which suggests any inference as to any fact in issue or relevant fact, and which is made by any of the persons, and under the circumstances, hereinafter mentioned.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Does a verbal statement about a debt qualify as a legal admission?

Rule.Section 21: Governs the parties who are permitted to prove an admission in court and against whom.
Rule.Section 17: Defines an admission as a statement suggesting an inference about a fact in issue.
Rule.Section 31: Dictates the evidentiary weight of admissions, stating they are not absolute proof.
Rule.Section 34: Governs the relevancy of entries made in commercial accounting books.
Rule.Section 24: Excludes confessions in criminal trials obtained through inducements or threats.

Application.Section 21: Fails because it regulates the process of proof rather than the threshold definition of what an admission actually is.
Application.Section 17: Governs directly because Salman's verbal statement to his friend suggests a clear inference that he owes the debt.
Application.Section 31: Fails as it addresses the final effect of the admission rather than its initial definition and relevancy.
Application.Section 34: Fails since a casual wedding conversation is not a formal entry in a business ledger.
Application.Section 24: Fails because the case is a civil debt matter and the statement was a casual remark to a friend.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 17, Salman's statement is an admission because it is an oral statement that suggests a direct inference about the unpaid debt.

Question: Scenario-based: A woman finds her neighbor's dog in her kitchen eating her dinner. She picks up a broom and hits the dog once to drive it out of the house. The dog is not injured. Is she guilty of mischief?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Whoever, with intent to cause, or knowing that he is likely to cause, wrongful loss or damage to the public or to any person, causes the destruction of any property, or any such change in any property or in the situation thereof as destroys or diminishes its value or utility, or affects it injuriously, commits "mischief".

Whoever commits mischief by killing, poisoning, maiming or rendering useless any animal or animals of the value of ten taka or upwards, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both.

Nothing is an offence by reason that it causes, or that it is intended to cause, or that it is known to be likely to cause, any harm, if that harm is so slight that no person of ordinary sense and temper would complain of such harm.

Every person has a right, subject to the restrictions contained in section 99, to defend: Firstly.- His own body, and the body of any other person against any offence affecting the human body; Secondly.- The property, whether moveable or immovable, of himself or of any other person, against any act which is an offence falling under the definition of theft, robbery, mischief or criminal trespass, or which is an attempt to commit theft, robbery, mischief or criminal trespass.
The word "animal" denotes any living creature, other than a human being.

Section Text: Nothing is an offence by reason that it causes, or that it is intended to cause, or that it is known to be likely to cause, any harm, if that harm is so slight that no person of ordinary sense and temper would complain of such harm.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is hitting a trespassing animal with minor force to remove it considered a crime?

Rule.Section 47: Provides the general legal definition of an 'animal' as any living creature except a human.
Rule.Section 95: Governs acts that cause only trivial or slight harm that an ordinary person would not complain about.
Rule.Section 97: Governs the right of private defense of property (including one's house and food) against mischief.
Rule.Section 425: Defines mischief as intentionally destroying or damaging property to cause loss to the owner.
Rule.Section 428: Governs the more serious offense of killing or maiming an animal as an act of mischief.

Application.Section 47: Fails because it is a definition of a term and does not define the criminal nature of the woman's act.
Application.Section 95: Governs directly because a single hit with a broom that causes no injury is a trivial harm that an ordinary person would ignore.
Application.Section 97: Fails because hit was to remove the dog, not necessarily a 'defense' against a criminal offense being committed by the dog.
Application.Section 425: Fails because the woman lacked the intent to 'destroy' the neighbor's property; she only wanted to protect her dinner.
Application.Section 428: Fails because the dog was not killed, poisoned, or maimed by the broom hit.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 95, the woman is not guilty because the harm caused to the dog was so slight that an ordinary person would not complain.

Question: Scenario-based: A man finds a gold watch on the road. He takes it home and, despite seeing the owner's name engraved on the back, decides to keep it for himself. Is this theft?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Whoever, intending to take dishonestly any moveable property out of the possession of any person without that person's consent, moves that property in order to such taking, is said to commit theft.

Whoever dishonestly misappropriates or converts to his own use any moveable property, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both.

Whoever, being in any manner entrusted with property, or with any dominion over property, dishonestly misappropriates or converts to his own use that property, or dishonestly uses or disposes of that property in violation of any direction of law prescribing the mode in which such trust is to be discharged, or of any legal contract, express or implied, which he has made touching the discharge of such trust, or wilfully suffers any other person so to do, commits "criminal breach of trust".

Whoever dishonestly receives or retains any stolen property, knowing or having reason to believe the same to be stolen property, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, or with fine, or with both.

The words "moveable property" are intended to include corporeal property of every description, except land and thing attached to the earth or permanently fastened to any thing which is attached to the earth.

Section Text: Whoever dishonestly misappropriates or converts to his own use any moveable property, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is keeping found property when the owner is known considered theft or misappropriation?

Rule.Section 22: Defines what physical objects qualify as 'moveable property' for the purpose of property offenses.
Rule.Section 378: Governs theft, which requires taking property directly 'out of the possession' of another person.
Rule.Section 403: Governs dishonest misappropriation, specifically addressing property found when not in anyone's active possession.
Rule.Section 405: Governs criminal breach of trust, which requires the property to have been 'entrusted' to the offender.
Rule.Section 411: Governs the dishonest receipt of property that has already been stolen by someone else.

Application.Section 22: Fails because it is a definition of the property type and does not define the criminal act of taking it.
Application.Section 378: Fails because the watch was lost on the road and was not in the active 'possession' of the owner when the man found it.
Application.Section 403: Governs directly because Explanation 2 specifically covers found property and makes it an offense to keep it once the owner is known.
Application.Section 405: Fails because the owner did not intentionally entrust the watch to the man; the man simply found it.
Application.Section 411: Fails because the property was lost, not stolen by a third party before the man acquired it.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 403, Explanation 2, the man has committed dishonest misappropriation because he kept the found property despite knowing who the owner was.

Question: Scenario-based: An accountant is accused of stealing money by making false entries in a ledger. He claims he just made a mistake. Can the prosecutor show that he made five other false entries that all benefited him personally?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Facts showing the existence of any state of mind, such as intention, knowledge, good faith, negligence, rashness, ill-will, or good-will towards any particular person, or showing the existence of any state of body or bodily feeling, are relevant, when the existence of any such state of mind or body or bodily feeling is in issue or relevant.
When there is a question whether a particular act was done, the existence of any course of business, according to which it naturally would have been done, is a relevant fact.

Entries in books of account [or digital record], regularly kept in the course of business, are relevant whenever they refer to a matter into which the Court has to inquire, but such statements shall not alone be sufficient evidence to charge any person with liability.

When there is a question whether an act was accidental or intentional, or done with a particular knowledge or intention, the fact that such act formed part of a series of similar occurrences, in each of which the person doing the act was concerned, is relevant.

When there is a question whether an act was accidental or intentional, or done with a particular knowledge or intention, the fact that such act formed part of a series of similar occurrences, in each of which the person doing the act was concerned, is relevant.

Section Text: When there is a question whether an act was accidental or intentional, or done with a particular knowledge or intention, the fact that such act formed part of a series of similar occurrences, in each of which the person doing the act was concerned, is relevant.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is a series of similar false acts relevant to prove that a specific act was intentional rather than accidental?

Rule.Section 14: Governs the relevancy of facts showing a person's state of mind such as intent.
Rule.Section 16: Governs the relevancy of a business routine to prove an act occurred.
Rule.Section 34: Governs the relevancy and weight of account book entries in establishing liability.
Rule.Section 32(2): Governs out-of-court business records when the maker is deceased.
Rule.Section 15: Governs the relevancy of a series of similar occurrences to prove intent or knowledge.

Application.Section 14: Fails because it identifies intent as relevant but doesn't provide the rule for using patterns of behavior to prove it.
Application.Section 16: Fails since the goal is to disprove an accident rather than to prove a standard business task was completed.
Application.Section 34: Fails because the issue is the accountant's criminal intent rather than the sufficiency of the book to prove a debt.
Application.Section 32(2): Fails as the accountant is alive and the entries are being used against him, not as a hearsay exception.
Application.Section 15: Governs directly because the five other false entries constitute a series of similar occurrences that suggest the current act was intentional.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 15, Illustration (b), the other false entries are relevant because they form part of a series of similar occurrences that show the act was intentional.

Question: Scenario-based: Rahim passed away. Before he died, he wrote a letter admitting that he owed Salman Taka 20,000 for a loan. Can Salman use this letter in court to prove the debt exists?

Possible Sections.Full Text: An admission is a statement, oral or documentary [or contained in digital record], which suggests any inference as to any fact in issue or relevant fact, and which is made by any of the persons, and under the circumstances, hereinafter mentioned.

Admissions are relevant and may be proved as against the person who makes them, or his representative in interest; but they can not be proved by or on behalf of the person who makes them or by his representative in interest, except in the following cases:–

When the statement is contained in any deed, will or other document which relates to any such transaction as is mentioned in section 13, clause (a).

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is a deceased person's admission of debt admissible as a statement against their financial interest?

Rule.Section 17: Defines what an admission is in general legal terms.
Rule.Section 21: Governs who can prove an admission and against whom.
Rule.Section 32: Governs the relevancy of out-of-court statements from deceased persons.
Rule.Section 32(2): Governs out-of-court business records specifically when the maker is deceased.
Rule.Section 32(7): Governs statements about rights found in historical deeds or wills.

Application.Section 17: Fails because it defines the statement but doesn't provide the hearsay exception required for a deceased person's words.
Application.Section 21: Fails as it regulates the logic of party-based proof rather than creating the exception for the deceased.
Application.Section 32: Governs directly because admitting a debt is against Rahim's pecuniary interest and he is now deceased.

Application.Section 32(7): Fails as a casual letter is not a formal 'deed' or 'will' relating to property rights.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 32, Subsection (3), the letter is relevant because it is a statement against the financial interest of the person who wrote it and has since died.

Question: Scenario-based: A technician found a suspect's fingerprint on a glass at a crime scene. Is there a specific legal category for fingerprints and DNA samples under the current evidence rules?

Possible Sections.Full Text: In this Act the following words and expressions are used in the following senses, unless a contrary intention appears from the context:- "Court" includes all Judges and Magistrates and all persons, except arbitrators, legally authorized to take evidence. "Fact" means and includes-

An admission is a statement, oral or documentary [or contained in digital record], which suggests any inference as to any fact in issue or relevant fact, and which is made by any of the persons, and under the circumstances, hereinafter mentioned.

Entries in books of account [or digital record], regularly kept in the course of business, are relevant whenever they refer to a matter into which the Court has to inquire, but such statements shall not alone be sufficient evidence to charge any person with liability.

Rule.Section 3: Defines 'physical or forensic evidence' to include DNA, body materials, and finger or palm impressions.
Rule.Section 17: Defines admissions as statements or digital records suggesting an inference about a fact.
Rule.Section 22A: Restricts oral evidence regarding digital record contents unless genuineness is disputed.
Rule.Section 34: Governs the relevancy of business entries in account books and digital logs.
Rule.Section 35: Governs official public records created by public servants in their line of duty.

Application.Section 3: Governs directly as it explicitly lists finger impressions and body materials as a distinct category of evidence.
Application.Section 17: Fails because a fingerprint is a physical impression rather than a communicative statement or digital record of an admission.
Application.Section 22A: Fails since the scenario concerns physical biological traces rather than spoken claims about computer files.
Application.Section 34: Fails as a fingerprint is a physical trace found at a scene, not a commercial entry in a ledger.
Application.Section 35: Fails because the fingerprint itself is primary physical evidence, not a record written by a government officer.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 3, Subsection (3), fingerprints and DNA are formally classified as 'physical or forensic evidence' used to link a person to an offence.

Question: Scenario-based: A man helped another person commit a crime. Since the main criminal is being tried by the Court of Session, which court must try the person who helped him?

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 28
Possible Sections.Full Text: Subject to the other provisions of this Code any offence under the Penal Code may be tried-

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Schedule II, Section 109
Possible Sections.Full Text: Abetment of any offence, if the act abetted is committed in consequence, and where no express provision is made for its punishment.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 29
Possible Sections.Full Text: Subject to the other provisions of this Code, any offence under any other law shall, when any Court is mentioned in this behalf in such law, be tried by such Court. When no Court is so mentioned, it may be tried subject as aforesaid by any Court constituted under this Code by which such offence is shown in the eighth column of the second schedule to be triable.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 30
Possible Sections.Full Text: [Omitted by section 2 and Schedule of the Law Reforms Ordinance, 1978 (Ordinance No. XLIX of 1978).]

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Schedule II, Section 120B
Possible Sections.Full Text: Criminal conspiracy to commit an offence punishable with death, transportation or rigorous imprisonment for a term of two years or upwards.

Relevant Section: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Schedule II, Section 109
Section Text: Abetment of any offence, if the act abetted is committed in consequence, and where no express provision is made for its punishment. Arrest: May arrest without warrant if arrest for the offence abetted may be made without warrant, but not otherwise. Warrant or Summons: According as a warrant or summons may issue for the offence abetted. Court_Triable: The Court by which the offence abetted is triable.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Which court has jurisdiction to try an individual for abetting a crime?

Rule.Section 28: Provides general trial jurisdiction for Penal Code offenses but yields to more specific schedule instructions.
Rule.Schedule II, Section 109: Mandates that the court trial for abetment must be the same court authorized to try the underlying offense.
Rule.Section 29: Governs offenses under laws other than the Penal Code, which is not applicable to general abetment here.
Rule.Section 30: Describes the government's power to expand the trial authority of specific high-ranking magistrates.
Rule.Schedule II, Section 120B: Governs the jurisdiction for criminal conspiracy, linking it to the court for abetment.

Application.Section 28: Fails because it gives a list of possible courts rather than the mandatory link between the abettor and the primary offender's court.
Application.Schedule II, Section 109: Governs directly because the 'Court_Triable' column explicitly binds the abettor to the court of the primary offense (Court of Session).
Application.Section 29: Fails because it relates to special laws rather than the general abetment rules under the Penal Code.
Application.Section 30: Fails because it defines a magistrate's extra powers rather than the jurisdictional rules for abetment charges.
Application.Schedule II, Section 120B: Fails because the scenario involves helping (abetting) rather than plotting (conspiracy), even though the jurisdictional outcome is similar.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Schedule II, Section 109, the helper must be tried by the Court of Session, as the law requires him to be tried by the same court handling the main crime.

Statements made by persons to whom a party to the suit has expressly referred for information in reference to a matter in dispute are admissions.
