Statements, written or verbal, of relevant facts made by a person who is dead, or who cannot be found, or who has become incapable of giving evidence, or whose attendance cannot be procured without an amount of delay or expense which under the circumstances of the case appears to the Court unreasonable, are themselves relevant facts in the following cases:–
When there is a question whether a particular act was done, the existence of any course of business, according to which it naturally would have been done, is a relevant fact.

Section Text: Admissions are relevant and may be proved as against the person who makes them, or his representative in interest; but they can not be proved by or on behalf of the person who makes them or by his representative in interest, except in the following cases:– (1) An admission may be proved by or on behalf of the person making it, when it is of such a nature that, if the person making it were dead, it would be relevant as between third persons under section 32.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Can an accused prove their own admission if it meets the business record exception?

Rule.Section 17: Defines an admission as a statement suggesting an inference about a fact in issue.
Rule.Section 11: Allows facts that are logically inconsistent with a fact in issue.
Rule.Section 21: Bars self-proof of admissions unless the statement would be relevant under Section 32 if the maker were dead.
Rule.Section 32: Governs out-of-court statements by dead or unavailable persons made in the course of business.
Rule.Section 16: Relates to the relevancy of a general course of business to prove an act happened.

Application.Section 17: Fails because it only defines the nature of an admission rather than providing the exception for self-proof.
Application.Section 11: Fails as the core legal hurdle is the bar against proving one's own out-of-court statements (hearsay/admissions).
Application.Section 21: Governs directly because a grocery receipt is a business record that would be admissible under Section 32 if Salman were dead.
Application.Section 32: Fails because Section 32 only applies if the person is actually dead; Section 21 is required for a live person.
Application.Section 16: Fails since the goal is to admit the specific written receipt as an admission rather than proving a general routine.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 21, Subsection (1), Salman can prove the receipt for his own benefit because it is a business record that would be admissible under Section 32 if he were deceased.

Question: Scenario-based: Jamil sees a man trying to kidnap a young woman on a quiet street. Jamil intervenes and hits the kidnapper to free her. Does Jamil have the legal right to use force to defend a complete stranger?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Nothing is an offence which is done in the exercise of the right of private defence.

Every person has a right, subject to the restrictions contained in section 99, to defend: Firstly.- His own body, and the body of any other person against any offence affecting the human body; Secondly.- The property, whether moveable or immovable, of himself or of any other person, against any act which is an offence falling under the definition of theft, robbery, mischief or criminal trespass, or which is an attempt to commit theft, robbery, mischief or criminal trespass.

If in the exercise of the right of private defence against an assault which reasonably causes the apprehension of death, the defender be so situated that he cannot effectually exercise that right without risk of harm to an innocent person, his right of private defence extends to the running of that risk.
Kidnapping is of two kinds: kidnapping from Bangladesh, and kidnapping from lawful guardianship.
Whoever by force compels, or by any deceitful means induces, any person to go from any place, is said to abduct that person.

Section Text: Every person has a right, subject to the restrictions contained in section 99, to defend: Firstly.- His own body, and the body of any other person against any offence affecting the human body; Secondly.- The property, whether moveable or immovable, of himself or of any other person, against any act which is an offence falling under the definition of theft, robbery, mischief or criminal trespass, or which is an attempt to commit theft, robbery, mischief or criminal trespass.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Does the right of private defense extend to protecting other persons besides oneself?

Rule.Section 96: Governs the general principle that actions taken in self-defense are not punishable crimes.
Rule.Section 97: Governs the specific scope of the right of defense, explicitly including the protection of the bodies of other persons.
Rule.Section 106: Governs extreme cases where defending oneself from death involves a risk to innocent bystanders.
Rule.Section 359: Governs the legal definitions and types of kidnapping recognized under the Code.
Rule.Section 362: Governs the definition of abduction involving the use of force or deceit to move a person.

Application.Section 96: Fails because it is a general statement of immunity rather than the specific rule defining whose body can be defended.
Application.Section 97: Governs directly because Clause Firstly specifically authorizes the defense of 'the body of any other person' against physical offenses.
Application.Section 106: Fails because Jamil's intervention did not involve a life-threatening risk to innocent third parties.
Application.Section 359: Fails because it defines the kidnapper's crime rather than Jamil's right to use force in response.
Application.Section 362: Fails because it is a definition of the underlying offense and does not address the justification for a rescuer's actions.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 97, Clause Firstly, Jamil has the right to intervene because the law allows every person to defend the body of any other person against physical offenses.

Question: Scenario-based: I have a spreadsheet on my computer that lists all the daily sales for my small electronics store over the last year. Is this digital record enough on its own to make the court order a customer to pay for an unpaid TV?

Possible Sections.Full Text: When there is a question whether a particular act was done, the existence of any course of business, according to which it naturally would have been done, is a relevant fact.

Statements, written or verbal, of relevant facts made by a person who is dead, or who cannot be found, or who has become incapable of giving evidence, or whose attendance cannot be procured without an amount of delay or expense which under the circumstances of the case appears to the Court unreasonable, are themselves relevant facts in the following cases:–

Entries in books of account [or digital record], regularly kept in the course of business, are relevant whenever they refer to a matter into which the Court has to inquire, but such statements shall not alone be sufficient evidence to charge any person with liability.

An entry in any public or other official book, register or record [,or digital record], stating a fact in issue or relevant fact, and made by a public servant in the discharge of his official duty, or by any other person in performance of a duty specially enjoined by the law of the country in which such book, register [, record or digital record] is kept, is itself a relevant fact.

An admission is a statement, oral or documentary [or contained in digital record], which suggests any inference as to any fact in issue or relevant fact, and which is made by any of the persons, and under the circumstances, hereinafter mentioned.

Section Text: Entries in books of account [or digital record], regularly kept in the course of business, are relevant whenever they refer to a matter into which the Court has to inquire, but such statements shall not alone be sufficient evidence to charge any person with liability.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is a digital business ledger sufficient evidence to establish financial liability for a debt?

Rule.Section 16: Governs the relevancy of routine business processes used to prove an act happened.
Rule.Section 32: Provides a hearsay exception for business records when the maker is deceased.
Rule.Section 34: Determines that account books and digital records are relevant but insufficient by themselves to prove liability.
Rule.Section 35: Governs official government records created by public servants.
Rule.Section 17: Defines what qualifies as a legal admission in a trial.

Application.Section 16: Fails because the goal is to prove a specific unpaid debt rather than a general routine of business.
Application.Section 32: Fails since the shopkeeper is presumably alive and offering his own records.
Application.Section 34: Governs directly because it explicitly states that digital business records 'shall not alone be sufficient evidence' to prove a debt.
Application.Section 35: Fails as a private spreadsheet is not an official public book kept by a government clerk.
Application.Section 17: Fails as it defines the statement without providing the mandatory rule for its limited evidentiary weight.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 34, the spreadsheet is relevant, but it is not enough on its own to prove the customer owes you money.

Question: Scenario-based: Javed cuts down a tree on Salim's land with the intent to steal it. He gets scared and leaves the tree on the ground without taking it away. Has Javed committed theft?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Whoever commits theft shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, or with fine, or with both.
Whoever, intending to take dishonestly any moveable property out of the possession of any person without that person's consent, moves that property in order to such taking, is said to commit theft.

Whoever dishonestly misappropriates or converts to his own use any moveable property, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both.

Whoever, with intent to cause, or knowing that he is likely to cause, wrongful loss or damage to the public or to any person, causes the destruction of any property, or any such change in any property or in the situation thereof as destroys or diminishes its value or utility, or affects it injuriously, commits "mischief".

Whoever intentionally puts any person in fear of any injury to that person, or to any other, and thereby dishonestly induces the person so put in fear to give donation or subscription of any kind or to deliver to any person any property or valuable security or anything signed or sealed which may be converted into a valuable security, commits 'extortion'.

Section Text: Whoever, intending to take dishonestly any moveable property out of the possession of any person without that person's consent, moves that property in order to such taking, is said to commit theft.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Does the act of severing a tree from the ground complete the offense of theft?

Rule.Section 378: Defines theft as moving property with dishonest intent, and clarifies that severing something from the earth counts as moving it.
Rule.Section 379: Provides the standard three-year prison sentence or fine for anyone who is convicted of the offense of theft.
Rule.Section 383: Defines extortion as forcing someone to hand over property by putting them in fear of an injury.
Rule.Section 403: Governs the dishonest misappropriation of property that is already in a person's possession or found by them.
Rule.Section 425: Defines mischief as the intentional destruction or alteration of property to cause wrongful loss or damage.

Application.Section 378: Governs directly because Explanation 2 states that the single act of cutting (severing) the tree also counts as moving it, completing the crime of theft.
Application.Section 379: Fails because it defines the punishment rather than the specific criteria for when the act of cutting a tree becomes theft.
Application.Section 383: Fails because there was no threat of injury or induction to deliver property; the act was a secret taking from Salim's land.
Application.Section 403: Fails because theft involves taking property out of another's possession, while misappropriation involves property not currently in the owner's immediate possession.
Application.Section 425: Fails because Javed's primary intent was to steal the tree for gain rather than simply to destroy it for Salim's loss.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 378, Explanation 2, Javed has committed theft because the act of cutting the tree also counts as moving it with the intent to steal.

Question: Scenario-based: I told the other side in a civil suit, 'Off the record, I'll pay half to end this.' We both agreed this would stay secret. Can they now use my offer to prove I am liable in court?

Possible Sections.Full Text: A confession made by an accused person is irrelevant in a criminal proceeding, if the making of the confession appears to the Court to have been caused by any inducement, threat or promise having reference to the charge against the accused person, proceeding from a person in authority and sufficient, in the opinion of the Court, to give the accused person grounds which would appear to him reasonable for supposing that by making it he would gain any advantage or avoid any evil of a temporal nature in reference to the proceedings against him.

In civil cases no admission is relevant, if it is made either upon an express condition that evidence of it is not to be given, or under circumstances from which the Court can infer that the parties agreed together that evidence of it should not be given.
Admissions are not conclusive proof of the matters admitted but they may operate as estoppels under the provisions hereinafter contained.

Admissions are relevant and may be proved as against the person who makes them, or his representative in interest; but they can not be proved by or on behalf of the person who makes them or by his representative in interest, except in the following cases:–

An admission is a statement, oral or documentary [or contained in digital record], which suggests any inference as to any fact in issue or relevant fact, and which is made by any of the persons, and under the circumstances, hereinafter mentioned.

Section Text: In civil cases no admission is relevant, if it is made either upon an express condition that evidence of it is not to be given, or under circumstances from which the Court can infer that the parties agreed together that evidence of it should not be given.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is a confidential settlement offer admissible in a civil court proceeding?

Rule.Section 24: Excludes confessions in criminal trials obtained through inducements from authorities.
Rule.Section 23: States that admissions in civil cases are irrelevant if made under an express condition of secrecy.
Rule.Section 31: Dictates that admissions are not absolute proof but can create a legal bar via estoppel.
Rule.Section 21: Governs the general rule that admissions are proved against the person who made them.
Rule.Section 17: Provides the general definition for what constitutes an admission.

Application.Section 24: Fails because the case is a civil lawsuit rather than a criminal proceeding involving the state.
Application.Section 23: Governs directly because the offer was made on the express condition of secrecy and the parties agreed to it.
Application.Section 31: Fails since an irrelevant admission under Section 23 cannot function as proof or estoppel in the first place.
Application.Section 21: Fails because Section 23 acts as a specific exclusion that overrides the general rule for proving admissions.
Application.Section 17: Fails because defining the statement as an admission does not address the legal bar created by the secrecy agreement.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 23, the offer is not relevant. The law protects admissions made in civil cases when both parties agree they will stay secret.

Question: Scenario-based: A man who has been diagnosed with a severe mental illness kills someone. At the time of the act, he truly believed he was fighting off a demon and did not realize he was hurting a person. Can he be punished for murder?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Nothing is an offence which is done by a person who, at the time of doing it, is, by reason of intoxication, incapable of knowing the nature of the act, or that he is doing what is either wrong, or contrary to law: provided that the thing which intoxicated him was administered to him without his knowledge or against his will.

Nothing is an offence which is done by accident or misfortune, and without any criminal intention or knowledge in the doing of a lawful act in a lawful manner by lawful means and with proper care and caution.

Nothing is an offence which is done by a person who, at the time of doing it, by reason of unsoundness of mind, is incapable of knowing the nature of the act, or that he is doing what is either wrong or contrary to law.
Nothing is an offence which is done by a child under nine years of age.

Except murder, and offences against the State punishable with death, nothing is an offence which is done by a person who is compelled to do it by threats, which, at the time of doing it, reasonably cause the apprehension that instant death to that person will otherwise be the consequence: Provided the person doing the act did not of his own accord, or from a reasonable apprehension of harm to himself short of instant death, place himself in the situation by which he became subject to such constraint.

Section Text: Nothing is an offence which is done by a person who, at the time of doing it, by reason of unsoundness of mind, is incapable of knowing the nature of the act, or that he is doing what is either wrong or contrary to law.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Does a mental illness that prevents understanding of an act's wrongfulness excuse criminal liability?

Rule.Section 85: Governs the exception for involuntary intoxication leading to mental incapacity.
Rule.Section 80: Governs acts that are accidents occurring during the careful performance of lawful duties.
Rule.Section 84: Exempts individuals from liability if 'unsoundness of mind' makes them incapable of knowing the nature or wrongfulness of their act.
Rule.Section 82: Grants absolute immunity from criminal prosecution to children under nine.
Rule.Section 94: Exempts acts performed under immediate threats of death, but explicitly excludes murder from this protection.

Application.Section 85: Fails because the incapacity resulted from a long-term medical diagnosis rather than the consumption of a drug or alcohol.
Application.Section 80: Fails because killing based on a hallucination is an intentional act (based on a false reality) rather than a physical accident.
Application.Section 84: Governs directly because the man's clinical 'unsoundness of mind' prevented him from knowing his act was contrary to law.
Application.Section 82: Fails because the scenario involves an adult with a mental illness rather than a child below the age threshold.
Application.Section 94: Fails because the man was driven by internal delusions rather than external threats from other people.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 84, the man cannot be punished because his unsoundness of mind made him incapable of knowing that his act was wrong or illegal.

Question: Scenario-based: A man convinces a 6-year-old child to steal a gold watch from a relative's house. Can the man be punished even if the child is too young to be legally charged with a crime?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Nothing is an offence which is done by a child under nine years of age.

A person abets the doing of a thing, who: Firstly.-Instigates any person to do that thing; or Secondly.-Engages with one or more other person or persons in any conspiracy for the doing of that thing, if an act or illegal omission takes place in pursuance of that conspiracy, and in order to the doing of that thing; or Thirdly.-Intentionally aids, by any act or illegal omission, the doing of that thing.

A person abets an offence, who abets either the commission of an offence, or the commission of an act which would be an offence, if committed by a person capable by law of committing an offence with the same intention or knowledge as that of the abettor.

Whoever abets any offence shall, if the act abetted is committed in consequence of the abetment, and no express provision is made by this Code for the punishment of such abetment, be punished with the punishment provided for the offence. Punishment of abetment if the act abetted is committed in consequence and where no express provision is made for its punishment
When a criminal act is done by several persons, in furtherance of the common intention of all, each of such persons is liable for that act in the same manner as if it were done by him alone.

Section Text: A person abets an offence, who abets either the commission of an offence, or the commission of an act which would be an offence, if committed by a person capable by law of committing an offence with the same intention or knowledge as that of the abettor.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is a person liable for abetment if the perpetrator they use is legally incapable of committing a crime?

Rule.Section 82: Governs the absolute immunity from criminal liability for children under the age of nine.
Rule.Section 107: Governs the basic definition of abetment by instigation, conspiracy, or intentional aid.
Rule.Section 108: Governs who qualifies as an abettor, with a specific rule regarding the incapacity of the person performing the act.
Rule.Section 109: Governs the standard punishment for abetment when no specific separate penalty is listed.
Rule.Section 34: Governs joint liability for criminal acts done by multiple persons with a shared intention.

Application.Section 82: Fails because it protects the child, but it does not address the legal responsibility of the adult who used the child.
Application.Section 107: Fails because it is a general definition, and the issue of 'incapacity' requires a more specific provision.
Application.Section 108: Governs directly because Explanation 3 states that abetment is established even if the person being used (the child) is legally incapable of committing the crime.
Application.Section 109: Fails because it specifies the 'how much' of the punishment rather than the 'whether' of the liability for using a child.
Application.Section 34: Fails because it requires all parties to share a criminal intent, which a 6-year-old child is legally presumed not to have.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 108, Explanation 3, the man is guilty because abetment is still a crime even if the person you use is legally incapable of being punished due to their age.

Question: Scenario-based: Karim is found guilty of intentionally killing his neighbor. What are the specific prison or death penalties the judge can choose from?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Whoever commits culpable homicide not amounting to murder, shall be punished with imprisonment for life, or imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine, if the act by which the death is caused is done with intention of causing death, or of causing such bodily injury as is likely to cause death; or with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to ten years, or with fine, or with both, if the act is done with the knowledge that it is likely to cause death, but without any intention to cause death or to cause such bodily injury as is likely to cause death.
Whoever commits murder shall be punished with death, or imprisonment for life, and shall also be liable to fine.
Whoever, being under sentence of imprisonment for life, commits murder, shall be punished with death.

Whoever does any act with such intention or knowledge, and under such circumstances that, if he by that act caused death, he would be guilty of murder, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine; and, if hurt is caused to any person by such act, the offender shall be liable either to imprisonment for life, or to such punishment as is hereinbefore mentioned. When any person offending under this section is under sentence of imprisonment for life, he may, if hurt is caused, be punished with death.
[Definition of Murder including the four clauses and the five exceptions governing when culpable homicide is or is not murder.]

Section Text: Whoever commits murder shall be punished with death, or imprisonment for life, and shall also be liable to fine.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: What is the prescribed punishment for the offense of murder?

Rule.Section 300: Sets out the legal definition of murder and identifies specific exceptions that reduce it to culpable homicide.
Rule.Section 302: Specifies that the penalty for murder is either the death sentence or life imprisonment, plus a fine.
Rule.Section 303: Mandates a death sentence for any person who commits murder while already serving a life sentence.
Rule.Section 304: Provides the sentencing range for culpable homicide when the act does not legally qualify as murder.
Rule.Section 307: Governs the punishment for attempted murder where the intended death does not actually occur.

Application.Section 300: Fails because it defines the elements of the crime rather than listing the specific punishments available to the judge.
Application.Section 302: Governs directly because Karim was found guilty of murder, triggering the two main sentencing options listed in this section.
Application.Section 303: Fails because there is no indication that Karim was already a life-convict at the time he killed his neighbor.
Application.Section 304: Fails because the offense was murder, not the lesser charge of culpable homicide not amounting to murder.
Application.Section 307: Fails because the neighbor was actually killed, so the rules for an unsuccessful 'attempt' no longer apply.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 302, the judge can sentence Karim to either death or imprisonment for life, and Karim must also pay a fine.

Question: Scenario-based: Rahim, a citizen of Bangladesh, commits a robbery in London. Can he be punished under the laws of Bangladesh when he returns home?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Every person shall be liable to punishment under this Code and not otherwise for every act or omission contrary to the provisions thereof, of which he shall be guilty within Bangladesh.

The provisions of this Code apply also to any offence committed by- (1) any citizen of Bangladesh in any place without and beyond Bangladesh; (2) [Omitted by Article 2 and Schedule of the Central Laws (Adaptation) Order, 1961.] (3) [Omitted by section 3 and 2nd Schedule of the Bangladesh Laws (Revision And Declaration) Act, 1973 (Act No. VIII of 1973).] (4) any person on any ship or aircraft registered in Bangladesh wherever it may be.
This Act shall be called the Penal Code, and shall take effect throughout Bangladesh.

Nothing in this Act is intended to repeal, vary, suspend, or affect [* * *] any of the provisions of any Act for punishing mutiny and desertion of officers, soldiers, sailors or airmen in the service of the [Republic], or of any special or local law.

Any person liable, by any Bangladesh Law, to be tried for an offence committed beyond Bangladesh shall be dealt with according to the provisions of this Code for any act committed beyond Bangladesh in the same manner as if such act had been committed within Bangladesh.

Section Text: The provisions of this Code apply also to any offence committed by- (1) any citizen of Bangladesh in any place without and beyond Bangladesh; (2) [Omitted by Article 2 and Schedule of the Central Laws (Adaptation) Order, 1961.] (3) [Omitted by section 3 and 2nd Schedule of the Bangladesh Laws (Revision And Declaration) Act, 1973 (Act No. VIII of 1973).] (4) any person on any ship or aircraft registered in Bangladesh wherever it may be.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Does the Code apply to crimes committed by Bangladeshi citizens in foreign territories?

Rule.Section 1: Defines the official title of the Act and its geographical extent within national borders.
Rule.Section 2: Establishes criminal liability for any individual who commits a prohibited act within Bangladesh.
Rule.Section 3: Sets the procedure for trying persons for foreign acts as if they occurred locally.
Rule.Section 4: Extends the Code's coverage to any citizen committing an offense beyond the country's limits.
Rule.Section 5: Preserves the authority of special, local, and military laws from being repealed by the Code.

Application.Section 1: Fails because it establishes general domestic application without addressing the legal status of extra-territorial acts.
Application.Section 2: Fails because it restricts liability to acts committed inside Bangladesh, which excludes Rahim's conduct in London.
Application.Section 3: Fails because it governs the procedural manner of trial rather than the substantive scope of extra-territoriality.
Application.Section 4: Governs directly because Subsection (1) specifically extends legal liability to citizens for offenses committed beyond national borders.
Application.Section 5: Fails because robbery is a general offense and does not trigger exclusions for military or local laws.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 4, Subsection (1), Rahim can be punished in Bangladesh because the law expressly applies to any offense committed by a citizen beyond the country's borders.

Question: Scenario-based: Rahim is walking down a public road. Salim stands in front of him and blocks his path, saying Rahim cannot go forward. Rahim is free to turn around or go sideways, but he cannot go in the direction he wanted. Is this a crime?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Whoever wrongfully restrains any person in such a manner as to prevent that person from proceeding beyond certain circumscribing limits, is said "wrongfully to confine" that person.

Whoever voluntarily obstructs any person so as to prevent that person from proceeding in any direction in which that person has a right to proceed, is said wrongfully to restrain that person. Exception. The obstruction of a private way over land or water which a person in good faith believes himself to have a lawful right to obstruct, is not an offence within the meaning of this section.
Whoever wrongfully restrains any person, shall be punished with simple imprisonment for a term which may extend to one month, or with fine which may extend to five hundred taka, or with both.

Whoever voluntarily obstructs any public servant in the discharge of his public functions, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three months, or with fine which may extend to five hundred taka, or with both.

Whoever wrongfully confines any person, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to one year, or with fine which may extend to one thousand taka, or with both.

Section Text: Whoever voluntarily obstructs any person so as to prevent that person from proceeding in any direction in which that person has a right to proceed, is said wrongfully to restrain that person. Exception. The obstruction of a private way over land or water which a person in good faith believes himself to have a lawful right to obstruct, is not an offence within the meaning of this section.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Does blocking a person's movement in one specific direction constitute a criminal act?

Rule.Section 340: Defines wrongful confinement as preventing movement outside of a specific boundary (like a room).
Rule.Section 339: Defines wrongful restraint as obstructing a person from proceeding in a specific direction they have a right to go.
Rule.Section 341: Sets the one-month prison sentence for the offense of wrongful restraint.
Rule.Section 186: Governs the obstruction of government officials rather than private citizens.
Rule.Section 342: Sets the one-year prison sentence for the offense of wrongful confinement.

Application.Section 340: Fails because Rahim was not trapped in a limited space (like a cell); he could still go backwards or sideways.
Application.Section 339: Governs directly because Salim blocked Rahim's specific 'direction' of travel, fulfilling the precise definition of wrongful restraint.
Application.Section 341: Fails because it provides the punishment rather than the primary definition that classifies Salim's behavior as illegal.
Application.Section 186: Fails because Rahim is a private citizen and not a public servant performing an official duty.
Application.Section 342: Fails because it is the penalty for a different crime (confinement) that requires total restriction of movement.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 339, Salim's act is called 'wrongful restraint' because he prevented Rahim from moving in a direction he had a legal right to go.

Question: Scenario-based: Rahim is accused of setting fire to a shop. To prove he did it, the prosecutor wants to show that Rahim had previously set fire to two other buildings owned by the same family. Is this allowed?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Facts showing the existence of any state of mind, such as intention, knowledge, good faith, negligence, rashness, ill-will, or good-will towards any particular person, or showing the existence of any state of body or bodily feeling, are relevant, when the existence of any such state of mind or body or bodily feeling is in issue or relevant.

Any fact is relevant which shows or constitutes a motive or preparation for any fact in issue or relevant fact. The conduct of any party, or of any agent to any party, to any suit or proceeding, in reference to such suit or proceeding, or in reference to any fact in issue therein or relevant thereto, and the conduct of any person an offence against whom is the subject of any proceeding, is relevant, if such conduct influences or is influenced by any fact, in issue or relevant fact, and whether it was previous subsequent thereto.

When there is a question whether an act was accidental or intentional, or done with a particular knowledge or intention, the fact that such act formed part of a series of similar occurrences, in each of which the person doing the act was concerned, is relevant.

Facts which are the occasions, cause or effect, immediate or otherwise, of relevant facts, or facts in issue, or which constitute the state of things under which they happened, or which afforded an opportunity for their occurrence or transaction, are relevant.
Facts not otherwise relevant are relevant–

Section Text: When there is a question whether an act was accidental or intentional, or done with a particular knowledge or intention, the fact that such act formed part of a series of similar occurrences, in each of which the person doing the act was concerned, is relevant.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Are prior similar acts relevant to proving intent and lack of accident?

Rule.Section 14: Governs relevancy of facts showing state of mind like intent or negligence.
Rule.Section 8: Relates to facts showing motive, preparation, or conduct.
Rule.Section 15: Governs a series of similar acts used to distinguish between accident and intentional conduct.
Rule.Section 7: Dictates that facts providing the cause, effect, or occasion for a crime are relevant.
Rule.Section 11: Determines relevancy based on inconsistency or high probability.

Application.Section 14: Fails because while it identifies intent as relevant, it doesn't provide the specific rule for using a pattern of events to prove it.
Application.Section 8: Fails since arson in the past is used here to prove a lack of accident, not to show preparation for the current fire.
Application.Section 15: Governs directly because the three fires form a 'series of similar occurrences' which proves the current act was not accidental.
Application.Section 7: Fails as the previous fires are not the immediate 'occasion' or 'effect' of the shop burning today.
Application.Section 11: Fails because the issue is intent through pattern recognition rather than logical contradiction or distance.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 15, the previous fires are relevant because they form part of a series of similar occurrences that suggest the act was intentional.

Question: Scenario-based: Salman is in a lawsuit regarding a debt. During a private conversation, Salman's authorized personal assistant admitted to the creditor that Salman still owes the money. Is this admission binding on Salman?

Possible Sections.Full Text: An admission is a statement, oral or documentary [or contained in digital record], which suggests any inference as to any fact in issue or relevant fact, and which is made by any of the persons, and under the circumstances, hereinafter mentioned.

Statements made by a party to the proceeding, or by an agent to any such party, whom the Court regards, under the circumstances of the case, as expressly or impliedly authorized by him to make them, are admissions. Statements made by parties to suits suing or sued in a representative character, are not admissions, unless they were made while the party making them held that character. Statements made by–

Statements made by persons whose position or liability it is necessary to prove as against any party to the suit, are admissions, if such statements would be relevant as against such persons in relation to such position or liability in a suit brought by or against them, and if they are made whilst the person making them occupies such position or is subject to such liability.
Statements made by persons to whom a party to the suit has expressly referred for information in reference to a matter in dispute are admissions.

Admissions are relevant and may be proved as against the person who makes them, or his representative in interest; but they can not be proved by or on behalf of the person who makes them or by his representative in interest, except in the following cases:–

Section Text: Statements made by a party to the proceeding, or by an agent to any such party, whom the Court regards, under the circumstances of the case, as expressly or impliedly authorized by him to make them, are admissions.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is a statement from an authorized agent admissible as an admission against the principal?

Rule.Section 17: Defines what an admission is in general legal terms.
Rule.Section 18: States that statements from authorized agents of a party are considered legal admissions against that party.
Rule.Section 19: Governs admissions from third parties whose liability is a necessary component of the case.
Rule.Section 20: Relates to admissions from persons to whom a party specifically directed an inquiry.
Rule.Section 21: Dictates who is allowed to prove admissions in court.

Application.Section 17: Fails because it defines the concept but does not establish the specific rule for agency-based admissions.
Application.Section 18: Governs directly because the personal assistant is an 'agent' authorized to speak for Salman, making their statement a valid admission.
Application.Section 19: Fails since the assistant is an agent acting for Salman rather than an independent third party whose liability must be established.
Application.Section 20: Fails because Salman did not specifically tell the creditor to 'go ask the assistant' for the information.
Application.Section 21: Fails as it governs the process of proof rather than the threshold definition of a corporate or personal agent's admission.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 18, the personal assistant's statement is an admission because the law treats words from an authorized agent as admissions against the person they represent.

Question: Scenario-based: I found an old, handwritten family tree in a deceased relative's trunk that names my great-grandfather and his children. Can this tree be used in court to settle an inheritance claim?

Possible Sections.Full Text: In this Act the following words and expressions are used in the following senses, unless a contrary intention appears from the context:- "Court" includes all Judges and Magistrates and all persons, except arbitrators, legally authorized to take evidence. "Fact" means and includes-

Statements, written or verbal, of relevant facts made by a person who is dead, or who cannot be found, or who has become incapable of giving evidence, or whose attendance cannot be procured without an amount of delay or expense which under the circumstances of the case appears to the Court unreasonable, are themselves relevant facts in the following cases:–
Where the question is as to the existence of any right of custom, the following facts are relevant:–

An entry in any public or other official book, register or record [,or digital record], stating a fact in issue or relevant fact, and made by a public servant in the discharge of his official duty, or by any other person in performance of a duty specially enjoined by the law of the country in which such book, register [, record or digital record] is kept, is itself a relevant fact.
Facts not otherwise relevant are relevant–

Section Text: When the statement relates to the existence of any relationship by blood, marriage or adoption between persons deceased, and is made in any will or deed relating to the affairs of the family to which any such deceased person belonged, or in any family pedigree or upon any tombstone, family portrait or other thing on which such statements are usually made, and when such statement was made before the question in dispute was raised.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is an old family pedigree admissible as evidence of deceased family relationships?

Rule.Section 3: Defines what qualifies as a document but doesn't grant relevancy to its contents.
Rule.Section 32: Allows out-of-court family statements found on tombstones or pedigree charts if made before a dispute.
Rule.Section 13: Outlines relevancy for historical transactions and instances where a right or custom was exercised.
Rule.Section 35: Governs official government records created by public servants performing their administrative duties.
Rule.Section 11: Dictates that facts making the existence of an issue probable or improbable are relevant.

Application.Section 3: Fails because identifying the paper as a 'document' does not override the standard hearsay exclusion for its contents.
Application.Section 32: Governs directly as the document is a 'family pedigree' recording relationships between deceased persons, made before the dispute.
Application.Section 13: Fails since genealogy is a matter of kinship history rather than a traditional community custom or right of usage.
Application.Section 35: Fails as a private, handwritten family tree is not an entry in an official registry book.
Application.Section 11: Fails as Section 32 provides the specific, specialized mechanism for the admissibility of historical family records.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 32, Subsection (6), the family tree is relevant because it is a family pedigree recording relationships between deceased members and was made before the dispute.

Question: Scenario-based: A suspect in a police lockup confessed to a robbery while talking to a visiting relative. A local Magistrate was present in the room and heard the entire conversation. Can the prosecutor prove this confession in court?

Possible Sections.Full Text: A confession made by an accused person is irrelevant in a criminal proceeding, if the making of the confession appears to the Court to have been caused by any inducement, threat or promise having reference to the charge against the accused person, proceeding from a person in authority and sufficient, in the opinion of the Court, to give the accused person grounds which would appear to him reasonable for supposing that by making it he would gain any advantage or avoid any evil of a temporal nature in reference to the proceedings against him.
No confession made to a police-officer shall be proved as against a person accused of any offence.

Provided that, when any fact is deposed to as discovered in consequence of information received from a person accused of any offence, in the custody of a police officer, so much of such information, whether it amounts to a confession or not, as relates distinctly to the fact thereby discovered, may be proved.
No confession made by any person whilst he is in the custody of a police-officer, unless it be made in the immediate presence of a Magistrate, shall be proved as against such person.

If such a confession as is referred to in section 24 is made after the impression caused by any such inducement, threat or promise has, in the opinion of the Court, been fully removed, it is relevant.

Section Text: No confession made by any person whilst he is in the custody of a police-officer, unless it be made in the immediate presence of a Magistrate, shall be proved as against such person.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is a custodial confession admissible if made in a Magistrate's presence?

Rule.Section 24: Excludes confessions resulting from authoritative inducements or threats regarding the criminal charge.
Rule.Section 25: Imposes an absolute bar on proving confessions made directly to police officers.
Rule.Section 27: Permits proving specific information from an accused in custody that leads to discovering physical evidence.
Rule.Section 26: Prohibits proving custodial confessions unless they are made in a Magistrate's immediate presence.
Rule.Section 28: Declares previously induced confessions relevant if the court finds the harmful influence was fully removed.

Application.Section 24: Fails because the scenario describes a voluntary statement to a relative rather than one coerced by an authoritative promise.
Application.Section 25: Fails as the confession was spoken to a family member, not directly to the police officer.
Application.Section 27: Fails since the admission did not result in the discovery of any physical objects or hidden facts.
Application.Section 26: Governs directly because the Magistrate's immediate presence during the custodial conversation satisfies the specific statutory exception for admissibility.
Application.Section 28: Fails as there was no prior illegal inducement mentioned that required neutralization for the statement to be relevant.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 26, the confession is admissible because it was made in the immediate presence of a Magistrate, which is the only legal exception for proving a statement made in police custody.

Question: Scenario-based: I told the other side in a civil suit, 'Look, I admit I was driving fast, but only if you agree we don't mention this to the judge.' Can they now use my words against me?

Possible Sections.Full Text: A confession made by an accused person is irrelevant in a criminal proceeding, if the making of the confession appears to the Court to have been caused by any inducement, threat or promise having reference to the charge against the accused person, proceeding from a person in authority and sufficient, in the opinion of the Court, to give the accused person grounds which would appear to him reasonable for supposing that by making it he would gain any advantage or avoid any evil of a temporal nature in reference to the proceedings against him.

In civil cases no admission is relevant, if it is made either upon an express condition that evidence of it is not to be given, or under circumstances from which the Court can infer that the parties agreed together that evidence of it should not be given.
Admissions are not conclusive proof of the matters admitted but they may operate as estoppels under the provisions hereinafter contained.

Admissions are relevant and may be proved as against the person who makes them, or his representative in interest; but they can not be proved by or on behalf of the person who makes them or by his representative in interest, except in the following cases:–

An admission is a statement, oral or documentary [or contained in digital record], which suggests any inference as to any fact in issue or relevant fact, and which is made by any of the persons, and under the circumstances, hereinafter mentioned.

Section Text: In civil cases no admission is relevant, if it is made either upon an express condition that evidence of it is not to be given, or under circumstances from which the Court can infer that the parties agreed together that evidence of it should not be given.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is a confidential admission made during civil talks admissible in court?

Rule.Section 24: Excludes confessions in criminal trials obtained through inducements from authorities.
Rule.Section 23: States that admissions in civil cases are irrelevant if made under an express condition of secrecy.
Rule.Section 31: Dictates that admissions are not absolute proof but can create a legal bar via estoppel.
Rule.Section 21: Governs the general rule that admissions are proved against the party who made them.
Rule.Section 17: Provides the general definition for what constitutes a legal admission.

Application.Section 24: Fails because the scenario is a civil accident case rather than a criminal proceeding involving the state.
Application.Section 23: Governs directly because the admission of speeding was made on the express condition of secrecy during private talks.
Application.Section 31: Fails as an irrelevant admission under Section 23 cannot function as evidence or as a basis for estoppel.
Application.Section 21: Fails since Section 23 creates a specific statutory exclusion that overrides the general ability of an opponent to use admissions.
Application.Section 17: Fails as defining the statement as an admission does not address the legal privilege protecting the confidential talk.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 23, the statement is not relevant. The law protects admissions in civil cases when there was an express condition that they would stay secret.

Question: Scenario-based: I found an old metal plate on a village wall that says 'Government Irrigation Limit 1940.' Can I use a photo of this plate in court as a 'document' to prove the water boundary?

Possible Sections.Full Text: An entry in any public or other official book, register or record [,or digital record], stating a fact in issue or relevant fact, and made by a public servant in the discharge of his official duty, or by any other person in performance of a duty specially enjoined by the law of the country in which such book, register [, record or digital record] is kept, is itself a relevant fact.

In this Act the following words and expressions are used in the following senses, unless a contrary intention appears from the context:- "Court" includes all Judges and Magistrates and all persons, except arbitrators, legally authorized to take evidence. "Fact" means and includes-

Entries in books of account [or digital record], regularly kept in the course of business, are relevant whenever they refer to a matter into which the Court has to inquire, but such statements shall not alone be sufficient evidence to charge any person with liability.
Where the question is as to the existence of any right of custom, the following facts are relevant:–

Statements of facts in issue or relevant facts, made in published [maps, charts or digital record] generally offered for public sale, or in [maps, plans or digital record] made under the authority of the Government, as to matters usually represented or stated in such [maps, charts, plans or digital record], are themselves relevant facts.

Section Text: "Document" means any matter expressed or described upon any substance by means of letters, figures or marks, or by more than one of those means, intended to be used, or which may be used, for the purpose of recording that matter [and includes any digital record]. A writing is a document: Words printed, lithographed or photographed are documents: A map or plan is a document: An inscription on a metal plate or stone is a document: A caricature is a document.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is an inscription on a metal plate legally defined as a 'document'?

Rule.Section 35: Governs the relevancy of entries in official books made by public servants.
Rule.Section 3: Defines documents to include any matter on any substance, specifically naming metal plate inscriptions.
Rule.Section 34: Governs the relevancy and weight of commercial business ledgers.
Rule.Section 13: Relates to historical transactions used to prove a custom or family right.
Rule.Section 36: Relates to the relevancy of government-authorized maps and survey charts.

Application.Section 35: Fails since an inscription on a wall plate is not a formal book or registry kept by a clerk in an office.
Application.Section 3: Governs directly because the statutory definition explicitly lists an 'inscription on a metal plate' as a document.
Application.Section 34: Fails as a metal plate is not a commercial accounting ledger kept for business transactions.
Application.Section 13: Fails since the plate is a physical record rather than a private transaction like a deed or sale.
Application.Section 36: Fails as a boundary marker is not a published chart offered for public sale.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 3, the metal plate is legally considered a 'document' because the definition specifically includes inscriptions on metal or stone.

Question: Scenario-based: A man is being chased by a violent mob that wants to kill him. To save his life, he hijacks a stranger's car and speeds away, damaging the stranger's garage door. Is he guilty of theft or mischief?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Nothing is an offence merely by reason of its being done with the knowledge that it is likely to cause harm, if it be done without any criminal intention to cause harm, and in good faith for the purpose of preventing or avoiding other harm to person or property.

Except murder, and offences against the State punishable with death, nothing is an offence which is done by a person who is compelled to do it by threats, which, at the time of doing it, reasonably cause the apprehension that instant death to that person will otherwise be the consequence: Provided the person doing the act did not of his own accord, or from a reasonable apprehension of harm to himself short of instant death, place himself in the situation by which he became subject to such constraint.
Nothing is an offence which is done in the exercise of the right of private defence.
Whoever, intending to take dishonestly any moveable property out of the possession of any person without that person's consent, moves that property in order to such taking, is said to commit theft.

Whoever, with intent to cause, or knowing that he is likely to cause, wrongful loss or damage to the public or to any person, causes the destruction of any property, or any such change in any property or in the situation thereof as destroys or diminishes its value or utility, or affects it injuriously, commits "mischief".

Section Text: Nothing is an offence merely by reason of its being done with the knowledge that it is likely to cause harm, if it be done without any criminal intention to cause harm, and in good faith for the purpose of preventing or avoiding other harm to person or property.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is a person liable for damaging property to avoid being killed by a mob?

Rule.Section 81: Governs the doctrine of necessity, permitting minor harm to be done in good faith to prevent a much greater harm.
Rule.Section 94: Governs acts done under compulsion from threats of instant death by another person or group.
Rule.Section 96: Governs the right of private defense of the person or property against an unlawful attack.
Rule.Section 378: Governs theft, requiring a dishonest intent to permanently deprive an owner of their property.
Rule.Section 425: Governs mischief, requiring an intent to cause damage or loss to another's property.

Application.Section 81: Governs directly because hijacking the car and damaging the door was a necessary act done in good faith to avoid the much greater harm of death.
Application.Section 94: Fails because the mob was trying to kill him, but they weren't 'compelling' him to hijack the car as a condition of his life.
Application.Section 96: Fails because his act was against a third-party stranger (the car owner), not against the mob members themselves.
Application.Section 378: Fails because the man lacked 'dishonest intent'; he intended to save his life, not to steal the car for profit.
Application.Section 425: Fails because the damage was an accidental result of his escape (necessity) rather than an intentional act to cause the stranger a loss.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 81, the man is not guilty because he acted in good faith to avoid the greater harm of being killed by the mob.

Question: Scenario-based: I confessed to a crime while in the back of a police van. My lawyer says the confession is useless to the prosecutor because only a driver was present. However, a local Magistrate was also in the van and heard me. Is it admissible?

Possible Sections.Full Text: A confession made by an accused person is irrelevant in a criminal proceeding, if the making of the confession appears to the Court to have been caused by any inducement, threat or promise having reference to the charge against the accused person, proceeding from a person in authority and sufficient, in the opinion of the Court, to give the accused person grounds which would appear to him reasonable for supposing that by making it he would gain any advantage or avoid any evil of a temporal nature in reference to the proceedings against him.
No confession made to a police-officer shall be proved as against a person accused of any offence.
No confession made by any person whilst he is in the custody of a police-officer, unless it be made in the immediate presence of a Magistrate, shall be proved as against such person.

Provided that, when any fact is deposed to as discovered in consequence of information received from a person accused of any offence, in the custody of a police officer, so much of such information, whether it amounts to a confession or not, as relates distinctly to the fact thereby discovered, may be proved.

If such a confession as is referred to in section 24 is made after the impression caused by any such inducement, threat or promise has, in the opinion of the Court, been fully removed, it is relevant.

Section Text: No confession made by any person whilst he is in the custody of a police-officer, unless it be made in the immediate presence of a Magistrate, shall be proved as against such person.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is a confession made in police custody admissible if a Magistrate is immediately present?

Rule.Section 24: Governs confessions obtained through illegal inducements, threats, or promises from persons in authority.
Rule.Section 25: Creates an absolute bar against proving confessions made directly to police officers.
Rule.Section 26: Bars confessions in police custody unless they occur in the immediate presence of a Magistrate.
Rule.Section 27: Allows for the limited proof of custodial information that leads to the physical discovery of evidence.
Rule.Section 28: Permits confessions previously barred by threats if the court finds the harmful impression was removed.

Application.Section 24: Fails because there is no mention of a threat or bribe being used to force the statement in the van.
Application.Section 25: Fails because while in a police vehicle, the confession's validity is governed by the custody rules rather than the recipient alone.
Application.Section 26: Governs directly because the Magistrate's immediate presence in the police van satisfies the statutory exception for confessions made in custody.
Application.Section 27: Fails since the confession was a general admission rather than a specific clue leading to the discovery of a physical object.
Application.Section 28: Fails as the scenario concerns current custodial conditions rather than the removal of a prior mental inducement.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 26, the confession can be proved because it was made in the immediate presence of a Magistrate, even though you were in police custody at the time.

Question: Scenario-based: While sitting in a police station, Salman tells an officer, 'I was the one who broke into the warehouse last night.' Can the prosecutor use this admission as proof of guilt in court?

Possible Sections.Full Text: A confession made by an accused person is irrelevant in a criminal proceeding, if the making of the confession appears to the Court to have been caused by any inducement, threat or promise having reference to the charge against the accused person, proceeding from a person in authority and sufficient, in the opinion of the Court, to give the accused person grounds which would appear to him reasonable for supposing that by making it he would gain any advantage or avoid any evil of a temporal nature in reference to the proceedings against him.
No confession made by any person whilst he is in the custody of a police-officer, unless it be made in the immediate presence of a Magistrate, shall be proved as against such person.
No confession made to a police-officer shall be proved as against a person accused of any offence.

Provided that, when any fact is deposed to as discovered in consequence of information received from a person accused of any offence, in the custody of a police officer, so much of such information, whether it amounts to a confession or not, as relates distinctly to the fact thereby discovered, may be proved.

If such a confession as is referred to in section 24 is made after the impression caused by any such inducement, threat or promise has, in the opinion of the Court, been fully removed, it is relevant.

Section Text: No confession made to a police-officer shall be proved as against a person accused of any offence.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is a confession made directly to a police officer admissible as evidence against the accused?

Rule.Section 24: Excludes confessions triggered by authoritative inducements, promises, or threats from being used in criminal proceedings.
Rule.Section 26: Bars confessions made by persons in police custody unless a Magistrate is physically present during the statement.
Rule.Section 25: Imposes an absolute bar on the use of any confession made to a police officer as evidence against an accused.
Rule.Section 27: Permits proving specific custodial information that relates distinctly to a fact discovered by the police.
Rule.Section 28: Declares an induced confession relevant if the influence of the initial promise or threat has been completely removed.

Application.Section 24: Fails because the scenario describes a voluntary statement rather than one coerced by a specific promise or threat from the officer.
Application.Section 26: Fails because Section 25 provides a more specific and absolute bar for statements made directly to police personnel regardless of location.
Application.Section 25: Governs directly because the confession was made to a police officer, triggering the absolute statutory exclusion regardless of the statement's voluntary nature.
Application.Section 27: Fails since the admission did not lead to the discovery of any physical objects like stolen property or tools.
Application.Section 28: Fails because there was no prior illegal inducement mentioned that required neutralization before the statement could be considered relevant.

Question: Scenario-based: Salman is accused of committing a theft in Dhaka at noon today. He produces a time-stamped video of himself signing a document in Chittagong at 12:05 PM today. Which rule allows this?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Facts not otherwise relevant are relevant–

Facts which are the occasions, cause or effect, immediate or otherwise, of relevant facts, or facts in issue, or which constitute the state of things under which they happened, or which afforded an opportunity for their occurrence or transaction, are relevant.

Facts necessary to explain or introduce a fact in issue or relevant fact, or which support or rebut an inference suggested by a fact in issue or relevant fact, or which establish the identity of anything or person whose identity is relevant, or fix the time or place at which any fact in issue or relevant fact happened or which show the relation of parties by whom any such fact was transacted, are relevant in so far as they are necessary for that purpose.

Facts showing the existence of any state of mind, such as intention, knowledge, good faith, negligence, rashness, ill-will, or good-will towards any particular person, or showing the existence of any state of body or bodily feeling, are relevant, when the existence of any such state of mind or body or bodily feeling is in issue or relevant.
When there is a question whether a particular act was done, the existence of any course of business, according to which it naturally would have been done, is a relevant fact.

Section Text: Facts not otherwise relevant are relevant– (1) If they are inconsistent with any fact in issue or relevant fact; (2) If by themselves or in connection with other facts they make the existence or non-existence of any fact in issue or relevant fact highly probable or improbable.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is a fact that makes the commission of a crime physically impossible admissible in court?

Rule.Section 11: Determines that facts which are inconsistent with a fact in issue or make its non-existence highly probable are relevant.
Rule.Section 7: Governs the relevancy of facts providing the occasion, cause, or opportunity for a crime to occur.
Rule.Section 9: Governs facts required to explain events or establish the time and place of a fact in issue.
Rule.Section 14: Outlines the relevancy of facts demonstrating a specific state of mind like intent or negligence.
Rule.Section 16: Governs the relevancy of routine business processes used to prove that a specific act naturally occurred.

Application.Section 11: Governs directly because being in Chittagong five minutes after a crime in Dhaka is physically inconsistent with committing that crime.
Application.Section 7: Fails because the video represents the impossibility of the theft rather than providing an occasion or opportunity for it.
Application.Section 9: Fails since the goal is to logically contradict the prosecution's case rather than merely introducing the scene of the theft.
Application.Section 14: Fails as the physical location and travel time of the accused are objective facts rather than internal states of mind.
Application.Section 16: Fails since signing a document is an individual act rather than a repetitive, professional course of business process.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 11, Subsection (1), the video is relevant because your presence in Chittagong is inconsistent with the fact that you committed the crime in Dhaka at that time.

Question: Scenario-based: Salim enters into a fenced yard at night to pick up his soccer ball that went over the fence. He does not intend to commit a crime or bother anyone. Has he committed the crime of 'criminal trespass'?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Whoever enters into or upon property in the possession of another with intent to commit an offence or to intimidate, insult or annoy any person in possession of such property, or, having lawfully entered into or upon such property, unlawfully remains there with intent thereby to intimidate, insult or annoy any such person, or with intent to commit an offence, is said to commit 'criminal trespass'.

Whoever commits criminal trespass by entering into or remaining in any building, tent or vessel used as a human dwelling or any building used as a place for worship, or as a place for the custody of property, is said to commit "house-trespass".

Whoever commits criminal trespass shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three months, or with fine which may extend to five hundred taka, or with both.
Whoever, intending to take dishonestly any moveable property out of the possession of any person without that person's consent, moves that property in order to such taking, is said to commit theft.

Nothing is an offence by reason that it causes, or that it is intended to cause, or that it is known to be likely to cause, any harm, if that harm is so slight that no person of ordinary sense and temper would complain of such harm.

Section Text: Whoever enters into or upon property in the possession of another with intent to commit an offence or to intimidate, insult or annoy any person in possession of such property, or, having lawfully entered into or upon such property, unlawfully remains there with intent thereby to intimidate, insult or annoy any such person, or with intent to commit an offence, is said to commit 'criminal trespass'.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Does entry onto private land without a criminal intent satisfy the definition of criminal trespass?

Rule.Section 441: Defines criminal trespass as entry with intent to commit an offense, intimidate, insult, or annoy.
Rule.Section 442: Defines house-trespass as entering a specific building used as a dwelling.
Rule.Section 447: Governs the three-month punishment for the act of criminal trespass.
Rule.Section 378: Governs the definition of theft, requiring the movement of property with dishonest intent.
Rule.Section 95: Governs the general exception for acts that cause only trivial or slight harm.

Application.Section 441: Governs directly because it establishes that a specific 'intent' (offense/annoyance) is required for the entry to be a crime; Salim lacked this intent.
Application.Section 442: Fails because the scenario takes place in a 'yard' rather than inside a building used as a human dwelling.
Application.Section 447: Fails because it is a penalty provision and does not provide the defining 'intent' criteria Salim is missing.
Application.Section 378: Fails because Salim was retrieving his own ball rather than taking someone else's property dishonestly.
Application.Section 95: Fails as a primary selection because Section 441's own definition explicitly excludes Salim's act due to the lack of criminal intent.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 441, Salim has not committed criminal trespass because he entered the yard only to retrieve his ball and not to commit an offense or annoy anyone.

Statements of facts in issue or relevant facts, made in published [maps, charts or digital record] generally offered for public sale, or in [maps, plans or digital record] made under the authority of the Government, as to matters usually represented or stated in such [maps, charts, plans or digital record], are themselves relevant facts.

Where the question is as to the existence of any right of custom, the following facts are relevant:–

In this Act the following words and expressions are used in the following senses, unless a contrary intention appears from the context:- "Court" includes all Judges and Magistrates and all persons, except arbitrators, legally authorized to take evidence. "Fact" means and includes-

Section Text: Statements of facts in issue or relevant facts, made in published [maps, charts or digital record] generally offered for public sale, or in [maps, plans or digital record] made under the authority of the Government, as to matters usually represented or stated in such [maps, charts, plans or digital record], are themselves relevant facts.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is a government-authorized survey map admissible as a relevant fact in court?

Rule.Section 35: Makes entries in official public registers relevant if made by public servants performing their duties.
Rule.Section 36: Grants relevancy to maps and charts published under government authority or for public sale.
Rule.Section 37: Governs the relevancy of public facts found in official government Gazette notifications.
