Application.Section 37: Fails since boundary lines are individual geographical facts rather than broad administrative announcements in a notification.
Application.Section 13: Fails because an official government survey is an administrative record rather than a private sale or mortgage transaction.
Application.Section 3: Fails because simply identifying the map as a 'document' does not grant inherent relevancy to its geographical statements.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 36, the map is a relevant fact because it was made under the authority of the Government to show territorial boundaries.

Question: Scenario-based: Salim is deeply depressed and decides to take his own life. He is caught by police while climbing over a high bridge railing. He is saved, but the police arrest him. Can he be punished for this act?

Possible Sections.Full Text: If any person commits suicide, whoever abets the commission of such suicide, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine.

Whoever attempts to commit suicide and does any act towards the commission of such offence, shall be punished with simple imprisonment for a term which may extend to one year, or with fine, or with both.

Whoever attempts to commit an offence punishable by this Code with imprisonment for life or imprisonment, or to cause such an offence to be committed, and in such attempt does any act towards the commission of the offence, shall, where no express provision is made by this Code for the punishment of such attempt, be punished with imprisonment of any description provided for the offence, for a term which may extend to one-half of the longest term of imprisonment provided for that offence, or with such fine as is provided for the offence, or with both.

Nothing is an offence which is done by a person who, at the time of doing it, by reason of unsoundness of mind, is incapable of knowing the nature of the act, or that he is doing what is either wrong or contrary to law.

Whoever does any act so rashly or negligently as to endanger human life or the personal safety of others, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three months, or with fine which may extend to two hundred and fifty taka, or with both.

Section Text: Whoever attempts to commit suicide and does any act towards the commission of such offence, shall be punished with simple imprisonment for a term which may extend to one year, or with fine, or with both.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is attempting to take one's own life a specific criminal offense under the Code?

Rule.Section 306: Governs the abetment of 'another' person's suicide and only applies if the person actually dies.
Rule.Section 309: Governs the individual's own attempt to commit suicide, carrying a maximum one-year sentence.
Rule.Section 511: Governs general attempts but is typically superseded by specific sections like 309.
Rule.Section 84: Provides a defense for insanity, which requires a complete loss of the power to understand right from wrong.
Rule.Section 336: Governs acts that endanger 'others' safety rather than self-harming acts.

Application.Section 306: Fails because Salim was acting alone and did not successfully complete the suicide.
Application.Section 309: Governs directly because Salim's act of climbing the bridge railing was an 'act towards the commission' of suicide, which is specifically criminalized.
Application.Section 511: Fails because the Penal Code contains a specific provision (309) for suicide, rendering the general attempt rule unnecessary.
Application.Section 84: Fails because depression or a suicide attempt does not automatically satisfy the extreme legal definition of total mental unsoundness.
Application.Section 336: Fails because the primary intent and act were focused on self-destruction rather than creating a hazard for other pedestrians.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 309, Salim is guilty because the law punishes any person who attempts to commit suicide with up to one year in prison.

Question: Scenario-based: While in police custody for a robbery, Abul said, 'I threw the stolen wallet into the old well behind my house.' The police went to the well and found the wallet exactly where he said. Is his statement allowed as evidence?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Provided that, when any fact is deposed to as discovered in consequence of information received from a person accused of any offence, in the custody of a police officer, so much of such information, whether it amounts to a confession or not, as relates distinctly to the fact thereby discovered, may be proved.
No confession made to a police-officer shall be proved as against a person accused of any offence.
No confession made by any person whilst he is in the custody of a police-officer, unless it be made in the immediate presence of a Magistrate, shall be proved as against such person.

A confession made by an accused person is irrelevant in a criminal proceeding, if the making of the confession appears to the Court to have been caused by any inducement, threat or promise having reference to the charge against the accused person, proceeding from a person in authority and sufficient, in the opinion of the Court, to give the accused person grounds which would appear to him reasonable for supposing that by making it he would gain any advantage or avoid any evil of a temporal nature in reference to the proceedings against him.

If such a confession as is referred to in section 24 is made after the impression caused by any such inducement, threat or promise has, in the opinion of the Court, been fully removed, it is relevant.

Section Text: Provided that, when any fact is deposed to as discovered in consequence of information received from a person accused of any offence, in the custody of a police officer, so much of such information, whether it amounts to a confession or not, as relates distinctly to the fact thereby discovered, may be proved.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Can a statement made in police custody be admitted if it leads to the discovery of physical evidence?

Rule.Section 27: Allows for the proof of information from an accused in custody only so far as it relates distinctly to a discovered fact.
Rule.Section 25: Creates an absolute bar against confessions made to police officers.
Rule.Section 26: Bars confessions made in custody unless a Magistrate is present.
Rule.Section 24: Excludes confessions triggered by inducements or threats from authorities.
Rule.Section 28: Permits confessions once the influence of a prior inducement has been removed.

Application.Section 27: Governs directly as the discovery of the wallet validates the specific part of the statement that led the police to the old well.
Application.Section 25: Fails because Section 27 acts as a specific proviso that overrides the general bar on police confessions when physical facts are discovered.
Application.Section 26: Fails since Section 27 provides an exception to the custody bar, allowing discovery-related facts even without a Magistrate.
Application.Section 24: Fails because there is no mention of a threat or promise being used to extract the location of the wallet.
Application.Section 28: Fails because the admissibility here depends on the physical discovery rather than the removal of a prior mental inducement.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 27, the part of the statement about the well is allowed because it led directly to the discovery of the stolen wallet.

Question: Scenario-based: Salman is suspected of burning down his warehouse to collect insurance money. Can the insurance company show the court that Salman's previous three warehouses also burned down exactly one month after he increased his insurance coverage?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Facts showing the existence of any state of mind, such as intention, knowledge, good faith, negligence, rashness, ill-will, or good-will towards any particular person, or showing the existence of any state of body or bodily feeling, are relevant, when the existence of any such state of mind or body or bodily feeling is in issue or relevant.

When there is a question whether an act was accidental or intentional, or done with a particular knowledge or intention, the fact that such act formed part of a series of similar occurrences, in each of which the person doing the act was concerned, is relevant.

Any fact is relevant which shows or constitutes a motive or preparation for any fact in issue or relevant fact. The conduct of any party, or of any agent to any party, to any suit or proceeding, in reference to such suit or proceeding, or in reference to any fact in issue therein or relevant thereto, and the conduct of any person an offence against whom is the subject of any proceeding, is relevant, if such conduct influences or is influenced by any fact, in issue or relevant fact, and whether it was previous subsequent thereto.

Facts which are the occasions, cause or effect, immediate or otherwise, of relevant facts, or facts in issue, or which constitute the state of things under which they happened, or which afforded an opportunity for their occurrence or transaction, are relevant.
Facts not otherwise relevant are relevant–

Section Text: When there is a question whether an act was accidental or intentional, or done with a particular knowledge or intention, the fact that such act formed part of a series of similar occurrences, in each of which the person doing the act was concerned, is relevant.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is a series of similar fires relevant to prove intent in an insurance fraud case?

Rule.Section 14: Governs the relevancy of facts demonstrating internal states like intent or knowledge.
Rule.Section 15: Governs a series of similar acts to distinguish between accident and intentional conduct.
Rule.Section 8: Relates to facts showing motive, preparation, or subsequent conduct of parties.
Rule.Section 7: Governs facts providing the occasion, cause, or opportunity for an event.
Rule.Section 11: Allows facts that are inconsistent with a claim or affect its probability.

Application.Section 14: Fails because identifying a state of mind does not provide the specific logic for using a pattern of events as proof.
Application.Section 15: Governs directly because the four fires form a 'series of similar occurrences' which proves the latest act was not accidental.
Application.Section 8: Fails as the previous fires are used to show a lack of accident rather than being preparation for the current fire.
Application.Section 7: Fails because the previous warehouse fires are not the immediate physical cause or effect of the latest fire.
Application.Section 11: Fails as the issue is intent through pattern recognition rather than logical contradiction or distance.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 15, Illustration (a), the previous fires are relevant because they suggest the current fire was intentional rather than a series of accidental events.

Question: Scenario-based: I found an old tombstone in a graveyard that has my family name and a birth date from 1850 on it. Can I use a photo of this tombstone in court to prove my ancestor's birth date?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Where the question is as to the existence of any right of custom, the following facts are relevant:–

Statements, written or verbal, of relevant facts made by a person who is dead, or who cannot be found, or who has become incapable of giving evidence, or whose attendance cannot be procured without an amount of delay or expense which under the circumstances of the case appears to the Court unreasonable, are themselves relevant facts in the following cases:–

Entries in books of account [or digital record], regularly kept in the course of business, are relevant whenever they refer to a matter into which the Court has to inquire, but such statements shall not alone be sufficient evidence to charge any person with liability.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is an inscription on a tombstone admissible to prove family relationships and birth dates?

Rule.Section 13: Relates to proving customs or rights through historical usage and transactions.
Rule.Section 32: Allows out-of-court statements by deceased persons, including those found on family tombstones and portraits.
Rule.Section 35: Governs the relevancy of entries in official public registers kept by government employees.

Application.Section 34: Fails as grave inscriptions are not commercial entries kept for tracing business debts.
Application.Section 3: Fails because simply being a 'document' does not make its contents relevant to proving birth history without Section 32(6).

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 32, Subsection (6), the inscription on the tombstone is a relevant fact that can be used to prove family history and relationships.

Question: Scenario-based: During a private mediation for a civil land dispute, I offered to pay the other side Tk. 1,00,000 to end the case, provided the offer was kept secret. Can they now use my offer to show the court that I admitted I was wrong?

Possible Sections.Full Text: An admission is a statement, oral or documentary [or contained in digital record], which suggests any inference as to any fact in issue or relevant fact, and which is made by any of the persons, and under the circumstances, hereinafter mentioned.

Admissions are relevant and may be proved as against the person who makes them, or his representative in interest; but they can not be proved by or on behalf of the person who makes them or by his representative in interest, except in the following cases:–

In civil cases no admission is relevant, if it is made either upon an express condition that evidence of it is not to be given, or under circumstances from which the Court can infer that the parties agreed together that evidence of it should not be given.

A confession made by an accused person is irrelevant in a criminal proceeding, if the making of the confession appears to the Court to have been caused by any inducement, threat or promise having reference to the charge against the accused person, proceeding from a person in authority and sufficient, in the opinion of the Court, to give the accused person grounds which would appear to him reasonable for supposing that by making it he would gain any advantage or avoid any evil of a temporal nature in reference to the proceedings against him.
Admissions are not conclusive proof of the matters admitted but they may operate as estoppels under the provisions hereinafter contained.

Section Text: In civil cases no admission is relevant, if it is made either upon an express condition that evidence of it is not to be given, or under circumstances from which the Court can infer that the parties agreed together that evidence of it should not be given.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is a confidential settlement offer admissible as an admission in a civil proceeding?

Rule.Section 17: Defines an admission as any statement suggesting an inference about a fact in issue.
Rule.Section 21: Governs the general rule that admissions are proved against the party who made them.
Rule.Section 23: Excludes admissions in civil cases made under an express or implied condition of secrecy.
Rule.Section 24: Bars confessions in criminal trials obtained through inducements or threats from authorities.
Rule.Section 31: States that admissions are not absolute proof but can create a legal bar via estoppel.

Application.Section 17: Fails because identifying the offer as an admission does not address the legal privilege protecting settlement talks.
Application.Section 21: Fails as Section 23 creates a specific statutory exclusion that overrides the general rule of proving admissions.
Application.Section 23: Governs directly because the payment offer was made on the express condition of secrecy during a civil mediation.
Application.Section 24: Fails as the dispute is a civil land matter rather than a criminal proceeding involving a state charge.
Application.Section 31: Fails since an irrelevant admission under Section 23 cannot function as proof or a basis for estoppel.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 23, the offer is not relevant. The law protects admissions made in civil cases when there is an agreement that the conversation will stay secret.

Question: Scenario-based: I found an old, signed entry in a deceased business owner's ledger where he noted that he received a deposit of Tk. 5,000 from my father. Can I use this entry to prove the payment happened?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Entries in books of account [or digital record], regularly kept in the course of business, are relevant whenever they refer to a matter into which the Court has to inquire, but such statements shall not alone be sufficient evidence to charge any person with liability.

Statements, written or verbal, of relevant facts made by a person who is dead, or who cannot be found, or who has become incapable of giving evidence, or whose attendance cannot be procured without an amount of delay or expense which under the circumstances of the case appears to the Court unreasonable, are themselves relevant facts in the following cases:–

An entry in any public or other official book, register or record [,or digital record], stating a fact in issue or relevant fact, and made by a public servant in the discharge of his official duty, or by any other person in performance of a duty specially enjoined by the law of the country in which such book, register [, record or digital record] is kept, is itself a relevant fact.
When there is a question whether a particular act was done, the existence of any course of business, according to which it naturally would have been done, is a relevant fact.

An admission is a statement, oral or documentary [or contained in digital record], which suggests any inference as to any fact in issue or relevant fact, and which is made by any of the persons, and under the circumstances, hereinafter mentioned.

Section Text: Statements, written or verbal, of relevant facts made by a person who is dead, or who cannot be found, or who has become incapable of giving evidence, or whose attendance cannot be procured without an amount of delay or expense which under the circumstances of the case appears to the Court unreasonable, are themselves relevant facts in the following cases:–

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is a deceased person's business ledger entry admissible as a relevant fact?

Rule.Section 34: Governs the relevancy and weight of account books in establishing financial liability.
Rule.Section 32: Governs out-of-court statements from deceased persons made in the ordinary course of business.
Rule.Section 35: Governs official entries made in government public records by public servants.
Rule.Section 16: Governs the existence of a routine business process to infer an act happened.
Rule.Section 17: Defines what qualifies as a legal admission in a trial.

Application.Section 34: Fails because it addresses the weight and sufficiency of records from living parties rather than the hearsay exception for the dead.
Application.Section 32: Governs directly because the ledger author is deceased and the entry was made in the ordinary course of his business duty.
Application.Section 35: Fails since a private shopkeeper's ledger is not an official public record kept by a government clerk.
Application.Section 16: Fails as the evidence is a specific written statement rather than proof of a general repetitive routine.
Application.Section 17: Fails as defining the entry as an admission does not overcome the hurdle of the author's death without Section 32.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 32, Subsection (2), the ledger entry is relevant because it is a business record made by a person who is now deceased.

Question: Scenario-based: I have a handwritten account book for my tailor shop showing that Abul owes me Taka 2,000 for a suit. Is this book enough by itself for the court to force him to pay?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Entries in books of account [or digital record], regularly kept in the course of business, are relevant whenever they refer to a matter into which the Court has to inquire, but such statements shall not alone be sufficient evidence to charge any person with liability.
When there is a question whether a particular act was done, the existence of any course of business, according to which it naturally would have been done, is a relevant fact.

Statements, written or verbal, of relevant facts made by a person who is dead, or who cannot be found, or who has become incapable of giving evidence, or whose attendance cannot be procured without an amount of delay or expense which under the circumstances of the case appears to the Court unreasonable, are themselves relevant facts in the following cases:–

An entry in any public or other official book, register or record [,or digital record], stating a fact in issue or relevant fact, and made by a public servant in the discharge of his official duty, or by any other person in performance of a duty specially enjoined by the law of the country in which such book, register [, record or digital record] is kept, is itself a relevant fact.

An admission is a statement, oral or documentary [or contained in digital record], which suggests any inference as to any fact in issue or relevant fact, and which is made by any of the persons, and under the circumstances, hereinafter mentioned.

Section Text: Entries in books of account [or digital record], regularly kept in the course of business, are relevant whenever they refer to a matter into which the Court has to inquire, but such statements shall not alone be sufficient evidence to charge any person with liability.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Are business account entries sufficient by themselves to prove a debt liability in court?

Rule.Section 34: Confirms business ledger entries are relevant but strictly insufficient on their own to create legal liability.
Rule.Section 16: Declares that the existence of a routine process is relevant to prove that a specific act occurred.
Rule.Section 32: Provides a hearsay exception for business records when the person who made them is deceased.
Rule.Section 35: Governs the relevancy of entries made by public servants in official government registers.
Rule.Section 17: Defines what qualifies as a legal admission based on its ability to suggest an inference.

Application.Section 34: Governs directly by identifying the tailor's ledger as relevant but explicitly requiring additional evidence to establish the debt liability.
Application.Section 16: Fails as the inquiry is about the finality of a debt record rather than proving a general routine was followed.
Application.Section 32: Fails because the tailor is alive to testify, meaning the hearsay exception for the dead is not applicable.
Application.Section 35: Fails since a tailor shop's private ledger is not an official public book kept by a government clerk.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 34, the book is relevant, but it is not sufficient on its own to charge Abul with liability for the unpaid suit.

Question: Scenario-based: I want to prove what was written in an email sent to my boss. Can I just repeat the words of the email from memory in court if no one is arguing that the email itself is fake?

Possible Sections.Full Text: An admission is a statement, oral or documentary [or contained in digital record], which suggests any inference as to any fact in issue or relevant fact, and which is made by any of the persons, and under the circumstances, hereinafter mentioned.

Oral admissions as to the contents of a document are not relevant, unless and until that the party proposing to prove them shows that he is entitled to give secondary evidence of the contents of such document
Oral admissions as to the contents of digital records are not relevant, unless the genuineness of the digital record produced is in question.

Entries in books of account [or digital record], regularly kept in the course of business, are relevant whenever they refer to a matter into which the Court has to inquire, but such statements shall not alone be sufficient evidence to charge any person with liability.

When any statement of which evidence is given forms part of a longer statement, or of a conversation or part of an isolated document, or is contained in a document which forms part of a book, or of part of a digital record or of a connected series of letters or papers, evidence shall be given of so much and no more of the statement, conversation, document, book of series of letters or papers as the Court considers necessary in that particular case to the full understanding of the nature and effect of the statement, and of the circumstances under which it was made.

Section Text: Oral admissions as to the contents of digital records are not relevant, unless the genuineness of the digital record produced is in question.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is oral testimony regarding digital record contents relevant without an authenticity dispute?

Rule.Section 17: Statutorily defines admissions but does not regulate the method of proving their digital contents.
Rule.Section 22: Governs oral admissions regarding physical documents and regulates when spoken secondary evidence is allowed.
Rule.Section 22A: Deems oral admissions regarding digital record contents irrelevant unless the record's genuineness is the specific point in question.
Rule.Section 34: Governs the relevancy of business records stored digitally but not the method of proving their text.
Rule.Section 39: Requires enough context for a full understanding of a statement but does not regulate the oral-digital barrier.

Application.Section 17: Fails as identifying the email as an admission does not provide the procedural rule for its digital proof.
Application.Section 22: Fails because the law distinguishes between traditional paper documents and the more restrictive rules for electronic digital records.
Application.Section 22A: Governs directly because the witness's verbal account of the email's text is irrelevant since the file's genuineness is not being challenged.
Application.Section 34: Fails since a personal email to a boss is not a commercial ledger kept for business inquiry.
Application.Section 39: Fails because providing more context does not cure the foundational bar against oral evidence for digital data.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 22A, you cannot repeat the email's words from memory. Oral statements about digital records are only relevant if there is a dispute about the record's genuineness.

Question: Scenario-based: A man was sentenced to death by the Sessions Court. His family is panicking and wants to know if he will be executed immediately tomorrow morning.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 31
Possible Sections.Full Text: (2) A Sessions Judge or Additional Sessions Judge may pass any sentence authorized by law; but any sentence of death passed by any such Judge shall be subject to confirmation by the High Court Division.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 374
Possible Sections.Full Text: When the Court of Session passes sentence of death, the proceedings shall be submitted to the High Court Division and the sentence shall not be executed unless it is confirmed by the High Court Division.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 410
Possible Sections.Full Text: Any person convicted on a trial held by a Sessions Judge or an Additional Sessions Judge may appeal to the High Court Division.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 381
Possible Sections.Full Text: When a sentence of death passed by a Court of Session is submitted to the High Court Division for confirmation, such Court of Session shall, on receiving the order of confirmation or other order of the High Court Division thereon, cause such order to be carried into effect by issuing a warrant or taking such other steps as may be necessary.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 401
Possible Sections.Full Text: (1) When any person has been sentenced to punishment for an offence, the Government may at any time without conditions or upon any conditions which the person sentenced accepts, suspend the execution of his sentence or remit the whole or any part of the punishment to which he has been sentenced.

Relevant Section: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 374
Section Text: When the Court of Session passes sentence of death, the proceedings shall be submitted to the High Court Division and the sentence shall not be executed unless it is confirmed by the High Court Division.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is a death sentence from a Sessions Court immediately enforceable without higher court confirmation?

Rule.Section 31: Confers general sentencing power on Sessions Judges while noting the necessity of confirmation for death penalties.
Rule.Section 374: Mandates the submission of death sentence proceedings to the High Court Division for compulsory confirmation before execution.
Rule.Section 410: Provides the right to appeal a conviction from a Sessions Court to the High Court Division.
Rule.Section 381: Directs the lower court on how to proceed with execution only after receiving the High Court's confirmation.
Rule.Section 401: Grants the executive government power to suspend or remit sentences independently of judicial confirmation processes.

Application.Section 31: Fails because it defines the judge's capacity to sentence rather than the specific procedural bar on immediate execution.
Application.Section 374: Governs directly as it explicitly forbids execution until the High Court Division formally confirms the proceedings and sentence.
Application.Section 410: Fails because an appeal is a voluntary action by the defendant, whereas the confirmation requirement is a mandatory procedural safeguard.
Application.Section 381: Fails because it describes the 'after-action' steps of execution once confirmation is already secured, which hasn't happened here.
Application.Section 401: Fails because it concerns executive clemency rather than the mandatory judicial oversight required for capital punishment cases.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, section 374, the man cannot be executed tomorrow; the law requires the Court of Session to submit the case for mandatory confirmation by the High Court Division.

Question: Scenario-based: In a dispute over a flooded field, a farmer brings a map prepared by the government's surveying department to show the field's boundaries. Can the court accept this map as relevant automatically?

When the Court has to form an opinion as to the existence of any fact of a public nature, any statement of it, made in a recital contained in any Act of Parliament of the United Kingdom, or in any [Act of Parliament] or in a Government notification is a relevant fact.

When the Court has to form an opinion as to a law of any country, any statement of such law contained in a book purporting to be printed or published under the authority of the Government of such country and to contain any such law, and any report of a ruling of the Courts of such country contained in a book purporting to be a report of such rulings, is relevant.

In this Act the following words and expressions are used in the following senses, unless a contrary intention appears from the context:- "Court" includes all Judges and Magistrates and all persons, except arbitrators, legally authorized to take evidence. "Fact" means and includes-
Where the question is as to the existence of any right of custom, the following facts are relevant:–

Section Text: Statements of facts in issue or relevant facts, made in published [maps, charts or digital record] generally offered for public sale, or in [maps, plans or digital record] made under the authority of the Government, as to matters usually represented or stated in such [maps, charts, plans or digital record], are themselves relevant facts.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is a map prepared by the government admissible as a relevant fact in boundary disputes?

Rule.Section 36: Makes statements in maps or plans prepared under government authority relevant facts.
Rule.Section 37: Outlines relevancy for facts of a public nature found in official government notifications.
Rule.Section 38: Makes law-books published by government authority relevant for proving foreign or domestic law.
Rule.Section 3: Defines what a document is, including maps, but doesn't state when their contents are inherently relevant.
Rule.Section 13: Relates to historical transactions used to prove a custom or right.

Application.Section 36: Governs directly because the map was made under government authority to represent land boundaries, which are facts in issue.
Application.Section 37: Fails because boundaries of a private field are not 'facts of a public nature' that would be published in a general Gazette notification.
Application.Section 38: Fails since the farmer is proving a physical boundary, not a point of legal doctrine or foreign law.
Application.Section 3: Fails because being a 'document' doesn't automatically make the statements within it 'relevant facts' without the specific trigger of Section 36.
Application.Section 13: Fails because a current government survey map is an official record rather than a private transaction like a deed or sale.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 36, the map is a relevant fact because it was made under the authority of the Government to show territorial matters.

Question: Scenario-based: Salim and Rahim decide to rob a store. Salim waits outside at the corner to watch for police, while Rahim goes inside to take the money. If Salim is caught outside while Rahim is inside, has Salim legally committed the robbery?

Possible Sections.Full Text: A person abets the doing of a thing, who: Firstly.-Instigates any person to do that thing; or Secondly.-Engages with one or more other person or persons in any conspiracy for the doing of that thing, if an act or illegal omission takes place in pursuance of that conspiracy, and in order to the doing of that thing; or Thirdly.-Intentionally aids, by any act or illegal omission, the doing of that thing.

Whenever any person, who if absent would be liable to be punished as an abettor, is present when the act or offence for which he would be punishable in consequence of the abetment is committed, he shall be deemed to have committed such act or offence.
When a criminal act is done by several persons, in furtherance of the common intention of all, each of such persons is liable for that act in the same manner as if it were done by him alone.

A person abets an offence, who abets either the commission of an offence, or the commission of an act which would be an offence, if committed by a person capable by law of committing an offence with the same intention or knowledge as that of the abettor.

If an offence is committed by any member of an unlawful assembly in prosecution of the common object of that assembly, or such as the members of that assembly knew to be likely to be committed in prosecution of that object, every person who, at the time of the committing of that offence, is a member of the same assembly, is guilty of that offence.

Question: Scenario-based: Salman was robbed in a quiet alley. To prove the theft could have happened, the prosecutor wants to show that the alley was poorly lit and had no witnesses nearby at that time of night. Is this relevant?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Facts which are the occasions, cause or effect, immediate or otherwise, of relevant facts, or facts in issue, or which constitute the state of things under which they happened, or which afforded an opportunity for their occurrence or transaction, are relevant.

Facts which, though not in issue, are so connected with a fact in issue as to form part of the same transaction, are relevant, whether they occurred at the same time and place or at different times and places.

The conduct of any party, or of any agent to any party, to any suit or proceeding, in reference to such suit or proceeding, or in reference to any fact in issue therein or relevant thereto, and the conduct of any person an offence against whom is the subject of any proceeding, is relevant, if such conduct influences or is influenced by any fact, in issue or relevant fact, and whether it was previous subsequent thereto.

Facts showing the existence of any state of mind, such as intention, knowledge, good faith, negligence, rashness, ill-will, or good-will towards any particular person, or showing the existence of any state of body or bodily feeling, are relevant, when the existence of any such state of mind or body or bodily feeling is in issue or relevant.

Question: Scenario-based: Salim and Jamil decide to rob a store. Salim waits outside with a gun to scare off anyone who might interfere, while Jamil goes inside to take the money. Has Salim legally committed the robbery himself?

Question: Scenario-based: A man pushes his neighbor during an argument. The neighbor falls and hits his head on a rock, dying instantly. The man only meant to push him away, not to kill him. What is the correct charge?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Whoever commits murder shall be punished with death, or imprisonment for life, and shall also be liable to fine.

Whoever commits culpable homicide not amounting to murder, shall be punished with imprisonment for life, or imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine, if the act by which the death is caused is done with intention of causing death, or of causing such bodily injury as is likely to cause death; or with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to ten years, or with fine, or with both, if the act is done with the knowledge that it is likely to cause death, but without any intention to cause death or to cause such bodily injury as is likely to cause death.

Whoever causes the death of any person by doing any rash or negligent act not amounting to culpable homicide shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to five years, or with fine, or with both.

Whoever, except in the case provided for by section 334, voluntarily causes hurt, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to one year, or with fine which may extend to one thousand taka, or with both.

Nothing is an offence which is done by accident or misfortune, and without any criminal intention or knowledge in the doing of a lawful act in a lawful manner by lawful means and with proper care and caution.

Section Text: Whoever causes the death of any person by doing any rash or negligent act not amounting to culpable homicide shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to five years, or with fine, or with both.

Rule.Section 302: Governs murder, which requires the specific intent to kill or knowledge that death is the almost certain result.
Rule.Section 304: Governs culpable homicide not amounting to murder, requiring an intent to cause injury likely to cause death.
Rule.Section 304A: Governs deaths caused by rash or negligent acts that do not meet the high intent threshold of homicide.
Rule.Section 323: Governs the voluntary causing of minor physical hurt where no death occurs.

Application.Section 80: Fails because pushing someone in an argument is an intentional, unlawful use of force, not a lawful act done with care.
Application.Section 302: Fails because the man lacked the murderous intent or knowledge that a simple push would certainly result in death.
Application.Section 304: Fails because a simple push does not typically carry the intent to cause an injury that is 'likely' to result in death.
Application.Section 304A: Governs directly because the man's act was rash and caused a death, but lacked the specific intent required for murder or homicide.
Application.Section 323: Fails because a death actually occurred, making the charge of simple hurt insufficient for the legal outcome.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 304A, the man is liable for causing death by a rash or negligent act because he lacked the specific intent required for murder.

Question: Scenario-based: Salman is facing a theft trial. He wants to show he was at a bank at the time, but the judge says the bank record he brought doesn't meet civil court rules for being used as evidence. Can he still use it here?

Possible Sections.Full Text: In this Act the following words and expressions are used in the following senses, unless a contrary intention appears from the context:- "Court" includes all Judges and Magistrates and all persons, except arbitrators, legally authorized to take evidence. "Fact" means and includes-
Evidence may be given in any suit or proceeding of the existence or non-existence of every fact in issue and of such other fact as are hereinafter declared to be relevant, and of no others.

This act may be called the Evidence Act, 1872. It extends to the whole of Bangladesh and applies to all judicial proceedings in or before any Court, including Courts-martial, other than Courts-martial convened under the Army Act, 1952, the Naval Discipline Ordinance, 1961 or the Air Force Act, 1953 but not to affidavits presented to any Court or officer, nor to proceedings before an arbitrator; And it shall come into force on the first day of September, 1872.

**"May presume"** Whenever it is provided by this Act that the Court may presume a fact, it may either regard such fact as proved, unless and until it is disproved, or may call for proof of it: **“Shall presume”** Whenever it is directed by this Act that the Court shall presume a fact, it shall regard such fact as proved, unless and until it is disproved: **“Conclusive proof”** When one fact is declared by this Act to be conclusive proof of another, the Court shall, on proof of the one fact, regard the other as proved, and shall not allow evidence to be given for the purpose of disproving it.
Facts not otherwise relevant are relevant–

Section Text: Evidence may be given in any suit or proceeding of the existence or non-existence of every fact in issue and of such other fact as are hereinafter declared to be relevant, and of no others. Explanation.—This section shall not enable any person to give evidence of a fact which he is disentitled to prove by any provision of the law for the time being in force relating to Civil Procedure.

Application.Section 11: Fails since the problem is a procedural bar to evidence rather than a question of relevancy logic.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 5, you cannot use this Act to bypass Civil Procedure rules if you are legally barred from proving that specific fact by those rules.
