Rule.Section 6: Makes facts that form part of the same transaction as a fact in issue relevant.

Application.Section 7: Fails because the bank slip represents the impossibility of the crime rather than an opportunity for it.
Application.Section 9: Fails since the purpose of the slip is to logically disprove the prosecution's claim rather than merely setting the scene.
Application.Section 11: Governs directly because being in a different city five minutes prior makes the commission of the crime in Dhaka 'highly improbable.'
Application.Section 8: Fails since visiting a bank in a different city is not a preparatory act for committing a crime later.
Application.Section 6: Fails because the bank visit occurred in a distant city and is not part of the immediate transaction of the crime.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 11, Subsection (2), the bank slip is relevant because it makes the fact that you committed the crime highly improbable due to the distance.

Question: Scenario-based: Salim is fixing a leak on his roof and accidentally knocks a heavy tile off the edge. He didn't look down first. The tile hits Jamil below, who dies instantly. Salim had no intent to kill. What is his likely charge?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Whoever commits murder shall be punished with death, or imprisonment for life, and shall also be liable to fine.

Whoever commits culpable homicide not amounting to murder, shall be punished with imprisonment for life, or imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine, if the act by which the death is caused is done with intention of causing death, or of causing such bodily injury as is likely to cause death; or with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to ten years, or with fine, or with both, if the act is done with the knowledge that it is likely to cause death, but without any intention to cause death or to cause such bodily injury as is likely to cause death.

Whoever causes the death of any person by doing any rash or negligent act not amounting to culpable homicide shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to five years, or with fine, or with both.

Nothing is an offence which is done by accident or misfortune, and without any criminal intention or knowledge in the doing of a lawful act in a lawful manner by lawful means and with proper care and caution.

Whoever does any act so rashly or negligently as to endanger human life or the personal safety of others, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three months, or with fine which may extend to two hundred and fifty taka, or with both.

Section Text: Whoever causes the death of any person by doing any rash or negligent act not amounting to culpable homicide shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to five years, or with fine, or with both.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Which section governs an accidental death caused by a reckless failure to use proper care?

Rule.Section 302: Governs murder, requiring specific intent to kill or knowledge of near-certain death.
Rule.Section 304: Governs culpable homicide, which requires an intent to cause injury likely to result in death.
Rule.Section 304A: Governs deaths resulting from rash or negligent behavior that does not meet the intent threshold for homicide.
Rule.Section 80: Exempts deaths from being offenses if they occur by pure accident during a 'lawful act' done in a 'lawful manner' with 'due care'.
Rule.Section 336: Governs general rash acts endangering safety, but is superseded when an actual death occurs.

Application.Section 302: Fails because Salim had no intention to kill the person below; it was a physical accident from the roof.
Application.Section 304: Fails because knocking a tile off while prying is a reckless act rather than a deliberate attack on Jamil's body.
Application.Section 304A: Governs directly because failing to look before dropping heavy items is a classic 'negligent act' that resulted in death.
Application.Section 80: Fails because Salim did not use 'due care' (he didn't look down), which is a mandatory requirement for the accident exception.
Application.Section 336: Fails because the act resulted in a death, triggering the more specific and severe Section 304A.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 304A, Salim is guilty because his failure to look down before dropping the tile was a negligent act that caused Jamil's death.

Question: Scenario-based: I found out that a person is being held against his will in an illegal clinic in Dhaka. Which high court division has the power to order his immediate release?

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 100
Possible Sections.Full Text: If any [Metropolitan Magistrate], Magistrate of the first class or [or an Executive Magistrate] has reason to believe that any person is confined under such circumstances that the confinement amounts to an offence, he may issue a search- warrant, and the person to whom such warrant is directed may search for the person so confined; and such search shall be made in accordance therewith, and the person, if found, shall be immediately taken before a Magistrate, who shall make such order as in the circumstances of the case seems proper.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 491
Possible Sections.Full Text: Being bound to attend on or supply the wants of a person who is helpless from youth, unsoundness of mind or disease, and voluntarily omitting to do so.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 61
Possible Sections.Full Text: No police-officer shall detain in custody a person arrested without warrant for a longer period than under all the circumstances of the case is reasonable, and such period shall not, in the absence of a special order of a Magistrate under section 167, exceed twenty-four hours exclusive of the time necessary for the journey from the place of arrest to the Magistrate's Court.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 496
Possible Sections.Full Text: A person with fraudulent intention going through the ceremony of being married, knowing that he is not thereby lawfully married.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 63
Possible Sections.Full Text: No person who has been arrested by a police-officer shall be discharged except on his own bond, or on bail, or under the special order of a Magistrate.

Relevant Section: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 491
Section Text: (1) The High Court Division may, whenever it thinks fit, direct- (b) that a person illegally or improperly detained in public or private custody within such limits be set at liberty;

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Does the High Court possess the power to liberate someone from illegal private detention?

Rule.Section 100: Grants lower magistrates the power to sign a search warrant to find a person being held against their will.
Rule.Section 491: Gives the High Court Division the superior power to order that anyone illegally detained in any custody be set at liberty.
Rule.Section 61: Regulates the short-term 24-hour limit for police detention after a warrantless arrest.
Rule.Section 496: Establishes a mandatory right to bail for suspects involved in minor, non-serious crimes.
Rule.Section 63: Sets out the formal documents required to release someone from police custody.

Application.Section 100: Fails because it is a lower court power to 'search' rather than the High Court's superior power to 'liberate' immediately.
Application.Section 491: Governs directly as it is the primary provision for Habeas Corpus, allowing the High Court to set any person free from illegal private clinics or homes.
Application.Section 61: Fails because it relates to police procedural limits rather than a civilian victim's rescue from a private medical facility.
Application.Section 496: Fails because the victim is not a suspect being held for a crime, but an innocent person being held against their will.
Application.Section 63: Fails because it defines the 'method' of release for criminals rather than the 'legal reason' for rescuing a kidnapping victim.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, section 491, Subsection (1), Clause (b), the High Court Division can order that the person be set free from illegal detention immediately.

Cited Acts and Sections: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 491(1)(b)
source_file: Q-As copy/English/Code of Criminal Procedure/Advanced selection

Question: Scenario-based: Salim cuts down a tree on Jamil's land with the intent to steal it. As soon as the tree falls, he hears a police whistle and runs away without touching the tree again. Has he committed theft?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Whoever, intending to take dishonestly any moveable property out of the possession of any person without that person's consent, moves that property in order to such taking, is said to commit theft.
Whoever commits theft shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, or with fine, or with both.

Whoever dishonestly misappropriates or converts to his own use any moveable property, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both.

Whoever, with intent to cause, or knowing that he is likely to cause, wrongful loss or damage to the public or to any person, causes the destruction of any property, or any such change in any property or in the situation thereof as destroys or diminishes its value or utility, or affects it injuriously, commits "mischief".

Whoever attempts to commit an offence punishable by this Code with imprisonment for life or imprisonment, or to cause such an offence to be committed, and in such attempt does any act towards the commission of the offence, shall, where no express provision is made by this Code for the punishment of such attempt, be punished with imprisonment of any description provided for the offence, for a term which may extend to one-half of the longest term of imprisonment provided for that offence, or with such fine as is provided for the offence, or with both.

Section Text: Whoever, intending to take dishonestly any moveable property out of the possession of any person without that person's consent, moves that property in order to such taking, is said to commit theft.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Does the act of severing property from the ground to steal it complete the offense of theft even if the item is not carried away?

Rule.Section 378: Defines theft as moving property with dishonest intent, and clarifies that the act of severing something from the earth counts as moving it.
Rule.Section 379: Prescribes the three-year prison sentence for individuals convicted of theft.
Rule.Section 403: Governs the misappropriation of property that is already in the offender's possession or found by them.
Rule.Section 425: Governs mischief, which involves destroying or damaging property rather than taking it for gain.
Rule.Section 511: Governs general attempts where the crime is not completed.

Application.Section 378: Governs directly because Explanation 2 specifies that the same act that cuts the tree (severance) also counts as moving it, completing the theft.
Application.Section 379: Fails because it provides the penalty for the crime rather than defining the specific threshold for when the theft is legally 'complete'.
Application.Section 403: Fails because theft involves taking from someone's possession, while misappropriation involves goods not currently possessed by the owner.
Application.Section 425: Fails because Salim's primary intent was to acquire the tree (theft) rather than to simply destroy Jamil's garden (mischief).
Application.Section 511: Fails because the law considers the act of cutting the tree down to be the completion of the theft rather than just an attempt.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 378, Explanation 2, Salim has committed theft because the act of cutting down the tree also counts as 'moving' it with the intent to steal.

Question: Scenario-based: Karim is accused of a crime that happened at 2:00 PM today. He has a video of himself eating lunch in a different city at 1:55 PM. Which rule makes this video relevant to his trial?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Facts not otherwise relevant are relevant–

Facts which are the occasions, cause or effect, immediate or otherwise, of relevant facts, or facts in issue, or which constitute the state of things under which they happened, or which afforded an opportunity for their occurrence or transaction, are relevant.

Facts necessary to explain or introduce a fact in issue or relevant fact, or which support or rebut an inference suggested by a fact in issue or relevant fact, or which establish the identity of anything or person whose identity is relevant, or fix the time or place at which any fact in issue or relevant fact happened or which show the relation of parties by whom any such fact was transacted, are relevant in so far as they are necessary for that purpose.

Any fact is relevant which shows or constitutes a motive or preparation for any fact in issue or relevant fact. The conduct of any party, or of any agent to any party, to any suit or proceeding, in reference to such suit or proceeding, or in reference to any fact in issue therein or relevant thereto, and the conduct of any person an offence against whom is the subject of any proceeding, is relevant, if such conduct influences or is influenced by any fact, in issue or relevant fact, and whether it was previous subsequent thereto.

Facts which, though not in issue, are so connected with a fact in issue as to form part of the same transaction, are relevant, whether they occurred at the same time and place or at different times and places.

Section Text: Facts not otherwise relevant are relevant– (1) If they are inconsistent with any fact in issue or relevant fact; (2) If by themselves or in connection with other facts they make the existence or non-existence of any fact in issue or relevant fact highly probable or improbable.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is a fact making a crime's commission highly improbable admissible in court?

Rule.Section 11: Determines that facts which make the existence of a fact in issue highly improbable are relevant.
Rule.Section 7: Dictates that facts providing the occasion, cause, or opportunity for a crime are relevant.
Rule.Section 9: Governs the relevancy of facts that explain context or establish the time and place of events.
Rule.Section 8: Specifies that facts showing motive, preparation, or conduct are relevant.
Rule.Section 6: Relates to facts forming part of the same transaction as the crime (Res Gestae).

Application.Section 11: Governs directly because being in a different city five minutes prior makes the commission of the crime 'highly improbable' due to travel time.
Application.Section 7: Fails as the lunch video does not provide the 'occasion' or 'opportunity' for the crime, but rather suggests its impossibility.
Application.Section 9: Fails since the goal is to disprove guilt through probability rather than to fix the 'time and place' of the actual crime scene.
Application.Section 8: Fails because eating lunch in another city is not a preparatory act for committing a crime later that day.
Application.Section 6: Fails since the lunch is a separate event occurring in a different city and is not part of the 'same transaction' as the crime.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 11, the video is relevant because it makes the fact that you committed the crime highly improbable due to the distance involved.

Question: Scenario-based: A judge is facing a very strange problem in court that is not described in any part of the rules. Can the judge make a special order to solve this problem fairly?

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 151
Possible Sections.Full Text: Nothing in this Code shall be deemed to limit or otherwise affect the inherent power of the Court to make such orders as may be necessary for the ends of justice or to prevent abuse of the process of the Court.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 152
Possible Sections.Full Text: Clerical or arithmetical mistakes in judgments, decrees or orders or errors arising therein from any accidental slip or omission may at any time be corrected by the Court either of its own motion or on the application of any of the parties.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 153
Possible Sections.Full Text: The Court may at any time, and on such terms as to costs or otherwise as it thinks fit, amend any defect or error in any proceeding in a suit; and all necessary amendments shall be made for the purpose of determining the real question or issue raised by or depending on such proceeding.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 114
Possible Sections.Full Text: Subject as aforesaid, any person considering himself aggrieved- (a) by a decree or order from which an appeal is allowed by this Code, but from which no appeal has been preferred, (b) by a decree or order from which no appeal is allowed by this Code, or (c) by a decision on a reference from a Court of Small Causes may apply for a view of judgment to the Court which passed the decree or made the order, and the Court may make such order thereon as it thinks fit.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 148
Possible Sections.Full Text: Where any period is fixed or granted by the Court for the doing of any act prescribed or allowed by this Code, the Court may, in its discretion, from time to time, enlarge such period, even though the period originally fixed or granted may have expired.

Relevant Section: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 151
Section Text: Nothing in this Code shall be deemed to limit or otherwise affect the inherent power of the Court to make such orders as may be necessary for the ends of justice or to prevent abuse of the process of the Court.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Does the court possess generalized powers to act for justice when specific rules are silent?

Rule.Section 151: Preserves the 'inherent power' of the court to issue orders needed for justice or to stop system abuse.
Rule.Section 152: Restricts the court to correcting minor typos or math errors in existing written judgments.
Rule.Section 153: Grants power to fix technical defects or formal errors in the court's paperwork.
Rule.Section 114: Provides a process for a party to ask the judge to re-decide a case due to major errors.
Rule.Section 148: Enables the court to extend deadlines and time periods it has previously set for parties.

Application.Section 151: Governs directly because it is the only provision that allows acting on 'ends of justice' when no specific rule exists.
Application.Section 152: Fails because the scenario involves a substantive problem, not a simple typo in a document.
Application.Section 153: Is inapplicable as it concerns amending defects in existing papers rather than creating a new solution for a missing rule.
Application.Section 114: Does not apply because the judge is trying to decide an active problem rather than re-deciding a finalized case.
Application.Section 148: Fails because the scenario is about a unique legal problem rather than a request for more time.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, section 151, the judge can make such an order — this section confirms the court's inherent power to do what is necessary for the ends of justice when rules are missing.

Cited Acts and Sections: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 151
source_file: Q-As copy/English/Code of Civil Procedure/Advanced selection

Question: Scenario-based: Someone's valuable gold ring was stolen four years ago. They only found out today that the ring is currently sitting in a display case at a local antique shop. When does their time to sue start?

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I Article 49
Possible Sections.Full Text: For other specific moveable property, or for compensation for wrongfully taking or injuring or wrongfully detaining the same.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I Article 48
Possible Sections.Full Text:
For specific moveable property lost or acquired by theft, or dishonest misappropriation or conversion, or for compensation for wrongfully taking or detaining the same.
1 [48A. To recover moveable property conveyed or bequeathed in trust, deposited or pawned, and afterwards bought from the trustee, depositary or pawnee for a valuable consideration.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I Article 36
Possible Sections.Full Text:
For compensation for any malfeasance, misfeasance or non-feasance independent of contract and not herein specially provided for.
*Part VI.—Three years.*

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I Article 120
Possible Sections.Full Text: Suit for which no period of limitation is provided elsewhere in this schedule. Six years.. When the right to sue accrues.

Where any person having a right to institute a suit or make an application has, by means of fraud, been kept from the knowledge of such right or of the title on which it is founded, or where any document necessary to establish such right has been fraudulently concealed from him, the time limited for instituting a suit or making an application-

Relevant Section: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I Article 48
Section Text: For specific moveable property lost or acquired by theft, or dishonest misappropriation or conversion, or for compensation for wrongfully taking or detaining the same. Three years. When the person having the right to the possession of the property first learns in whose possession it is.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: When does the limitation period begin for a suit to recover stolen moveable property?

Rule.Schedule I Article 49: Provides a three-year limit for general property issues, starting the clock on the day the item was first taken.
Rule.Schedule I Article 48: Mandates a three-year period for stolen or lost property, starting only once the owner discovers who actually has the item.
Rule.Schedule I Article 36: Sets a general two-year deadline for civil wrongs that do not involve contracts and are not listed elsewhere.
Rule.Schedule I Article 120: A six-year catch-all deadline for any lawsuits that are not specifically named in other articles.
Rule.Section 18: Pauses the clock if the defendant used active fraud or intentional lies to hide the plaintiff's right to sue.

Application.Schedule I Article 49: Fails because it starts the clock at the time of the 'taking', which would already have barred the owner's suit since the theft was four years ago.
Application.Schedule I Article 48: Governs directly because the property was 'acquired by theft' and the clock starts today, when the owner 'first learns' who has the ring.
Application.Schedule I Article 36: Fails as Article 48 provides a much more specific rule and a longer (three-year) period for this exact type of stolen property case.
Application.Schedule I Article 120: Fails as stolen property recovery is already specifically covered by Article 48, making the catch-all category unnecessary.
Application.Section 18: Fails because the owner's right is automatically protected by Article 48's 'discovery' rule, regardless of whether the shopkeeper used active fraud.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I, Article 48, the owner's three-year deadline to sue starts today, which is when they first learned who is in possession of the stolen ring.

Cited Acts and Sections: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I, Article 48

Question: Scenario-based: In a complex land dispute, Karim claims his family has always held the right to fish in a pond. To prove this, he offers evidence of several times in the past when his father's right to fish was stopped by neighbors. Is this allowed?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Facts not otherwise relevant are relevant– (1) If they are inconsistent with any fact in issue or relevant fact; (2) If by themselves or in connection with other facts they make the existence or non-existence of any fact in issue or relevant fact highly probable or improbable.

When the statement gives the opinion of any such person as to the existence of any public right or custom or matter of public or general interest, of the existence of which, if it existed, he would have been likely to be aware, and when such statement was made before any controversy as to such right, custom or matter has arisen.

An entry in any public or other official book, register or record [,or digital record], stating a fact in issue or relevant fact, and made by a public servant in the discharge of his official duty, or by any other person in performance of a duty specially enjoined by the law of the country in which such book, register [, record or digital record] is kept, is itself a relevant fact.
Where the question is as to the existence of any right of custom, the following facts are relevant:–

Judgments, orders or decrees, other than those mentioned in section 41, are relevant if they relate to matters of a public nature relevant to the enquiry; but such judgments, orders or decrees are not conclusive proof of what they state.

Section Text: Where the question is as to the existence of any right of custom, the following facts are relevant:– (a) any transaction by which the right or custom in question was created, claimed, modified, recognized, asserted or denied, or which was inconsistent with its existence; (b) particular instances in which the right or custom was claimed, recognized or exercised, or in which its exercise was disputed, asserted or departed from.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Are specific instances where a right was disputed relevant to prove that right's existence?

Rule.Section 11: Governs facts that are inconsistent with a claim or make its existence highly probable.
Rule.Section 32(4): Governs out-of-court opinions from deceased persons regarding public rights or customs.
Rule.Section 35: Governs the relevancy of entries made in official public records by public servants.
Rule.Section 13: Governs the relevancy of transactions or particular instances where a right or custom was claimed or disputed.
Rule.Section 42: Governs the relevancy of judgments relating to matters of a public nature.

Application.Section 11: Fails because it deals with logical probability rather than the specific history of a right's exercise.
Application.Section 32(4): Fails since the scenario involves historical instances of dispute rather than a deceased person's out-of-court opinion.
Application.Section 35: Fails as the disputes with neighbors are private incidents, not entries in a government registry.
Application.Section 13: Governs directly because the neighbors stopping the father are particular instances where the exercise of the fishing right was disputed.
Application.Section 42: Fails because the scenario describes physical incidents of dispute rather than a prior court judgment.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 13, Clause (b), the instances where the fishing right was stopped are relevant as particular instances in which the exercise of the right was disputed.

Question: Scenario-based: I need to prove that a ship departed from the Chittagong harbor on a specific Tuesday ten years ago. Can I use a business letter written by a now-deceased merchant that explicitly mentions the ship's departure date?

Possible Sections.Full Text: When there is a question whether a particular act was done, the existence of any course of business, according to which it naturally would have been done, is a relevant fact.

Statements, written or verbal, of relevant facts made by a person who is dead, or who cannot be found, or who has become incapable of giving evidence, or whose attendance cannot be procured without an amount of delay or expense which under the circumstances of the case appears to the Court unreasonable, are themselves relevant facts in the following cases:–

Entries in books of account [or digital record], regularly kept in the course of business, are relevant whenever they refer to a matter into which the Court has to inquire, but such statements shall not alone be sufficient evidence to charge any person with liability.

An entry in any public or other official book, register or record [,or digital record], stating a fact in issue or relevant fact, and made by a public servant in the discharge of his official duty, or by any other person in performance of a duty specially enjoined by the law of the country in which such book, register [, record or digital record] is kept, is itself a relevant fact.

An admission is a statement, oral or documentary [or contained in digital record], which suggests any inference as to any fact in issue or relevant fact, and which is made by any of the persons, and under the circumstances, hereinafter mentioned.

Section Text: Statements, written or verbal, of relevant facts made by a person who is dead, or who cannot be found, or who has become incapable of giving evidence, or whose attendance cannot be procured without an amount of delay or expense which under the circumstances of the case appears to the Court unreasonable, are themselves relevant facts in the following cases:–

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is a deceased person's dated business letter admissible to prove the timing of a commercial event?

Rule.Section 16: Governs the relevancy of a business process to prove an act happened.
Rule.Section 32: Governs out-of-court statements from deceased persons made in the course of business.
Rule.Section 34: Governs the relevancy of accounting entries in business books to establish liability.
Rule.Section 35: Governs entries in public records made by public servants.
Rule.Section 17: Defines what constitutes an admission in a legal trial.

Application.Section 16: Fails because it uses the existence of a routine to infer an act, rather than admitting a specific written statement as evidence.
Application.Section 32: Governs directly because the merchant is deceased and the letter is a dated business record, which is a specific hearsay exception.
Application.Section 34: Fails since the letter is a commercial correspondence rather than an account book entry used to charge someone with debt.
Application.Section 35: Fails as the merchant is a private businessman, not a public servant recording facts in an official government book.
Application.Section 17: Fails because while the letter is an admission, Section 32 provides the necessary hearsay exception for the deceased author.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 32, Illustration (d), the letter is a relevant fact because it was a dated business document written by a person who is now deceased.

Question: Scenario-based: A person discovered today that a manager of a local charity sold a piece of the charity's land ten years ago without permission. How long do they have to sue to cancel that sale?

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I Article 134
Possible Sections.Full Text:
To recover possession of immoveable property conveyed or bequeathed in trust or mortgaged and afterwards transferred by the trustee or mortgagee for a valuable consideration.
1 [134A. To set aside a transfer of immoveable property comprised in a Hindu, Muslim or Buddhist religious or charitable endowment, made by a manager thereof for a valuable consideration.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I Article 134A
Possible Sections.Full Text: To set aside a transfer of immoveable property comprised in a Hindu, Muslim or Buddhist religious or charitable endowment, made by a manager thereof for a valuable consideration. Twelve years.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I Article 12
Possible Sections.Full Text: To set aside any of the following sales:— (a) sale in execution of a decree of a Civil Court; (b) sale in pursuance of a decree or order of a Collector or other officer of revenue; (c) sale for arrears of Government revenue, or for any demand recoverable as such arrears;

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I Article 91
Possible Sections.Full Text: To cancel or set aside an instrument not otherwise provided for.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I Article 48B
Possible Sections.Full Text: To set aside sale of moveable property comprised in a Hindu, Muslim or Buddhist religious or charitable endowment, made by a manager thereof for a valuable consideration.

Relevant Section: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I Article 134A
Section Text: To set aside a transfer of immoveable property comprised in a Hindu, Muslim or Buddhist religious or charitable endowment, made by a manager thereof for a valuable consideration. Twelve years. When the transfer becomes known to the plaintiff.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: What is the limitation period for challenging an unauthorized land transfer made by a charity manager?

Rule.Schedule I Article 134: Governs property recovery where a generic private trustee (non-religious) sold land that was meant to be held in trust.
Rule.Schedule I Article 134A: Provides a twelve-year limit for challenging land transfers involving religious or charitable 'endowments' once the sale is discovered.
Rule.Schedule I Article 12: Applies to sales forced by a court or government order, giving only one year to challenge the official confirmation.
Rule.Schedule I Article 91: Sets a general three-year limit for cancelling any signed document that does not fit into a more specific profession or topic.
Rule.Schedule I Article 48B: Gives a shorter three-year period to challenge the sale of 'moveable' items like furniture belonging to a religious charity.

Application.Schedule I Article 134: Fails because Article 134A is the more specific rule for 'religious or charitable endowments', which governs this charity land scenario exactly.
Application.Schedule I Article 134A: Governs directly because the property is land (immoveable) and belonged to a 'charitable endowment' sold by its manager.
Application.Schedule I Article 12: Fails because the manager sold the land privately rather than as part of a court-ordered auction or government execution.
Application.Schedule I Article 91: Fails as the law provides a much longer twelve-year specific window for religious/charitable land issues, overriding the general three-year document rule.
Application.Schedule I Article 48B: Fails because the scenario involves 'land', which is immoveable, while 48B is strictly limited to moveable property like jewels or equipment.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I, Article 134A, the person has twelve years to sue, starting from today when they first learned about the manager's sale.

Cited Acts and Sections: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I, Article 134A

Question: Scenario-based: I am suing a debt collection agency for failing to recover a specific loan. The actual borrower recently sent a letter to the agency admitting that the full debt was still outstanding. Can I use this borrower's statement as evidence in my suit against the agency?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Statements made by a party to the proceeding, or by an agent to any such party, whom the Court regards, under the circumstances of the case, as expressly or impliedly authorized by him to make them, are admissions.

Statements made by persons whose position or liability it is necessary to prove as against any party to the suit, are admissions, if such statements would be relevant as against such persons in relation to such position or liability in a suit brought by or against them, and if they are made whilst the person making them occupies such position or is subject to such liability.
Statements made by persons to whom a party to the suit has expressly referred for information in reference to a matter in dispute are admissions.

Statements, written or verbal, of relevant facts made by a person who is dead, or who cannot be found, or who has become incapable of giving evidence, or whose attendance cannot be procured without an amount of delay or expense which under the circumstances of the case appears to the Court unreasonable, are themselves relevant facts in the following cases:–

Entries in books of account [or digital record], regularly kept in the course of business, are relevant whenever they refer to a matter into which the Court has to inquire, but such statements shall not alone be sufficient evidence to charge any person with liability.

Section Text: Statements made by persons whose position or liability it is necessary to prove as against any party to the suit, are admissions, if such statements would be relevant as against such persons in relation to such position or liability in a suit brought by or against them, and if they are made whilst the person making them occupies such position or is subject to such liability.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is a third-party admission admissible when proving their liability is necessary for the main suit?

Rule.Section 18: Defines statements by parties or their authorized agents as admissions.
Rule.Section 19: Governs admissions by third parties whose liability must be established against a party to the suit.
Rule.Section 20: Relates to admissions made by persons to whom a party expressly directed an inquiry.
Rule.Section 32: Outlines hearsay exceptions for statements made by persons who are deceased or unavailable.
Rule.Section 34: States that business account entries are relevant but insufficient as sole proof of liability.

Application.Section 18: Fails because the borrower is an independent debtor rather than an authorized agent of the collection agency.
Application.Section 19: Governs directly because proving the borrower's liability is a prerequisite to finding the agency negligent for failing to collect.
Application.Section 20: Fails since the agency did not specifically refer the plaintiff to the borrower for information on the debt.
Application.Section 32: Fails as there is no evidence that the borrower is dead, missing, or otherwise legally unavailable to testify.
Application.Section 34: Fails because the evidence is a specific personal letter of admission rather than a formal commercial ledger entry.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 19, the borrower's letter is an admission because you must prove their liability for the debt to win your case against the collection agency.

Question: Scenario-based: During a trial, a witness described seeing Salman standing next to a broken fence with a hammer in his hand. Is the physical presence of Salman and the hammer considered a 'fact' in a legal sense?

Possible Sections.Full Text: In this Act the following words and expressions are used in the following senses, unless a contrary intention appears from the context:- "Court" includes all Judges and Magistrates and all persons, except arbitrators, legally authorized to take evidence. "Fact" means and includes-

Facts showing the existence of any state of mind, such as intention, knowledge, good faith, negligence, rashness, ill-will, or good-will towards any particular person, or showing the existence of any state of body or bodily feeling, are relevant, when the existence of any such state of mind or body or bodily feeling is in issue or relevant.

Facts which are the occasions, cause or effect, immediate or otherwise, of relevant facts, or facts in issue, or which constitute the state of things under which they happened, or which afforded an opportunity for their occurrence or transaction, are relevant.
Any fact is relevant which shows or constitutes a motive or preparation for any fact in issue or relevant fact.

Facts necessary to explain or introduce a fact in issue or relevant fact, or which support or rebut an inference suggested by a fact in issue or relevant fact, or which establish the identity of anything or person whose identity is relevant, or fix the time or place at which any fact in issue or relevant fact happened or which show the relation of parties by whom any such fact was transacted, are relevant in so far as they are necessary for that purpose.

Section Text: In this Act the following words and expressions are used in the following senses, unless a contrary intention appears from the context:- "Court" includes all Judges and Magistrates and all persons, except arbitrators, legally authorized to take evidence. "Fact" means and includes-

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Does a physically observable state of things fall within the legal definition of a 'fact'?

Rule.Section 3: Defines a 'Fact' as anything, including physical states or objects, that can be perceived by human senses.
Rule.Section 14: Governs the relevancy of facts demonstrating an internal state of mind or body during an event.
Rule.Section 7: Establishes the relevancy of facts that serve as the cause, effect, or opportunity of an issue.
Rule.Section 8: Relates to facts showing motive, preparation, or subsequent conduct influenced by an event.
Rule.Section 9: Allows facts necessary to introduce or explain events or establish the identity of people.

Application.Section 3: Governs directly because a person standing with a hammer is a physical state of things 'capable of being perceived by the senses' (sight).
Application.Section 14: Fails as it addresses the 'relevancy' of mental or bodily states rather than the foundational definition of what constitutes a 'fact.'
Application.Section 7: Fails since the scene described is the actual event itself rather than a resultant 'effect' or an external 'occasion' for it.
Application.Section 8: Fails because the witness is reporting a current observation rather than a prior preparation or a separate motive.
Application.Section 9: Fails since the question asks for a definition of the scene rather than a rule for using it to explain other facts.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 3, Subsection (1), Salman's presence and the hammer are 'facts.' The law defines any physical state or objects that can be seen or heard as a fact.

Question: Scenario-based: Salim borrows his neighbor's lawnmower for an hour. He instead keeps it for two weeks to mow lawns for money, intending to return it eventually. Is this considered a crime even if the intent was only temporary?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Whoever, intending to take dishonestly any moveable property out of the possession of any person without that person's consent, moves that property in order to such taking, is said to commit theft.

Whoever dishonestly misappropriates or converts to his own use any moveable property, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both.

Whoever, being in any manner entrusted with property, or with any dominion over property, dishonestly misappropriates or converts to his own use that property, or dishonestly uses or disposes of that property in violation of any direction of law prescribing the mode in which such trust is to be discharged, or of any legal contract, express or implied, which he has made touching the discharge of such trust, or wilfully suffers any other person so to do, commits "criminal breach of trust".

Whoever, by deceiving any person, fraudulently or dishonestly induces the person so deceived to deliver any property to any person, or to consent that any person shall retain any property, or intentionally induces the person so deceived to do or omit to do anything which he would not do or omit if he were not so deceived, and which act or omission causes or is likely to cause damage or harm to that person in body, mind, reputation or property, is said to "cheat".
Whoever does anything with the intention of causing wrongful gain to one person or wrongful loss to another person, is said to do that thing "dishonestly".

Section Text: Whoever dishonestly misappropriates or converts to his own use any moveable property, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is temporary dishonest use of another person's property legally classified as misappropriation?

Rule.Section 378: Governs theft, which usually implies an intent to permanently deprive the owner at the time of taking.
Rule.Section 403: Governs dishonest misappropriation, explicitly clarifying that temporary misappropriation is still a crime.
Rule.Section 405: Governs criminal breach of trust, which requires property to have been formally 'entrusted' for a specific duty.
Rule.Section 415: Governs cheating, which involves using a lie to get someone to hand over property.
Rule.Section 24: Provides the general definition of acting 'dishonestly' to gain property or cause loss.

Application.Section 378: Fails because Salim had consent to borrow it; theft requires taking it 'out of possession' without consent.
Application.Section 403: Governs directly because Explanation 1 specifies that misappropriation 'for a time only' (two weeks) still counts as the crime.
Application.Section 405: Fails because while it was a neighborly loan, Section 403 is the more standard fit for simple misuse of borrowed personal items.
Application.Section 415: Fails because Salim borrowed it honestly and only later decided to use it dishonestly for his own business.
Application.Section 24: Fails because it is a definition section rather than a substantive provision that establishes the crime of misappropriation.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 403, Explanation 1, Salim is guilty because the law states that dishonest misappropriation, even if only for a limited time, is still a crime.

Question: Scenario-based: I was arrested for a minor crime where getting bail is normally a right. The police officer says I can only leave if I provide two rich relatives as guarantors. Is he allowed to set these high conditions?

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 496
Possible Sections.Full Text: A person with fraudulent intention going through the ceremony of being married, knowing that he is not thereby lawfully married.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 498
Possible Sections.Full Text: Enticing or taking away or detaining with a criminal intent a married woman.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 497
Possible Sections.Full Text: Adultery

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 63
Possible Sections.Full Text: No person who has been arrested by a police-officer shall be discharged except on his own bond, or on bail, or under the special order of a Magistrate.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 76
Possible Sections.Full Text: Any Court issuing a warrant for the arrest of any person may in its discretion direct by endorsement on the warrant that, if such person executes a bond with sufficient sureties for his attendance before the Court at a specified time and thereafter until otherwise directed by the Court, the officer to whom the warrant is directed shall take such security and shall release such person from custody. The endorsement shall state- the number of sureties; the amount in which they and the person for whose arrest the warrant is issued, are to be respectively bound; and the time at which he is to attend before the Court.

Relevant Section: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 498
Section Text: The amount of every bond executed under this Chapter shall be fixed with due regard to the circumstances of the case, and shall not be excessive; and the High Court Division or Court of Session may, in any case, whether there be an appeal on conviction or not, direct that any person be admitted to bail, or that the bail required by a police-officer or Magistrate be reduced.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is there a legal restriction against setting excessive bail amounts or unreasonable surety requirements?

Rule.Section 496: Confers an absolute right to bail for individuals accused of offenses that are not legally classified as non-bailable.
Rule.Section 498: Mandates that bail amounts must be reasonable for the case and empowers superior courts to reduce excessive bail set by police.
Rule.Section 497: Describes the circumstances under which a judge may choose to release someone accused of a serious, non-bailable crime.
Rule.Section 63: Establishes that police cannot unilaterally let someone go without a formal bond or judicial intervention.
Rule.Section 76: Allows a court to permit police to release a person immediately upon catching them if they provide the bond noted on the warrant.

Application.Section 496: Fails because it establishes the 'right' to get bail rather than the 'rules' for determining the amount or reducing it.
Application.Section 498: Governs directly because it contains the explicit command that bail 'shall not be excessive' and provides the remedy for reduction if police set it too high.
Application.Section 497: Fails because it concerns the 'decision' to grant bail in serious cases rather than the 'amount' of bail in minor ones.
Application.Section 63: Fails because it identifies the valid types of release documents rather than the fairness of the conditions contained within them.
Application.Section 76: Fails because it relates to warrants with pre-authorized bail instructions rather than the general fairness of bail conditions at a station.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, section 498, the officer is not allowed to set unreasonable conditions; the law states that the amount of every bond must not be excessive and must be fair for the case.

Cited Acts and Sections: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 498

Question: Scenario-based: A smith is kidnapped by a gang of robbers. They point a gun at his head and threaten to kill him instantly unless he opens a safe for them. He opens it. Can he be punished?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Nothing is an offence merely by reason of its being done with the knowledge that it is likely to cause harm, if it be done without any criminal intention to cause harm, and in good faith for the purpose of preventing or avoiding other harm to person or property.
Nothing is an offence which is done in the exercise of the right of private defence.

Except murder, and offences against the State punishable with death, nothing is an offence which is done by a person who is compelled to do it by threats, which, at the time of doing it, reasonably cause the apprehension that instant death to that person will otherwise be the consequence: Provided the person doing the act did not of his own accord, or from a reasonable apprehension of harm to himself short of instant death, place himself in the situation by which he became subject to such constraint.
Nothing is an offence which is done by a person who is, or who by reason of a mistake of fact and not by reason of a mistake of law in good faith believes himself to be, bound by law to do it.

Nothing is an offence by reason that it causes, or that it is intended to cause, or that it is known to be likely to cause, any harm, if that harm is so slight that no person of ordinary sense and temper would complain of such harm.

Section Text: Except murder, and offences against the State punishable with death, nothing is an offence which is done by a person who is compelled to do it by threats, which, at the time of doing it, reasonably cause the apprehension that instant death to that person will otherwise be the consequence: Provided the person doing the act did not of his own accord, or from a reasonable apprehension of harm to himself short of instant death, place himself in the situation by which he became subject to such constraint.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is a person liable for a crime committed under the threat of immediate death?

Rule.Section 76: Exempts acts performed by persons who believe in good faith they are legally bound to perform the duty.
Rule.Section 81: Permits acts likely to cause harm if done in good faith to prevent a much larger disaster.
Rule.Section 94: Exempts individuals from liability for non-capital crimes committed under a reasonable fear of instant death.
Rule.Section 95: Rules that trivial harms that wouldn't bother an ordinary person do not constitute criminal offenses.
Rule.Section 96: States that actions taken while exercising the legal right of self-defense are not punishable crimes.

Application.Section 76: Fails because the smith was acting under physical coercion from criminals rather than a good-faith belief in a legal duty.
Application.Section 81: Fails because it addresses the choice of lesser harm in emergencies, not coercion by human threats to commit a specific crime.
Application.Section 94: Governs directly because the smith was forced to commit a non-murder offense by a credible threat of immediate lethal force.
Application.Section 95: Fails because assisting in a robbery is a significant legal harm that far exceeds the threshold of a slight or trivial matter.
Application.Section 96: Fails because the smith was performing a criminal act for the robbers rather than using force to defend himself against them.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 94, the smith is not punishable because he was compelled to act by a threat that caused a reasonable fear of instant death for a non-capital offense.

Question: Scenario-based: Salim is racing his car at high speeds through a crowded pedestrian market. He swerves between shoppers and almost hits several people. He hasn't caused an accident yet. Has he committed a crime?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Whoever drives any vehicle, or rides, on any public way in a manner so rash or negligent as to endanger human life, or to be likely to cause hurt or injury to any other person, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, or with fine which may, subject to the minimum of one thousand taka, extend to five thousand taka, or with both.

Whoever causes the death of any person by doing any rash or negligent act not amounting to culpable homicide shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to five years, or with fine, or with both.

Whoever does any act so rashly or negligently as to endanger human life or the personal safety of others, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three months, or with fine which may extend to two hundred and fifty taka, or with both.

Whoever, by doing any act, or by omitting to take order with any property in his possession or under his charge, causes danger, obstruction or injury to any person in any public way or public line of navigation, shall be punished with fine which may extend to two hundred taka.

Nothing is an offence by reason that it causes, or that it is intended to cause, or that it is known to be likely to cause, any harm, if that harm is so slight that no person of ordinary sense and temper would complain of such harm.

Section Text: Whoever drives any vehicle, or rides, on any public way in a manner so rash or negligent as to endanger human life, or to be likely to cause hurt or injury to any other person, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, or with fine which may, subject to the minimum of one thousand taka, extend to five thousand taka, or with both.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is dangerous driving on a public road a crime if no one is actually injured?

Rule.Section 279: Governs rash or negligent driving on public roads that endangers life or risks causing injury.
Rule.Section 304A: Governs rash or negligent acts only when they result in the actual death of a human being.
Rule.Section 336: Governs general rash behavior endangering safety, but carries a much lighter penalty than specific driving offenses.
Rule.Section 283: Governs the creation of physical obstructions or hazards on roads rather than the conduct of driving itself.
Rule.Section 95: Exempts trivial harms from being treated as criminal offenses.

Application.Section 279: Governs directly because it specifically targets 'driving' on a 'public way' in a 'rash' manner that 'endangers life', even without an accident.
Application.Section 304A: Fails because the swimmers were only endangered; no death occurred to trigger this section.
Application.Section 336: Fails as a primary selection because Section 279 is the more specialized and severe provision specifically for vehicle-related recklessness.
Application.Section 283: Fails because it targets the 'obstruction' of a path rather than the dangerous manner in which a vehicle is being operated.
Application.Section 95: Fails because high-speed driving through a pedestrian crowd is a major public safety threat rather than a slight or trivial harm.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 279, Salim is guilty of a crime because he drove so rashly on a public road that he endangered human life.

Question: Scenario-based: Salim borrows his neighbor's sheep for a weekend display. On Sunday, Salim decides the sheep is delicious and slaughters it to feed his family. Has he committed theft or misappropriation?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Whoever, intending to take dishonestly any moveable property out of the possession of any person without that person's consent, moves that property in order to such taking, is said to commit theft.

Whoever dishonestly misappropriates or converts to his own use any moveable property, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both.

Whoever commits mischief by killing, poisoning, maiming or rendering useless, any elephant, camel, horse, mule, buffalo, bull, cow or ox, whatever may be the value thereof, or any other animal of the value of fifty taka or upwards, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to five years, or with fine, or with both.

Whoever, being in any manner entrusted with property, or with any dominion over property, dishonestly misappropriates or converts to his own use that property, or dishonestly uses or disposes of that property in violation of any direction of law prescribing the mode in which such trust is to be discharged, or of any legal contract, express or implied, which he has made touching the discharge of such trust, or wilfully suffers any other person so to do, commits "criminal breach of trust".

Whoever dishonestly receives or retains any stolen property, knowing or having reason to believe the same to be stolen property, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, or with fine, or with both.

Section Text: Whoever dishonestly misappropriates or converts to his own use any moveable property, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both. Explanation 1.—A dishonest misappropriation for a time only is a misappropriation within the meaning of this section.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is misusing property that was voluntarily given for a limited time classified as theft or misappropriation?

Rule.Section 378: Governs theft, requiring a taking without consent from the start.
Rule.Section 403: Governs dishonest misappropriation, including property acquired with consent but later misused.
Rule.Section 429: Governs mischief involving the killing of animals to cause loss, rather than for personal consumption/gain.
Rule.Section 405: Governs criminal breach of trust, which involves the betrayal of a specific formal entrustment.
Rule.Section 411: Governs the receipt of property that was already stolen by a third party.

Application.Section 378: Fails because Salim had consent to possess the sheep; theft requires a non-consensual taking from the outset.
Application.Section 403: Governs directly because Salim honestly acquired the sheep but later 'converted it to his own use' by eating it.
Application.Section 429: Fails because Salim's goal was personal gain (food) rather than malicious destruction of the neighbor's property.
Application.Section 405: Fails as a primary selection because a simple neighborly loan is the classic case for Section 403's broader misappropriation rule.
Application.Section 411: Fails because the sheep was not stolen; it was legally borrowed by Salim before the dishonest act occurred.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 403, Explanation 1, Salim is guilty because he dishonestly converted property that he originally borrowed for a different use.

Question: Scenario-based: To prove that Salman was the owner of a ship, a lawyer wants to show the judge the government's official registration records for vessels. Can the judge automatically accept this as a relevant fact?

Possible Sections.Full Text: In this Act the following words and expressions are used in the following senses, unless a contrary intention appears from the context:- "Court" includes all Judges and Magistrates and all persons, except arbitrators, legally authorized to take evidence. "Fact" means and includes-

Entries in books of account [or digital record], regularly kept in the course of business, are relevant whenever they refer to a matter into which the Court has to inquire, but such statements shall not alone be sufficient evidence to charge any person with liability.

An entry in any public or other official book, register or record [,or digital record], stating a fact in issue or relevant fact, and made by a public servant in the discharge of his official duty, or by any other person in performance of a duty specially enjoined by the law of the country in which such book, register [, record or digital record] is kept, is itself a relevant fact.

Statements of facts in issue or relevant facts, made in published [maps, charts or digital record] generally offered for public sale, or in [maps, plans or digital record] made under the authority of the Government, as to matters usually represented or stated in such [maps, charts, plans or digital record], are themselves relevant facts.

When the Court has to form an opinion as to the existence of any fact of a public nature, any statement of it, made in a recital contained in any Act of Parliament of the United Kingdom, or in any [Act of Parliament] or in a Government notification is a relevant fact.

Section Text: An entry in any public or other official book, register or record [,or digital record], stating a fact in issue or relevant fact, and made by a public servant in the discharge of his official duty, or by any other person in performance of a duty specially enjoined by the law of the country in which such book, register [, record or digital record] is kept, is itself a relevant fact.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is an entry in an official vessel registry admissible as a relevant fact?

Rule.Section 3: Provides the general definition of a document in a legal context.
Rule.Section 34: Governs the relevancy of private commercial accounting entries.
Rule.Section 35: Makes entries in official public registers relevant if made by a public servant in their duty.
Rule.Section 36: Governs the relevancy of government-authorized maps and plans.
Rule.Section 37: Relates to government notifications regarding facts of a public nature.

Application.Section 3: Fails because being a 'document' doesn't automatically make its specific contents relevant to proving ownership.
Application.Section 34: Fails as a ship registry is a government record rather than a private ledger for tracking commercial debts.
Application.Section 35: Governs directly because the vessel registry is an official public book and the entry was made by a public servant in official duty.
Application.Section 36: Fails since a registry is a written record of ownership rather than a diagrammatic survey map or chart.
Application.Section 37: Fails as specific ownership data is an individual fact rather than a broad 'fact of a public nature' in a Gazette notice.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 35, the registration entry is a relevant fact because it was made in an official register by a public servant performing their duty.

Question: Scenario-based: The Government of Bangladesh discovered that a person has been illegally occupying a large public forest area for the last 30 years. Does the Government still have time to sue to get the land back?

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I Article 142
Possible Sections.Full Text: For possession of immoveable property when the plaintiff, while in possession of the property, has been dispossessed or has discontinued the possession.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I Article 144
Possible Sections.Full Text: For possession of immoveable property or any interest therein not hereby otherwise specially provided for. Twelve years. When the possession of the defendant becomes adverse to the plaintiff.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I Article 149
Possible Sections.Full Text: Any suit by or on behalf of the Government. Sixty years.. When the period of limitation would begin to run under this Act against a like suit by a private person.
At the determination of the period hereby limited to any person for instituting a suit for possession of any property, his right to such property shall be extinguished.

Notwithstanding anything hereinbefore contained, no suit against a person in whom property has become vested in trust for any specific purpose, or against his legal representatives or assigns (not being assigns for valuable consideration), for the purpose of following in his or their hands such property or the proceeds thereof, or for an account of such property or proceeds, shall be barred by any length of time. For the purposes of this section any property comprised in a Hindu, Muslim or Buddhist religious or charitable endowment shall be deemed to be property vested in trust for a specific purpose, and the manager of any such property shall be deemed to be the trustee thereof.

Relevant Section: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I Article 149
Section Text: Any suit by or on behalf of the Government. Sixty years.. When the period of limitation would begin to run under this Act against a like suit by a private person.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: What is the limitation period for the Government to sue for possession of land?

Rule.Schedule I Article 142: Requires private citizens to sue for land possession within twelve years of being dispossessed.
Rule.Schedule I Article 144: Sets a twelve-year limit for general property recovery based on an intruder's adverse or hostile possession.
Rule.Schedule I Article 149: Mandates a sixty-year limitation period specifically for any civil suit brought by or on behalf of the Government.
Rule.Section 28: Terminates legal ownership rights once the specific time limit for filing a possession suit has fully expired.
Rule.Section 10: Removes all time limits for suits against express trustees to recover property given for a specific purpose.

Application.Schedule I Article 142: Fails because the plaintiff is the Government, which is granted a much longer period than the twelve years allowed for private individuals.
Application.Schedule I Article 144: Fails as Article 149 provides the specific, longer timeframe required for state-led lawsuits regarding property recovery.
Application.Schedule I Article 149: Governs directly because the suit is for land possession initiated by the Government, triggering the special sixty-year limitation period.
Application.Section 28: Fails because the Government's sixty-year deadline has not yet expired, so their underlying right to the forest remains active.
Application.Section 10: Fails because the illegal occupant is a trespasser rather than a person who was given the land in trust for a specific purpose.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I, Article 149, the Government has sixty years to sue to get the forest land back, so they are still well within the deadline.

Cited Acts and Sections: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I, Article 149

Question: Scenario-based: An eight-year-old child was cheated. Can the child file the lawsuit themselves, or does an adult need to sign for them?

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Order XXXII, Rule 1
Possible Sections.Full Text: Every suit by a minor shall be instituted in his name by a person who in such suit shall be called the next friend of the minor.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Order XXXIII, Rule 1
Possible Sections.Full Text:
Subject to the following provisions, any suit may be instituted by a pauper.

**Explanation.**—A person is a "pauper" when he is not possessed of sufficient means to enable him to pay the fee prescribed by law for the plaint in such suit, or, where no such fee is prescribed, when he is not entitled to property worth <sup>1</sup>[five thousand Taka] other than his necessary wearing-apparel and the subject-matter of the suit.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 147
Possible Sections.Full Text: In all suits to which any person under disability is a party, any consent or agreement, as to any proceeding shall, if given or made with the express leave of the Court by the next friend or guardian for the suit, have the same force and effect as if such person, were under no disability and had given such consent or made such agreement.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Order I, Rule 1
Possible Sections.Full Text: All persons may be joined in one suit as plaintiffs in whom any right to relief in respect of or arising out of the same act or transaction or series of acts or transactions is alleged to exist, whether jointly, severally or in the alternative, where, if such persons brought separate suits, any common question of law or fact would arise.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 132
Possible Sections.Full Text: (1) Women who, according to the customs and manners of the country, ought not to be compelled to appear in public shall be exempt from personal appearance in Court. (2) Nothing herein contained shall be deemed to exempt such women from arrest in execution of civil process in any case in which the arrest of women is not prohibited by this Code.

Relevant Section: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Order XXXII, Rule 1
Section Text: Every suit by a minor shall be instituted in his name by a person who in such suit shall be called the next friend of the minor.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is an adult representative required to institute a lawsuit on behalf of a minor child?

Rule.Order XXXII, Rule 1: Requires that any legal action on behalf of a child (minor) must be started by an adult called a 'next friend'.
Rule.Order XXXIII, Rule 1: Allows people with no money to sue for free if they can prove their status as a 'pauper'.
Rule.Section 147: Confirms that an agreement made by a guardian for a child in court is as legally binding as an adult's agreement.
Rule.Order I, Rule 1: Defines which groups of people are allowed to join together as the suing parties in a single trial.
Rule.Section 132: Exempts certain women from showing up in a public courtroom based on their cultural or family customs.

Application.Order XXXII, Rule 1: Governs directly because the eight-year-old is a minor and must have an adult 'next friend' to officially file the lawsuit.
Application.Order XXXIII, Rule 1: Fails because the scenario addresses 'age' rather than 'financial' status as a poor person.
Application.Section 147: Does not apply here as it governs 'settlements' rather than the 'institution' or start of the case.
Application.Order I, Rule 1: Is inapplicable as it defines 'groups' rather than the 'capacity' of a child to sue alone.
Application.Section 132: Fails because the issue is legal age rather than gender or cultural customs regarding public appearance.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Order XXXII, Rule 1, the child cannot sue alone — an adult must start the case for them acting as the child's 'next friend'.

Cited Acts and Sections: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Order XXXII, Rule 1
