Question: Scenario-based: I am being tried for a crime. Can the prosecutor show a government-issued map to the judge to prove that my house is located within the city limits where the incident happened?

Possible Sections.Full Text: In this Act the following words and expressions are used in the following senses, unless a contrary intention appears from the context:- "Court" includes all Judges and Magistrates and all persons, except arbitrators, legally authorized to take evidence. "Fact" means and includes-

An entry in any public or other official book, register or record [,or digital record], stating a fact in issue or relevant fact, and made by a public servant in the discharge of his official duty, or by any other person in performance of a duty specially enjoined by the law of the country in which such book, register [, record or digital record] is kept, is itself a relevant fact.

When the Court has to form an opinion as to the existence of any fact of a public nature, any statement of it, made in a recital contained in any Act of Parliament of the United Kingdom, or in any [Act of Parliament] or in a Government notification is a relevant fact.

When the Court has to form an opinion as to a law of any country, any statement of such law contained in a book purporting to be printed or published under the authority of the Government of such country and to contain any such law, and any report of a ruling of the Courts of such country contained in a book purporting to be a report of such rulings, is relevant.

Statements of facts in issue or relevant facts, made in published [maps, charts or digital record] generally offered for public sale, or in [maps, plans or digital record] made under the authority of the Government, as to matters usually represented or stated in such [maps, charts, plans or digital record], are themselves relevant facts.

Section Text: Statements of facts in issue or relevant facts, made in published [maps, charts or digital record] generally offered for public sale, or in [maps, plans or digital record] made under the authority of the Government, as to matters usually represented or stated in such [maps, charts, plans or digital record], are themselves relevant facts.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is a government-authorized map relevant to proving geographical facts in a trial?

Rule.Section 3: Defines what a document is and specifically includes maps and plans.
Rule.Section 35: Governs relevancy of official public register entries made by public servants.
Rule.Section 37: Relates to government notifications regarding facts of a public nature.
Rule.Section 38: Governs the relevancy of law books published by the government.
Rule.Section 36: Declares that facts stated in government-authorized maps and charts are relevant facts.

Application.Section 3: Fails because being a 'document' does not inherently make its specific contents 'relevant facts' without a further rule.
Application.Section 35: Fails since a map is a visual chart rather than an official register or written record of a clerk's duty.
Application.Section 37: Fails as a local boundary is a physical fact rather than a broad public announcement in a notification.
Application.Section 38: Fails because the issue concerns physical locations rather than legal principles or foreign statutes.
Application.Section 36: Governs directly as it explicitly makes statements within maps made under Government authority relevant for proving facts.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 36, the map is a relevant fact because it was prepared under government authority to show geographical matters like city limits.

Question: Scenario-based: Salim and Jamil plan to steal a car. Salim waits at the corner to watch for police, while Jamil uses a tool to open the car door. Before Jamil can start the engine, they are both arrested. Can Salim be charged with theft?

Possible Sections.Full Text: A person abets the doing of a thing, who: Firstly.-Instigates any person to do that thing; or Secondly.-Engages with one or more other person or persons in any conspiracy for the doing of that thing, if an act or illegal omission takes place in pursuance of that conspiracy, and in order to the doing of that thing; or Thirdly.-Intentionally aids, by any act or illegal omission, the doing of that thing.

If an offence is committed by any member of an unlawful assembly in prosecution of the common object of that assembly, or such as the members of that assembly knew to be likely to be committed in prosecution of that object, every person who, at the time of the committing of that offence, is a member of the same assembly, is guilty of that offence.
When a criminal act is done by several persons, in furtherance of the common intention of all, each of such persons is liable for that act in the same manner as if it were done by him alone.

When two or more persons agree to do, or cause to be done,- (1) an illegal act, or (2) an act which is not illegal by illegal means, such an agreement is designated a criminal conspiracy: Provided that no agreement except an agreement to commit an offence shall amount to a criminal conspiracy unless some act besides the agreement is done by one or more parties to such agreement in pursuance thereof.
Where several persons are engaged or concerned in the commission of a criminal act, they may be guilty of different offences by means of that act.

Section Text: When a criminal act is done by several persons, in furtherance of the common intention of all, each of such persons is liable for that act in the same manner as if it were done by him alone.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is a person liable as a principal for a crime committed by their partner during a shared criminal act?

Rule.Section 107: Governs abetment, which often applies to individuals who assist from a distance or before the crime occurs.
Rule.Section 149: Governs joint liability for members of a large 'unlawful assembly' of five or more persons.
Rule.Section 34: Governs joint liability for a small group of people acting with a shared common intention.
Rule.Section 120A: Defines the offense of criminal conspiracy based on the initial agreement to commit a crime.
Rule.Section 38: Governs the possibility of different participants in one act being guilty of different crimes.

Application.Section 107: Fails as a primary charge because Salim's active participation as a lookout at the scene makes him a co-perpetrator rather than a mere abettor.
Application.Section 149: Fails because the scenario only involves two people, which is below the five-person threshold required for an unlawful assembly.
Application.Section 34: Governs directly because the two men acted with a 'common intention' to steal the car, making Salim liable for Jamil's actions as if he did them alone.
Application.Section 120A: Fails because while a conspiracy existed, Section 34 provides the substantive rule for joint liability in the actual attempt/execution of the act.
Application.Section 38: Fails because the two men shared the exact same goal and performed the same criminal act (theft), resulting in the same offense.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 34, Salim is guilty of theft just like Jamil because they acted with a common intention to commit the crime together.

Question: Scenario-based: A judge is reviewing a case and realizes the plaintiff filed it in the wrong district. Must the judge give the papers back to the plaintiff?

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Order VII, Rule 11
Possible Sections.Full Text:
The plaint shall be rejected in the following cases:— where it does not disclose a cause of action: where the relief claimed is undervalued, and the plaintiff, on being required by the Court to correct the valuation within a time to be fixed by the Court, fails to do so: where the relief claimed is properly valued, but the plaint is written upon paper insufficiently stamped, and the plaintiff, on being required by the Court to supply the requisite stamp-paper within a time to be fixed by the Court, fails to do so: where the suit appears from the statement in the plaint to be barred by any law<sup>1</sup> [:

Provided that the time fixed by the Court for the correction of the valuation or supplying of the requisite stamp-paper shall not exceed twenty-one days.]

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Order VII, Rule 10
Possible Sections.Full Text: 10.(1) The plaint shall at any stage of the suit be returned to be presented to the Court in which the suit should have been instituted.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 21
Possible Sections.Full Text: 21. No objection as to the place of suing shall be allowed by any appellate or revisional Court unless such objection was taken in the Court of first instance at the earliest possible opportunity and in all cases where issues are settled at or before such settlement, and unless there has been a consequent failure of justice.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 24
Possible Sections.Full Text: 24.(1) On the application of any of the parties and after notice to the parties and after hearing such of them as desire to be heard, or of its own motion without such notice, the High Court Division or the District Court may at any stage-

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Order IX, Rule 3
Possible Sections.Full Text: Where neither party appears when the suit is called on for hearing, the Court may make an order that the suit be dismissed.

Relevant Section: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Order VII, Rule 10
Section Text: 10.(1) The plaint shall at any stage of the suit be returned to be presented to the Court in which the suit should have been instituted.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: What must a judge do if a suit lacks proper territorial jurisdiction?

Rule.Order VII, Rule 11: Lists the mandatory grounds for the permanent rejection and termination of a case at the outset.
Rule.Order VII, Rule 10: Requires the court to return the original paperwork to the plaintiff so it can be refiled in the correct district.
Rule.Section 21: Prevents a party from waiting until the appeal stage to complain about a location mistake.
Rule.Section 24: Gives the High Court authority to move an existing case from one lower court to another.
Rule.Order IX, Rule 3: Allows a judge to dismiss a lawsuit if both the person suing and the person being sued fail to attend.

Application.Order VII, Rule 11: Fails because 'rejection' is a final act for bad claims, whereas 'return' is for geographic mistakes.
Application.Order VII, Rule 10: Governs directly as it mandates the 'return' of the plaint whenever the case belongs in a different court.
Application.Section 21: Does not apply as it governs late objections rather than the trial judge's initial duty.
Application.Section 24: Fails because it describes a superior court 'transfer' rather than a simple 'return' of papers.
Application.Order IX, Rule 3: Is inapplicable as the issue is location rather than the physical absence of parties.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Order VII, Rule 10, Sub-rule (1), the judge must return the papers — the law requires the court to give the lawsuit back so it can be filed in the correct district.

Cited Acts and Sections: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Order VII, Rule 10

Question: Scenario-based: After a case is finished, Rahim finds out the judge's final order contains a minor calculation error in the total amount. Can Rahim ask the judge to fix this simple math mistake?

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 151
Possible Sections.Full Text: Nothing in this Code shall be deemed to limit or otherwise affect the inherent power of the Court to make such orders as may be necessary for the ends of justice or to prevent abuse of the process of the Court.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 152
Possible Sections.Full Text: Clerical or arithmetical mistakes in judgments, decrees or orders or errors arising therein from any accidental slip or omission may at any time be corrected by the Court either of its own motion or on the application of any of the parties.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 114
Possible Sections.Full Text: Subject as aforesaid, any person considering himself aggrieved- (a) by a decree or order from which an appeal is allowed by this Code, but from which no appeal has been preferred, (b) by a decree or order from which no appeal is allowed by this Code, or (c) by a decision on a reference from a Court of Small Causes may apply for a view of judgment to the Court which passed the decree or made the order, and the Court may make such order thereon as it thinks fit.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 153
Possible Sections.Full Text: The Court may at any time, and on such terms as to costs or otherwise as it thinks fit, amend any defect or error in any proceeding in a suit; and all necessary amendments shall be made for the purpose of determining the real question or issue raised by or depending on such proceeding.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Order XX, Rule 3
Possible Sections.Full Text: The Judgment shall be dated and signed by the Judge in open Court at the time of pronouncing it and, when once signed, shall not afterwards be altered or added to, save as provided by section 152 or on review.

Relevant Section: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 152
Section Text: Clerical or arithmetical mistakes in judgments, decrees or orders or errors arising therein from any accidental slip or omission may at any time be corrected by the Court either of its own motion or on the application of any of the parties.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is a court permitted to correct obvious math errors in a finalized judgment?

Rule.Section 151: Preserves the broad backup power of the court to act for justice when no specific written rule applies.
Rule.Section 152: Allows judges to fix simple math errors, typos, or accidental slips in their own orders at any time.
Rule.Section 114: Grants a party the right to a formal review of a case if there was a major mistake or new evidence found.
Rule.Section 153: Empowers the court to fix technical defects in the general lawsuit paperwork and proceedings.
Rule.Order XX, Rule 3: States that once a judge signs a judgment, they generally cannot change it, with specific exceptions for clerical fixes.

Application.Section 151: Fails because it is a general residual power that is not needed when a specific rule like Section 152 exists for math errors.
Application.Section 152: Governs directly because an 'arithmetical mistake' is the exact definition of the error Rahim wants fixed.
Application.Section 114: Is too complex as it is used for substantive legal errors rather than simple mechanical math mistakes.
Application.Section 153: Fails because it handles errors in the 'proceedings' rather than the final 'judgment' or 'decree' document itself.
Application.Order XX, Rule 3: Does not govern here because it establishes the general rule of finality but yields to the specific exception for clerical fixes.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, section 152, the judge can fix it immediately — the law permits courts to correct simple clerical or math mistakes in judgments at any time.

Cited Acts and Sections: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 152

Question: Scenario-based: I am suing my agent because he failed to collect rent from my tenant. If the tenant admits to me in a letter that he actually owed the rent, can I use that letter as evidence against my agent?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Statements made by a party to the proceeding, or by an agent to any such party, whom the Court regards, under the circumstances of the case, as expressly or impliedly authorized by him to make them, are admissions. Statements made by parties to suits suing or sued in a representative character, are not admissions, unless they were made while the party making them held that character. Statements made by–

Admissions are relevant and may be proved as against the person who makes them, or his representative in interest; but they can not be proved by or on behalf of the person who makes them or by his representative in interest, except in the following cases:–

Statements made by persons whose position or liability it is necessary to prove as against any party to the suit, are admissions, if such statements would be relevant as against such persons in relation to such position or liability in a suit brought by or against them, and if they are made whilst the person making them occupies such position or is subject to such liability.
Statements made by persons to whom a party to the suit has expressly referred for information in reference to a matter in dispute are admissions.

Statements made by persons whose position or liability it is necessary to prove as against any party to the suit, are admissions, if such statements would be relevant as against such persons in relation to such position or liability in a suit brought by or against them, and if they are made whilst the person making them occupies such position or is subject to such liability.

Section Text: Statements made by persons whose position or liability it is necessary to prove as against any party to the suit, are admissions, if such statements would be relevant as against such persons in relation to such position or liability in a suit brought by or against them, and if they are made whilst the person making them occupies such position or is subject to such liability.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is a third party's admission admissible when proving their liability is a prerequisite for the main case?

Rule.Section 18: Governs admissions made by parties to a suit or their authorized agents.
Rule.Section 21: Governs the parties who are permitted to prove an admission in court.
Rule.Section 19: Governs admissions by third parties whose liability must be established against a party to the suit.
Rule.Section 20: Governs admissions from third parties to whom a party directed an inquiry.
Rule.Section 32(3): Governs out-of-court statements made against interest when the person is deceased or unavailable.

Application.Section 18: Fails because the tenant is not a party to the suit or the agent's authorized representative.
Application.Section 21: Fails as it defines the process of proof rather than identifying the tenant's words as a valid admission.
Application.Section 19: Governs directly because the owner must prove the tenant's liability for rent to win the case against the negligent agent.
Application.Section 20: Fails because the agent did not specifically refer the owner to the tenant for information.
Application.Section 32(3): Fails as there is no evidence the tenant is deceased or missing; Section 19 handles this as a live admission.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 19, the tenant's letter is an admission because you must prove the tenant's liability to win your case against the agent.

Question: Scenario-based: A man finds a person lying injured on the road. He takes the person's wallet, containing money and ID, intending to return it. Later, he sees a news report about the accident and decides to spend the money instead of returning it. Is this theft?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Whoever, intending to take dishonestly any moveable property out of the possession of any person without that person's consent, moves that property in order to such taking, is said to commit theft.

Whoever dishonestly misappropriates or converts to his own use any moveable property, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both.

Whoever, being in any manner entrusted with property, or with any dominion over property, dishonestly misappropriates or converts to his own use that property, or dishonestly uses or disposes of that property in violation of any direction of law prescribing the mode in which such trust is to be discharged, or of any legal contract, express or implied, which he has made touching the discharge of such trust, or wilfully suffers any other person so to do, commits "criminal breach of trust".

Whoever dishonestly receives or retains any stolen property, knowing or having reason to believe the same to be stolen property, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, or with fine, or with both.

Whoever, with intent to cause, or knowing that he is likely to cause, wrongful loss or damage to the public or to any person, causes the destruction of any property, or any such change in any property or in the situation thereof as destroys or diminishes its value or utility, or affects it injuriously, commits "mischief".

Section Text: Whoever dishonestly misappropriates or converts to his own use any moveable property, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Does changing one's mind to keep found property after originally intending to return it constitute a crime?

Rule.Section 378: Governs theft, which requires the dishonest intent to be present at the exact moment the property is moved from the owner.
Rule.Section 403: Governs dishonest misappropriation, including property found innocently where the dishonest intent develops later.
Rule.Section 405: Governs criminal breach of trust, which requires that the property was given to the person in trust by the owner.
Rule.Section 411: Governs receiving stolen goods from another person while knowing they were already stolen.
Rule.Section 425: Governs the intentional destruction or damage of property to cause loss to the owner.

Application.Section 378: Fails because the man's original 'taking' was not dishonest (he meant to protect it), and theft requires dishonesty at the start.
Application.Section 403: Governs directly because Explanation 2 covers found property where the intent to keep it develops after discovering the owner's identity.
Application.Section 405: Fails because the injured owner did not 'entrust' the wallet to the man; the man simply picked it up from the ground.
Application.Section 411: Fails because the property was lost on the road, not stolen by someone else before the man found it.
Application.Section 425: Fails because the man spent the money (misappropriation) rather than destroying the wallet or ID to cause a loss.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 403, Explanation 2, the man is guilty of dishonest misappropriation because he kept and spent the found money after discovering who the owner was.

Question: Scenario-based: I found an old newspaper announcement where the government officially declared a new public park. Can I use this notification in court to prove the park exists?

Possible Sections.Full Text: An entry in any public or other official book, register or record [,or digital record], stating a fact in issue or relevant fact, and made by a public servant in the discharge of his official duty, or by any other person in performance of a duty specially enjoined by the law of the country in which such book, register [, record or digital record] is kept, is itself a relevant fact.

When the Court has to form an opinion as to the existence of any fact of a public nature, any statement of it, made in a recital contained in any Act of Parliament of the United Kingdom, or in any [Act of Parliament] or in a Government notification is a relevant fact.

Statements of facts in issue or relevant facts, made in published [maps, charts or digital record] generally offered for public sale, or in [maps, plans or digital record] made under the authority of the Government, as to matters usually represented or stated in such [maps, charts, plans or digital record], are themselves relevant facts.

When the Court has to form an opinion as to a law of any country, any statement of such law contained in a book purporting to be printed or published under the authority of the Government of such country and to contain any such law, and any report of a ruling of the Courts of such country contained in a book purporting to be a report of such rulings, is relevant.

Facts necessary to explain or introduce a fact in issue or relevant fact, or which support or rebut an inference suggested by a fact in issue or relevant fact, or which establish the identity of anything or person whose identity is relevant, or fix the time or place at which any fact in issue or relevant fact happened or which show the relation of parties by whom any such fact was transacted, are relevant in so far as they are necessary for that purpose.

Section Text: When the Court has to form an opinion as to the existence of any fact of a public nature, any statement of it, made in a recital contained in any Act of Parliament of the United Kingdom, or in any [Act of Parliament] or in a Government notification is a relevant fact.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is a government notification relevant to proving a fact of a public nature?

Rule.Section 35: Governs official registers and books of record kept by public servants.
Rule.Section 37: Relates to government notifications regarding facts of a public nature.
Rule.Section 36: Governs government-authorized maps, charts, and plans.
Rule.Section 38: Governs law books and court reports published under foreign government authority.
Rule.Section 9: Allows evidence that explains or introduces facts or identifies persons.

Application.Section 35: Fails as an announcement in a Gazette is an official notice rather than a line-item entry in a registry book.
Application.Section 37: Governs directly because the creation of a park is a fact of a public nature announced through a Government notification.
Application.Section 36: Fails since a general textual announcement is not a diagrammatic map or survey chart.
Application.Section 38: Fails because the creation of a park is a specific physical/legal fact rather than a principle of international law.
Application.Section 9: Fails because Section 37 is the specialized provision for government-issued public notices.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 37, the Government notification is a relevant fact because it contains an official statement about a fact of a public nature.

Question: Scenario-based: A woodcutter is working with an axe when the head suddenly flies off, hitting and killing a bystander. He was working in a safe area and using proper care. Is he guilty of murder?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Whoever causes the death of any person by doing any rash or negligent act not amounting to culpable homicide shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to five years, or with fine, or with both.

Nothing is an offence which is done by accident or misfortune, and without any criminal intention or knowledge in the doing of a lawful act in a lawful manner by lawful means and with proper care and caution.

Nothing is an offence merely by reason of its being done with the knowledge that it is likely to cause harm, if it be done without any criminal intention to cause harm, and in good faith for the purpose of preventing or avoiding other harm to person or property.
Nothing is an offence which is done by a person who is, or who by reason of a mistake of fact and not by reason of a mistake of law in good faith believes himself to be, bound by law to do it.

Except in the cases hereinafter excepted, culpable homicide is murder, if the act by which the death is caused is done with the intention of causing death, or- Secondly.-If it is done with the intention of causing such bodily injury as the offender knows to be likely to cause the death of the person to whom the harm is caused, or – Thirdly.-If it is done with the intention of causing bodily injury to any person and the bodily injury intended to be inflicted is sufficient in the ordinary course of nature to cause death, or – Fourthly.--If the person committing the act knows that it is so imminently dangerous that it must, in all probability, cause death, or such bodily injury as is likely to cause death, and commits such act without any excuse for incurring the risk of causing death or such injury as aforesaid.

Section Text: Nothing is an offence which is done by accident or misfortune, and without any criminal intention or knowledge in the doing of a lawful act in a lawful manner by lawful means and with proper care and caution.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Does an accidental death during lawful work with proper care constitute an offense?

Rule.Section 304A: Governs deaths caused by rash or negligent behavior that lacks murderous intent.
Rule.Section 80: Governs acts caused by accident or misfortune while performing a lawful task with due care and no criminal intent.
Rule.Section 81: Governs acts of necessity where harm is caused intentionally to prevent a greater disaster.
Rule.Section 76: Governs acts performed due to a good-faith mistake of fact by a person believing they are bound by law.
Rule.Section 300: Governs the definition of murder, requiring specific criminal intent or extreme knowledge of danger.

Application.Section 304A: Fails because the woodcutter used 'proper care,' which excludes the 'negligent' or 'rash' behavior required for this charge.
Application.Section 80: Governs directly because the death was a pure accident resulting from a lawful act (woodcutting) done with caution and no intent to kill.
Application.Section 81: Fails because the harm was not a choice made to avoid another harm; it was an unforeseen misfortune.
Application.Section 76: Fails because the woodcutter was not acting under a mistaken belief of a legal obligation.
Application.Section 300: Fails because there was no intention to cause death or knowledge that the axe would fail.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 80, the woodcutter is not guilty because he was doing a lawful act with proper care and the death was caused by a pure accident.

Question: Scenario-based: A judge is hearing a case where there is no specific written rule in the Code to solve a unique problem. Can the judge make an order based on fairness to ensure a just trial?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Nothing in this Code shall be deemed to limit or otherwise affect the inherent power of the Court to make such orders as may be necessary for the ends of justice or to prevent abuse of the process of the Court.

Possible Sections.Full Text: Clerical or arithmetical mistakes in judgments, decrees or orders or errors arising therein from any accidental slip or omission may at any time be corrected by the Court either of its own motion or on the application of any of the parties.

Possible Sections.Full Text: Subject as aforesaid, any person considering himself aggrieved- (a) by a decree or order from which an appeal is allowed by this Code, but from which no appeal has been preferred, (b) by a decree or order from which no appeal is allowed by this Code, or (c) by a decision on a reference from a Court of Small Causes may apply for a view of judgment to the Court which passed the decree or made the order, and the Court may make such order thereon as it thinks fit.

Possible Sections.Full Text: Where any period is fixed or granted by the Court for the doing of any act prescribed or allowed by this Code, the Court may, in its discretion, from time to time, enlarge such period, even though the period originally fixed or granted may have expired.

Relevant Section: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 151
Section Text: Nothing in this Code shall be deemed to limit or otherwise affect the inherent power of the Court to make such orders as may be necessary for the ends of justice or to prevent abuse of the process of the Court.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Does the court possess generalized powers to act for justice when specific rules are silent?

Rule.Section 151: Preserves the 'inherent power' of the court to issue orders required to achieve justice or stop the abuse of court systems.
Rule.Section 152: Restricts the court's power to correcting minor typos, math errors, or accidental slips in existing written judgments.
Rule.Section 153: Grants power to amend technical defects or formal errors in the court's paperwork and proceedings.
Rule.Section 114: Allows a party to ask for a reconsideration of a judgment based on newly discovered facts.
Rule.Section 148: Enables the court to extend deadlines and time periods that it has previously set for parties.

Application.Section 152: Fails because the scenario involves a substantive unique problem, not a simple typo in a document.
Application.Section 153: Is inapplicable as it concerns amending defects in existing papers rather than creating a solution for a missing rule.
Application.Section 114: Does not apply because the judge is trying to decide an active problem, not re-decide a finalized case.
Application.Section 148: Fails because the scenario is about a unique legal problem, not a request for more time to finish paperwork.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, section 151, the judge can make such an order — this section confirms the court's inherent power to do what is necessary for the ends of justice when rules are missing.

Question: Scenario-based: A locksmith is kidnapped and taken to a vault. The kidnappers point a gun at his head and tell him they will kill him instantly unless he picks the lock. He does so. Can the locksmith be punished for helping with the robbery?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Nothing is an offence merely by reason of its being done with the knowledge that it is likely to cause harm, if it be done without any criminal intention to cause harm, and in good faith for the purpose of preventing or avoiding other harm to person or property.

Except murder, and offences against the State punishable with death, nothing is an offence which is done by a person who is compelled to do it by threats, which, at the time of doing it, reasonably cause the apprehension that instant death to that person will otherwise be the consequence: Provided the person doing the act did not of his own accord, or from a reasonable apprehension of harm to himself short of instant death, place himself in the situation by which he became subject to such constraint.

A person abets the doing of a thing, who: Firstly.-Instigates any person to do that thing; or Secondly.-Engages with one or more other person or persons in any conspiracy for the doing of that thing, if an act or illegal omission takes place in pursuance of that conspiracy, and in order to the doing of that thing; or Thirdly.-Intentionally aids, by any act or illegal omission, the doing of that thing.
When a criminal act is done by several persons, in furtherance of the common intention of all, each of such persons is liable for that act in the same manner as if it were done by him alone.
Nothing is an offence which is done in the exercise of the right of private defence.

Section Text: Except murder, and offences against the State punishable with death, nothing is an offence which is done by a person who is compelled to do it by threats, which, at the time of doing it, reasonably cause the apprehension that instant death to that person will otherwise be the consequence: Provided the person doing the act did not of his own accord, or from a reasonable apprehension of harm to himself short of instant death, place himself in the situation by which he became subject to such constraint.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is a person liable for a crime they were forced to commit under a threat of immediate death?

Rule.Section 81: Governs acts of 'necessity' where the actor chooses the lesser harm in a natural emergency.
Rule.Section 94: Governs 'compulsion' by human threats, exempting non-capital acts performed under fear of instant death.
Rule.Section 107: Governs the definition of abetment, which includes intentionally aiding the commission of a crime.
Rule.Section 34: Governs the shared liability of multiple people who act with a joint common intention.
Rule.Section 96: Governs the legality of using force to defend oneself from an attack.

Application.Section 81: Fails because it covers objective necessity rather than coercion by a criminal threat.
Application.Section 94: Governs directly because the locksmith was forced into a non-murder crime by an immediate threat of death.
Application.Section 107: Fails because while he aided the crime, the law provides a specific 'excuse' for aid given under extreme duress.
Application.Section 34: Fails because the locksmith did not 'share' the intention of the robbers; he acted solely to survive.
Application.Section 96: Fails because the locksmith was performing a task for the robbers rather than using force to stop their attack.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 94, the locksmith is not guilty because he was compelled to act by a threat that caused a reasonable fear of instant death.

Question: Scenario-based: Salim borrows his neighbor's lawnmower for one hour. He instead keeps it for two weeks to mow lawns for money, ignoring the neighbor's requests. Is this a crime if Salim always intended to return it one day?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Whoever, intending to take dishonestly any moveable property out of the possession of any person without that person's consent, moves that property in order to such taking, is said to commit theft.

Whoever dishonestly misappropriates or converts to his own use any moveable property, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both.

Whoever, being in any manner entrusted with property, or with any dominion over property, dishonestly misappropriates or converts to his own use that property, or dishonestly uses or disposes of that property in violation of any direction of law prescribing the mode in which such trust is to be discharged, or of any legal contract, express or implied, which he has made touching the discharge of such trust, or wilfully suffers any other person so to do, commits "criminal breach of trust".

Whoever, by deceiving any person, fraudulently or dishonestly induces the person so deceived to deliver any property to any person, or to consent that any person shall retain any property, or intentionally induces the person so deceived to do or omit to do anything which he would not do or omit if he were not so deceived, and which act or omission causes or is likely to cause damage or harm to that person in body, mind, reputation or property, is said to "cheat".

Whoever dishonestly receives or retains any stolen property, knowing or having reason to believe the same to be stolen property, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, or with fine, or with both.

Section Text: Whoever dishonestly misappropriates or converts to his own use any moveable property, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is a temporary dishonest use of borrowed property considered a criminal act?

Rule.Section 378: Governs theft, requiring property to be moved out of the owner's possession without their consent.
Rule.Section 403: Governs dishonest misappropriation, clarifying that even a temporary misappropriation is a crime.
Rule.Section 405: Governs criminal breach of trust, triggered when property is formally 'entrusted' to someone who then misuses it.
Rule.Section 415: Governs cheating, where the offender uses a lie to get the owner to hand over property from the start.
Rule.Section 411: Governs the receipt of property that has already been stolen by someone else.

Application.Section 378: Fails because Salim had consent to move the ladder initially; theft requires a non-consensual taking from the start.
Application.Section 403: Governs directly because Explanation 1 specifies that misappropriating property 'for a time only' (two weeks) is still a crime.
Application.Section 405: Fails because a neighborly loan is generally treated under misappropriation unless there was a formal business-like entrustment.
Application.Section 415: Fails because Salim borrowed it honestly and only later became dishonest; he didn't trick his neighbor to get the ladder.
Application.Section 411: Fails because Salim was the original borrower, not a third party receiving a stolen ladder from a thief.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 403, Explanation 1, Salim is guilty because the law states that dishonest misappropriation, even for a short time, is a crime.

Question: Scenario-based: Rahim was injured by Salman's aggressive dog. To prove Salman was irresponsible, can Rahim show that the dog had previously bitten two other people and Salman had paid for their bandages?

Possible Sections.Full Text: When there is a question whether an act was accidental or intentional, or done with a particular knowledge or intention, the fact that such act formed part of a series of similar occurrences, in each of which the person doing the act was concerned, is relevant.

Any fact is relevant which shows or constitutes a motive or preparation for any fact in issue or relevant fact. The conduct of any party, or of any agent to any party, to any suit or proceeding, in reference to such suit or proceeding, or in reference to any fact in issue therein or relevant thereto, and the conduct of any person an offence against whom is the subject of any proceeding, is relevant, if such conduct influences or is influenced by any fact, in issue or relevant fact, and whether it was previous subsequent thereto.

Facts which are the occasions, cause or effect, immediate or otherwise, of relevant facts, or facts in issue, or which constitute the state of things under which they happened, or which afforded an opportunity for their occurrence or transaction, are relevant.
Evidence may be given in any suit or proceeding of the existence or non-existence of every fact in issue and of such other fact as are hereinafter declared to be relevant, and of no others.

Facts showing the existence of any state of mind, such as intention, knowledge, good faith, negligence, rashness, ill-will, or good-will towards any particular person, or showing the existence of any state of body or bodily feeling, are relevant, when the existence of any such state of mind or body or bodily feeling is in issue or relevant.

Section Text: Facts showing the existence of any state of mind, such as intention, knowledge, good faith, negligence, rashness, ill-will, or good-will towards any particular person, or showing the existence of any state of body or bodily feeling, are relevant, when the existence of any such state of mind or body or bodily feeling is in issue or relevant.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Are prior similar incidents relevant to proving a party's knowledge and negligence?

Rule.Section 15: Governs a series of similar acts to distinguish between accident and intent.
Rule.Section 8: Governs motive, preparation, and prior or subsequent conduct.
Rule.Section 7: Governs facts showing the cause, effect, or occasion of a fact in issue.
Rule.Section 5: Limits all evidence to facts in issue and specifically declared relevant facts.
Rule.Section 14: Specifically makes facts relevant if they demonstrate a state of mind like knowledge or negligence.

Application.Section 15: Fails because the issue is Salman's 'knowledge' of the dog's danger, not whether the dog bit Rahim 'by accident' or 'on purpose.'
Application.Section 8: Fails since paying for bandages in the past is used to prove Salman's prior knowledge, not a motive for a current crime.
Application.Section 7: Fails because the previous bites are not the immediate cause or effect of the current injury to Rahim.
Application.Section 5: Fails because it is a general enabling section that doesn't provide the specific rule for state-of-mind relevancy.
Application.Section 14: Governs directly because the previous bites and Salman's payment prove he had 'knowledge' of the dog's ferocity, proving negligence.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 14, Illustration (c), the previous incidents are relevant because they show that Salman knew the dog was dangerous, proving his negligence.

Question: Scenario-based: Karim is accused of waging war against Bangladesh by taking part in an armed riot where three government buildings were damaged. Karim says he was only at the first building. Can the damage at the other two buildings be used as evidence against him?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Facts which are the occasions, cause or effect, immediate or otherwise, of relevant facts, or facts in issue, or which constitute the state of things under which they happened, or which afforded an opportunity for their occurrence or transaction, are relevant.

Where there is reasonable ground to believe that two or more persons have conspired together to commit an offence or an actionable wrong, anything said, done or written by any one of such persons in reference to their common intention, after the time when such intention was first entertained by any one of them, is a relevant fact as against each of the persons believed to be so conspiring, as well for the purpose of proving the existence of the conspiracy as for the purpose of showing that any such person was a party to it.

Facts which, though not in issue, are so connected with a fact in issue as to form part of the same transaction, are relevant, whether they occurred at the same time and place or at different times and places.

Any fact is relevant which shows or constitutes a motive or preparation for any fact in issue or relevant fact. The conduct of any party, or of any agent to any party, to any suit or proceeding, in reference to such suit or proceeding, or in reference to any fact in issue therein or relevant thereto, and the conduct of any person an offence against whom is the subject of any proceeding, is relevant, if such conduct influences or is influenced by any fact, in issue or relevant fact, and whether it was previous subsequent thereto.
Facts not otherwise relevant are relevant–

Section Text: Facts which, though not in issue, are so connected with a fact in issue as to form part of the same transaction, are relevant, whether they occurred at the same time and place or at different times and places.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Are separate acts of violence relevant if they form part of a single general transaction or insurrection?

Rule.Section 7: Governs facts that are the cause, effect, or opportunity for a crime.
Rule.Section 10: Governs statements and acts of conspirators made during a shared plan.
Rule.Section 6: Makes all facts that form part of the 'general transaction' relevant, even if the accused was not present at every act.
Rule.Section 8: Governs motive, preparation, and the conduct of parties influenced by facts in issue.
Rule.Section 11: Relates to facts that make an event's occurrence highly probable or improbable.

Application.Section 7: Fails because the damage at other buildings is part of the event itself rather than a prior occasion or a resultant effect on the accused.
Application.Section 10: Fails as the insurrection acts are part of a shared transaction regardless of whether a formal 'conspiracy' is established first.
Application.Section 6: Governs directly as the damage to multiple buildings is part of the 'general transaction' of the insurrection, as seen in Illustration (b).
Application.Section 8: Fails as the damage at the other buildings is the crime itself rather than a motive or preparatory step taken by Karim.
Application.Section 11: Fails because the issue is the unified nature of a collective event rather than the probability of Karim's individual participation.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 6, Illustration (b), the damage to all three buildings is relevant because they all form part of the same general riot transaction.

Question: Scenario-based: Arpan instigates his friend to punch a man. The friend goes to the man's house, punches him, and also decides to steal the man's watch on his own. Is Arpan liable for the theft of the watch?

Possible Sections.Full Text: When an act is abetted and a different act is done, the abettor is liable for the act done, in the same manner and to the same extent as if he had directly abetted it: Provided the act done was a probable consequence of the abetment, and was committed under the influence of the instigation, or with the aid or in pursuance of the conspiracy which constituted the abetment.

If the act for which the abettor is liable under the last preceding section is committed in addition to the act abetted, and constitutes a distinct offence, the abettor is liable to punishment for each of the offences.

Whenever any person, who if absent would be liable to be punished as an abettor, is present when the act or offence for which he would be punishable in consequence of the abetment is committed, he shall be deemed to have committed such act or offence.
Whoever, intending to take dishonestly any moveable property out of the possession of any person without that person's consent, moves that property in order to such taking, is said to commit theft.

A person abets the doing of a thing, who: Firstly.-Instigates any person to do that thing; or Secondly.-Engages with one or more other person or persons in any conspiracy for the doing of that thing, if an act or illegal omission takes place in pursuance of that conspiracy, and in order to the doing of that thing; or Thirdly.-Intentionally aids, by any act or illegal omission, the doing of that thing.

Section Text: When an act is abetted and a different act is done, the abettor is liable for the act done, in the same manner and to the same extent as if he had directly abetted it: Provided the act done was a probable consequence of the abetment, and was committed under the influence of the instigation, or with the aid or in pursuance of the conspiracy which constituted the abetment.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is an abettor liable for a distinct crime committed by the perpetrator that was not a likely result of the original plan?

Rule.Section 111: Governs liability for different acts, establishing that the abettor is only responsible if the new act was a 'probable consequence' of the abetment.
Rule.Section 112: Governs the cumulative punishment for an abettor when they are actually liable for multiple crimes committed together.
Rule.Section 114: Governs the legal fiction where an abettor present at the scene is treated as the principal perpetrator.
Rule.Section 378: Governs the basic elements of the crime of theft committed by the friend.
Rule.Section 107: Governs the general definition of abetment by instigation.

Application.Section 111: Governs directly because Illustration (b) clarifies that theft is a 'distinct act' and not a 'probable consequence' of an assault, meaning Arpan is not liable.
Application.Section 112: Fails because this section only applies once liability is proven under Section 111, which is not the case here.
Application.Section 114: Fails because Arpan was not present when his friend committed the theft.
Application.Section 378: Fails because it explains the friend's crime rather than determining the limits of Arpan's liability.
Application.Section 107: Fails because it is the initial definition and does not resolve the complex issue of secondary, unintended crimes.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 111, Illustration (b), Arpan is not liable for the theft because stealing a watch is a distinct act and was not a probable result of telling his friend to punch someone.

Question: Scenario-based: Salim borrows his neighbor's ladder for one hour to fix his roof. He instead keeps it for two months to use for his construction job, ignoring the neighbor's requests. Is this a crime if Salim always intended to return it one day?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Whoever, intending to take dishonestly any moveable property out of the possession of any person without that person's consent, moves that property in order to such taking, is said to commit theft.

Whoever dishonestly misappropriates or converts to his own use any moveable property, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both.

Whoever, being in any manner entrusted with property, or with any dominion over property, dishonestly misappropriates or converts to his own use that property, or dishonestly uses or disposes of that property in violation of any direction of law prescribing the mode in which such trust is to be discharged, or of any legal contract, express or implied, which he has made touching the discharge of such trust, or wilfully suffers any other person so to do, commits "criminal breach of trust".

Whoever, by deceiving any person, fraudulently or dishonestly induces the person so deceived to deliver any property to any person, or to consent that any person shall retain any property, or intentionally induces the person so deceived to do or omit to do anything which he would not do or omit if he were not so deceived, and which act or omission causes or is likely to cause damage or harm to that person in body, mind, reputation or property, is said to "cheat".

Whoever dishonestly receives or retains any stolen property, knowing or having reason to believe the same to be stolen property, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, or with fine, or with both.

Section Text: Whoever dishonestly misappropriates or converts to his own use any moveable property, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is a temporary dishonest use of borrowed property considered a criminal act?

Rule.Section 378: Governs theft, requiring property to be moved out of the owner's possession without their consent.
Rule.Section 403: Governs dishonest misappropriation, clarifying that even a temporary misappropriation is a crime.
Rule.Section 405: Governs criminal breach of trust, triggered when property is formally 'entrusted' to someone who then misuses it.
Rule.Section 415: Governs cheating, where the offender uses a lie to get the owner to hand over property from the start.
Rule.Section 411: Governs the receipt of property that has already been stolen by someone else.

Application.Section 378: Fails because Salim had consent to move the ladder initially; theft requires a non-consensual taking from the start.
Application.Section 403: Governs directly because Explanation 1 specifies that misappropriating property 'for a time only' (two months) is still a crime.
Application.Section 405: Fails because a neighborly loan is generally treated under misappropriation unless there was a formal business-like entrustment.
Application.Section 415: Fails because Salim borrowed it honestly and only later became dishonest; he didn't trick his neighbor to get the ladder.
Application.Section 411: Fails because Salim was the original borrower, not a third party receiving a stolen ladder from a thief.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 403, Explanation 1, Salim is guilty because the law states that dishonest misappropriation, even for a short time, is a crime.

Question: Scenario-based: In my case, the Evidence Act states that the court 'may presume' a certain document is authentic. Does this mean the judge must accept it as true, or can they still ask me to bring a witness to verify it?

Possible Sections.Full Text: In this Act the following words and expressions are used in the following senses, unless a contrary intention appears from the context:- "Court" includes all Judges and Magistrates and all persons, except arbitrators, legally authorized to take evidence. "Fact" means and includes-

**"May presume"** Whenever it is provided by this Act that the Court may presume a fact, it may either regard such fact as proved, unless and until it is disproved, or may call for proof of it: **“Shall presume”** Whenever it is directed by this Act that the Court shall presume a fact, it shall regard such fact as proved, unless and until it is disproved: **“Conclusive proof”** When one fact is declared by this Act to be conclusive proof of another, the Court shall, on proof of the one fact, regard the other as proved, and shall not allow evidence to be given for the purpose of disproving it.
Evidence may be given in any suit or proceeding of the existence or non-existence of every fact in issue and of such other fact as are hereinafter declared to be relevant, and of no others.
Admissions are not conclusive proof of the matters admitted but they may operate as estoppels under the provisions hereinafter contained.
Facts not otherwise relevant are relevant–

Section Text: **"May presume"** Whenever it is provided by this Act that the Court may presume a fact, it may either regard such fact as proved, unless and until it is disproved, or may call for proof of it: **“Shall presume”** Whenever it is directed by this Act that the Court shall presume a fact, it shall regard such fact as proved, unless and until it is disproved: **“Conclusive proof”** When one fact is declared by this Act to be conclusive proof of another, the Court shall, on proof of the one fact, regard the other as proved, and shall not allow evidence to be given for the purpose of disproving it.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Does the 'may presume' directive give the judge discretion to demand further proof?

Rule.Section 3: Defines the standard of belief required for a court to consider a fact 'proved' during a normal trial.
Rule.Section 4: Dictates that 'may presume' allows the court to either accept a fact as proved or demand formal proof of it.
Rule.Section 5: Authorizes parties to present evidence of relevant facts to the court but doesn't define presumptions.
Rule.Section 31: Governs the evidentiary weight of personal admissions made by a party.
Rule.Section 11: Allows facts that are inconsistent with an opponent's claim to be relevant.

Application.Section 3: Fails because it describes the judge's subjective weighing of final proof rather than the initial discretionary handling of a presumption.
Application.Section 4: Governs directly because 'may presume' explicitly gives the judge the choice to either accept the document or 'call for proof of it.'
Application.Section 5: Fails as it enables the giving of evidence but does not define the judicial discretion found in a 'may' presumption.
Application.Section 31: Fails as it applies to party-made admissions rather than a statutory instruction for the judge's behavior.
Application.Section 11: Fails since the issue is a procedural rule for judicial discretion rather than the logical relevancy of contradictory facts.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 4, the judge is not forced to accept the document. They have the legal choice to either presume it is true or ask you to provide witnesses as proof.

Question: Scenario-based: I found an entry in an official birth register at the District Council office. It was recorded by the registrar as part of his daily government job. Can I use this entry to prove my age?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Entries in books of account [or digital record], regularly kept in the course of business, are relevant whenever they refer to a matter into which the Court has to inquire, but such statements shall not alone be sufficient evidence to charge any person with liability.

An entry in any public or other official book, register or record [,or digital record], stating a fact in issue or relevant fact, and made by a public servant in the discharge of his official duty, or by any other person in performance of a duty specially enjoined by the law of the country in which such book, register [, record or digital record] is kept, is itself a relevant fact.

Statements of facts in issue or relevant facts, made in published [maps, charts or digital record] generally offered for public sale, or in [maps, plans or digital record] made under the authority of the Government, as to matters usually represented or stated in such [maps, charts, plans or digital record], are themselves relevant facts.

In this Act the following words and expressions are used in the following senses, unless a contrary intention appears from the context:- "Court" includes all Judges and Magistrates and all persons, except arbitrators, legally authorized to take evidence. "Fact" means and includes-

Statements, written or verbal, of relevant facts made by a person who is dead, or who cannot be found, or who has become incapable of giving evidence, or whose attendance cannot be procured without an amount of delay or expense which under the circumstances of the case appears to the Court unreasonable, are themselves relevant facts in the following cases:–

Section Text: An entry in any public or other official book, register or record [,or digital record], stating a fact in issue or relevant fact, and made by a public servant in the discharge of his official duty, or by any other person in performance of a duty specially enjoined by the law of the country in which such book, register [, record or digital record] is kept, is itself a relevant fact.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is an entry in an official registry book admissible as a relevant fact?

Rule.Section 34: Governs the relevancy of private commercial accounting ledgers.
Rule.Section 35: Makes entries in official public registers relevant if made by public servants during their duty.
Rule.Section 36: Grants relevancy to government-authorized maps and geographical plans.
Rule.Section 3: Provides the general legal definition for what constitutes a document in court.
Rule.Section 32: Governs out-of-court statements from deceased or missing persons.

Application.Section 34: Fails since a birth registry is a government record rather than a private ledger for tracking commercial debts.
Application.Section 35: Governs directly because the birth registry is an official public book and the entry was made by a public servant in official duty.
Application.Section 36: Fails as a birth record is personal data rather than a geographical chart or survey map.
Application.Section 3: Fails because identifying the register as a document does not grant inherent relevancy to its contents without Section 35.
Application.Section 32: Fails because Section 35 provides a primary rule for public records that does not depend on the specific clerk being deceased.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 35, the register entry is a relevant fact because it was made in an official public book by a public servant performing their duty.

Question: Scenario-based: Karim is accused of a murder that happened during a bank robbery. Can the prosecutor prove that a separate mob of people outside the bank was chanting for the robbers to come out?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Facts which are the occasions, cause or effect, immediate or otherwise, of relevant facts, or facts in issue, or which constitute the state of things under which they happened, or which afforded an opportunity for their occurrence or transaction, are relevant.

Any fact is relevant which shows or constitutes a motive or preparation for any fact in issue or relevant fact. The conduct of any party, or of any agent to any party, to any suit or proceeding, in reference to such suit or proceeding, or in reference to any fact in issue therein or relevant thereto, and the conduct of any person an offence against whom is the subject of any proceeding, is relevant, if such conduct influences or is influenced by any fact, in issue or relevant fact, and whether it was previous subsequent thereto.

Facts necessary to explain or introduce a fact in issue or relevant fact, or which support or rebut an inference suggested by a fact in issue or relevant fact, or which establish the identity of anything or person whose identity is relevant, or fix the time or place at which any fact in issue or relevant fact happened or which show the relation of parties by whom any such fact was transacted, are relevant in so far as they are necessary for that purpose.

Facts which, though not in issue, are so connected with a fact in issue as to form part of the same transaction, are relevant, whether they occurred at the same time and place or at different times and places.
Facts not otherwise relevant are relevant–

Section Text: Facts which, though not in issue, are so connected with a fact in issue as to form part of the same transaction, are relevant, whether they occurred at the same time and place or at different times and places. Illustration (a) A is accused of the murder of B by beating him. Whatever was said or done by A or B or the by-standers at the beating, or so shortly before or after it as to form part of the transaction, is a relevant fact.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Are spontaneous utterances by a crowd during a crime admissible as part of the transaction?

Rule.Section 7: Outlines relevancy for facts providing the cause, effect, or occasion for a fact in issue.
Rule.Section 8: Governs the relevancy of motive, preparation, and prior or subsequent conduct of the parties.
Rule.Section 9: Allows evidence that introduces context, identifies persons, or explains introductory facts.
Rule.Section 6: Makes facts that form part of the same transaction as a fact in issue legally relevant.
Rule.Section 11: Determines relevancy based on logical inconsistency or high probability of a fact's existence.

Application.Section 7: Fails because the chanting is a part of the event's atmosphere rather than a physical cause or a resultant effect.
Application.Section 8: Fails as the crowd's reaction is an involuntary statement by third parties rather than the deliberate conduct of the robbers.
Application.Section 9: Fails since the chants are primary narrative parts of the crime rather than mere introductory context for other facts.
Application.Section 6: Governs directly as the chanting happened during the robbery, making it part of the 'same transaction' (Res Gestae).
Application.Section 11: Fails because the chants do not contradict the crime but rather directly confirm its occurrence in real-time.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 6, the mob's chanting is relevant because it is so closely connected to the bank robbery that it forms part of the same transaction.

Question: Scenario-based: I found an old family tree drawn on the back of a portrait showing my great-grandfather's marriage in 1890. Can I use this tree to prove the family relationship in court today?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Statements, written or verbal, of relevant facts made by a person who is dead, or who cannot be found, or who has become incapable of giving evidence, or whose attendance cannot be procured without an amount of delay or expense which under the circumstances of the case appears to the Court unreasonable, are themselves relevant facts in the following cases:–

When the statement relates to the existence of any relationship by blood, marriage or adoption between person as to whose relationship by blood, marriage or adoption the person making the statement had special means of knowledge, and when the statement was made before the question in dispute was raised.
When the statement is contained in any deed, will or other document which relates to any such transaction as is mentioned in section 13, clause (a).

In this Act the following words and expressions are used in the following senses, unless a contrary intention appears from the context:- "Court" includes all Judges and Magistrates and all persons, except arbitrators, legally authorized to take evidence. "Fact" means and includes-

An entry in any public or other official book, register or record [,or digital record], stating a fact in issue or relevant fact, and made by a public servant in the discharge of his official duty, or by any other person in performance of a duty specially enjoined by the law of the country in which such book, register [, record or digital record] is kept, is itself a relevant fact.

Section Text: When the statement relates to the existence of any relationship by blood, marriage or adoption between persons deceased, and is made in any will or deed relating to the affairs of the family to which any such deceased person belonged, or in any family pedigree or upon any tombstone, family portrait or other thing on which such statements are usually made, and when such statement was made before the question in dispute was raised.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is a family pedigree on a portrait admissible to prove historical relationships?

Rule.Section 32: Governs out-of-court statements found on tombstones, portraits, or family pedigrees to prove relationships between the deceased.
Rule.Section 32(5): Governs verbal or written statements from relatives who had special knowledge of a relationship.
Rule.Section 32(7): Governs statements about rights found in historical deeds or wills.
Rule.Section 3: Defines what a document is in a general legal sense.
Rule.Section 35: Governs entries in official public registers kept by government employees.

Application.Section 32: Governs directly because the family tree on the back of the portrait is a pedigree recording a relationship between deceased members.
Application.Section 32(5): Fails since the evidence is a physical record found on a portrait rather than a direct statement from a specific known relative.
Application.Section 32(7): Fails as the family tree is a genealogy record rather than a deed documenting a property transaction.
Application.Section 3: Fails because simply being a 'document' does not make its family-tree contents relevant evidence without the specific genealogy rule.
Application.Section 35: Fails since a family portrait is a private artifact, not an official book recorded in a government office.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 32, Subsection (6), the family tree on the portrait is a relevant fact because it is a pedigree recording the relationship between deceased family members.

Question: Scenario-based: Abul was sued for a debt and the court officially ruled last year that he did not owe the money. Now, the same person is trying to sue Abul again for that exact same debt. Can Abul stop this?

Possible Sections.Full Text: A final judgment, order or decree of a competent Court, in the exercise of probate, matrimonial, admiralty or insolvency jurisdiction, which confers upon or takes away from any person any legal character, or which declares any person to be entitled to any such character, or to be entitled to any specific thing, not as against any specified person but absolutely, is relevant when the existence of any such legal character, or the title of any such person to any such thing, is relevant. Such judgment, order or decree is conclusive proof- that any legal character which it confers accrued at the time when such judgment, order or decree come into operation; that any legal character, to which it declares any such person to be entitled, accrued to that person at the time when such judgment, order or decree declares it to have accrued to that person; that any legal character which it takes away from any such person ceased at the time from which such judgment, order or decree declared that it had ceased or should cease; and that anything to which it declares any person to be so entitled was the property of that person at the time from which such judgment, order or decree declares that it had been or should be his property.
Facts not otherwise relevant are relevant–
Where the question is as to the existence of any right of custom, the following facts are relevant:–
Admissions are not conclusive proof of the matters admitted but they may operate as estoppels under the provisions hereinafter contained.

The existence of any judgment, order or decree which by law prevents any Court from taking cognizance of a suit or holding a trial, is a relevant fact when the question is whether such Court ought to take cognizance of such suit or to hold such trial.

Section Text: The existence of any judgment, order or decree which by law prevents any Court from taking cognizance of a suit or holding a trial, is a relevant fact when the question is whether such Court ought to take cognizance of such suit or to hold such trial.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is a previous court judgment relevant to barring a new suit on the same matter?

Rule.Section 41: Grants absolute finality to judgments concerning legal character like marriage or insolvency.
Rule.Section 11: Allows facts that are logically inconsistent with the opponent's case.
Rule.Section 13: Outlines relevancy for historical transactions that established a right.
Rule.Section 31: Dictates the weight of party admissions and the nature of estoppel.
Rule.Section 40: Establishes that a previous judgment is a relevant fact to show a court should not hear a second trial.

Application.Section 41: Fails because a simple debt dispute is a private matter, not a declaration of 'legal character' like probate or admiralty status.
Application.Section 11: Fails since the prior win is a legal bar (Res Judicata) rather than a mere piece of inconsistent factual evidence.
Application.Section 13: Fails because the issue is a resolved court case, not an ancient transaction or a village custom.
Application.Section 31: Fails as the judgment is a command of the state, not an informal admission made by one of the parties.
Application.Section 40: Governs directly because Abul's prior winning judgment is the legal instrument used to prevent the court from hearing the new case.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 40, Abul can use the previous judgment to show the court that it should not hold a second trial for the same debt.

Question: Scenario-based: Rahim is on trial for a robbery that happened in Dhaka at midnight. He has a witness ready to swear that Rahim was actually in Sylhet at that exact time attending a wedding. Which rule allows this evidence to be presented?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Facts which are the occasions, cause or effect, immediate or otherwise, of relevant facts, or facts in issue, or which constitute the state of things under which they happened, or which afforded an opportunity for their occurrence or transaction, are relevant.

Facts necessary to explain or introduce a fact in issue or relevant fact, or which support or rebut an inference suggested by a fact in issue or relevant fact, or which establish the identity of anything or person whose identity is relevant, or fix the time or place at which any fact in issue or relevant fact happened or which show the relation of parties by whom any such fact was transacted, are relevant in so far as they are necessary for that purpose.

Facts not otherwise relevant are relevant– (1) If they are inconsistent with any fact in issue or relevant fact; (2) If by themselves or in connection with other facts they make the existence or non-existence of any fact in issue or relevant fact highly probable or improbable.

Facts showing the existence of any state of mind, such as intention, knowledge, good faith, negligence, rashness, ill-will, or good-will towards any particular person, or showing the existence of any state of body or bodily feeling, are relevant, when the existence of any such state of mind or body or bodily feeling is in issue or relevant.

When there is a question whether an act was accidental or intentional, or done with a particular knowledge or intention, the fact that such act formed part of a series of similar occurrences, in each of which the person doing the act was concerned, is relevant.

Section Text: Facts not otherwise relevant are relevant– (1) If they are inconsistent with any fact in issue or relevant fact; (2) If by themselves or in connection with other facts they make the existence or non-existence of any fact in issue or relevant fact highly probable or improbable.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Does proof of being in another city during a crime qualify as relevant evidence?

Rule.Section 7: Dictates that facts providing the occasion, cause, or opportunity for a crime are relevant.
Rule.Section 9: Governs the relevancy of facts that explain or introduce key aspects or establish identities.
Rule.Section 11: Determines that facts inconsistent with a fact in issue or making its existence highly improbable are relevant.
Rule.Section 14: Outlines the relevancy of facts demonstrating a specific state of mind or bodily feeling.
Rule.Section 15: Applies to situations where a series of similar acts prove whether an act was intentional.

Application.Section 7: Fails because the alibi does not represent the occasion or opportunity of the robbery but rather its impossibility.
Application.Section 9: Fails as the evidence is used for contradiction rather than identifying parties or explaining introductory circumstances.
Application.Section 11: Governs directly because Rahim's presence in Sylhet is physically inconsistent with committing a crime in Dhaka simultaneously.
Application.Section 14: Fails since the physical location of the accused is a matter of fact, not an internal state of mind.
Application.Section 15: Fails because the scenario involves a single event's impossibility rather than a pattern of repeated occurrences.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 11, Subsection (1), Rahim may present the wedding evidence because being in Sylhet is inconsistent with the fact that he was in Dhaka during the crime.

Question: Scenario-based: A man finds a gold necklace in a park. He takes it home and, although he sees a missing person report for the necklace on a flyer, he gives it to his wife as a birthday gift. What is the correct charge?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Whoever, intending to take dishonestly any moveable property out of the possession of any person without that person's consent, moves that property in order to such taking, is said to commit theft.

Whoever dishonestly misappropriates or converts to his own use any moveable property, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both.

Whoever dishonestly receives or retains any stolen property, knowing or having reason to believe the same to be stolen property, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, or with fine, or with both.
