Whoever, being in any manner entrusted with property, or with any dominion over property, dishonestly misappropriates or converts to his own use that property, or dishonestly uses or disposes of that property in violation of any direction of law prescribing the mode in which such trust is to be discharged, or of any legal contract, express or implied, which he has made touching the discharge of such trust, or wilfully suffers any other person so to do, commits "criminal breach of trust".

Whoever, by deceiving any person, fraudulently or dishonestly induces the person so deceived to deliver any property to any person, or to consent that any person shall retain any property, or intentionally induces the person so deceived to do or omit to do anything which he would not do or omit if he were not so deceived, and which act or omission causes or is likely to cause damage or harm to that person in body, mind, reputation or property, is said to "cheat".

Section Text: Whoever dishonestly misappropriates or converts to his own use any moveable property, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is giving found property to another person as a gift considered criminal misappropriation?

Rule.Section 378: Governs theft, requiring a taking from the owner's active possession with immediate dishonest intent.
Rule.Section 403: Governs dishonest misappropriation, specifically addressing the decision to keep or give away found items once the owner is known.
Rule.Section 411: Governs the act of receiving or keeping property that someone else has already stolen.
Rule.Section 405: Governs criminal breach of trust, requiring that the property was given to the person in trust by the owner.
Rule.Section 415: Governs cheating, which requires using deception to trick an owner into handing over their property.

Application.Section 378: Fails because the necklace was lost in a park and not in the owner's active possession when the man found it.
Application.Section 403: Governs directly because Explanation 2 covers found property and punishes the decision to 'appropriate it to his own use' (including gifting it) after discovering the owner.
Application.Section 411: Fails because the necklace was lost, not previously stolen by a third party before the man picked it up.
Application.Section 405: Fails because the necklace was not 'entrusted' to the man by the owner for any specific purpose.
Application.Section 415: Fails because the man did not use any deception to get the necklace from the owner; he found it by chance.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 403, Explanation 2, the man is guilty of dishonest misappropriation because he gave the found necklace away after learning who the owner was.

Question: Scenario-based: I found an old newspaper announcement from twenty years ago where the government officially designated a specific area as a protected forest. Can I use this notification in court to prove the land's status?

Possible Sections.Full Text: An entry in any public or other official book, register or record [,or digital record], stating a fact in issue or relevant fact, and made by a public servant in the discharge of his official duty, or by any other person in performance of a duty specially enjoined by the law of the country in which such book, register [, record or digital record] is kept, is itself a relevant fact.

When the Court has to form an opinion as to the existence of any fact of a public nature, any statement of it, made in a recital contained in any Act of Parliament of the United Kingdom, or in any [Act of Parliament] or in a Government notification is a relevant fact.

Statements of facts in issue or relevant facts, made in published [maps, charts or digital record] generally offered for public sale, or in [maps, plans or digital record] made under the authority of the Government, as to matters usually represented or stated in such [maps, charts, plans or digital record], are themselves relevant facts.

When the Court has to form an opinion as to a law of any country, any statement of such law contained in a book purporting to be printed or published under the authority of the Government of such country and to contain any such law, and any report of a ruling of the Courts of such country contained in a book purporting to be a report of such rulings, is relevant.

Facts necessary to explain or introduce a fact in issue or relevant fact, or which support or rebut an inference suggested by a fact in issue or relevant fact, or which establish the identity of anything or person whose identity is relevant, or fix the time or place at which any fact in issue or relevant fact happened or which show the relation of parties by whom any such fact was transacted, are relevant in so far as they are necessary for that purpose.

Section Text: When the Court has to form an opinion as to the existence of any fact of a public nature, any statement of it, made in a recital contained in any Act of Parliament of the United Kingdom, or in any [Act of Parliament] or in a Government notification is a relevant fact.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is an official government notification relevant to proving a fact of a public nature?

Rule.Section 35: Governs the relevancy of entries in public records made by public servants.
Rule.Section 37: Relates to government notifications regarding facts of a public nature.
Rule.Section 36: Governs the relevancy of maps and charts published under government authority.
Rule.Section 38: Governs the relevancy of law books published by foreign government authority.
Rule.Section 9: Allows evidence that introduces or explains context or identifies parties.

Application.Section 35: Fails as an announcement in a Gazette is an official notice rather than a row entry in a registry book.
Application.Section 37: Governs directly because the forest designation is a 'fact of a public nature' officially stated in a government notification.
Application.Section 36: Fails since a textual announcement is not a diagrammatic survey map or chart.
Application.Section 38: Fails as the forest status is a specific administrative fact rather than a legal principle from a foreign country.
Application.Section 9: Fails because Section 37 is the specialized provision for government-issued public announcements.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 37, the Government notification is a relevant fact because it officially states a fact of a public nature.

Question: Scenario-based: Salman sold a plot of land to Abul. While Salman still owned the land, he told a neighbor that there was a hidden easement across the property. Can Abul's neighbors now use that statement against Abul in court?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Statements made by persons whose position or liability it is necessary to prove as against any party to the suit, are admissions, if such statements would be relevant as against such persons in relation to such position or liability in a suit brought by or against them, and if they are made whilst the person making them occupies such position or is subject to such liability.

Admissions are relevant and may be proved as against the person who makes them, or his representative in interest; but they can not be proved by or on behalf of the person who makes them or by his representative in interest, except in the following cases:–

Statements made by a party to the proceeding, or by an agent to any such party, whom the Court regards, under the circumstances of the case, as expressly or impliedly authorized by him to make them, are admissions. Statements made by parties to suits suing or sued in a representative character, are not admissions, unless they were made while the party making them held that character. Statements made by–
When the statement is contained in any deed, will or other document which relates to any such transaction as is mentioned in section 13, clause (a).

Statements made by a party to the proceeding, or by an agent to any such party, whom the Court regards, under the circumstances of the case, as expressly or impliedly authorized by him to make them, are admissions. Statements made by parties to suits suing or sued in a representative character, are not admissions, unless they were made while the party making them held that character. Statements made by–

Section Text: Statements made by– (2) persons from whom the parties to the suit have derived their interest in the subject-matter of the suit, interest derived are admissions, if they are made during the continuance of the interest of the persons making the statements.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Are admissions by a previous owner binding on the person who purchased the property from them?

Rule.Section 19: Governs admissions by third parties whose liability is a component of the suit.
Rule.Section 21: Governs the general rule for proving admissions against a party's representative in interest.
Rule.Section 32(3): Governs hearsay statements against proprietary interest when the person is deceased or unavailable.
Rule.Section 32(7): Governs statements about rights found in historical deeds or wills by unavailable persons.
Rule.Section 18: Governs admissions made by persons from whom a party derived their interest while that interest still existed.

Application.Section 19: Fails because Salman is a predecessor-in-title, not a third party whose liability is being established within the suit.
Application.Section 21: Fails as it provides the method of proof rather than the threshold definition of what counts as a predecessor's admission.
Application.Section 32(3): Fails since there is no evidence Salman is unavailable; Section 18 allows this as a primary admission.
Application.Section 32(7): Fails as the statement was made to a neighbor rather than being written in a formal deed or will.
Application.Section 18: Governs directly because Abul derived his land interest from Salman, and Salman made the statement while he still owned the plot.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 18, Subsection (2), Salman's statement is an admission because you derived your interest in the land from him and he spoke while he still owned it.

Question: Scenario-based: I need to prove my official date of birth for a land claim. Can I use the entry from the local government's official birth register that was filled out by a public clerk twenty years ago?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Entries in books of account [or digital record], regularly kept in the course of business, are relevant whenever they refer to a matter into which the Court has to inquire, but such statements shall not alone be sufficient evidence to charge any person with liability.

An entry in any public or other official book, register or record [,or digital record], stating a fact in issue or relevant fact, and made by a public servant in the discharge of his official duty, or by any other person in performance of a duty specially enjoined by the law of the country in which such book, register [, record or digital record] is kept, is itself a relevant fact.

Statements of facts in issue or relevant facts, made in published [maps, charts or digital record] generally offered for public sale, or in [maps, plans or digital record] made under the authority of the Government, as to matters usually represented or stated in such [maps, charts, plans or digital record], are themselves relevant facts.

When the Court has to form an opinion as to the existence of any fact of a public nature, any statement of it, made in a recital contained in any Act of Parliament of the United Kingdom, or in any [Act of Parliament] or in a Government notification is a relevant fact.

Statements, written or verbal, of relevant facts made by a person who is dead, or who cannot be found, or who has become incapable of giving evidence, or whose attendance cannot be procured without an amount of delay or expense which under the circumstances of the case appears to the Court unreasonable, are themselves relevant facts in the following cases:–

Section Text: An entry in any public or other official book, register or record [,or digital record], stating a fact in issue or relevant fact, and made by a public servant in the discharge of his official duty, or by any other person in performance of a duty specially enjoined by the law of the country in which such book, register [, record or digital record] is kept, is itself a relevant fact.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is a government birth register entry admissible to prove a person's age?

Rule.Section 34: Governs relevancy of private business accounts and ledgers.
Rule.Section 35: Makes entries in public registers relevant if made by a public servant in their official duty.
Rule.Section 36: Relates to the relevancy of maps and charts published under government authority.
Rule.Section 37: Relates to government notifications regarding facts of a public nature.
Rule.Section 32: Governs out-of-court statements made by people who are now deceased or unavailable.

Application.Section 34: Fails because a birth register is a government document, not a private business book for tracking grocery sales or debts.
Application.Section 35: Governs directly as the birth register is an official public book and the clerk is a public servant acting in official duty.
Application.Section 36: Fails since a birth register records personal history rather than geographic or territorial data found in maps.
Application.Section 37: Fails because birth records are individual facts rather than matters of general public interest announced in a Gazette.
Application.Section 32: Fails because Section 35 provides a specific rule for public records regardless of whether the specific clerk is alive or dead.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 35, the register entry is relevant because it is an official record made by a public servant while performing their duties.

Question: Scenario-based: Jamil finds a wallet on the floor of a crowded bus. He picks it up, sees the owner's ID inside, but immediately puts it in his own pocket intending to keep the cash. Has he committed theft?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Whoever, intending to take dishonestly any moveable property out of the possession of any person without that person's consent, moves that property in order to such taking, is said to commit theft.

Whoever, being in any manner entrusted with property, or with any dominion over property, dishonestly misappropriates or converts to his own use that property, or dishonestly uses or disposes of that property in violation of any direction of law prescribing the mode in which such trust is to be discharged, or of any legal contract, express or implied, which he has made touching the discharge of such trust, or wilfully suffers any other person so to do, commits "criminal breach of trust".

Whoever dishonestly receives or retains any stolen property, knowing or having reason to believe the same to be stolen property, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, or with fine, or with both.

Whoever dishonestly misappropriates or converts to his own use any moveable property, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both.

Whoever, by deceiving any person, fraudulently or dishonestly induces the person so deceived to deliver any property to any person, or to consent that any person shall retain any property, or intentionally induces the person so deceived to do or omit to do anything which he would not do or omit if he were not so deceived, and which act or omission causes or is likely to cause damage or harm to that person in body, mind, reputation or property, is said to "cheat".

Section Text: Whoever dishonestly misappropriates or converts to his own use any moveable property, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is keeping a found wallet with ID considered theft or dishonest misappropriation?

Rule.Section 378: Governs theft, requiring property to be moved out of the active possession of another person without consent.
Rule.Section 405: Governs criminal breach of trust, requiring that the property was originally entrusted to the offender by the owner.
Rule.Section 411: Governs the dishonest receipt or retention of property that has already been stolen by a third party.
Rule.Section 403: Governs dishonest misappropriation, specifically addressing property found when not in any person's immediate possession.
Rule.Section 415: Governs cheating, which requires a deceptive inducement to get an owner to hand over their property.

Application.Section 378: Fails because the wallet was already on the floor and not in the owner's active possession when Jamil moved it.
Application.Section 405: Fails because the owner did not voluntarily hand over or entrust the wallet to Jamil for any purpose.
Application.Section 411: Fails because the property was lost by the owner and found by Jamil, rather than being previously stolen by someone else.
Application.Section 403: Governs directly because Explanation 2 clarifies that keeping found property when the owner is identifiable via ID constitutes dishonest misappropriation.
Application.Section 415: Fails because Jamil did not use any trick or deception to acquire the wallet; he simply found it by chance.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 403, Explanation 2, Jamil has committed dishonest misappropriation because he kept found property despite knowing who the owner was from the ID.

Question: Scenario-based: To prove that Salman was poisoned, the doctor wants to testify about the specific descriptions of stomach pain and nausea Salman gave while lying in the hospital bed. Which section allows these statements?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Facts which are the occasions, cause or effect, immediate or otherwise, of relevant facts, or facts in issue, or which constitute the state of things under which they happened, or which afforded an opportunity for their occurrence or transaction, are relevant.

Any fact is relevant which shows or constitutes a motive or preparation for any fact in issue or relevant fact. The conduct of any party, or of any agent to any party, to any suit or proceeding, in reference to such suit or proceeding, or in reference to any fact in issue therein or relevant thereto, and the conduct of any person an offence against whom is the subject of any proceeding, is relevant, if such conduct influences or is influenced by any fact, in issue or relevant fact, and whether it was previous subsequent thereto.

When the statement is made by person as to the cause of his death, or as to any of the circumstances of the transaction which resulted in his death, in cases in which the cause of that person's death comes into question.

Facts showing the existence of any state of mind, such as intention, knowledge, good faith, negligence, rashness, ill-will, or good-will towards any particular person, or showing the existence of any state of body or bodily feeling, are relevant, when the existence of any such state of mind or body or bodily feeling is in issue or relevant.

Facts showing the existence of any state of mind, such as intention, knowledge, good faith, negligence, rashness, ill-will, or good-will towards any particular person, or showing the existence of any state of body or bodily feeling, are relevant, when the existence of any such state of mind or body or bodily feeling is in issue or relevant.

Section Text: Facts showing the existence of any state of mind, such as intention, knowledge, good faith, negligence, rashness, ill-will, or good-will towards any particular person, or showing the existence of any state of body or bodily feeling, are relevant, when the existence of any such state of mind or body or bodily feeling is in issue or relevant.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Are statements describing a person's current bodily feelings relevant to prove their physical state?

Rule.Section 7: Governs facts that are the cause or effect of an issue.
Rule.Section 8: Governs motive, preparation, and the physical conduct of parties.
Rule.Section 32(1): Governs dying declarations regarding the cause or circumstances of death.
Rule.Section 32(2): Governs out-of-court statements made in the ordinary course of professional business.
Rule.Section 14: Governs the relevancy of facts showing a specific state of body or bodily feeling.

Application.Section 7: Fails because the patient's verbal report is a communicative fact rather than a physical effect like a bruise.
Application.Section 8: Fails since Salman's description of pain is a statement rather than physical conduct influenced by the crime.
Application.Section 32(1): Fails because the scenario doesn't establish that Salman actually died or was speaking about the cause of his death.
Application.Section 32(2): Fails as the patient is not speaking in the course of his own business or professional duty.
Application.Section 14: Governs directly because Salman's report of pain is a statement of bodily feeling relevant to proving the poisoning.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 14, Illustration (l), the doctor can testify about Salman's statements because they describe his bodily feelings at the time of the illness.

Question: Scenario-based: After a fight in a park, the police found scuff marks and deep footprints in the mud where the struggle happened. Can these marks be used in court to prove a fight took place?

Possible Sections.Full Text: In this Act the following words and expressions are used in the following senses, unless a contrary intention appears from the context:- "Court" includes all Judges and Magistrates and all persons, except arbitrators, legally authorized to take evidence. "Fact" means and includes-

Facts which are the occasions, cause or effect, immediate or otherwise, of relevant facts, or facts in issue, or which constitute the state of things under which they happened, or which afforded an opportunity for their occurrence or transaction, are relevant.

Facts necessary to explain or introduce a fact in issue or relevant fact, or which support or rebut an inference suggested by a fact in issue or relevant fact, or which establish the identity of anything or person whose identity is relevant, or fix the time or place at which any fact in issue or relevant fact happened or which show the relation of parties by whom any such fact was transacted, are relevant in so far as they are necessary for that purpose.

Any fact is relevant which shows or constitutes a motive or preparation for any fact in issue or relevant fact. The conduct of any party, or of any agent to any party, to any suit or proceeding, in reference to such suit or proceeding, or in reference to any fact in issue therein or relevant thereto, and the conduct of any person an offence against whom is the subject of any proceeding, is relevant, if such conduct influences or is influenced by any fact, in issue or relevant fact, and whether it was previous subsequent thereto.
Facts not otherwise relevant are relevant–

Section Text: Facts which are the occasions, cause or effect, immediate or otherwise, of relevant facts, or facts in issue, or which constitute the state of things under which they happened, or which afforded an opportunity for their occurrence or transaction, are relevant. Illustration (b) The question is, whether A murdered B. Marks on the ground, produced by a struggle at or near the place where the murder was committed, are relevant facts.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Are physical effects left at a scene, such as struggle marks, considered relevant evidence?

Rule.Section 3: Provides the general definition of what qualifies as a 'fact' in the legal sense.
Rule.Section 7: Dictates that facts which are the immediate effects of a fact in issue are legally relevant.
Rule.Section 9: Allows evidence required to fix the time and place of an event or introduce context.
Rule.Section 8: Relates to motive, preparation, and the conduct of parties after an event.
Rule.Section 11: Determines relevancy based on the high probability or logical inconsistency of facts.

Application.Section 3: Fails since defining the marks as a 'fact' does not establish the legal rule for why they are relevant to the trial.
Application.Section 7: Governs directly because the scuff marks and footprints are direct physical 'effects' of the struggle, explicitly mentioned in Illustration (b).
Application.Section 9: Fails since the marks are primary results of the crime rather than information needed to introduce other facts.
Application.Section 8: Fails as ground marks are inanimate results of a fight rather than the deliberate subsequent conduct of a suspect.
Application.Section 11: Fails because the issue is the physical trace of an event rather than the logical probability of its occurrence.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 7, Illustration (b), the marks in the mud are relevant because they are physical effects of the struggle that occurred in the park.

Question: Scenario-based: A man is accused of hitting a neighbor with a stick. Witnesses say they heard the neighbor scream 'Don't kill me!' as the man was raising the stick. Can this scream be used in court?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Facts which are the occasions, cause or effect, immediate or otherwise, of relevant facts, or facts in issue, or which constitute the state of things under which they happened, or which afforded an opportunity for their occurrence or transaction, are relevant.

Any fact is relevant which shows or constitutes a motive or preparation for any fact in issue or relevant fact. The conduct of any party, or of any agent to any party, to any suit or proceeding, in reference to such suit or proceeding, or in reference to any fact in issue therein or relevant thereto, and the conduct of any person an offence against whom is the subject of any proceeding, is relevant, if such conduct influences or is influenced by any fact, in issue or relevant fact, and whether it was previous subsequent thereto.

Facts necessary to explain or introduce a fact in issue or relevant fact, or which support or rebut an inference suggested by a fact in issue or relevant fact, or which establish the identity of anything or person whose identity is relevant, or fix the time or place at which any fact in issue or relevant fact happened or which show the relation of parties by whom any such fact was transacted, are relevant in so far as they are necessary for that purpose.

Facts which, though not in issue, are so connected with a fact in issue as to form part of the same transaction, are relevant, whether they occurred at the same time and place or at different times and places.
Facts not otherwise relevant are relevant–

Section Text: Facts which, though not in issue, are so connected with a fact in issue as to form part of the same transaction, are relevant, whether they occurred at the same time and place or at different times and places. Illustration (a) A is accused of the murder of B by beating him. Whatever was said or done by A or B or the by-standers at the beating, or so shortly before or after it as to form part of the transaction, is a relevant fact.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Are spontaneous utterances made during a criminal act admissible as part of the transaction?

Rule.Section 7: Outlines relevancy for the cause, effect, or occasion of a fact in issue.
Rule.Section 8: Governs the relevancy of motive, preparation, and prior or subsequent conduct.
Rule.Section 9: Allows facts that introduce or explain context, identities, or the time and place of events.
Rule.Section 10: None (Typo in rule object check). Section 6: Makes facts that form part of the same transaction as a fact in issue relevant.
Rule.Section 11: Determines relevancy based on inconsistency or the probability of a fact's existence.

Application.Section 7: Fails because the scream is a part of the beating itself rather than an prior occasion or a resultant effect like a wound.
Application.Section 8: Fails as the neighbor's scream is an involuntary reaction during the crime, not a deliberate motive or subsequent conduct of the accused.
Application.Section 9: Fails since the scream is a primary part of the event's narrative rather than a piece of introductory or identifying context.
Application.Section 6: Governs directly as the scream happened during the beating, making it part of the 'same transaction' (Res Gestae).
Application.Section 11: Fails because the scream doesn't contradict the crime but rather directly confirms its occurrence in real-time.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 6, the scream is relevant because it is so closely connected to the beating that it forms part of the same transaction.

Question: Scenario-based: Salim finds his neighbor's cow eating his expensive imported flowers. In a fit of rage, he stabs and kills the cow. The cow is worth 500 Taka. What is the correct charge?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Whoever, with intent to cause, or knowing that he is likely to cause, wrongful loss or damage to the public or to any person, causes the destruction of any property, or any such change in any property or in the situation thereof as destroys or diminishes its value or utility, or affects it injuriously, commits "mischief".

Whoever commits mischief by killing, poisoning, maiming or rendering useless any animal or animals of the value of ten taka or upwards, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both.

Whoever commits mischief by killing, poisoning, maiming or rendering useless, any elephant, camel, horse, mule, buffalo, bull, cow or ox, whatever may be the value thereof, or any other animal of the value of fifty taka or upwards, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to five years, or with fine, or with both.
Whoever, intending to take dishonestly any moveable property out of the possession of any person without that person's consent, moves that property in order to such taking, is said to commit theft.

If the offence, the committing of which, or the attempting to commit which, occasions the exercise of the right of private defence, be theft, mischief, or criminal trespass, not of any of the descriptions enumerated in the last preceding section, that right does not extend to the voluntary causing of death, but does extend, subject to the restrictions mentioned in section 99, to the voluntary causing to the wrong-doer of any harm other than death.

Section Text: Whoever commits mischief by killing, poisoning, maiming or rendering useless, any elephant, camel, horse, mule, buffalo, bull, cow or ox, whatever may be the value thereof, or any other animal of the value of fifty taka or upwards, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to five years, or with fine, or with both.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is killing a neighbor's cow considered theft or a specific, aggravated type of mischief?

Rule.Section 425: Governs the general definition of mischief involving destruction of property to cause loss.
Rule.Section 428: Governs mischief involving the killing of any animal worth ten Taka or more with a two-year sentence.
Rule.Section 429: Governs mischief involving the killing of valuable animals like cows (regardless of value) or any animal worth 50+ Taka.
Rule.Section 378: Governs theft, requiring a dishonest intent to take and move property for personal gain.
Rule.Section 104: Governs the limitations on using deadly force to defend property against mischief.

Application.Section 425: Fails because it is the general definition, and the law has a more specific and harsher rule for the killing of valuable livestock.
Application.Section 428: Fails because cows are specifically listed in Section 429 as triggering a higher five-year punishment level.
Application.Section 429: Governs directly because killing a cow is a specific category of mischief that carries a maximum five-year prison term.
Application.Section 378: Fails because Salim killed the cow out of anger to cause loss to the neighbor, not to steal it for his own profit.
Application.Section 104: Fails because it defines the limits of defense rather than classifying the specific crime Salim committed by killing the animal.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 429, Salim is guilty of mischief because he intentionally killed a cow, which is a specific animal protected by this five-year penalty section.

Question: Scenario-based: I want to prove my sister's marriage date for an insurance claim. I have an old Bible where my father, who died ten years ago, wrote down the names and wedding dates of all his children. Is this admissible?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Statements, written or verbal, of relevant facts made by a person who is dead, or who cannot be found, or who has become incapable of giving evidence, or whose attendance cannot be procured without an amount of delay or expense which under the circumstances of the case appears to the Court unreasonable, are themselves relevant facts in the following cases:–

In this Act the following words and expressions are used in the following senses, unless a contrary intention appears from the context:- "Court" includes all Judges and Magistrates and all persons, except arbitrators, legally authorized to take evidence. "Fact" means and includes-
Where the question is as to the existence of any right of custom, the following facts are relevant:–

An entry in any public or other official book, register or record [,or digital record], stating a fact in issue or relevant fact, and made by a public servant in the discharge of his official duty, or by any other person in performance of a duty specially enjoined by the law of the country in which such book, register [, record or digital record] is kept, is itself a relevant fact.

When the statement relates to the existence of any relationship by blood, marriage or adoption between person as to whose relationship by blood, marriage or adoption the person making the statement had special means of knowledge, and when the statement was made before the question in dispute was raised.

Section Text: When the statement relates to the existence of any relationship by blood, marriage or adoption between persons deceased, and is made in any will or deed relating to the affairs of the family to which any such deceased person belonged, or in any family pedigree or upon any tombstone, family portrait or other thing on which such statements are usually made, and when such statement was made before the question in dispute was raised.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is a family record written in a Bible admissible to prove a marriage date?

Rule.Section 32: Governs out-of-court statements found on tombstones, family pedigrees, or portraits to prove relationships between the deceased.
Rule.Section 3: Defines what qualifies as a document but does not grant inherent relevancy to its written contents.
Rule.Section 13: Relates to historical usage and transactions used to prove a communal custom or right.
Rule.Section 35: Governs official government records created by public servants performing their administrative duties.
Rule.Section 32(5): Governs verbal or written statements from relatives who had special knowledge of family ties.

Application.Section 32: Governs directly because the Bible entries constitute a 'family pedigree' recording relationships, created before the current dispute began.
Application.Section 3: Fails as identifying the Bible as a 'document' does not override the standard hearsay exclusion for the facts written inside it.
Application.Section 13: Fails since a family wedding is a matter of kinship history rather than a traditional community custom or right.
Application.Section 35: Fails as a private family Bible is not an official book recorded in a government registry office.
Application.Section 32(5): Fails because Section 32(6) is the more specific rule for records found in family artifacts like books and portraits.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 32, Subsection (6), the Bible entry is a relevant fact because it is a family pedigree recording a relationship made before the dispute arose.

Question: Scenario-based: Salman is in police custody. He tells an officer, 'I sold the stolen gold to the jeweler on Hospital Road.' If the police go there and find the gold, what part of Salman's statement can be used in court?

Possible Sections.Full Text: No confession made to a police-officer shall be proved as against a person accused of any offence.
No confession made by any person whilst he is in the custody of a police-officer, unless it be made in the immediate presence of a Magistrate, shall be proved as against such person.

Provided that, when any fact is deposed to as discovered in consequence of information received from a person accused of any offence, in the custody of a police officer, so much of such information, whether it amounts to a confession or not, as relates distinctly to the fact thereby discovered, may be proved.

A confession made by an accused person is irrelevant in a criminal proceeding, if the making of the confession appears to the Court to have been caused by any inducement, threat or promise having reference to the charge against the accused person, proceeding from a person in authority and sufficient, in the opinion of the Court, to give the accused person grounds which would appear to him reasonable for supposing that by making it he would gain any advantage or avoid any evil of a temporal nature in reference to the proceedings against him.

If such a confession is otherwise relevant, it does not become irrelevant merely because it was made under a promise of secrecy, or in consequence of a deception practised on the accused person for the purpose of obtaining it, or when he was drunk, or because it was made in answer to questions which he need not have answered, whatever may have been the form of those questions, or because he was not warned that he was not bound to make such confession, and that evidence of it might be given against him.

Section Text: Provided that, when any fact is deposed to as discovered in consequence of information received from a person accused of any offence, in the custody of a police officer, so much of such information, whether it amounts to a confession or not, as relates distinctly to the fact thereby discovered, may be proved.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is a confession in custody admissible if it leads to the discovery of physical evidence?

Rule.Section 25: Bars all confessions made directly to police officers from being proved against an accused.
Rule.Section 26: Bars confessions made in custody unless a Magistrate is present during the statement.
Rule.Section 27: Allows for the proof of information from an accused in custody only so far as it relates distinctly to a discovered fact.
Rule.Section 24: Excludes confessions obtained through inducements, promises, or threats from authorities.
Rule.Section 29: States that a relevant confession remains admissible even if obtained through deception.

Application.Section 25: Fails because Section 27 acts as a specific exception that permits discovery-related statements to be proved.
Application.Section 26: Fails since Section 27 overrides the custody bar for information leading to a physical discovery.
Application.Section 27: Governs directly because the jeweler's location led to finding the gold, allowing the part of the statement identifying the shop to be proved.
Application.Section 24: Fails because there is no mention of a threat or promise used to extract the location of the gold.
Application.Section 29: Fails as the admissibility depends on the physical discovery rather than a lack of warning or deception.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 27, the part of the statement about the jeweler's location is allowed because it led directly to the discovery of the stolen gold.

Question: Scenario-based: Jamil raises his fist at Salim and shouts, 'I am going to punch your face right now!' He hasn't touched Salim yet, but Salim is terrified that he is about to be hit. Has Jamil committed a crime?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Whoever intentionally uses force to any person, without that person's consent, in order to the committing of any offence, or intending by the use of such force to cause, or knowing it to be likely that by the use of such force he will cause injury, fear or annoyance to the person to whom the force is used, is said to use criminal force to that other.

Whoever makes any gesture, or any preparation intending or knowing it to be likely that such gesture or preparation will cause any person present to apprehend that he who makes that gesture or preparation is about to use criminal force to that person, is said to commit an assault.

Whoever assaults or uses criminal force to any person otherwise than on grave and sudden provocation given by that person, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three months, or with fine which may extend to five hundred taka, or with both.

Whoever threatens another with any injury to his person, reputation or property, or to the person or reputation of any one in whom that person is interested, with intent to cause to alarm to that person, or to cause that person to do any act which he is not legally bound to do, or to omit to do any act which that person is legally entitled to do, as the means of avoiding the execution of such threat, commits criminal intimidation.

Whoever intentionally insults, and thereby gives provocation to any person, intending or knowing it to be likely that such provocation will cause him to break the public peace, or to commit any other offence, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both.

Section Text: Whoever makes any gesture, or any preparation intending or knowing it to be likely that such gesture or preparation will cause any person present to apprehend that he who makes that gesture or preparation is about to use criminal force to that person, is said to commit an assault.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Does a gesture that creates a fear of immediate physical force constitute the offense of assault?

Rule.Section 350: Governs the actual application of physical force to another person's body.
Rule.Section 351: Defines assault as a 'gesture or preparation' that makes a person fear 'immediate' criminal force.
Rule.Section 352: Sets out the punishment for the crimes of assault or criminal force.
Rule.Section 503: Governs criminal intimidation, which involves threats of 'future' injury to cause general alarm.
Rule.Section 504: Governs insults intended to provoke a person into starting a fight or breaking the peace.

Application.Section 350: Fails because Jamil did not actually touch or use physical power against Salim's body yet.
Application.Section 351: Governs directly because raising a fist is a 'gesture' that caused Salim to 'apprehend' (fear) that force was 'about' to be used.
Application.Section 352: Fails because it provides the penalty for the act rather than defining the legal category Jamil's gesture falls into.
Application.Section 503: Fails because Jamil's threat was of 'immediate' force (assault) rather than a threat of 'future' injury meant to cause long-term alarm.
Application.Section 504: Fails because the primary issue is the fear of being hit (assault) rather than the offensiveness of Jamil's insults.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 351, Jamil has committed an assault because he made a gesture that caused Salim to fear that immediate force was about to be used against him.

Question: Scenario-based: I found my neighbor's lost wallet. I am now accused of stealing it. To prove I acted in good faith, can I show the court a copy of the public notice I posted in the local market trying to find the owner?

Possible Sections.Full Text: When there is a question whether an act was accidental or intentional, or done with a particular knowledge or intention, the fact that such act formed part of a series of similar occurrences, in each of which the person doing the act was concerned, is relevant.

Any fact is relevant which shows or constitutes a motive or preparation for any fact in issue or relevant fact. The conduct of any party, or of any agent to any party, to any suit or proceeding, in reference to such suit or proceeding, or in reference to any fact in issue therein or relevant thereto, and the conduct of any person an offence against whom is the subject of any proceeding, is relevant, if such conduct influences or is influenced by any fact, in issue or relevant fact, and whether it was previous subsequent thereto.

Facts showing the existence of any state of mind, such as intention, knowledge, good faith, negligence, rashness, ill-will, or good-will towards any particular person, or showing the existence of any state of body or bodily feeling, are relevant, when the existence of any such state of mind or body or bodily feeling is in issue or relevant.

Admissions are relevant and may be proved as against the person who makes them, or his representative in interest; but they can not be proved by or on behalf of the person who makes them or by his representative in interest, except in the following cases:–

Facts which are the occasions, cause or effect, immediate or otherwise, of relevant facts, or facts in issue, or which constitute the state of things under which they happened, or which afforded an opportunity for their occurrence or transaction, are relevant.

Section Text: Facts showing the existence of any state of mind, such as intention, knowledge, good faith, negligence, rashness, ill-will, or good-will towards any particular person, or showing the existence of any state of body or bodily feeling, are relevant, when the existence of any such state of mind or body or bodily feeling is in issue or relevant.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is a public notice relevant to proving a person's good faith or knowledge regarding property?

Rule.Section 15: Governs patterns of similar occurrences to prove intent or accident.
Rule.Section 8: Governs motive, preparation, and prior or subsequent conduct.
Rule.Section 14: Specifies that facts demonstrating state of mind like 'good faith' or 'knowledge' are relevant.
Rule.Section 21: Governs when a party can prove their own admissions or statements about their state of mind.
Rule.Section 7: Outlines relevancy for the occasion or opportunity of an event.

Application.Section 15: Fails because there is no series of lost wallets mentioned, only a single event requiring a check of good faith.
Application.Section 8: Fails as the notice is evidence of mental state rather than a physical preparation for the act of misappropriation.
Application.Section 14: Governs directly as the public notice provides evidence for the client's 'good faith' regarding the wallet's owner.
Application.Section 21: Fails because the notice is a physical fact offered for relevancy under Section 14, rather than a verbal 'admission' being proved for Rahim's benefit.
Application.Section 7: Fails since the notice was created after the wallet was found, rather than providing the 'occasion' for finding it.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 14, Illustration (h), the public notice is relevant because it provides evidence of your good faith regarding the real owner of the wallet.

Question: Scenario-based: A husband and wife signed a property agreement while living in Japan. Now they are suing each other in a Dhaka court. Which country's time limit rules must the court use?

Possible Sections.Full Text: This Act may be called the Limitation Act, 1908. It extends to the whole of Bangladesh.

In computing the period of limitation prescribed for any suit, the time during which the defendant has been absent from Bangladesh and from the territories beyond Bangladesh under the administration of the Government shall be excluded.

Nothing in this Act shall affect section 25 of the Contract Act, 1872. Where any special law prescribes for any suit, appeal or application a period of limitation different from the period prescribed therefore by the first schedule, the provision of section 3 shall apply, as if such period were prescribed therefor in that schedule, and for the purpose of determining any period of limitation prescribed for any suit, appeal or application by any special law- the provisions contained in section 4, sections 9 to 18, and section 22 shall apply only in so far as, and to the extent to which, they are not expressly excluded by such special law; and the remaining provisions of this Act shall not apply. Nothing in this Act shall apply to suits under the Divorce Act. Sections 26 and 27 and the definition of “easement” in section 2 shall not apply to cases arising in territories to which the Easements Act, 1882, may for the time being extend.

Subject to the provisions contained in sections 4 to 25 (inclusive), every suit instituted, appeal preferred, and application made, after the period of limitation prescribed therefor by the first schedule shall be dismissed, although limitation has not been set up as a defence.
Suits instituted in Bangladesh on contracts entered into in a foreign country are subject to the rules of limitation contained in this Act.

Section Text: Suits instituted in Bangladesh on contracts entered into in a foreign country are subject to the rules of limitation contained in this Act.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Which jurisdiction's limitation laws apply to a foreign contract being litigated in Bangladesh?

Rule.Section 1: Defines the territorial reach of the Act as covering the entire geographic area of Bangladesh.
Rule.Section 13: Excludes the time a person being sued spends outside the country from the total limitation period.
Rule.Section 29: Explicitly excludes cases brought under the Divorce Act from the scope of the Limitation Act's rules.
Rule.Section 3: Mandates that all lawsuits filed in Bangladesh after the local deadline has expired must be dismissed automatically.
Rule.Section 11: Establishes that suits filed in Bangladesh on foreign contracts must follow the limitation rules of the Bangladesh Act.

Application.Section 1: Fails because it describes general geography but doesn't solve the specific conflict between local and foreign contract rules.
Application.Section 13: Fails as the question is about which set of rules to use, not about deducting days for being out of the country.
Application.Section 29: Fails because the scenario describes a suit on a 'property agreement' (a contract) rather than a formal divorce petition.
Application.Section 3: Fails because it provides the penalty for lateness but doesn't clarify which country's law defines what counts as 'late'.
Application.Section 11: Governs directly because it explicitly addresses 'contracts entered into in a foreign country' and applies local Bangladesh limitation rules.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Limitation Act, 1908, section 11, Subsection (1), the court in Dhaka must apply the limitation rules of Bangladesh, regardless of where the contract was signed.

Question: Scenario-based: I found an old metal plaque on a property wall that says 'Property of the Village Council, 1920.' Can I use this plaque in court as a 'document' to prove who owns the land?

Possible Sections.Full Text: An entry in any public or other official book, register or record [,or digital record], stating a fact in issue or relevant fact, and made by a public servant in the discharge of his official duty, or by any other person in performance of a duty specially enjoined by the law of the country in which such book, register [, record or digital record] is kept, is itself a relevant fact.

In this Act the following words and expressions are used in the following senses, unless a contrary intention appears from the context:- "Court" includes all Judges and Magistrates and all persons, except arbitrators, legally authorized to take evidence. "Fact" means and includes-

Entries in books of account [or digital record], regularly kept in the course of business, are relevant whenever they refer to a matter into which the Court has to inquire, but such statements shall not alone be sufficient evidence to charge any person with liability.

Statements, written or verbal, of relevant facts made by a person who is dead, or who cannot be found, or who has become incapable of giving evidence, or whose attendance cannot be procured without an amount of delay or expense which under the circumstances of the case appears to the Court unreasonable, are themselves relevant facts in the following cases:–

Statements of facts in issue or relevant facts, made in published [maps, charts or digital record] generally offered for public sale, or in [maps, plans or digital record] made under the authority of the Government, as to matters usually represented or stated in such [maps, charts, plans or digital record], are themselves relevant facts.

Section Text: "Document" means any matter expressed or described upon any substance by means of letters, figures or marks, or by more than one of those means, intended to be used, or which may be used, for the purpose of recording that matter [and includes any digital record]. A writing is a document: Words printed, lithographed or photographed are documents: A map or plan is a document: An inscription on a metal plate or stone is a document: A caricature is a document.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is an inscription on a metal plate legally defined as a 'document'?

Rule.Section 35: Governs the relevancy of official public register entries made by public servants.
Rule.Section 3: Defines documents to include any matter on any substance, specifically including metal plate inscriptions.
Rule.Section 34: Governs the relevancy and sufficiency of business account entries.
Rule.Section 32: Governs out-of-court family statements found on tombstones or portraits.
Rule.Section 36: Relates to the relevancy of government-authorized maps and plans.

Application.Section 35: Fails because an inscription on a wall is not a formal 'book' or 'register' kept by a government clerk.
Application.Section 3: Governs directly because the plaque is an inscription on a metal plate, which the definition explicitly labels as a document.
Application.Section 34: Fails since a metal plaque is not a commercial accounting ledger kept for business transactions.
Application.Section 32: Fails as the plaque concerns property ownership rather than family relationships or lineages.
Application.Section 36: Fails because a commemorative or boundary plaque is not a published chart or plan offered for public sale.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 3, the metal plaque is legally considered a 'document.' The law specifically includes inscriptions on metal plates in its definition of documentary evidence.

Question: Scenario-based: Salim dumps a bucket of laundry chemicals into a public village well that serves as the primary drinking water source for the community. What is the maximum time he can be jailed for this act?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Whoever voluntarily corrupts or fouls the water of any public spring or reservoir, so as to render it less fit for the purpose for which it is ordinarily used, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three months, or with fine which may extend to five hundred taka or with both.

Whoever voluntarily vitiates the atmosphere in any place so as to make it noxious to the health of persons in general dwelling or carrying on business in the neighborhood or passing along a public way, shall be punished with fine which may extend to five hundred taka.

Whoever, with intent to cause, or knowing that he is likely to cause, wrongful loss or damage to the public or to any person, causes the destruction of any property, or any such change in any property or in the situation thereof as destroys or diminishes its value or utility, or affects it injuriously, commits "mischief".

A person is guilty of a public nuisance who does any act or is guilty of an illegal omission which causes any common injury, danger or annoyance to the public or to the people in general who dwell or occupy property in the vicinity, or which must necessarily cause injury, obstruction, dangers or annoyance to persons who may have occasion to use any public right. A common nuisance is not excused on the ground that it causes some convenience or advantage.

Whoever, by doing any act, or by omitting to take order with any property in his possession or under his charge, causes danger, obstruction or injury to any person in any public way or public line of navigation, shall be punished with fine which may extend to two hundred taka.

Section Text: Whoever voluntarily corrupts or fouls the water of any public spring or reservoir, so as to render it less fit for the purpose for which it is ordinarily used, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three months, or with fine which may extend to five hundred taka, or with both.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: What is the penalty for polluting a public drinking water source?

Rule.Section 277: Governs the specific act of fouling or corrupting a public spring or reservoir, carrying a three-month jail limit.
Rule.Section 278: Governs the pollution of the air or 'atmosphere' to make it harmful to health.
Rule.Section 425: Governs general mischief involving the destruction of property to cause financial loss.
Rule.Section 268: Governs the general definition of a public nuisance without specifying a specialized punishment for water.
Rule.Section 283: Governs physical obstructions or dangers specifically on public roads or navigation routes.

Application.Section 277: Governs directly because the village well is a public reservoir and dumping chemicals into it rendered it unfit for its ordinary drinking purpose.
Application.Section 278: Fails because the pollution occurred in the water supply rather than the air or atmosphere.
Application.Section 425: Fails because the act is a public health crime specifically regulated by the public safety chapter rather than simple property damage.
Application.Section 268: Fails because Section 277 is the specific provision within the Code meant for water pollution, carrying its own penalty.
Application.Section 283: Fails because a village well is a static resource rather than a 'public way' used for transit or travel.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 277, Salim can be punished with a maximum of three months in prison for fouling the community's primary water source.

Question: Scenario-based: A female suspect has been arrested and needs to be searched for evidence. There are only male officers at the station. Can one of them perform the search if he is respectful?

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 46
Possible Sections.Full Text: In making an arrest the police-officer or other person making the same shall actually touch or confine the body of the person to be arrested, unless there be a submission to the custody by word or action.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 50
Possible Sections.Full Text: The person arrested shall not be subjected to more restraint than is necessary to prevent his escape.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 51
Possible Sections.Full Text: Whenever a person is arrested by a police-officer under a warrant which does not provide for the taking of bail, or under a warrant which provides for the taking of bail but the person arrested cannot furnish bail, and Whenever a person is arrested without warrant, or by a private person under a warrant, and cannot legally be admitted to bail, or is unable to furnish bail, the officer making the arrest or, when the arrest is made by a private person, the police-officer to whom he makes over the person arrested, may search such person, and place in safe custody all articles, other than necessary wearing-apparel, found upon him; and where any article is seized from the arrested person, the officer shall prepare a list in the presence of a witness and obtain his signature, if practicable, and shall serve a copy thereof to the arrested person or to any person nominated by him].

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 52
Possible Sections.Full Text: Whenever it is necessary to cause a woman to be searched, the search shall be made by another woman, with strict regard to decency.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 53
Possible Sections.Full Text: The officer or other person making any arrest under this Code may take from the person arrested any offensive weapons which he has about his person, and shall deliver all weapons so taken to the Court or officer before which or whom the officer or person making the arrest is required by this Code to produce the person arrested.

Relevant Section: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 52
Section Text: Whenever it is necessary to cause a woman to be searched, the search shall be made by another woman, with strict regard to decency.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: May a male officer legally conduct a search of a female suspect's person?

Rule.Section 46: Governs the physical methodology and necessary force allowed when taking an individual into legal custody.
Rule.Section 50: Prohibits the use of excessive physical restraint beyond what is required to prevent a suspect's flight.
Rule.Section 51: Grants the general authority for police to search an arrested person and inventory their personal items.
Rule.Section 52: Mandates that searches of women must be conducted exclusively by other women to ensure privacy.
Rule.Section 53: Authorizes an arresting officer to seize and deliver offensive weapons found on the person of the accused.

Application.Section 46: Fails because it defines the physical start of an arrest rather than the rules for subsequent personal searches.
Application.Section 50: Fails because the legal problem relates to the identity of the officer rather than the intensity of force.
Application.Section 51: Fails because it provides the general power to search but omits the mandatory gender-based privacy exceptions.
Application.Section 52: Directly and exclusively governs because its specific gender requirement overrides general police powers, strictly banning males from searching females.
Application.Section 53: Fails because it concerns the seizure of weapons rather than the procedural requirements for general evidence searches.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, section 52, a male officer is strictly prohibited from searching her; the law requires that any search of a woman must be done only by another woman.

Cited Acts and Sections: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 52

Question: Scenario-based: I need to prove my official date of birth for a passport application. Can I use the entry in my local government's official birth registry that was filled out by a clerk fifteen years ago?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Entries in books of account [or digital record], regularly kept in the course of business, are relevant whenever they refer to a matter into which the Court has to inquire, but such statements shall not alone be sufficient evidence to charge any person with liability.

Statements, written or verbal, of relevant facts made by a person who is dead, or who cannot be found, or who has become incapable of giving evidence, or whose attendance cannot be procured without an amount of delay or expense which under the circumstances of the case appears to the Court unreasonable, are themselves relevant facts in the following cases:–

An entry in any public or other official book, register or record [,or digital record], stating a fact in issue or relevant fact, and made by a public servant in the discharge of his official duty, or by any other person in performance of a duty specially enjoined by the law of the country in which such book, register [, record or digital record] is kept, is itself a relevant fact.

Statements of facts in issue or relevant facts, made in published [maps, charts or digital record] generally offered for public sale, or in [maps, plans or digital record] made under the authority of the Government, as to matters usually represented or stated in such [maps, charts, plans or digital record], are themselves relevant facts.

In this Act the following words and expressions are used in the following senses, unless a contrary intention appears from the context:- "Court" includes all Judges and Magistrates and all persons, except arbitrators, legally authorized to take evidence. "Fact" means and includes-

Section Text: An entry in any public or other official book, register or record [,or digital record], stating a fact in issue or relevant fact, and made by a public servant in the discharge of his official duty, or by any other person in performance of a duty specially enjoined by the law of the country in which such book, register [, record or digital record] is kept, is itself a relevant fact.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is an entry in an official government registry admissible as a relevant fact?

Rule.Section 34: Governs private commercial accounting ledgers and their limited evidentiary weight.
Rule.Section 32: Outlines hearsay exceptions for business statements from persons who are deceased or unavailable.
Rule.Section 35: Makes entries in official public registers relevant if made by public servants in their line of duty.
Rule.Section 36: Relates to the relevancy of government-authorized survey maps and charts.
Rule.Section 3: Provides the foundational legal definition of what constitutes a document.

Application.Section 34: Fails because a birth register is a state document rather than a private ledger for tracking grocery or hardware sales.
Application.Section 32: Fails since Section 35 provides a primary rule for public records that does not require the clerk to be deceased.
Application.Section 35: Governs directly because the birth registry is an official public book and the entry was made by a public servant performing government duties.
Application.Section 36: Fails since a birth record is personal data rather than a territorial chart or survey map.
Application.Section 3: Fails because identifying the register as a document does not make its specific factual contents relevant without Section 35.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 35, the register entry is a relevant fact because it was made in an official public book by a public servant during their duty.

Question: Scenario-based: While in police custody, Abul said, 'I sold the stolen watch to the jeweler at the New Market crossroads.' If the police find the watch there, what part of Abul's statement is allowed in court?

Possible Sections.Full Text: No confession made to a police-officer shall be proved as against a person accused of any offence.
No confession made by any person whilst he is in the custody of a police-officer, unless it be made in the immediate presence of a Magistrate, shall be proved as against such person.

Provided that, when any fact is deposed to as discovered in consequence of information received from a person accused of any offence, in the custody of a police officer, so much of such information, whether it amounts to a confession or not, as relates distinctly to the fact thereby discovered, may be proved.

A confession made by an accused person is irrelevant in a criminal proceeding, if the making of the confession appears to the Court to have been caused by any inducement, threat or promise having reference to the charge against the accused person, proceeding from a person in authority and sufficient, in the opinion of the Court, to give the accused person grounds which would appear to him reasonable for supposing that by making it he would gain any advantage or avoid any evil of a temporal nature in reference to the proceedings against him.

If such a confession is otherwise relevant, it does not become irrelevant merely because it was made under a promise of secrecy, or in consequence of a deception practised on the accused person for the purpose of obtaining it, or when he was drunk, or because it was made in answer to questions which he need not have answered, whatever may have been the form of those questions, or because he was not warned that he was not bound to make such confession, and that evidence of it might be given against him.

Section Text: Provided that, when any fact is deposed to as discovered in consequence of information received from a person accused of any offence, in the custody of a police officer, so much of such information, whether it amounts to a confession or not, as relates distinctly to the fact thereby discovered, may be proved.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is a confession in custody admissible if it leads to the physical discovery of evidence?

Rule.Section 25: Bars confessions made directly to police officers from being proved against an accused.
Rule.Section 26: Bars confessions in custody unless a Magistrate is present during the statement.
Rule.Section 27: Allows for the proof of information from an accused in custody only so far as it relates distinctly to a discovered fact.
Rule.Section 24: Excludes confessions triggered by inducements, promises, or threats from authorities.
Rule.Section 29: States that a relevant confession remains admissible even if obtained through deception or secrecy.

Application.Section 25: Fails because Section 27 acts as a specific statutory exception that permits discovery-related statements to be proved.
Application.Section 26: Fails since Section 27 overrides the custody bar for information that is validated by a physical discovery.
Application.Section 27: Governs directly because the location of the watch validates the specific part of the statement identifying the New Market jeweler.
Application.Section 24: Fails because there is no evidence of a threat or bribe used to extract the watch's location from the accused.
Application.Section 29: Fails since the admissibility depends on the physical discovery rather than the lack of a legal warning.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 27, only the part of the statement identifying the specific jeweler at the New Market crossroads is allowed because it led to the discovery of the watch.

Question: Scenario-based: A defendant wants to move a case from Dhaka to Chittagong. When is the very latest time he can ask the judge for this move?

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 21
Possible Sections.Full Text: 21. No objection as to the place of suing shall be allowed by any appellate or revisional Court unless such objection was taken in the Court of first instance at the earliest possible opportunity and in all cases where issues are settled at or before such settlement, and unless there has been a consequent failure of justice.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 22
Possible Sections.Full Text: 22. Where a suit may be instituted in any one of two or more Courts and is instituted in one of such Courts, any defendant, after notice to the other parties, may, at the earliest possible opportunity and in all cases where issues are settled at or before such settlement, apply to have the suit transferred to another Court, and the Court to which such application is made, after considering the objections of the other parties (if any), shall determine in which of the several Courts having jurisdiction the suit shall proceed.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 24
Possible Sections.Full Text: 24.(1) On the application of any of the parties and after notice to the parties and after hearing such of them as desire to be heard, or of its own motion without such notice, the High Court Division or the District Court may at any stage-

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 23
Possible Sections.Full Text: 23.(1) Where the several Courts having jurisdiction are subordinate to the same Appellate Court, an application under section 22 shall be made to the Appellate Court.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 15
Possible Sections.Full Text: Every suit shall be instituted in the Court of the lowest grade competent to try it.

Relevant Section: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 22
Section Text: Where a suit may be instituted in any one of two or more Courts and is instituted in one of such Courts, any defendant, after notice to the other parties, may, at the earliest possible opportunity and in all cases where issues are settled at or before such settlement, apply to have the suit transferred to another Court, and the Court to which such application is made, after considering the objections of the other parties (if any), shall determine in which of the several Courts having jurisdiction the suit shall proceed.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: What is the deadline for a defendant to apply for a case transfer?

Rule.Section 21: Prevents a person from waiting until an appeal to complain that the trial was held in the wrong district.
Rule.Section 22: Directs that transfer requests must be made at the first possible chance and no later than when the judge settles the issues.
Rule.Section 24: Grants the High Court Division broad power to move cases at any point during the proceedings for administrative reasons.
Rule.Section 23: Specifies which higher court must receive the transfer application based on the locations of the lower courts.
Rule.Section 15: Forces all plaintiffs to begin their legal actions at the most junior rank of court authorized.

Application.Section 21: Fails because it addresses late objections rather than setting the deadline for a voluntary transfer request.
Application.Section 22: Governs directly as it explicitly sets the deadline at or before the 'settlement of issues' for a transfer application.
Application.Section 24: Does not apply as the scenario describes a party's deadline rather than the court's administrative authority.
Application.Section 23: Fails because it defines the destination of paperwork rather than the deadline for submission.
Application.Section 15: Is inapplicable as it concerns the vertical rank of the judge rather than the horizontal move.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, section 22, the defendant must ask at the earliest possible opportunity — the application must be filed no later than when the court settles the legal issues of the case.

Cited Acts and Sections: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 22
