Question: Scenario-based: I found an entry in an official birth register at the local government office. It was recorded by the registrar as part of his daily job. Can I use this entry to prove my age?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Entries in books of account [or digital record], regularly kept in the course of business, are relevant whenever they refer to a matter into which the Court has to inquire, but such statements shall not alone be sufficient evidence to charge any person with liability.

An entry in any public or other official book, register or record [,or digital record], stating a fact in issue or relevant fact, and made by a public servant in the discharge of his official duty, or by any other person in performance of a duty specially enjoined by the law of the country in which such book, register [, record or digital record] is kept, is itself a relevant fact.

Statements, written or verbal, of relevant facts made by a person who is dead, or who cannot be found, or who has become incapable of giving evidence, or whose attendance cannot be procured without an amount of delay or expense which under the circumstances of the case appears to the Court unreasonable, are themselves relevant facts in the following cases:–

Statements of facts in issue or relevant facts, made in published [maps, charts or digital record] generally offered for public sale, or in [maps, plans or digital record] made under the authority of the Government, as to matters usually represented or stated in such [maps, charts, plans or digital record], are themselves relevant facts.

In this Act the following words and expressions are used in the following senses, unless a contrary intention appears from the context:- "Court" includes all Judges and Magistrates and all persons, except arbitrators, legally authorized to take evidence. "Fact" means and includes-

Section Text: An entry in any public or other official book, register or record [,or digital record], stating a fact in issue or relevant fact, and made by a public servant in the discharge of his official duty, or by any other person in performance of a duty specially enjoined by the law of the country in which such book, register [, record or digital record] is kept, is itself a relevant fact.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is an entry in an official registry book admissible as a relevant fact?

Rule.Section 34: Governs the relevancy of private commercial accounting ledgers and their limited weight.
Rule.Section 35: Makes entries in official public registers relevant if made by public servants during their official duty.
Rule.Section 32: Provides a hearsay exception for business records specifically when the person who made them is deceased.
Rule.Section 36: Grants relevancy to government-authorized survey maps and geographical charts.
Rule.Section 3: Provides the general legal definition for what constitutes a document in court.

Application.Section 34: Fails since a birth registry is a government record rather than a private ledger for tracking tailor or grocery sales.
Application.Section 35: Governs directly because the birth registry is an official public book and the entry was made by a public servant in the line of duty.
Application.Section 32: Fails as Section 35 provides a primary rule for public records that does not require the registrar to be deceased.
Application.Section 36: Fails as a birth record is personal administrative data rather than a territorial chart or map.
Application.Section 3: Fails because simply identifying the book as a 'document' does not grant inherent relevancy to its contents without Section 35.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 35, the register entry is a relevant fact because it was made in an official public book by a public servant while performing their duties.

Question: Scenario-based: Salman is suing a construction firm for a building collapse. He wants to show that several other buildings constructed by the same firm in the same area also collapsed due to similar structural flaws within the same year. Is this relevant?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Facts showing the existence of any state of mind, such as intention, knowledge, good faith, negligence, rashness, ill-will, or good-will towards any particular person, or showing the existence of any state of body or bodily feeling, are relevant, when the existence of any such state of mind or body or bodily feeling is in issue or relevant.

When there is a question whether an act was accidental or intentional, or done with a particular knowledge or intention, the fact that such act formed part of a series of similar occurrences, in each of which the person doing the act was concerned, is relevant.
When there is a question whether a particular act was done, the existence of any course of business, according to which it naturally would have been done, is a relevant fact.

Facts which are the occasions, cause or effect, immediate or otherwise, of relevant facts, or facts in issue, or which constitute the state of things under which they happened, or which afforded an opportunity for their occurrence or transaction, are relevant.

Any fact is relevant which shows or constitutes a motive or preparation for any fact in issue or relevant fact. The conduct of any party, or of any agent to any party, to any suit or proceeding, in reference to such suit or proceeding, or in reference to any fact in issue therein or relevant thereto, and the conduct of any person an offence against whom is the subject of any proceeding, is relevant, if such conduct influences or is influenced by any fact, in issue or relevant fact, and whether it was previous subsequent thereto.

Section Text: When there is a question whether an act was accidental or intentional, or done with a particular knowledge or intention, the fact that such act formed part of a series of similar occurrences, in each of which the person doing the act was concerned, is relevant.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is a series of similar structural failures relevant to proving intentional negligence?

Rule.Section 14: Specifies that facts demonstrating internal mental states like negligence or intention are relevant.
Rule.Section 15: Governs the relevancy of a series of similar occurrences to prove an act was not accidental.
Rule.Section 16: Establishes that a standard course of business is relevant to prove a specific act occurred.
Rule.Section 7: Makes facts relevant if they are the cause, effect, or occasion of a fact in issue.
Rule.Section 8: Determines relevancy for facts showing motive, preparation, or subsequent conduct of parties.

Application.Section 14: Fails because it defines the relevancy of the state of mind itself rather than the specific evidentiary logic of using a pattern of events.
Application.Section 15: Governs directly because the collapses form a series of similar occurrences that suggest the current failure was not an isolated accident.
Application.Section 16: Fails since the collapses are repeated errors or failures rather than a routine, professional course of business intended to produce results.
Application.Section 7: Fails because previous building collapses are not the immediate physical cause or effect of the current building's failure.
Application.Section 8: Fails as the previous collapses do not constitute Salman's active preparation or a specific motive for the current failure.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 15, the other building collapses are relevant because they form part of a series of similar occurrences that suggest the firm's act was intentional or negligent rather than accidental.

Question: Scenario-based: Salman confessed to a theft because he thought he was talking to a childhood friend, but the person was actually a police officer in plain clothes who had tricked him. Is the confession admissible?

Possible Sections.Full Text: A confession made by an accused person is irrelevant in a criminal proceeding, if the making of the confession appears to the Court to have been caused by any inducement, threat or promise having reference to the charge against the accused person, proceeding from a person in authority and sufficient, in the opinion of the Court, to give the accused person grounds which would appear to him reasonable for supposing that by making it he would gain any advantage or avoid any evil of a temporal nature in reference to the proceedings against him.
No confession made to a police-officer shall be proved as against a person accused of any offence.
No confession made by any person whilst he is in the custody of a police-officer, unless it be made in the immediate presence of a Magistrate, shall be proved as against such person.

If such a confession is otherwise relevant, it does not become irrelevant merely because it was made under a promise of secrecy, or in consequence of a deception practised on the accused person for the purpose of obtaining it, or when he was drunk, or because it was made in answer to questions which he need not have answered, whatever may have been the form of those questions, or because he was not warned that he was not bound to make such confession, and that evidence of it might be given against him.

If such a confession as is referred to in section 24 is made after the impression caused by any such inducement, threat or promise has, in the opinion of the Court, been fully removed, it is relevant.

Section Text: If such a confession is otherwise relevant, it does not become irrelevant merely because it was made under a promise of secrecy, or in consequence of a deception practised on the accused person for the purpose of obtaining it, or when he was drunk, or because it was made in answer to questions which he need not have answered, whatever may have been the form of those questions, or because he was not warned that he was not bound to make such confession, and that evidence of it might be given against him.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Does deception by an undercover officer make an otherwise relevant confession inadmissible?

Rule.Section 24: Excludes confessions motivated by threats or promises from authorities regarding the charges.
Rule.Section 25: Creates an absolute bar for confessions made to a known police officer.
Rule.Section 26: Bars confessions made by persons in police custody without a Magistrate present.
Rule.Section 29: States that a relevant confession does not become irrelevant simply because it was obtained through deception.
Rule.Section 28: Governs confessions made after a prior threat has been neutralized.

Application.Section 24: Fails because Salman was tricked into feeling comfortable rather than being threatened or promised a lighter sentence.
Application.Section 25: Fails as Salman did not know the person was a police officer, making it a statement to a 'friend' rather than a formal police confession.
Application.Section 26: Fails since Salman was not under arrest or in custody at the time he spoke to the undercover officer.
Application.Section 29: Governs directly because the use of deception (pretending to be a friend) is explicitly listed as a condition that does not disqualify a confession.
Application.Section 28: Fails because there was no prior inducement from which Salman's mind needed to be cleared.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 29, the confession is admissible because the law says a confession does not become irrelevant just because it was obtained through deception.

Question: Scenario-based: Arpan shows Salim a fake photo of Salim's wife in a compromising situation and says, 'Give me 10,000 Taka or I will post this on Facebook tonight.' Salim is terrified for his reputation and pays. What is the correct charge?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Whoever, intending to take dishonestly any moveable property out of the possession of any person without that person's consent, moves that property in order to such taking, is said to commit theft.

Whoever intentionally puts any person in fear of any injury to that person, or to any other, and thereby dishonestly induces the person so put in fear to give donation or subscription of any kind or to deliver to any person any property or valuable security or anything signed or sealed which may be converted into a valuable security, commits 'extortion'.

Whoever, by deceiving any person, fraudulently or dishonestly induces the person so deceived to deliver any property to any person, or to consent that any person shall retain any property, or intentionally induces the person so deceived to do or omit to do anything which he would not do or omit if he were not so deceived, and which act or omission causes or is likely to cause damage or harm to that person in body, mind, reputation or property, is said to "cheat".

In all robbery there is either theft or extortion. When theft is robbery Theft is "robbery" if, in order to the committing of the theft, or in committing the theft, or in carrying away or attempting to carry away property obtained by the theft, the offender, for that end, voluntarily causes or attempts to cause to any person death or hurt or wrongful restraint, or fear of instant death or of instant hurt, or of instant wrongful restraint.
Whoever commits extortion shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, or with fine, or with both.

Section Text: Whoever intentionally puts any person in fear of any injury to that person, or to any other, and thereby dishonestly induces the person so put in fear to give donation or subscription of any kind or to deliver to any person any property or valuable security or anything signed or sealed which may be converted into a valuable security, commits 'extortion'.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is forcing someone to hand over money through fear of reputational harm classified as extortion?

Rule.Section 378: Governs theft, where property is taken secretly or without any 'delivery' by the owner.
Rule.Section 383: Defines extortion as inducing a person to 'deliver' property by putting them in fear of 'any injury' (including reputation).
Rule.Section 415: Governs cheating, where property is given based on a 'deception' rather than 'fear'.
Rule.Section 390: Defines robbery, which requires the fear of 'instant' death, hurt, or restraint.
Rule.Section 384: Sets the three-year prison punishment for the offense of extortion.

Application.Section 378: Fails because Salim 'delivered' the money himself due to fear, whereas theft involves a taking without the owner's participation.
Application.Section 383: Governs directly because Arpan used the fear of 'injury' (reputational harm) to 'induce' Salim to 'deliver' the cash.
Application.Section 415: Fails because Salim was motivated by the threat of exposure (fear) rather than believing the fake photo was true (deception).
Application.Section 390: Fails because the threat was to post a photo later tonight, not a threat of 'instant' physical violence required for robbery.
Application.Section 384: Fails because it provides the penalty for the crime rather than the legal definition of why Arpan's act counts as extortion.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 383, Arpan is guilty of extortion because he induced Salim to deliver money by putting him in fear of reputational injury.

Question: Scenario-based: Jamil encourages his friend to beat a man severely, knowing it is likely to cause grievous hurt. The friend beats the man so badly that he actually dies from the injuries. Is Jamil liable for the death?

Possible Sections.Full Text: When an act is abetted and a different act is done, the abettor is liable for the act done, in the same manner and to the same extent as if he had directly abetted it: Provided the act done was a probable consequence of the abetment, and was committed under the influence of the instigation, or with the aid or in pursuance of the conspiracy which constituted the abetment.

When an act is abetted with the intention on the part of the abettor of causing a particular effect, and an act for which the abettor is liable in consequence of the abetment, causes a different effect from that intended by the abettor, the abettor is liable for the effect caused, in the same manner and to the same extent as if he had abetted the act with the intention of causing that effect, provided he knew that the act abetted was likely to cause that effect.

Whoever abets any offence shall, if the act abetted is committed in consequence of the abetment, and no express provision is made by this Code for the punishment of such abetment, be punished with the punishment provided for the offence. Punishment of abetment if the act abetted is committed in consequence and where no express provision is made for its punishment

Whoever, except in the case provided for by section 335, voluntarily causes grievous hurt, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine.
Whoever commits murder shall be punished with death, or imprisonment for life, and shall also be liable to fine.

Section Text: When an act is abetted with the intention on the part of the abettor of causing a particular effect, and an act for which the abettor is liable in consequence of the abetment, causes a different effect from that intended by the abettor, the abettor is liable for the effect caused, in the same manner and to the same extent as if he had abetted the act with the intention of causing that effect, provided he knew that the act abetted was likely to cause that effect.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is an abettor liable for a more severe outcome than intended if they knew that result was a likely risk?

Rule.Section 111: Governs liability for a 'different act' (like fire vs theft) rather than a 'different result' of the same act.
Rule.Section 113: Governs liability for an abettor when the planned act results in a more severe 'effect' than intended, provided the risk was known.
Rule.Section 109: Governs the standard punishment for the specific crime that was planned.
Rule.Section 325: Governs the substantive offense of causing grievous hurt to a person.
Rule.Section 302: Governs the substantive offense and penalty for committing murder.

Application.Section 111: Fails because the friend performed the exact act abetted (beating); the issue is the 'effect' of that beating, not a separate act.
Application.Section 113: Governs directly because death is a 'different effect' from the intended hurt, and Jamil knew that a severe beating was likely to kill.
Application.Section 109: Fails because it doesn't account for the increased severity of the actual outcome (death) versus the intended act (hurt).
Application.Section 325: Fails because it covers only the intended hurt, ignoring the fact that a death actually occurred.
Application.Section 302: Fails because it is the main penalty section and doesn't explain the rules for Jamil's secondary liability as an abettor.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 113, Jamil is liable for the death because he knew that the severe beating he encouraged was likely to cause that specific result.

Question: Scenario-based: An employee confessed to stealing cash from the office. However, he only spoke because his boss told him, 'Confess now and I promise you will keep your job and I won't call the police.' Can this confession be used in court?

Possible Sections.Full Text: No confession made to a police-officer shall be proved as against a person accused of any offence.
No confession made by any person whilst he is in the custody of a police-officer, unless it be made in the immediate presence of a Magistrate, shall be proved as against such person.

A confession made by an accused person is irrelevant in a criminal proceeding, if the making of the confession appears to the Court to have been caused by any inducement, threat or promise having reference to the charge against the accused person, proceeding from a person in authority and sufficient, in the opinion of the Court, to give the accused person grounds which would appear to him reasonable for supposing that by making it he would gain any advantage or avoid any evil of a temporal nature in reference to the proceedings against him.

If such a confession as is referred to in section 24 is made after the impression caused by any such inducement, threat or promise has, in the opinion of the Court, been fully removed, it is relevant.

If such a confession is otherwise relevant, it does not become irrelevant merely because it was made under a promise of secrecy, or in consequence of a deception practised on the accused person for the purpose of obtaining it, or when he was drunk, or because it was made in answer to questions which he need not have answered, whatever may have been the form of those questions, or because he was not warned that he was not bound to make such confession, and that evidence of it might be given against him.

Section Text: A confession made by an accused person is irrelevant in a criminal proceeding, if the making of the confession appears to the Court to have been caused by any inducement, threat or promise having reference to the charge against the accused person, proceeding from a person in authority and sufficient, in the opinion of the Court, to give the accused person grounds which would appear to him reasonable for supposing that by making it he would gain any advantage or avoid any evil of a temporal nature in reference to the proceedings against him.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is a confession obtained through a promise of job security and immunity by an employer admissible?

Rule.Section 25: Prohibits the use of confessions made to police officers.
Rule.Section 26: Excludes confessions made in police custody unless a Magistrate is present.
Rule.Section 24: Makes confessions irrelevant if caused by a promise of temporal advantage from a person in authority.
Rule.Section 28: Permits confessions once the pressure of an inducement has been fully removed from the accused's mind.
Rule.Section 29: Governs confessions made under deception or secrecy, which remain relevant.

Application.Section 25: Fails because the confession was made to a private employer, not a police officer.
Application.Section 26: Fails since the employee was not under arrest or in police custody when he spoke to his boss.
Application.Section 24: Governs directly because the boss is a person in authority and the promise of keeping his job is a clear temporal advantage.
Application.Section 28: Fails because the confession was made precisely while the inducement was still active and fresh in the employee's mind.
Application.Section 29: Fails because the scenario involves a coercive promise of benefit rather than a mere trick or a promise of secrecy.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 24, the confession is irrelevant because it was obtained through a promise of advantage made by a person in authority (the boss).

Question: Scenario-based: A judge is reviewing a case and realizes the plaintiff filed it in the wrong city. Can the judge give the papers back to the plaintiff so they can go to the right court?

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Order VII, Rule 11
Possible Sections.Full Text:
The plaint shall be rejected in the following cases:— where it does not disclose a cause of action: where the relief claimed is undervalued, and the plaintiff, on being required by the Court to correct the valuation within a time to be fixed by the Court, fails to do so: where the relief claimed is properly valued, but the plaint is written upon paper insufficiently stamped, and the plaintiff, on being required by the Court to supply the requisite stamp-paper within a time to be fixed by the Court, fails to do so: where the suit appears from the statement in the plaint to be barred by any law<sup>1</sup> [:

Provided that the time fixed by the Court for the correction of the valuation or supplying of the requisite stamp-paper shall not exceed twenty-one days.]

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Order VII, Rule 10
Possible Sections.Full Text: 10.(1) The plaint shall at any stage of the suit be returned to be presented to the Court in which the suit should have been instituted.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 21
Possible Sections.Full Text: 21. No objection as to the place of suing shall be allowed by any appellate or revisional Court unless such objection was taken in the Court of first instance at the earliest possible opportunity and in all cases where issues are settled at or before such settlement, and unless there has been a consequent failure of justice.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 24
Possible Sections.Full Text: 24.(1) On the application of any of the parties and after notice to the parties and after hearing such of them as desire to be heard, or of its own motion without such notice, the High Court Division or the District Court may at any stage-

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Order IX, Rule 3
Possible Sections.Full Text: Where neither party appears when the suit is called on for hearing, the Court may make an order that the suit be dismissed.

Relevant Section: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Order VII, Rule 10
Section Text: 10.(1) The plaint shall at any stage of the suit be returned to be presented to the Court in which the suit should have been instituted.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: What must a judge do if a lawsuit is filed in a court without territorial jurisdiction?

Rule.Order VII, Rule 11: Lists the mandatory grounds for the permanent rejection and termination of a lawsuit at the outset.
Rule.Order VII, Rule 10: Requires the court to return the original paperwork to the plaintiff so it can be refiled in the correct district.
Rule.Section 21: Prevents a party from waiting until the appeal stage to complain about a mistake in the trial's location.
Rule.Section 24: Gives the High Court the power to move an existing case from one lower court to a different one.
Rule.Order IX, Rule 3: Allows a judge to dismiss a case if both the person suing and the person being sued fail to attend the hearing.

Application.Order VII, Rule 11: Fails because 'rejection' is a final act for bad claims, whereas 'return' is for claims filed in the wrong geographic location.
Application.Order VII, Rule 10: Governs directly as it mandates the 'return' of the plaint whenever it is discovered the case belongs in a different court.
Application.Section 21: Does not apply here as it governs late objections to mistakes rather than the judge's initial duty to correct them.
Application.Section 24: Fails because it describes a 'transfer' by a superior court rather than a simple 'return' of papers by the trial judge.
Application.Order IX, Rule 3: Is inapplicable as the issue is a mistake in location rather than the physical absence of the parties.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Order VII, Rule 10, Sub-rule (1), the judge must return the papers — the law requires the court to give the lawsuit back so it can be filed in the correct district.

Cited Acts and Sections: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Order VII, Rule 10

Question: Scenario-based: A court wants me to appear as a witness. The police gave the paper to my 20-year-old son because I wasn't home. Does this count as me being 'officially' served?

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 68
Possible Sections.Full Text: Every summons issued by a Court under this Code shall be in writing in duplicate, signed and sealed by the presiding officer of such Court, or by such other officer as the Supreme Court may, from time to time, by rule, direct.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 69
Possible Sections.Full Text: The summons shall, if practicable, be served personally on the person summoned, by delivering or tendering to him one of the duplicates of the summons.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 70
Possible Sections.Full Text: Where the person summoned cannot by the exercise of due diligence be found, the summons may be served by leaving one of the duplicates for him with some adult [***] member of his family, and the person with whom the summons is so left shall, if so required by the serving officer, sign a receipt therefore on the back of the other duplicate.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 71
Possible Sections.Full Text: If service in the manner mentioned in sections 69 and 70 cannot by the exercise of due diligence be effected, the serving officer shall affix one of the duplicates of the summons to some conspicuous part of the house or homestead in which the person summoned ordinarily resides; and thereupon the summons shall be deemed to have been duly served.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 74
Possible Sections.Full Text: When a summons issued by a Court is served outside the local limits of its jurisdiction, and in any case where the officer who has served a summons is not present at the hearing of the case, an affidavit, purporting to be made before a Magistrate, that such summons has been served, and a duplicate of the summons purporting to be endorsed (in manner provided by section 69 or section 70) by the person to whom it was delivered or tendered or with whom it was left, shall be admissible in evidence, and the statements made therein shall be deemed to be correct unless and until the contrary is proved. The affidavit mentioned in this section may be attached to the duplicate of the summons and returned to the Court.

Relevant Section: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 69
Section Text: The summons shall, if practicable, be served personally on the person summoned, by delivering or tendering to him one of the duplicates of the summons.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is service of a summons on an adult male family member legally valid?

Rule.Section 68: Defines the formal technical requirements of the summons document itself.
Rule.Section 69: Provides the hierarchy of service methods, including personal service and service on adult male relatives.
Rule.Section 70: Outlines the method of posting the summons on a house wall if no family members are available.
Rule.Section 71: Governs the procedure for sending summonses to people living in other districts or jurisdictions.
Rule.Section 74: Describes how a serving officer can prove they delivered the paper through a written affidavit.

Application.Section 68: Fails because it concerns the 'appearance' of the paper rather than the 'method' of its delivery.
Application.Section 69: Governs directly because Subsection (2) explicitly authorizes serving an 'adult male member' of the family when the primary person isn't home.
Application.Section 70: Fails because the police found a family member, so they don't need to resort to sticking the paper on the house.
Application.Section 71: Fails because the scenario doesn't suggest that the person lives in a different city or jurisdiction.
Application.Section 74: Fails because it explains how to 'prove' service in court rather than the 'validity' of the delivery method itself.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, section 69, Subsection (2), yes, this is official; the law allows a summons to be left with any adult male member of your family who lives with you.

Cited Acts and Sections: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 69(2)
source_file: Q-As copy/English/Code of Criminal Procedure/Advanced selection

Question: Scenario-based: I have a contract with two parts. The first part is to sell a house, which is fine. The second part is to help the buyer perform a crime, which is illegal. Can the court still force the house sale part?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Where a party to a contract is unable to perform the whole of his part of it, but the part which must be left unperformed bears only a small proportion to the whole in value, and admits of compensation in money, the Court may, at the suit of either party, direct the specific performance of so much of the contract as can be performed, and award compensation in money for the deficiency.

Where a party to a contract is unable to perform the whole of his part of it, and the part which must be left unperformed forms a considerable portion of the whole, or does not admit compensation in money, he is not entitled to obtain a decree for specific performance. But the Court may, at the suit of the other party, direct the party in default to perform specifically so much of his part of the contract as he can perform, provided that the plaintiff relinquishes all claim to further performance, and all right to compensation either for the deficiency, or for the loss or damage sustained by him through the default of the defendant.

When a part of a contract which, taken by itself, can and ought to be specifically performed, stands on a separate and independent footing from another part of the same contract which cannot or ought not to be specifically performed, the Court may direct specific performance of the former part.
The Court shall not direct the specific performance of a part of a contract except in cases coming under one or other of the three last preceding sections.
Specific relief cannot be granted for the mere purpose of enforcing a penal law.

Section Text: When a part of a contract which, taken by itself, can and ought to be specifically performed, stands on a separate and independent footing from another part of the same contract which cannot or ought not to be specifically performed, the Court may direct specific performance of the former part.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: May a valid, independent part of a contract be enforced if another part is illegal or unenforceable?

Rule.Section 14: Allows for part performance if the missing piece is small and can be satisfied with a cash discount.
Rule.Section 15: Prohibits enforcing a deal if a large and important part cannot be finished by the person selling.
Rule.Section 16: Enables the court to enforce a 'separate and independent' portion of a deal even if other parts are bad.
Rule.Section 17: Acts as a general bar that stops the court from enforcing only parts of contracts unless they fit sections 14, 15, or 16.
Rule.Section 7: Bars the court from using specific relief powers solely for the purpose of carrying out a criminal or penal regulation.

Application.Section 14: Fails because the crime isn't a 'small proportion' of the house; it's a separate independent issue altogether.
Application.Section 15: Fails as the crime is not an 'unperformed' portion of the house sale itself, but a distinct illegal agreement.
Application.Section 16: Governs directly because it allows the house sale to be enforced since it stands on a 'separate and independent footing' from the crime.
Application.Section 17: Fails as it is a general rule that yields to the specific 'independent part' exception found in Section 16.
Application.Section 7: Fails because enforcing the house sale is a civil property matter, not an attempt to enforce the criminal act.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Specific Relief Act, 1877, section 16, the court can force the house sale — the law allows an independent part of a contract to be enforced even if another part is illegal.

Question: Scenario-based: Salim is found guilty of a crime, but the judge is unsure if the specific act was theft or criminal breach of trust. Both crimes have different punishment lengths. Which sentence must the judge give?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Where anything which is an offence is made up of parts, any of which parts is itself an offence, the offender shall not be punished with the punishment of more than one of such his offences, unless it be so expressly provided. Where anything is an offence falling within two or more separate definitions of any law in force for the time being by which offences are defined or punished, or Where several acts, of which one or more than one would by itself or themselves constitute an offence, constitute, when combined, a different offence, the offender shall not be punished with a more severe punishment than the Court which tries him could award for any one of such offences.

In all cases in which judgment is given that a person is guilty of one of several offences specified in the judgment, but that it is doubtful of which these offences he is guilty, the offender shall be punished for the offence for which the lowest punishment is provided if the same punishment is not provided for all.

In every case in which an offender is punishable with imprisonment which may be of either description, it shall be competent to the Court which sentences such offender to direct in the sentence that such imprisonment shall be wholly rigorous, or that such imprisonment shall be wholly simple, or that any part of such imprisonment shall be rigorous and the rest simple.
When a criminal act is done by several persons, in furtherance of the common intention of all, each of such persons is liable for that act in the same manner as if it were done by him alone.
Where several persons are engaged or concerned in the commission of a criminal act, they may be guilty of different offences by means of that act.

Section Text: In all cases in which judgment is given that a person is guilty of one of several offences specified in the judgment, but that it is doubtful of which these offences he is guilty, the offender shall be punished for the offence for which the lowest punishment is provided if the same punishment is not provided for all.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: What is the sentencing rule when there is doubt about which specific offense was committed?

Rule.Section 71: Prevents multiple punishments for a single act that contains several smaller offenses.
Rule.Section 72: Mandates the application of the lowest punishment when guilt for one of several offenses is certain but the specific one is doubtful.
Rule.Section 60: Allows judges to choose between rigorous and simple imprisonment for a sentence.
Rule.Section 34: Governs joint liability for a shared criminal act performed by multiple people.
Rule.Section 38: Allows participants in a joint act to be convicted of different crimes based on their individual roles.

Application.Section 71: Fails because it addresses the combination of acts rather than the court's uncertainty about the legal classification.
Application.Section 72: Governs directly because it explicitly requires the 'lowest punishment' to be applied in cases of judicial doubt between offenses.
Application.Section 60: Fails because it deals with the nature of labor in prison rather than resolving a conflict between different penal sections.
Application.Section 34: Fails because the scenario concerns a single offender's specific charge rather than the liability of a group.
Application.Section 38: Fails because the issue is a single person's ambiguous act rather than multiple people performing a joint act.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 72, if a judge is doubtful about which offense was committed, they must apply the lowest punishment provided for the possible crimes.

Question: Scenario-based: Salim is caught in possession of a box of fake 100-Taka coins. He knew they were fake when he bought them from a dealer, and he was keeping them to use as change in his shop. What is the correct charge?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Whoever counterfeits, or knowingly performs any part of the process of counterfeiting Bangladesh coin, shall be punished with imprisonment for life, or with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine.

Whoever, having any counterfeit coin which, at the time when he became possessed of it, he knew to be counterfeit, fraudulently or with intent that fraud may be committed, delivers the same to any person, or attempts to induce any person to receive it, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to five years, and shall also be liable to fine.

Whoever, fraudulently or with intent that fraud may be committed, is in possession of counterfeit coin, which is a counterfeit of Bangladesh coin, having known at the time when he became possessed of it that it was counterfeit, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine.

Whoever, fraudulently or with intent that fraud may be committed, is in possession of counterfeit coin, having known at the time when he became possessed thereof that such coin was counterfeit, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, and shall also be liable to fine.

Coin is metal used for the time being as money, and stamped and issued by the authority of some State or Sovereign Power in order to be so used. Bangladesh coin is metal stamped and issued by the authority of the Government<sup>84</sup> [* * *] in order to be used as money; and metal which has been so stamped and issued shall continue to be Bangladesh coin for the purposes of this Chapter, notwithstanding that it may have ceased to be used as money.

Section Text: Whoever, fraudulently or with intent that fraud may be committed, is in possession of counterfeit coin, which is a counterfeit of Bangladesh coin, having known at the time when he became possessed of it that it was counterfeit, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: What is the penalty for possessing fake national currency with the intent to use it for fraud?

Rule.Section 232: Governs the actual manufacture or creation of fake Bangladesh coins.
Rule.Section 239: Governs the actual act of spending or delivering fake general coins to another person.
Rule.Section 243: Governs the simple possession of fake 'Bangladesh coin' with a fraudulent intent.
Rule.Section 242: Governs the simple possession of 'general' fake coins with a fraudulent intent.
Rule.Section 230: Provides the legal definition of what qualifies as a Bangladesh coin.

Application.Section 232: Fails because Salim was caught 'possessing' the coins rather than being caught in the act of 'making' them.
Application.Section 239: Fails because Salim had not yet 'delivered' or spent the coins; he was only holding them in his shop.
Application.Section 243: Governs directly because it targets the possession of fake national currency (Bangladesh coin) with the intent to commit fraud.
Application.Section 242: Fails because the coins were Bangladesh Taka, which triggers the specific and more severe Section 243 for national currency.
Application.Section 230: Fails because it is a definition section and does not establish a crime or a specific punishment.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 243, Salim is guilty because he was in possession of counterfeit Bangladesh coins with the intent to commit fraud.

Question: Scenario-based: A judge is about to charge me with 'Theft in a building.' Does the judge have to tell me the specific section of of The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898 the law I broke, or is just telling me the name of the crime enough?

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 242
Possible Sections.Full Text: If, after such consideration and hearing as aforesaid, the Magistrate is of opinion that there is ground for presuming that the accused has committed an offence, the Magistrate shall frame a formal charge relating to the offence of which he is accused and he shall be asked whether he admits that he has committed the offence with which he is charged.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 221
Possible Sections.Full Text: (4) The law and section of the law against which the offence is said to have been committed shall be mentioned in the charge.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 222
Possible Sections.Full Text: The charge shall contain such particulars as to the time and place of the alleged offence, and the person (if any) against whom, or the thing (if any) in respect of which, it was committed, as are reasonably sufficient to give the accused notice of the matter with which he is charged. When the accused is charged with criminal breach of trust or dishonest misappropriation of money, it shall be sufficient to specify the gross sum in respect of which the offence is alleged to have been committed, and the dates between which the offence is alleged to have been committed, without specifying particular items or exact dates, and the charge so framed shall be deemed to be a charge of one offence within the meaning of section 234:

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 225
Possible Sections.Full Text: No error in stating either the offence or the particulars required to be stated in the charge, and no omission to state the offence or those particulars, shall be regarded at any stage of the case as material, unless the accused was in fact misled by such error or omission, and it has occasioned a failure of justice.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 221
Possible Sections.Full Text: Every charge under this Code shall state the offence with which the accused is charged.

Relevant Section: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 221
Section Text: (4) The law and section of the law against which the offence is said to have been committed shall be mentioned in the charge.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is it mandatory to include the specific section number in a formal criminal charge document?

Rule.Section 242: Requires only the general facts of the crime to be explained to the accused in simple summons cases.
Rule.Section 221(4): Mandates that every formal charge document must explicitly state the specific law and the section number involved.
Rule.Section 222: Directs that the charge must include details about the time and location of the alleged criminal act.
Rule.Section 225: Establishes that small mistakes in a charge are not fatal to the case unless they mislead the suspect.
Rule.Section 221(5): Declares that the act of making a charge implies all legal requirements for that crime have been met.

Application.Section 242: Fails because building theft is a serious case requiring a 'formal' charge under Section 221, not just an informal talk.
Application.Section 221(4): Governs directly because it contains the specific statutory command that the 'section of the law' must be mentioned in the charge.
Application.Section 222: Fails because it relates to 'factual' details (where and when) rather than the 'legal' details (which section number).
Application.Section 225: Fails because it describes the 'consequence' of a mistake rather than the mandatory 'contents' for a valid document.
Application.Section 221(5): Fails because it describes the 'legal meaning' of a charge rather than the 'technical requirements' for drafting one.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, section 221, Subsection (4), the judge is required to tell you both the name of the law and the specific section number you are accused of breaking.

Cited Acts and Sections: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 221(4)
source_file: Q-As copy/English/Code of Criminal Procedure/Advanced selection

Question: Scenario-based: Salim is walking down a street when a stranger suddenly attacks him with a stick. Salim blocks the hit and punches the attacker back once, causing a bruise. Can Salim be charged with assault?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Every person has a right, subject to the restrictions contained in section 99, to defend: Firstly.- His own body, and the body of any other person against any offence affecting the human body; Secondly.- The property, whether moveable or immovable, of himself or of any other person, against any act which is an offence falling under the definition of theft, robbery, mischief or criminal trespass, or which is an attempt to commit theft, robbery, mischief or criminal trespass.
Nothing is an offence which is done in the exercise of the right of private defence.

There is no right of private defence against an act which does not reasonably cause the apprehension of death or of grievous hurt, if done, or attempted to be done by a public servant acting in good faith under colour of his office, though that act may not be strictly justifiable by law. There is no right of private defence against an act which does not reasonably cause the apprehension of death or of grievous hurt, if done, or attempted to be done, by the direction of a public servant acting in good faith under colour of his office, though that act may not be strictly justifiable by law. There is no right of private defence in cases in which there is time to have recourse to the protection of the public authorities. Extent to which the right may be exercised The right of private defence in no case extends to the inflicting of more harm than it is necessary to inflict for the purpose of defence.

Whoever makes any gesture, or any preparation intending or knowing it to be likely that such gesture or preparation will cause any person present to apprehend that he who makes that gesture or preparation is about to use criminal force to that person, is said to commit an assault.

Whoever, except in the case provided for by section 334, voluntarily causes hurt, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to one year, or with fine which may extend to one thousand taka, or with both.

Section Text: Nothing is an offence which is done in the exercise of the right of private defence.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is force used in self-defense considered a criminal offense?

Rule.Section 97: Governs the scope of what can be defended, including the human body and property.
Rule.Section 96: Governs the general legal status of acts done in private defense, declaring them not to be offenses.
Rule.Section 99: Governs the limitations on defense, such as the requirement of proportionality and the exclusion of certain public servant acts.
Rule.Section 351: Governs the technical definition of the crime of assault based on gestures and apprehension.
Rule.Section 323: Governs the standard punishment for the offense of voluntarily causing simple hurt.

Application.Section 97: Fails because it defines the targets of the right rather than providing the primary legal justification for the act itself.
Application.Section 96: Governs directly because it provides the blanket exception that justifies Salim's punch as a non-criminal act.
Application.Section 99: Fails because Salim's single punch back was proportionate, so the restrictive conditions of Section 99 do not change the outcome.
Application.Section 351: Fails because while Salim used force, Section 96 overrides the definition of assault when defense is involved.
Application.Section 323: Fails because a conviction for hurt is impossible when a general exception like private defense is established.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 96, Salim cannot be charged because the law states that nothing done while exercising the right of private defense is an offense.

Question: Scenario-based: A man gets into an argument with a local government official who is inspecting a construction site. The man shouts insults at the official to make him leave. Has he committed a crime?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Whoever voluntarily obstructs any public servant in the discharge of his public functions, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three months, or with fine which may extend to five hundred taka, or with both.

Whoever holds out any threat of injury to any public servant, or to any person in whom he believes that public servant to be interested, for the purpose of inducing that public servant to do any act, or to forbear or delay to do any act, connected with the exercise of the public functions of such public servant, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both.

Whoever intentionally offers any insult, or causes any interruption to any public servant, while such public servant is sitting in any stage of a judicial proceeding, shall be punished with simple imprisonment for a term which may extend to six months, or with fine which may extend to one thousand taka, or with both.

Whoever intentionally insults, and thereby gives provocation to any person, intending or knowing it to be likely that such provocation will cause him to break the public peace, or to commit any other offence, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both.

Whoever assaults or uses criminal force to any person being a public servant in the execution of his duty as such public servant, or with intent to prevent or deter that person from discharging his duty as such public servant, or in consequence of anything done or attempted to be done by such person in the lawful discharge of his duty as such public servant, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, or with fine, or with both.

Section Text: Whoever voluntarily obstructs any public servant in the discharge of his public functions, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three months, or with fine which may extend to five hundred taka, or with both.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is using verbal insults to stop a government official from doing their job considered a crime?

Rule.Section 186: Governs the general 'voluntary obstruction' of a public servant during their official duties.
Rule.Section 189: Governs the use of actual 'threats of injury' to influence or stop a public servant.
Rule.Section 228: Governs insults specifically directed at a judge or official during a formal court trial.
Rule.Section 504: Governs insults aimed at a private person to provoke a fight or break the peace.
Rule.Section 353: Governs the use of actual physical force or assault against a public official on duty.

Application.Section 186: Governs directly because the man's verbal abuse was a 'voluntary obstruction' aimed at stopping the official from completing the site inspection.
Application.Section 189: Fails because shouting insults is not the same as making a specific 'threat of physical injury' against the official.
Application.Section 228: Fails because the construction site is not a 'judicial proceeding' or a formal courtroom setting.
Application.Section 504: Fails because the target was an official in their duty, which triggers a specific crime against public authority, not just a breach of peace.
Application.Section 353: Fails because no physical force or assault was used; the interaction was entirely verbal.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 186, the man is guilty because he voluntarily obstructed a public servant who was trying to perform their official duties.

Question: Scenario-based: A woman finds her neighbor's goat eating her vegetable garden. She is so angry that she stabs the goat with a kitchen knife, killing it. What is the correct charge?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Whoever, with intent to cause, or knowing that he is likely to cause, wrongful loss or damage to the public or to any person, causes the destruction of any property, or any such change in any property or in the situation thereof as destroys or diminishes its value or utility, or affects it injuriously, commits "mischief".

Whoever commits mischief by killing, poisoning, maiming or rendering useless any animal or animals of the value of ten taka or upwards, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both.

Whoever commits mischief by killing, poisoning, maiming or rendering useless, any elephant, camel, horse, mule, buffalo, bull, cow or ox, whatever may be the value thereof, or any other animal of the value of fifty taka or upwards, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to five years, or with fine, or with both.
Whoever, intending to take dishonestly any moveable property out of the possession of any person without that person's consent, moves that property in order to such taking, is said to commit theft.

Every person has a right, subject to the restrictions contained in section 99, to defend: Firstly.- His own body, and the body of any other person against any offence affecting the human body; Secondly.- The property, whether moveable or immovable, of himself or of any other person, against any act which is an offence falling under the definition of theft, robbery, mischief or criminal trespass, or which is an attempt to commit theft, robbery, mischief or criminal trespass.

Section Text: Whoever commits mischief by killing, poisoning, maiming or rendering useless, any elephant, camel, horse, mule, buffalo, bull, cow or ox, whatever may be the value thereof, or any other animal of the value of fifty taka or upwards, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to five years, or with fine, or with both.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is killing an animal for damaging a garden considered mischief or self-defense?

Rule.Section 425: Defines the general act of mischief as intentionally causing destruction or damage to property to cause loss.
Rule.Section 428: Governs mischief involving the killing of any animal valued at ten Taka or more, with a two-year penalty.
Rule.Section 429: Governs mischief involving the killing of valuable animals or any animal worth fifty Taka or more, with a five-year penalty.
Rule.Section 378: Governs theft, requiring a dishonest intent to take and move property for personal gain.
Rule.Section 97: Governs the right of private defense of property against specific crimes like theft or mischief.

Application.Section 425: Fails because it is the general definition of mischief, and the law has a more specific section for the killing of animals.
Application.Section 428: Fails because a goat is typically valued over fifty Taka in modern contexts, triggering the more severe punishment in Section 429.
Application.Section 429: Governs directly because killing an animal worth over fifty Taka (like a goat) is specifically categorized as a serious form of mischief.
Application.Section 378: Fails because the woman killed the goat out of anger to cause a loss to the neighbor, rather than taking it dishonestly for her own profit.
Application.Section 97: Fails because the right of defense does not extend to killing an animal that is trespassing; one must use reasonable force to remove it, not destroy it.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 429, the woman is guilty of mischief because she killed an animal of value to cause wrongful loss to her neighbor.

Question: Scenario-based: I found my neighbor's stolen bike. I told the police where it was, but only because an officer threatened to arrest my wife if I didn't talk. Can the prosecutor use my statement to convict me?

Possible Sections.Full Text: No confession made to a police-officer shall be proved as against a person accused of any offence.
No confession made by any person whilst he is in the custody of a police-officer, unless it be made in the immediate presence of a Magistrate, shall be proved as against such person.

A confession made by an accused person is irrelevant in a criminal proceeding, if the making of the confession appears to the Court to have been caused by any inducement, threat or promise having reference to the charge against the accused person, proceeding from a person in authority and sufficient, in the opinion of the Court, to give the accused person grounds which would appear to him reasonable for supposing that by making it he would gain any advantage or avoid any evil of a temporal nature in reference to the proceedings against him.

If such a confession is otherwise relevant, it does not become irrelevant merely because it was made under a promise of secrecy, or in consequence of a deception practised on the accused person for the purpose of obtaining it, or when he was drunk, or because it was made in answer to questions which he need not have answered, whatever may have been the form of those questions, or because he was not warned that he was not bound to make such confession, and that evidence of it might be given against him.

Provided that, when any fact is deposed to as discovered in consequence of information received from a person accused of any offence, in the custody of a police officer, so much of such information, whether it amounts to a confession or not, as relates distinctly to the fact thereby discovered, may be proved.

Section Text: A confession made by an accused person is irrelevant in a criminal proceeding, if the making of the confession appears to the Court to have been caused by any inducement, threat or promise having reference to the charge against the accused person, proceeding from a person in authority and sufficient, in the opinion of the Court, to give the accused person grounds which would appear to him reasonable for supposing that by making it he would gain any advantage or avoid any evil of a temporal nature in reference to the proceedings against him.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is a confession obtained through a threat against a family member admissible in court?

Rule.Section 25: Bars all confessions made directly to police officers.
Rule.Section 26: Bars confessions in custody unless made before a Magistrate.
Rule.Section 24: Makes confessions irrelevant if obtained through threats or promises from authorities.
Rule.Section 29: States that confessions obtained by deception or while drunk remain relevant.
Rule.Section 27: Allows information from an accused that leads to the discovery of physical evidence.

Application.Section 25: Fails because while the statement was to a police officer, the primary legal problem is the coercive threat used.
Application.Section 26: Fails since the problem is the coercion, not just the physical location or custodial status of the person.
Application.Section 24: Governs directly because the officer is a person in authority and the threat against the wife is an 'evil of a temporal nature.'
Application.Section 29: Fails because a threat of arrest is a form of coercion that outweighs mere deception or lack of warning.
Application.Section 27: Fails since Section 24 provides the fundamental rule for excluding coerced confessions even if they lead to discoveries.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 24, the confession is irrelevant because it was obtained through a threat made by a person in authority (the officer).

Question: Scenario-based: In my lawsuit, I need to show what the current labor laws of India are. Can I bring an official law book printed by the Indian government as evidence of their legal rules?

Possible Sections.Full Text: When the Court has to form an opinion as to the existence of any fact of a public nature, any statement of it, made in a recital contained in any Act of Parliament of the United Kingdom, or in any [Act of Parliament] or in a Government notification is a relevant fact.

When the Court has to form an opinion as to a law of any country, any statement of such law contained in a book purporting to be printed or published under the authority of the Government of such country and to contain any such law, and any report of a ruling of the Courts of such country contained in a book purporting to be a report of such rulings, is relevant.

Statements of facts in issue or relevant facts, made in published [maps, charts or digital record] generally offered for public sale, or in [maps, plans or digital record] made under the authority of the Government, as to matters usually represented or stated in such [maps, charts, plans or digital record], are themselves relevant facts.

An entry in any public or other official book, register or record [,or digital record], stating a fact in issue or relevant fact, and made by a public servant in the discharge of his official duty, or by any other person in performance of a duty specially enjoined by the law of the country in which such book, register [, record or digital record] is kept, is itself a relevant fact.

This act may be called the Evidence Act, 1872. It extends to the whole of Bangladesh and applies to all judicial proceedings in or before any Court, including Courts-martial, other than Courts-martial convened under the Army Act, 1952, the Naval Discipline Ordinance, 1961 or the Air Force Act, 1953 but not to affidavits presented to any Court or officer, nor to proceedings before an arbitrator; And it shall come into force on the first day of September, 1872.

Section Text: When the Court has to form an opinion as to a law of any country, any statement of such law contained in a book purporting to be printed or published under the authority of the Government of such country and to contain any such law, and any report of a ruling of the Courts of such country contained in a book purporting to be a report of such rulings, is relevant.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is a foreign government's law book admissible to prove the law of that country?

Rule.Section 37: Relates to government notifications regarding facts of a broad public nature.
Rule.Section 38: Grants relevancy to law books and court reports published under the authority of any country's government.
Rule.Section 36: Governs the relevancy of government-authorized maps and plans.
Rule.Section 35: Governs the relevancy of official records created by public servants performing their duties.
Rule.Section 1: Defines the territorial reach and commencement of the Evidence Act in Bangladesh.

Application.Section 37: Fails because legal statutes are matters of law rather than specific 'facts of a public nature' announced in a Gazette.
Application.Section 38: Governs directly because the book contains statements of foreign law published under the authority of the Indian government.
Application.Section 36: Fails since the issue concerns legal text rather than geographical charts or survey maps.
Application.Section 35: Fails as a law book is a published authority rather than an internal registry entry of a government clerk.
Application.Section 1: Fails because identifying the trial location as Bangladesh doesn't provide the rule for proving foreign statutes.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 38, the law book is relevant because it was published under the authority of the Indian government to explain their laws.

Question: Scenario-based: Arpan gives his friend a set of lock-picking tools specifically to help him break into a warehouse. Is Arpan guilty of a crime even though he didn't go to the warehouse himself?

Possible Sections.Full Text: A person abets an offence, who abets either the commission of an offence, or the commission of an act which would be an offence, if committed by a person capable by law of committing an offence with the same intention or knowledge as that of the abettor.

Whenever any person, who if absent would be liable to be punished as an abettor, is present when the act or offence for which he would be punishable in consequence of the abetment is committed, he shall be deemed to have committed such act or offence.

A person abets the doing of a thing, who: Firstly.-Instigates any person to do that thing; or Secondly.-Engages with one or more other person or persons in any conspiracy for the doing of that thing, if an act or illegal omission takes place in pursuance of that conspiracy, and in order to the doing of that thing; or Thirdly.-Intentionally aids, by any act or illegal omission, the doing of that thing.

When an act is abetted and a different act is done, the abettor is liable for the act done, in the same manner and to the same extent as if he had directly abetted it: Provided the act done was a probable consequence of the abetment, and was committed under the influence of the instigation, or with the aid or in pursuance of the conspiracy which constituted the abetment.

Whoever abets any offence shall, if the act abetted is committed in consequence of the abetment, and no express provision is made by this Code for the punishment of such abetment, be punished with the punishment provided for the offence.

Section Text: A person abets the doing of a thing, who: Firstly.-Instigates any person to do that thing; or Secondly.-Engages with one or more other person or persons in any conspiracy for the doing of that thing, if an act or illegal omission takes place in pursuance of that conspiracy, and in order to the doing of that thing; or Thirdly.-Intentionally aids, by any act or illegal omission, the doing of that thing.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Does providing tools to assist in a crime constitute the offense of abetment?

Rule.Section 107: Defines abetment as instigating, conspiring, or intentionally aiding the commission of an act through physical help or omission.
Rule.Section 108: Specifies who qualifies as an abettor and details the various scenarios involving incapable perpetrators or failed attempts.
Rule.Section 109: Prescribes the punishment for abetment when the target crime is committed and no specific penalty for abetment is listed.
Rule.Section 111: Determines the liability of an abettor when the perpetrator commits a different act than the one originally planned.
Rule.Section 114: Deems an abettor to be the principal offender if they are physically present during the commission of the crime.

Application.Section 107: Governs directly because Arpan's act of providing lock-picking tools is a form of intentional aid, fulfilling the third clause of the definition.
Application.Section 108: Fails because it defines the status of an abettor rather than the primary act of aiding that constitutes the offense.
Application.Section 109: Fails because it concerns the calculation of the final sentence rather than defining whether the act of giving tools is illegal.
Application.Section 111: Fails because the crime committed was exactly what Arpan aided, so the rule for 'different acts' does not apply.
Application.Section 114: Fails because the scenario explicitly states Arpan was not present at the warehouse during the break-in.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 107, Clause Thirdly, Arpan is guilty of abetment because he intentionally aided the commission of the crime by providing the tools needed to commit it.

Question: Scenario-based: Rahim is being sued for not paying a debt. To show that he meant to pay, Rahim wants to tell the court that he went to a bank and applied for a loan just to pay off that debt. Is this allowed?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Any fact is relevant which shows or constitutes a motive or preparation for any fact in issue or relevant fact. The conduct of any party, or of any agent to any party, to any suit or proceeding, in reference to such suit or proceeding, or in reference to any fact in issue therein or relevant thereto, and the conduct of any person an offence against whom is the subject of any proceeding, is relevant, if such conduct influences or is influenced by any fact, in issue or relevant fact, and whether it was previous subsequent thereto.

Admissions are relevant and may be proved as against the person who makes them, or his representative in interest; but they can not be proved by or on behalf of the person who makes them or by his representative in interest, except in the following cases:–

Facts showing the existence of any state of mind, such as intention, knowledge, good faith, negligence, rashness, ill-will, or good-will towards any particular person, or showing the existence of any state of body or bodily feeling, are relevant, when the existence of any such state of mind or body or bodily feeling is in issue or relevant.

Statements made by a party to the proceeding, or by an agent to any such party, whom the Court regards, under the circumstances of the case, as expressly or impliedly authorized by him to make them, are admissions. Statements made by parties to suits suing or sued in a representative character, are not admissions, unless they were made while the party making them held that character. Statements made by–

An admission is a statement, oral or documentary [or contained in digital record], which suggests any inference as to any fact in issue or relevant fact, and which is made by any of the persons, and under the circumstances, hereinafter mentioned.

Section Text: Admissions are relevant and may be proved as against the person who makes them, or his representative in interest; but they can not be proved by or on behalf of the person who makes them or by his representative in interest, except in the following cases:–

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Can a party prove their own statement of intent if it is supported by believable conduct?

Rule.Section 8: Relates to facts showing motive, preparation, or conduct influenced by facts in issue.
Rule.Section 21: Bars parties from proving their own admissions except when describing a state of mind supported by improbable conduct.
Rule.Section 14: Outlines the relevancy of facts showing state of mind like intent or good faith.
Rule.Section 18: Governs admissions made by a party that are typically used against them.
Rule.Section 17: Defines what an admission is but doesn't regulate who can prove it.

Application.Section 8: Fails because the issue is the admissibility of Rahim's self-serving statement about his intent rather than the physical act of applying.
Application.Section 21: Governs directly because it provides the exception allowing Rahim to prove his own 'state of mind' when accompanied by the bank application (improbable falsehood).
Application.Section 14: Fails since identifying a state of mind as relevant doesn't override the general bar on proving one's own admissions.
Application.Section 18: Fails because it primarily addresses admissions being proved against a party, not by them for their own benefit.
Application.Section 17: Fails as it is a definition section that does not contain the specific 'state of mind' exception for self-proof.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 21, Subsection (2), Rahim can prove his own statement because it describes his state of mind and is backed by the physical act of applying for a loan.

Question: Scenario-based: Salim sees a man trying to smash the window of a house at night to enter. Thinking the man is a burglar, Salim tackles him to the ground. It turns out the man was actually the owner who had lost his keys. Can Salim be charged with assault?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Nothing is an offence which is done by a person who is, or who by reason of a mistake of fact and not by reason of a mistake of law in good faith believes himself to be, bound by law to do it.

Nothing is an offence which is done by any person who is justified by law, or who by reason of a mistake of fact and not by reason of a mistake of law in good faith, believes himself to be justified by law, in doing it.

Nothing is an offence which is done by accident or misfortune, and without any criminal intention or knowledge in the doing of a lawful act in a lawful manner by lawful means and with proper care and caution.
Nothing is an offence which is done in the exercise of the right of private defence.

The right of private defence of the body extends, under the restrictions mentioned in the last preceding section, to the voluntary causing of death or of any other harm to the assailant, if the offence which occasions the exercise of the right be of any of the descriptions hereinafter enumerated, namely: Firstly.-Such an assault as may reasonably cause the apprehension that death will otherwise be the consequence of such assault; Secondly.-Such an assault as may reasonably cause the apprehension that grievous hurt will otherwise be the consequence of such assault; Thirdly.-An assault with the intention of committing rape; Fourthly.-An assault with the intention of gratifying unnatural lust; Fifthly.-An assault with the intention of kidnapping or abducting; Sixthly.-An assault with the intention of wrongfully confining a person, under circumstances which may reasonably cause him to apprehend that he will be unable to have recourse to the public authorities for his release.

Section Text: Nothing is an offence which is done by any person who is justified by law, or who by reason of a mistake of fact and not by reason of a mistake of law in good faith, believes himself to be justified by law, in doing it.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is a private citizen liable for an act they believed was a legally justified intervention due to a mistake of fact?

Rule.Section 76: Governs mistakes of fact for persons who believe they are legally 'bound' or duty-obligated to act.
Rule.Section 79: Governs mistakes of fact where a person believes in good faith that their action is 'justified' or permitted by law.
Rule.Section 80: Governs accidental outcomes of lawful acts performed with due care and no criminal intent.
Rule.Section 96: States the general principle that legitimate acts of self-defense or defense of others are not offenses.
Rule.Section 100: Specifies the conditions under which the right of private defense allows for the use of lethal force.

Application.Section 76: Fails because Salim was a private citizen choosing to help (justified) rather than an official performing a required duty (bound).
Application.Section 79: Governs directly because Salim's mistake of fact led him to believe he was performing a legally justified citizen's arrest of a burglar.
Application.Section 80: Fails because the tackle was an intentional physical move, not an unintentional 'accident' like a tool breaking.
Application.Section 96: Fails because while defense of property is a right, Section 79 specifically provides the 'mistake of fact' umbrella for when the person being attacked is actually innocent.
Application.Section 100: Fails because the scenario does not involve lethal force or an apprehension of immediate death.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 79, Salim is not guilty because he acted in good faith based on a mistake of fact and believed his actions were justified by law.

Question: Scenario-based: Karim is accused of poisoning his neighbor. The police discovered that just three days before the death, Karim visited a remote chemical store and purchased the exact type of arsenic used in the crime. Is this purchase relevant?
