Possible Sections.Full Text: Facts which are the occasions, cause or effect, immediate or otherwise, of relevant facts, or facts in issue, or which constitute the state of things under which they happened, or which afforded an opportunity for their occurrence or transaction, are relevant.

Facts showing the existence of any state of mind, such as intention, knowledge, good faith, negligence, rashness, ill-will, or good-will towards any particular person, or showing the existence of any state of body or bodily feeling, are relevant, when the existence of any such state of mind or body or bodily feeling is in issue or relevant.

When there is a question whether an act was accidental or intentional, or done with a particular knowledge or intention, the fact that such act formed part of a series of similar occurrences, in each of which the person doing the act was concerned, is relevant.

Facts which, though not in issue, are so connected with a fact in issue as to form part of the same transaction, are relevant, whether they occurred at the same time and place or at different times and places.

Any fact is relevant which shows or constitutes a motive or preparation for any fact in issue or relevant fact. The conduct of any party, or of any agent to any party, to any suit or proceeding, in reference to such suit or proceeding, or in reference to any fact in issue therein or relevant thereto, and the conduct of any person an offence against whom is the subject of any proceeding, is relevant, if such conduct influences or is influenced by any fact, in issue or relevant fact, and whether it was previous subsequent thereto.

Section Text: Any fact is relevant which shows or constitutes a motive or preparation for any fact in issue or relevant fact. The conduct of any party, or of any agent to any party, to any suit or proceeding, in reference to such suit or proceeding, or in reference to any fact in issue therein or relevant thereto, and the conduct of any person an offence against whom is the subject of any proceeding, is relevant, if such conduct influences or is influenced by any fact, in issue or relevant fact, and whether it was previous subsequent thereto.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Does obtaining the tools for a crime constitute relevant preparation for that crime?

Rule.Section 7: Dictates that facts constituting the occasion or providing an opportunity for a crime are relevant.
Rule.Section 14: Covers the relevancy of facts showing a state of mind like intent or knowledge.
Rule.Section 15: Applies when a series of similar acts prove intent or lack of accident.
Rule.Section 6: Relates to facts forming part of the same transaction (Res Gestae).
Rule.Section 8: Specifically makes facts showing preparation for a fact in issue relevant.

Application.Section 7: Fails as the purchase of poison is an active preparatory step rather than a passive opportunity or state of things.
Application.Section 14: Fails because buying a chemical is a physical preparatory act, even if it later helps infer a state of mind.
Application.Section 15: Fails since the scenario describes a single act of procurement rather than a series of repeated poisoning attempts.
Application.Section 6: Fails because the purchase happened three days prior at a remote store, meaning it isn't part of the immediate transaction of the death.
Application.Section 8: Governs directly as procuring the specific murder weapon or instrument (arsenic) constitutes 'preparation' for the crime.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 8, the arsenic purchase is relevant because it shows preparation for the alleged crime of poisoning.

Question: Scenario-based: Salman confessed to a crime after being threatened with physical harm by an officer. Two days later, a judge formally assured him of his safety and confirmed the officer was suspended. Salman then confessed again. Is this second statement admissible?

Possible Sections.Full Text: A confession made by an accused person is irrelevant in a criminal proceeding, if the making of the confession appears to the Court to have been caused by any inducement, threat or promise having reference to the charge against the accused person, proceeding from a person in authority and sufficient, in the opinion of the Court, to give the accused person grounds which would appear to him reasonable for supposing that by making it he would gain any advantage or avoid any evil of a temporal nature in reference to the proceedings against him.
No confession made by any person whilst he is in the custody of a police-officer, unless it be made in the immediate presence of a Magistrate, shall be proved as against such person.

If such a confession as is referred to in section 24 is made after the impression caused by any such inducement, threat or promise has, in the opinion of the Court, been fully removed, it is relevant.

If such a confession is otherwise relevant, it does not become irrelevant merely because it was made under a promise of secrecy, or in consequence of a deception practised on the accused person for the purpose of obtaining it, or when he was drunk, or because it was made in answer to questions which he need not have answered, whatever may have been the form of those questions, or because he was not warned that he was not bound to make such confession, and that evidence of it might be given against him.

When more persons than one are being tried jointly for the same offence, and a confession made by one of such persons affecting himself and some other of such persons is proved, the Court may take into consideration such confession as against such other persons as well as against the person who makes such confession.

Section Text: If such a confession as is referred to in section 24 is made after the impression caused by any such inducement, threat or promise has, in the opinion of the Court, been fully removed, it is relevant.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is a confession relevant if the influence of a prior threat has been neutralized before the statement is made?

Rule.Section 24: Renders confessions irrelevant if they are caused by threats or promises from authorities.
Rule.Section 26: Bars confessions in custody unless made before a Magistrate.
Rule.Section 28: Declares a confession relevant if the impression from a prior inducement or threat has been fully removed.
Rule.Section 29: States that deception or lack of warning does not make an otherwise relevant confession inadmissible.
Rule.Section 30: Allows the court to consider one person's confession against co-defendants in a joint trial.

Application.Section 24: Fails because it only identifies the cause for exclusion, not the rule for rehabilitation of a confession after the threat ends.
Application.Section 26: Fails as the issue centers on the mental influence of a threat rather than the physical location or presence of a Magistrate.
Application.Section 28: Governs directly because the judge's formal assurance and the officer's suspension 'fully removed' the impression of the initial threat before the second confession.
Application.Section 29: Fails since the scenario involves a coercive threat (violence) rather than a simple trick or a lack of legal warning.
Application.Section 30: Fails because the problem concerns the voluntary nature of one person's statement rather than its use against others.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 28, the second confession is relevant because it was made after the judge fully removed the impression of the initial threat.

Question: Scenario-based: Salman is accused of poisoning his wife. To prove the husband had the opportunity to commit the crime, the prosecutor wants to show evidence of the victim's daily medication habits and Salman's knowledge of them. Which section makes these details relevant?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Facts which are the occasions, cause or effect, immediate or otherwise, of relevant facts, or facts in issue, or which constitute the state of things under which they happened, or which afforded an opportunity for their occurrence or transaction, are relevant.

Facts which, though not in issue, are so connected with a fact in issue as to form part of the same transaction, are relevant, whether they occurred at the same time and place or at different times and places.

Any fact is relevant which shows or constitutes a motive or preparation for any fact in issue or relevant fact. The conduct of any party, or of any agent to any party, to any suit or proceeding, in reference to such suit or proceeding, or in reference to any fact in issue therein or relevant thereto, and the conduct of any person an offence against whom is the subject of any proceeding, is relevant, if such conduct influences or is influenced by any fact, in issue or relevant fact, and whether it was previous subsequent thereto.

Facts necessary to explain or introduce a fact in issue or relevant fact, or which support or rebut an inference suggested by a fact in issue or relevant fact, or which establish the identity of anything or person whose identity is relevant, or fix the time or place at which any fact in issue or relevant fact happened or which show the relation of parties by whom any such fact was transacted, are relevant in so far as they are necessary for that purpose.

Facts showing the existence of any state of mind, such as intention, knowledge, good faith, negligence, rashness, ill-will, or good-will towards any particular person, or showing the existence of any state of body or bodily feeling, are relevant, when the existence of any such state of mind or body or bodily feeling is in issue or relevant.

Section Text: Facts which are the occasions, cause or effect, immediate or otherwise, of relevant facts, or facts in issue, or which constitute the state of things under which they happened, or which afforded an opportunity for their occurrence or transaction, are relevant.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Which section governs facts providing an opportunity for a transaction's occurrence?

Rule.Section 7: Governs the relevancy of facts that constitute the occasion, cause, or opportunity for facts in issue.
Rule.Section 6: Governs facts so connected to an issue as to form part of the same transaction.
Rule.Section 8: Governs the relevancy of facts showing motive, preparation, or subsequent conduct of the parties.
Rule.Section 9: Governs facts necessary to explain, introduce, or establish the identity or location of relevant facts.
Rule.Section 14: Governs the relevancy of facts showing the existence of a specific state of mind or body.

Application.Section 7: Governs directly because knowledge of habits specifically afforded the opportunity for the poisoning to occur.
Application.Section 6: Fails because the habits are conditions providing opportunity rather than being the crime's actual transaction.
Application.Section 8: Fails as the victim's habits do not constitute Salman's active preparation or personal motive.
Application.Section 9: Fails because the habits serve as the crime's occasion rather than introductory or explanatory context.
Application.Section 14: Fails since a victim's routine is an external state of things, not an internal mental state.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 7, Illustration (c), the victim's health and the suspect's knowledge of her habits are relevant because they afforded the husband an opportunity to administer the poison.

Question: Scenario-based: A family gave their land to a temple manager fifty years ago specifically for a charity hospital. The manager is now selling it privately. Is there any deadline to sue?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Notwithstanding anything hereinbefore contained, no suit against a person in whom property has become vested in trust for any specific purpose, or against his legal representatives or assigns (not being assigns for valuable consideration), for the purpose of following in his or their hands such property or the proceeds thereof, or for an account of such property or proceeds, shall be barred by any length of time. For the purposes of this section any property comprised in a Hindu, Muslim or Buddhist religious or charitable endowment shall be deemed to be property vested in trust for a specific purpose, and the manager of any such property shall be deemed to be the trustee thereof.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I Article 134A
Possible Sections.Full Text: To set aside a transfer of immoveable property comprised in a Hindu, Muslim or Buddhist religious or charitable endowment, made by a manager thereof for a valuable consideration. Twelve years.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I Article 134
Possible Sections.Full Text: To recover possession of immoveable property conveyed or bequeathed in trust or mortgaged and afterwards transferred by the trustee or mortgagee for a valuable consideration. Twelve years.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I Article 120
Possible Sections.Full Text: Suit for which no period of limitation is provided elsewhere in this schedule. Six years.. When the right to sue accrues.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I Article 98
Possible Sections.Full Text: To make good out of the general estate of a deceased trustee the loss occasioned by a breach of trust. Three years.. The date of the trustee's death.

Section Text: Notwithstanding anything hereinbefore contained, no suit against a person in whom property has become vested in trust for any specific purpose, or against his legal representatives or assigns (not being assigns for valuable consideration), for the purpose of following in his or their hands such property or the proceeds thereof, or for an account of such property or proceeds, shall be barred by any length of time.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is there a time limit to recover property given to an express trustee for a specific purpose?

Rule.Section 10: Removes all time limits for suits against trustees where property was specifically vested for a defined purpose.
Rule.Schedule I Article 134A: Provides a twelve-year limit for challenging land transfers made by managers of religious or charitable endowments to third parties.
Rule.Schedule I Article 134: Sets a twelve-year deadline for recovering trust property that was sold to a buyer who paid for it.
Rule.Schedule I Article 120: Functions as a six-year catch-all deadline for any lawsuit types not specifically listed in the rest of the law.
Rule.Schedule I Article 98: Limits the time to three years for suing a deceased trustee's estate to recover money lost through broken promises.

Application.Section 10: Governs directly because the land was given for a specific charitable hospital purpose, meaning the lawsuit to follow the property has no deadline.
Application.Schedule I Article 134A: Fails because it applies to suits against third-party buyers, whereas the family is suing the trustee who still holds the property.
Application.Schedule I Article 134: Fails as it limits suits against buyers, not the primary trustee who holds property for an express family-defined purpose.
Application.Schedule I Article 120: Fails because Section 10 provides a specific rule for express trusts that overrides the general catch-all category.
Application.Schedule I Article 98: Fails because the manager is still alive and the suit is for the land itself, not for money from a dead person's estate.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Limitation Act, 1908, section 10, the family can sue the manager at any time — the law says there is no time limit for cases involving property given for a specific purpose.

Question: Scenario-based: I found an old law book from England that explains how their property taxes were calculated in 1950. Can I bring this book to my trial in Dhaka to prove what the English tax law was at that time?

Possible Sections.Full Text: When the Court has to form an opinion as to the existence of any fact of a public nature, any statement of it, made in a recital contained in any Act of Parliament of the United Kingdom, or in any [Act of Parliament] or in a Government notification is a relevant fact.

An entry in any public or other official book, register or record [,or digital record], stating a fact in issue or relevant fact, and made by a public servant in the discharge of his official duty, or by any other person in performance of a duty specially enjoined by the law of the country in which such book, register [, record or digital record] is kept, is itself a relevant fact.

When the Court has to form an opinion as to a law of any country, any statement of such law contained in a book purporting to be printed or published under the authority of the Government of such country and to contain any such law, and any report of a ruling of the Courts of such country contained in a book purporting to be a report of such rulings, is relevant.

In this Act the following words and expressions are used in the following senses, unless a contrary intention appears from the context:- "Court" includes all Judges and Magistrates and all persons, except arbitrators, legally authorized to take evidence. "Fact" means and includes-

This act may be called the Evidence Act, 1872. It extends to the whole of Bangladesh and applies to all judicial proceedings in or before any Court, including Courts-martial, other than Courts-martial convened under the Army Act, 1952, the Naval Discipline Ordinance, 1961 or the Air Force Act, 1953 but not to affidavits presented to any Court or officer, nor to proceedings before an arbitrator; And it shall come into force on the first day of September, 1872.

Section Text: When the Court has to form an opinion as to a law of any country, any statement of such law contained in a book purporting to be printed or published under the authority of the Government of such country and to contain any such law, and any report of a ruling of the Courts of such country contained in a book purporting to be a report of such rulings, is relevant.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is an official foreign law book admissible to prove that country's legal rules?

Rule.Section 37: Relates to official government notifications regarding facts of a public nature.
Rule.Section 35: Governs the relevancy of entries in public records made by public servants in their line of duty.
Rule.Section 38: Grants relevancy to law books and court reports published under the authority of any foreign government.
Rule.Section 3: Provides the foundational legal definition for what constitutes a document.
Rule.Section 1: Establishes the territorial reach and commencement of the Evidence Act in Bangladesh.

Application.Section 37: Fails because tax statutes are legal principles rather than specific 'facts of a public nature' found in a domestic Gazette.
Application.Section 35: Fails as a published book is an authority rather than an internal registry entry of a government clerk.
Application.Section 38: Governs directly because the book contains statements of foreign law published under the authority of the English government.
Application.Section 3: Fails because simply being a 'document' does not grant the contents inherent relevancy for proving foreign statutes.
Application.Section 1: Fails as identifying the location of the trial doesn't provide the rule for proving foreign legal content.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 38, the book is relevant because it was published under the authority of the English government to contain their laws.

Question: Scenario-based: I claim I have a traditional right to walk across my neighbor's yard to reach the market. Can I show the judge a deed from 40 years ago where my grandfather officially bought this right from the previous owner?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Facts not otherwise relevant are relevant–
When the statement is contained in any deed, will or other document which relates to any such transaction as is mentioned in section 13, clause (a).
Where the question is as to the existence of any right of custom, the following facts are relevant:–

An entry in any public or other official book, register or record [,or digital record], stating a fact in issue or relevant fact, and made by a public servant in the discharge of his official duty, or by any other person in performance of a duty specially enjoined by the law of the country in which such book, register [, record or digital record] is kept, is itself a relevant fact.

A final judgment, order or decree of a competent Court, in the exercise of probate, matrimonial, admiralty or insolvency jurisdiction, which confers upon or takes away from any person any legal character, or which declares any person to be entitled to any such character, or to be entitled to any specific thing, not as against any specified person but absolutely, is relevant when the existence of any such legal character, or the title of any such person to any such thing, is relevant. Such judgment, order or decree is conclusive proof- that any legal character which it confers accrued at the time when such judgment, order or decree come into operation; that any legal character, to which it declares any such person to be entitled, accrued to that person at the time when such judgment, order or decree declares it to have accrued to that person; that any legal character which it takes away from any such person ceased at the time from which such judgment, order or decree declared that it had ceased or should cease; and that anything to which it declares any person to be so entitled was the property of that person at the time from which such judgment, order or decree declares that it had been or should be his property.

Section Text: Where the question is as to the existence of any right of custom, the following facts are relevant:–

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is a past transaction that created a right relevant to proving the current existence of that right?

Rule.Section 11: Determines relevancy based on the logical probability or inconsistency of a fact's occurrence.
Rule.Section 32: Governs out-of-court statements about rights found in documents when the author is deceased or unavailable.
Rule.Section 13: Governs the relevancy of any transaction by which a right or custom was created, recognized, or asserted.
Rule.Section 35: Governs entries in official public registers created by government clerks performing their official duties.
Rule.Section 41: Grants absolute finality to judgments from specialized courts regarding legal character like marriage.

Application.Section 11: Fails because the deed is historical evidence of the right's creation rather than a logical contradiction of a current claim.
Application.Section 32: Fails because it defines the hearsay exception for the document's text rather than the primary relevancy of the purchase itself.
Application.Section 13: Governs directly because the grandfather's purchase is the 'transaction' by which the right to walk was created and recognized.
Application.Section 35: Fails as a private property deed is not a formal entry in a public government registry book.
Application.Section 41: Fails since a private land sale is a transaction between parties rather than a final decree from a competent court.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 13, Clause (a), the old deed is a relevant fact because it records a transaction by which your right to the yard was created and recognized.

Question: Scenario-based: I found an old law book from the UK that explains a specific legal rule. Can I bring this book to my trial in Bangladesh to prove what that foreign law actually is?

Possible Sections.Full Text: When the Court has to form an opinion as to the existence of any fact of a public nature, any statement of it, made in a recital contained in any Act of Parliament of the United Kingdom, or in any [Act of Parliament] or in a Government notification is a relevant fact.

When the Court has to form an opinion as to a law of any country, any statement of such law contained in a book purporting to be printed or published under the authority of the Government of such country and to contain any such law, and any report of a ruling of the Courts of such country contained in a book purporting to be a report of such rulings, is relevant.

Statements of facts in issue or relevant facts, made in published [maps, charts or digital record] generally offered for public sale, or in [maps, plans or digital record] made under the authority of the Government, as to matters usually represented or stated in such [maps, charts, plans or digital record], are themselves relevant facts.

An entry in any public or other official book, register or record [,or digital record], stating a fact in issue or relevant fact, and made by a public servant in the discharge of his official duty, or by any other person in performance of a duty specially enjoined by the law of the country in which such book, register [, record or digital record] is kept, is itself a relevant fact.

This act may be called the Evidence Act, 1872. It extends to the whole of Bangladesh and applies to all judicial proceedings in or before any Court, including Courts-martial, other than Courts-martial convened under the Army Act, 1952, the Naval Discipline Ordinance, 1961 or the Air Force Act, 1953 but not to affidavits presented to any Court or officer, nor to proceedings before an arbitrator; And it shall come into force on the first day of September, 1872.

Section Text: When the Court has to form an opinion as to a law of any country, any statement of such law contained in a book purporting to be printed or published under the authority of the Government of such country and to contain any such law, and any report of a ruling of the Courts of such country contained in a book purporting to be a report of such rulings, is relevant.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is a foreign law book relevant when proving the law of another country in court?

Rule.Section 37: Outlines relevancy for facts of a public nature found in official government notifications.
Rule.Section 38: Grants relevancy to law books and court reports published under the authority of a foreign government.
Rule.Section 36: Governs the relevancy of government-authorized maps and charts.
Rule.Section 35: Governs official records created by public servants performing their official duties.
Rule.Section 1: Defines the territorial extent and commencement of the Evidence Act in Bangladesh.

Application.Section 37: Fails because legal principles are matters of law rather than specific 'facts of a public nature' found in Gazette notifications.
Application.Section 38: Governs directly because the book is a statement of foreign law published under the authority of that country's government.
Application.Section 36: Fails since the issue concerns legal text and statutes rather than territorial diagrams or maps.
Application.Section 35: Fails as a law book is a published authority rather than an internal administrative register or public record entry.
Application.Section 1: Fails because identifying the location of the trial does not provide the rule for proving foreign law.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 38, the book is relevant because it was published under the authority of the UK government and contains the law of that country.

Question: Scenario-based: A judge is about to charge me with 'Murder.' Does he have to tell me the specific section of of The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898 the law I broke, or is just telling me the name of the crime enough?

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 242
Possible Sections.Full Text: If, after such consideration and hearing as aforesaid, the Magistrate is of opinion that there is ground for presuming that the accused has committed an offence, the Magistrate shall frame a formal charge relating to the offence of which he is accused and he shall be asked whether he admits that he has committed the offence with which he is charged.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 221
Possible Sections.Full Text: (4) The law and section of the law against which the offence is said to have been committed shall be mentioned in the charge.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 222
Possible Sections.Full Text: The charge shall contain such particulars as to the time and place of the alleged offence, and the person (if any) against whom, or the thing (if any) in respect of which, it was committed, as are reasonably sufficient to give the accused notice of the matter with which he is charged. When the accused is charged with criminal breach of trust or dishonest misappropriation of money, it shall be sufficient to specify the gross sum in respect of which the offence is alleged to have been committed, and the dates between which the offence is alleged to have been committed, without specifying particular items or exact dates, and the charge so framed shall be deemed to be a charge of one offence within the meaning of section 234:

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 225
Possible Sections.Full Text: No error in stating either the offence or the particulars required to be stated in the charge, and no omission to state the offence or those particulars, shall be regarded at any stage of the case as material, unless the accused was in fact misled by such error or omission, and it has occasioned a failure of justice.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 221
Possible Sections.Full Text: Every charge under this Code shall state the offence with which the accused is charged.

Relevant Section: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 221
Section Text: (4) The law and section of the law against which the offence is said to have been committed shall be mentioned in the charge.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is it mandatory to include the specific section number in a formal criminal charge document?

Rule.Section 242: Requires only the general 'particulars' of the crime to be explained to the accused in simple summons cases.
Rule.Section 221(4): Mandates that every formal charge document must explicitly state the specific law and the section number involved.
Rule.Section 222: Directs that the charge must include details about the time, location, and victim of the alleged criminal act.
Rule.Section 225: States that small mistakes or omissions in a charge are not fatal to the case unless they mislead the accused person.
Rule.Section 221(5): Declares that the act of making a charge implies all legal requirements for that crime have been met by the facts.

Application.Section 242: Fails because it describes the 'informal' process for minor cases, whereas 'Murder' always requires a 'formal' charge under Section 221.
Application.Section 221(4): Governs directly because it contains the specific statutory command that the 'section of the law' must be mentioned in the charge.
Application.Section 222: Fails because it relates to the 'narrative' details (when and where) rather than the 'legal' details (which section number).
Application.Section 225: Fails because it describes the 'consequence' of a mistake rather than the 'requirement' for the original drafting of the charge.
Application.Section 221(5): Fails because it describes the 'legal effect' of the charge rather than the 'contents' required to make the document valid.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, section 221, Subsection (4), the judge is required to tell you both the name of the law and the specific section number you are being accused of breaking.

Cited Acts and Sections: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 221(4)

Question: Scenario-based: A man is asked to take an oath in a court of law to tell the truth. He refuses to take the oath, saying he doesn't believe in the court's authority. Can he be punished for this refusal?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Whoever refuses to bind himself by an oath or affirmation to state the truth, when required so to bind himself by a public servant legally competent to require that he shall so bind himself, shall be punished with simple imprisonment for a term which may extend to six months, or with fine which may extend to one thousand taka, or with both.

Whoever, being legally bound to state the truth on any subject to any public servant, refuses to answer any question demanded of him touching that subject by such public servant in the exercise of the legal powers of such public servant, shall be punished with simple imprisonment for a term which may extend to six months, or with fine which may extend to one thousand taka, or with both.

Whoever, being legally bound by an oath or affirmation to state the truth on any subject to any public servant or other person authorized by law to administer such oath or affirmation, makes, to such public servant or other person or as aforesaid, touching that subject, any statement which is false, and which he either knows or believes to be false or does not believe to be true, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, and shall also be liable to fine.

Whoever intentionally offers any insult, or causes any interruption to any public servant, while such public servant is sitting in any stage of a judicial proceeding, shall be punished with simple imprisonment for a term which may extend to six months, or with fine which may extend to one thousand taka, or with both.

Whoever being legally bound by an oath or by an express provision of law to state the truth, or being bound by law to make a declaration upon any subject, makes any statement which is false, and which he either knows or believes to be false or does not believe to be true, is said to give false evidence.

Section Text: Whoever refuses to bind himself by an oath or affirmation to state the truth, when required so to bind himself by a public servant legally competent to require that he shall so bind himself, shall be punished with simple imprisonment for a term which may extend to six months, or with fine which may extend to one thousand taka, or with both.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is refusing to take a required legal oath a criminal act?

Rule.Section 178: Governs the refusal to take an oath or affirmation when required by a competent public official.
Rule.Section 179: Governs the refusal to answer specific questions after an oath has already been taken.
Rule.Section 181: Governs making false statements to an official after having already taken an oath.
Rule.Section 228: Governs insults or interruptions directed at a judge or official during a trial.
Rule.Section 191: Defines the crime of giving false evidence (lying) under oath in court.

Application.Section 178: Governs directly because the man refused the 'oath' itself, which is the exact act prohibited by this section.
Application.Section 179: Fails because the man never reached the stage of being asked questions since he refused the oath that precedes them.
Application.Section 181: Fails because the man did not make a statement; he only refused the preliminary requirement to take the oath.
Application.Section 228: Fails because while his reason may be insulting, the specific legal violation is the refusal of the oath rather than general courtroom misbehavior.
Application.Section 191: Fails because the man did not lie; he refused to enter the process of giving evidence entirely.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 178, the man is guilty because he refused to bind himself by an oath to state the truth when legally required by the court.

Question: Scenario-based: Kamal wants to move his case from Chittagong to Dhaka. When is the very latest time he can ask the judge to make this transfer?

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 21
Possible Sections.Full Text: 21. No objection as to the place of suing shall be allowed by any appellate or revisional Court unless such objection was taken in the Court of first instance at the earliest possible opportunity and in all cases where issues are settled at or before such settlement, and unless there has been a consequent failure of justice.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 22
Possible Sections.Full Text: 22. Where a suit may be instituted in any one of two or more Courts and is instituted in one of such Courts, any defendant, after notice to the other parties, may, at the earliest possible opportunity and in all cases where issues are settled at or before such settlement, apply to have the suit transferred to another Court, and the Court to which such application is made, after considering the objections of the other parties (if any), shall determine in which of the several Courts having jurisdiction the suit shall proceed.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 24
Possible Sections.Full Text: 24.(1) On the application of any of the parties and after notice to the parties and after hearing such of them as desire to be heard, or of its own motion without such notice, the High Court Division or the District Court may at any stage-

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 23
Possible Sections.Full Text: 23.(1) Where the several Courts having jurisdiction are subordinate to the same Appellate Court, an application under section 22 shall be made to the Appellate Court.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 15
Possible Sections.Full Text: Every suit shall be instituted in the Court of the lowest grade competent to try it.

Relevant Section: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 22
Section Text: 22. Where a suit may be instituted in any one of two or more Courts and is instituted in one of such Courts, any defendant, after notice to the other parties, may, at the earliest possible opportunity and in all cases where issues are settled at or before such settlement, apply to have the suit transferred to another Court, and the Court to which such application is made, after considering the objections of the other parties (if any), shall determine in which of the several Courts having jurisdiction the suit shall proceed.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: What is the mandatory deadline for a defendant to apply for a case transfer?

Rule.Section 21: Prevents a party from waiting until the appeal stage to complain that the trial was held in the wrong district.
Rule.Section 22: Directs that transfer requests must be made at the first possible chance and no later than when the judge settles the issues.
Rule.Section 24: Grants superior courts broad ongoing power to move cases at any point during the proceedings for administrative reasons.
Rule.Section 23: Specifies which higher court must receive the transfer application based on the locations of the lower courts involved.
Rule.Section 15: Forces all legal actions to begin at the most junior rank of court that is authorized to handle the claim.

Application.Section 21: Fails because it addresses late objections to mistakes rather than setting the deadline for a voluntary transfer request.
Application.Section 22: Governs directly because it explicitly sets the deadline at or before the 'settlement of issues' for a defendant's application.
Application.Section 24: Does not apply here as the scenario describes a party's deadline rather than the court's general administrative authority.
Application.Section 23: Fails because it defines the destination of the paperwork rather than the deadline by which the request must be submitted.
Application.Section 15: Is inapplicable as it concerns the vertical rank of the judge rather than the horizontal move between different cities.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, section 22, Kamal must ask at the earliest possible opportunity — the application must be filed no later than when the court settles the legal issues of the case.

Cited Acts and Sections: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 22

Question: Scenario-based: Salim keeps a large, aggressive guard dog in his yard. He knows the dog is dangerous but leaves the gate open while he goes to the market. The dog runs out and scares several children on the street. Is Salim liable?

Possible Sections.Full Text: A person is guilty of a public nuisance who does any act or is guilty of an illegal omission which causes any common injury, danger or annoyance to the public or to the people in general who dwell or occupy property in the vicinity, or which must necessarily cause injury, obstruction, dangers or annoyance to persons who may have occasion to use any public right. A common nuisance is not excused on the ground that it causes some convenience or advantage.

Whoever knowingly or negligently omits to take such order with any animal in his possession as is sufficient to guard against any probable danger to human life, or any probable danger of grievous hurt from such animal, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to six months, or with fine which may extend to one thousand taka, or with both.

Whoever does any act so rashly or negligently as to endanger human life or the personal safety of others, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three months, or with fine which may extend to two hundred and fifty taka, or with both.

Whoever, with intent to cause, or knowing that he is likely to cause, wrongful loss or damage to the public or to any person, causes the destruction of any property, or any such change in any property or in the situation thereof as destroys or diminishes its value or utility, or affects it injuriously, commits "mischief".

Nothing is an offence by reason that it causes, or that it is intended to cause, or that it is known to be likely to cause, any harm, if that harm is so slight that no person of ordinary sense and temper would complain of such harm.

Section Text: Whoever knowingly or negligently omits to take such order with any animal in his possession as is sufficient to guard against probable danger to human life, or any probable danger of grievous hurt from such animal, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to six months, or with fine which may extend to five hundred taka, or with both.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is a pet owner liable for failing to secure a dangerous animal?

Rule.Section 268: Governs the broad category of public nuisance through acts or omissions causing general public danger.
Rule.Section 289: Governs the specific negligence of an owner failing to guard against the dangers posed by an animal in their possession.
Rule.Section 336: Governs general rashness endangering safety, usually for physical acts like throwing objects.
Rule.Section 425: Governs the crime of mischief, which involves the destruction or damage of property.
Rule.Section 95: Governs acts that cause only trivial or slight harm below the legal threshold for complaint.

Application.Section 268: Fails as a primary charge because the Code provides a much more specific rule for animal-related negligence in Section 289.
Application.Section 289: Governs directly because Salim's failure to close the gate was a negligent omission to 'take order' with a dangerous animal he possessed.
Application.Section 336: Fails because Section 289 is the specialized provision for animal management, taking priority over general safety rules.
Application.Section 425: Fails because the dog caused a safety risk to humans rather than being used to destroy someone else's physical property.
Application.Section 95: Fails because an aggressive guard dog loose near children represents a 'probable danger' rather than a trivial annoyance.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 289, Salim is guilty because he negligently failed to secure a dangerous animal, creating a probable risk to human safety.

Question: Scenario-based: Salim hands his gold necklace to a jeweler to be cleaned. The jeweler sells the necklace to pay his own personal debt. What crime has the jeweler committed?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Whoever, intending to take dishonestly any moveable property out of the possession of any person without that person's consent, moves that property in order to such taking, is said to commit theft.

Whoever dishonestly misappropriates or converts to his own use any moveable property, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both.

Whoever, being in any manner entrusted with property, or with any dominion over property, dishonestly misappropriates or converts to his own use that property, or dishonestly uses or disposes of that property in violation of any direction of law prescribing the mode in which such trust is to be discharged, or of any legal contract, express or implied, which he has made touching the discharge of such trust, or wilfully suffers any other person so to do, commits "criminal breach of trust".

Whoever, by deceiving any person, fraudulently or dishonestly induces the person so deceived to deliver any property to any person, or to consent that any person shall retain any property, or intentionally induces the person so deceived to do or omit to do anything which he would not do or omit if he were not so deceived, and which act or omission causes or is likely to cause damage or harm to that person in body, mind, reputation or property, is said to "cheat".
Whoever commits criminal breach of trust shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, or with fine, or with both.

Section Text: Whoever, being in any manner entrusted with property, or with any dominion over property, dishonestly misappropriates or converts to his own use that property, or dishonestly uses or disposes of that property in violation of any direction of law prescribing the mode in which such trust is to be discharged, or of any legal contract, express or implied, which he has made touching the discharge of such trust, or wilfully suffers any other person so to do, commits "criminal breach of trust".

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is misusing property given to you for a specific professional service considered theft or a breach of trust?

Rule.Section 378: Governs theft, requiring property to be taken from someone's possession without their consent.
Rule.Section 403: Governs dishonest misappropriation of found or acquired property where no formal entrustment exists.
Rule.Section 405: Governs criminal breach of trust, requiring that the property was 'entrusted' for a specific purpose.
Rule.Section 415: Governs cheating, where deception is used from the start to trick the owner into handing over property.
Rule.Section 406: Sets the three-year prison sentence for individuals convicted of criminal breach of trust.

Application.Section 378: Fails because the jeweler had Salim's consent to possess the necklace for cleaning; there was no non-consensual 'taking'.
Application.Section 403: Fails because the law provides a more specific category for property that is handed over through a professional relationship (trust).
Application.Section 405: Governs directly because the jeweler was 'entrusted' with the necklace for work and dishonestly sold it for his own use.
Application.Section 415: Fails because the jeweler received the necklace through a valid business agreement and only later decided to steal it; he didn't use a trick to get it.
Application.Section 406: Fails because it provides the penalty rather than the legal definition that classifies the jeweler's act as a crime.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 405, the jeweler is guilty of criminal breach of trust because he was entrusted with property and used it for his own benefit.

Question: Scenario-based: Salman was arrested and told the police, 'I'll tell you everything if you promise the judge will give me a light sentence.' The police agreed, and Salman confessed. Can this be used?

Possible Sections.Full Text: No confession made to a police-officer shall be proved as against a person accused of any offence.

A confession made by an accused person is irrelevant in a criminal proceeding, if the making of the confession appears to the Court to have been caused by any inducement, threat or promise having reference to the charge against the accused person, proceeding from a person in authority and sufficient, in the opinion of the Court, to give the accused person grounds which would appear to him reasonable for supposing that by making it he would gain any advantage or avoid any evil of a temporal nature in reference to the proceedings against him.
No confession made by any person whilst he is in the custody of a police-officer, unless it be made in the immediate presence of a Magistrate, shall be proved as against such person.

If such a confession is otherwise relevant, it does not become irrelevant merely because it was made under a promise of secrecy, or in consequence of a deception practised on the accused person for the purpose of obtaining it, or when he was drunk, or because it was made in answer to questions which he need not have answered, whatever may have been the form of those questions, or because he was not warned that he was not bound to make such confession, and that evidence of it might be given against him.

If such a confession as is referred to in section 24 is made after the impression caused by any such inducement, threat or promise has, in the opinion of the Court, been fully removed, it is relevant.

Section Text: A confession made by an accused person is irrelevant in a criminal proceeding, if the making of the confession appears to the Court to have been caused by any inducement, threat or promise having reference to the charge against the accused person, proceeding from a person in authority and sufficient, in the opinion of the Court, to give the accused person grounds which would appear to him reasonable for supposing that by making it he would gain any advantage or avoid any evil of a temporal nature in reference to the proceedings against him.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is a confession obtained through a promise of leniency from the police admissible?

Rule.Section 25: Bars confessions made directly to police officers regardless of any inducements used.
Rule.Section 24: Makes confessions irrelevant if they are caused by a promise of temporal advantage from a person in authority.
Rule.Section 26: Bars confessions in police custody unless a Magistrate is present during the statement.
Rule.Section 29: States that confessions obtained through secrecy or deception remain relevant if otherwise legal.
Rule.Section 28: Permits confessions once the influence of a prior threat or inducement has been removed.

Application.Section 25: Fails because while the person is a police officer, the core legal problem is the coercive promise made to the accused.
Application.Section 24: Governs directly because the police promise of a lighter sentence is a clear 'inducement' and 'temporal advantage,' making the confession irrelevant.
Application.Section 26: Fails as the presence of a Magistrate would not cure a confession that was already poisoned by a prior illegal promise.
Application.Section 29: Fails because a promise of leniency is a coercive bribe rather than a simple trick or a lack of legal warning.
Application.Section 28: Fails as the confession was made precisely while the illegal inducement was the active motivation for the suspect.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 24, the confession is irrelevant because it was obtained through a promise of advantage made by the police, who are persons in authority.

Question: Scenario-based: Salim enters into a formal agreement with a businessman to buy a warehouse. During the signing, Salim uses a fake identity to trick the businessman into agreeing to terms he would have otherwise rejected. Has Salim committed cheating?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Whoever dishonestly misappropriates or converts to his own use any moveable property, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both.

Whoever, by deceiving any person, fraudulently or dishonestly induces the person so deceived to deliver any property to any person, or to consent that any person shall retain any property, or intentionally induces the person so deceived to do or omit to do anything which he would not do or omit if he were not so deceived, and which act or omission causes or is likely to cause damage or harm to that person in body, mind, reputation or property, is said to "cheat".

A person is said to "cheat by personation" if he cheats by pretending to be some other person, or by knowingly substituting one person for another, or representing that he or any other person is a person other than he or such other person really is.
Whoever cheats shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to one year, or with fine, or with both.

Whoever cheats and thereby dishonestly induces the person deceived to deliver any property to any person, or to make, alter or destroy the whole or any part of a valuable security, or anything which is signed or sealed, and which is capable of being converted into a valuable security, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine.

Section Text: Whoever, by deceiving any person, fraudulently or dishonestly induces the person so deceived to deliver any property to any person, or to consent that any person shall retain any property, or intentionally induces the person so deceived to do or omit to do anything which he would not do or omit if he were not so deceived, and which act or omission causes or is likely to cause damage or harm to that person in body, mind, reputation or property, is said to "cheat".

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Does using deception to induce someone into a contract they would have otherwise avoided constitute the legal definition of cheating?

Rule.Section 403: Governs the dishonest misuse of moveable property that was already in the offender's possession.
Rule.Section 415: Defines cheating as using deception to induce an act or omission that causes harm or wouldn't otherwise occur.
Rule.Section 416: Specifies a particular sub-type of cheating where the deception involves assuming a false identity.
Rule.Section 417: Governs the basic punishment for simple cheating where no property is delivered.
Rule.Section 420: Governs aggravated cheating where the victim is induced to hand over property or sign valuable securities.

Application.Section 403: Fails because the act involves a deceptive contract negotiation rather than the misappropriation of existing moveable goods.
Application.Section 415: Governs directly because it defines the core elements of deception and induced behavior that characterize Salim's actions.
Application.Section 416: Fails because while it describes the 'method' (personation), Section 415 is the foundational definition that establishes the act as 'cheating'.
Application.Section 417: Fails because it is a penalty provision rather than the definition requested to categorize the behavior.
Application.Section 420: Fails because the scenario centers on the inducement into the contract terms rather than the actual delivery of the warehouse itself.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 415, Salim has committed cheating because he used deception to induce the businessman to perform an act (signing) he would not have otherwise done.

Question: Scenario-based: Salim is caught in the market with a sack of very high-quality fake coins that he made using a machine at his home. He was about to spend them at various stalls. What is the most severe punishment Salim could face?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Whoever counterfeits or knowingly performs any part of the process of counterfeiting coin, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine.

Whoever counterfeits, or knowingly performs any part of the process of counterfeiting Bangladesh coin, shall be punished with imprisonment for life, or with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine.

Whoever is in possession of any instrument or material, for the purpose of using the same for counterfeiting coin, or knowing or having reason to believe that the same is intended to be used for that purpose, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, and shall also be liable to fine; and if the coin to be counterfeited is Bangladesh coin, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine.

Whoever, having any counterfeit coin, which is a counterfeit of Bangladesh coin, and which, at the time when he became possessed of it, he knew to be a counterfeit of Bangladesh coin, fraudulently or with intent that fraud may be committed, delivers the same to any person, or attempts to induce any person to receive it, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine.

Whoever, fraudulently or with intent that fraud may be committed, is in possession of counterfeit coin, which is a counterfeit of Bangladesh coin, having known at the time when he became possessed of it that it was counterfeit, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine.

Section Text: Whoever counterfeits, or knowingly performs any part of the process of counterfeiting Bangladesh coin, shall be punished with imprisonment for life, or with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: What is the maximum penalty for the act of manufacturing fake national currency?

Rule.Section 231: Governs the general offense of counterfeiting any type of coin, with a seven-year penalty.
Rule.Section 232: Governs the counterfeiting specifically of 'Bangladesh coin', allowing for a maximum of life imprisonment.
Rule.Section 235: Governs the possession of tools and machines designed for counterfeiting coin.
Rule.Section 240: Governs the actual delivery or spending of fake Bangladesh coins while knowing they are counterfeit.
Rule.Section 243: Governs the simple possession of fake Bangladesh coins with a fraudulent intent.

Application.Section 231: Fails because Salim made Bangladesh takas (coins), which triggers a higher penalty category.
Application.Section 232: Governs directly because the act of 'making' (counterfeiting) national currency is the primary and most severe offense in the scenario.
Application.Section 235: Fails because although he used a machine, the scenario focuses on the completed coins he produced, which is a more serious crime.
Application.Section 240: Fails because Salim was caught before he could actually 'deliver' or spend the coins at the market stalls.
Application.Section 243: Fails because while he was in possession, he was the actual 'maker' of the coins, making Section 232 the more appropriate and severe charge.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 232, Salim faces up to life imprisonment or ten years in prison because he was caught counterfeiting Bangladesh coins.

Question: Scenario-based: A witness is too ill to leave their bed. Can the judge send an official to record their statement for the case?

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 30
Possible Sections.Full Text: Subject to such conditions and limitations as may be prescribed, the Court may, at any time, either of its own motion or on the application of any party,- (a) make such orders as may be necessary or reasonable in all matters relating to the delivery and answering of interrogatories, the admission of documents and facts, and the discovery, inspection, production, impounding and return of documents or other material objects producible as evidence; (b) issue summonses to persons whose attendance is required either to give evidence or to produce documents or such other objects as aforesaid; (c) order any fact to be proved by affidavit.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 75
Possible Sections.Full Text: Subject to such conditions and limitations as may be prescribed, the Court may issue a commission- (a) to examine any person; (b) to make a local investigation; (c) to examine or adjust accounts; or (d) to make a partition.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Order XXVI, Rule 1
Possible Sections.Full Text: Any Court may in any suit issue a commission for the examination on interrogatories or otherwise of any person resident within the local limits of its jurisdiction who is exempted under this Code from attending the Court or who is from sickness or infirmity unable to attend it.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 32
Possible Sections.Full Text: The Court may compel the attendance of any person to whom a summons has been issued under section 30 and for that purpose may- (a) issue a warrant for his arrest; (b) attach and sell his property; (c) impose a fine upon him not exceeding five hundred Taka; (d) order him to furnish security for his appearance and in default commit him to the civil prison.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 132
Possible Sections.Full Text: (1) Women who, according to the customs and manners of the country, ought not to be compelled to appear in public shall be exempt from personal appearance in Court. (2) Nothing herein contained shall be deemed to exempt such women from arrest in execution of civil process in any case in which the arrest of women is not prohibited by this Code.

Relevant Section: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 75
Section Text: Subject to such conditions and limitations as may be prescribed, the Court may issue a commission- (a) to examine any person; (b) to make a local investigation; (c) to examine or adjust accounts; or (d) to make a partition.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Does the Code provide authority to examine a witness through a commission outside the courtroom?

Rule.Section 30: Provides the general power to call witnesses to the courthouse using a standard summons.
Rule.Section 75: Grants the primary legislative power to the court to issue 'commissions' for tasks like examining people outside court.
Rule.Order XXVI, Rule 1: Describes the specific procedural circumstances, such as illness, that justify using a commission.
Rule.Section 32: Governs the use of force (arrest warrants) to make a witness physically attend the trial.
Rule.Section 132: Exempts certain women from showing up in public but does not address general illness.

Application.Section 30: Fails because it assumes the witness is capable of coming to court, which is impossible due to illness.
Application.Section 75: Governs directly because Clause (a) is the substantive enabling provision allowing a commission to examine a person.
Application.Order XXVI, Rule 1: Is a procedural rule that details the 'how', but Section 75 remains the primary source of 'power' in the Code.
Application.Section 32: Is inapplicable because the witness is physically unable to move; an arrest warrant would not solve the medical impossibility.
Application.Section 132: Does not apply because the witness's excuse is physical health rather than cultural custom or gender.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, section 75, Clause (a), the judge can do this — the court has the power to issue a commission to examine a person who cannot come to the courtroom.

Cited Acts and Sections: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 75(a)
source_file: Q-As copy/English/Code of Civil Procedure/Advanced selection

Question: Scenario-based: Salman identified a suspect in a police lineup. To confirm the identity, can the prosecutor show the judge that the suspect has a distinctive red scar on his left arm, just as Salman described?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Any fact is relevant which shows or constitutes a motive or preparation for any fact in issue or relevant fact. The conduct of any party, or of any agent to any party, to any suit or proceeding, in reference to such suit or proceeding, or in reference to any fact in issue therein or relevant thereto, and the conduct of any person an offence against whom is the subject of any proceeding, is relevant, if such conduct influences or is influenced by any fact, in issue or relevant fact, and whether it was previous subsequent thereto.

Facts necessary to explain or introduce a fact in issue or relevant fact, or which support or rebut an inference suggested by a fact in issue or relevant fact, or which establish the identity of anything or person whose identity is relevant, or fix the time or place at which any fact in issue or relevant fact happened or which show the relation of parties by whom any such fact was transacted, are relevant in so far as they are necessary for that purpose.

Facts showing the existence of any state of mind, such as intention, knowledge, good faith, negligence, rashness, ill-will, or good-will towards any particular person, or showing the existence of any state of body or bodily feeling, are relevant, when the existence of any such state of mind or body or bodily feeling is in issue or relevant.
Facts not otherwise relevant are relevant–

In this Act the following words and expressions are used in the following senses, unless a contrary intention appears from the context:- "Court" includes all Judges and Magistrates and all persons, except arbitrators, legally authorized to take evidence. "Fact" means and includes-

Section Text: Facts necessary to explain or introduce a fact in issue or relevant fact, or which support or rebut an inference suggested by a fact in issue or relevant fact, or which establish the identity of anything or person whose identity is relevant, or fix the time or place at which any fact in issue or relevant fact happened or which show the relation of parties by whom any such fact was transacted, are relevant in so far as they are necessary for that purpose.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is a distinctive physical mark relevant as evidence to establish a person's identity?

Rule.Section 8: Governs motive, preparation, and the conduct of parties influenced by facts in issue during a crime.
Rule.Section 9: Declares that facts necessary to establish the identity of a person whose identity is relevant are considered relevant evidence.
Rule.Section 14: Outlines the relevancy of facts showing internal state of mind like intent or good faith.
Rule.Section 11: Determines relevancy based on logical inconsistency or high probability of an event.
Rule.Section 3: Statutorily defines what qualifies as a 'fact' in the legal sense but not the rules for identity.

Application.Section 8: Fails because a physical scar is an inherent physical trait rather than a suspect's active preparation or motive.
Application.Section 9: Governs directly because the scar is a fact 'necessary to establish the identity' of the suspect who Salman picked out in the lineup.
Application.Section 14: Fails since a red scar is a physical bodily mark rather than an internal mental state like intention or ill-will.
Application.Section 11: Fails because Section 9 provides the specific mechanism for identifying persons rather than using broad probability logic.
Application.Section 3: Fails since defining the scar as a 'fact' does not establish the specific rule for identifying a suspect in court.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 9, the red scar is relevant because it is a fact necessary to establish the identity of the person picked out in the lineup.

Question: Scenario-based: A neighbor is about to cut down my family's heritage tree. The court issued an order preventing him from doing so. What is the official name for this category of relief under the Act?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Preventive relief is granted at the discretion of the Court by injunction, temporary or perpetual.

Temporary injunctions are such as are to continue until a specified time, or until the further order of the Court. They may be granted at any period of a suit, and are regulated by the Code of Civil Procedure. A perpetual injunction can only be granted by the decree made at the hearing and upon the merits of the suit: the defendant is thereby perpetually enjoined from the assertion of a right, or from the commission of an act, which would be contrary to the rights of the plaintiff.
Specific relief granted under clause (c) of section 5 is called preventive relief.

Subject to the other provisions contained in, or referred to by, this Chapter, a perpetual injunction may be granted to prevent the breach of an obligation existing in favour of the applicant, whether expressly or by implication. When such obligation arises from contract, the Court shall be guided by the rules and provisions contained in Chapter II of this Act. When the defendant invades or threatens to invade the plaintiff's right to, or enjoyment of, property, the Court may grant a perpetual injunction in the following cases (namely):- (a) where the defendant is trustee of the property for the plaintiff; (b) where there exists no standard for ascertaining the actual damage caused, or likely to be caused, by the invasion; (c) where the invasion is such that pecuniary compensation would not afford adequate relief; (d) where it is probable that pecuniary compensation cannot be got for the invasion; (e) where the injunction is necessary to prevent a multiplicity of judicial proceedings. Explanation - For the purpose of this section a trademark is property.

In this Act, unless there be something repugnant in the subject or context,- "obligation" includes every duty enforceable by law: "trust" includes every species of express, implied, or constructive fiduciary ownership: "trustee" includes every person holding, expressly, by implication, or constructively, a fiduciary character:

Section Text: Specific relief granted under clause (c) of section 5 is called preventive relief.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: What is the statutory designation for relief intended to prevent a party from performing a prohibited act?

Rule.Section 52: Specifies that the court grants preventive relief using either temporary or permanent injunction orders.
Rule.Section 53: Explains the difference between short-term orders and final judgments in injunction cases.
Rule.Section 6: Provides the formal definition for relief that stops an act, naming it 'preventive relief'.
Rule.Section 54: Provides the substantive rules for when a perpetual injunction can be granted to protect property.
Rule.Section 3: Defines core legal concepts such as obligation and duty for interpreting the Act.

Application.Section 52: Fails as it explains 'how' the relief is given rather than providing the official 'name' of the category.
Application.Section 53: Fails because it addresses the duration of orders rather than the naming of the relief type.
Application.Section 6: Governs directly because it is the specific section that defines stop-orders as 'preventive relief'.
Application.Section 54: Fails because it addresses the specific conditions for property protection rather than naming the overall category.
Application.Section 3: Fails as a definition section that doesn't name categories of court-granted relief.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Specific Relief Act, 1877, section 6, this is officially called preventive relief — the law uses this name for any help given to stop someone from doing an act.

Question: Scenario-based: Salim finds a stray cow on the road. He takes it home and feeds it for three days. On the fourth day, he decides to sell the cow and keep the money. Is he guilty of theft?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Whoever, intending to take dishonestly any moveable property out of the possession of any person without that person's consent, moves that property in order to such taking, is said to commit theft.

Whoever dishonestly misappropriates or converts to his own use any moveable property, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both.

Whoever dishonestly receives or retains any stolen property, knowing or having reason to believe the same to be stolen property, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, or with fine, or with both.

Whoever commits mischief by killing, poisoning, maiming or rendering useless, any elephant, camel, horse, mule, buffalo, bull, cow or ox, whatever may be the value thereof, or any other animal of the value of fifty taka or upwards, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to five years, or with fine, or with both.

The right of private defence of property commences when a reasonable apprehension of danger to the property commences. The right of private defence of property against theft continues till the offender has effected his retreat with the property or either the assistance of the public authorities is obtained, or the property has been recovered. The right of private defence of property against robbery continues as long as the offender causes or attempts to cause to any person death or hurt or wrongful restraint or as long as the fear of instant death or of instant hurt or of instant personal restraint continues. The right of private defence of property against criminal trespass or mischief continues as long as the offender continues in the commission of criminal trespass or mischief. The right of private defence of property against house-breaking by night continues as long as the house-trespass which has been begun by such house-breaking continues.

Section Text: Whoever dishonestly misappropriates or converts to his own use any moveable property, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is selling a found animal after a period of care considered theft or misappropriation?

Rule.Section 105: Governs the duration of the right of private defense of property during a theft.
Rule.Section 378: Governs theft, requiring property to be taken from the owner's active possession with immediate dishonest intent.
Rule.Section 403: Governs dishonest misappropriation of found property, specifically when a person chooses to keep or sell items they found.
Rule.Section 411: Governs the receipt or retention of property that was already stolen by a third party.
Rule.Section 429: Governs mischief involving animals, focusing on killing or injuring them rather than selling them.

Application.Section 105: Fails because it defines a right of defense and does not define the crime committed by Salim.
Application.Section 378: Fails because the cow was stray (lost) and not in the owner's possession when Salim took it; he also lacked dishonest intent at the start.
Application.Section 403: Governs directly because Explanation 2 covers property found in public and punishes the decision to 'convert' it to one's own use (selling it).
Application.Section 411: Fails because the cow was lost, not stolen by someone else before Salim found it.
Application.Section 429: Fails because Salim sold the cow for money rather than killing or poisoning it.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 403, Explanation 2, Salim is guilty of dishonest misappropriation because he sold the cow for his own profit after finding it.

Question: Scenario-based: I found an old inscription on a stone boundary pillar on my farm that says 'Limit of District Council Road.' Can I use a photo of this stone in court as a 'document'?

Possible Sections.Full Text: An entry in any public or other official book, register or record [,or digital record], stating a fact in issue or relevant fact, and made by a public servant in the discharge of his official duty, or by any other person in performance of a duty specially enjoined by the law of the country in which such book, register [, record or digital record] is kept, is itself a relevant fact.

In this Act the following words and expressions are used in the following senses, unless a contrary intention appears from the context:- "Court" includes all Judges and Magistrates and all persons, except arbitrators, legally authorized to take evidence. "Fact" means and includes-

Statements, written or verbal, of relevant facts made by a person who is dead, or who cannot be found, or who has become incapable of giving evidence, or whose attendance cannot be procured without an amount of delay or expense which under the circumstances of the case appears to the Court unreasonable, are themselves relevant facts in the following cases:–

Statements of facts in issue or relevant facts, made in published [maps, charts or digital record] generally offered for public sale, or in [maps, plans or digital record] made under the authority of the Government, as to matters usually represented or stated in such [maps, charts, plans or digital record], are themselves relevant facts.

**"May presume"** Whenever it is provided by this Act that the Court may presume a fact, it may either regard such fact as proved, unless and until it is disproved, or may call for proof of it: **“Shall presume”** Whenever it is directed by this Act that the Court shall presume a fact, it shall regard such fact as proved, unless and until it is disproved: **“Conclusive proof”** When one fact is declared by this Act to be conclusive proof of another, the Court shall, on proof of the one fact, regard the other as proved, and shall not allow evidence to be given for the purpose of disproving it.

Section Text: "Document" means any matter expressed or described upon any substance by means of letters, figures or marks, or by more than one of those means, intended to be used, or which may be used, for the purpose of recording that matter [and includes any digital record]. A writing is a document: Words printed, lithographed or photographed are documents: A map or plan is a document: An inscription on a metal plate or stone is a document: A caricature is a document.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is an inscription on a stone legally defined as a 'document'?

Rule.Section 35: Governs the relevancy of entries in official books made by public servants.
Rule.Section 3: Defines documents to include any matter on any substance, specifically naming stone inscriptions.
Rule.Section 32: Governs out-of-court family statements found on tombstones or portraits.
