Rule.Section 36: Relates to the relevancy of government-authorized maps and plans.
Rule.Section 4: Outlines the legal definitions of presumptions used during a trial.

Application.Section 35: Fails because an inscription on a pillar is not a formal book or registry kept by a clerk.
Application.Section 3: Governs directly because the definition explicitly lists an 'inscription on a... stone' as a document.
Application.Section 32: Fails as the pillar concerns a district boundary rather than family kinship or lineages.
Application.Section 36: Fails because a physical boundary stone is not a published chart offered for public sale.
Application.Section 4: Fails because the question is about a definition rather than the weight of a presumption.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 3, the inscription on the stone is legally considered a 'document.' The definition of document explicitly includes any inscription on a stone.

Question: Scenario-based: Salim is participating in a high-stakes local election. He knows his opponent is a very honest person, but Salim tells a large crowd that the opponent was once jailed for theft in another district. He knows this is a lie but wants to win. Is this a crime?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Whoever with intent to affect the result of an election makes or publishes any statement purporting to be a statement of fact which is false and which he either knows or believes to be or does not believe to be true, in relation to the personal character or conduct of any candidate shall be punished with fine.

Whoever by words either spoken or intended to be read, or by signs or by visible representations, makes or published any imputation concerning any person intending to harm, or knowing or having reason to believe that such imputation will harm, the reputation or such person, is said, except in the cases hereinafter excepted, to defame that person.
Whoever gives to any public servant any information which he knows or believes to be false, intending thereby to cause, or knowing it to be likely that he will thereby cause, such public servant—

(1) Whoever voluntarily interferes or attempts to interfere with the free exercise of any electoral right commits the offence of undue influence at an election. (2) Without prejudice to the generality of the provisions of sub-section (1), whoever− (3) A declaration of public policy or a promise of public action, or the mere exercise of a legal right without intent to interfere with an electoral right, shall not be deemed to be interference within the meaning of this section.

Whoever being legally bound by an oath or by an express provision of law to state the truth, or being bound by law to make a declaration upon any subject, makes any statement which is false, and which he either knows or believes to be false or does not believe to be true, is said to give false evidence.

Section Text: Whoever with intent to affect the result of an election makes or publishes any statement purporting to be a statement of fact which is false and which he either knows or believes to be or does not believe to be true, in relation to the personal character or conduct of any candidate shall be punished with fine.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is spreading false information about a candidate's character during an election a specific offense?

Rule.Section 171G: Governs false statements made with the specific intent to influence election results by targeting a candidate's character.
Rule.Section 499: Governs the general offense of defamation involving harm to reputation in non-electoral contexts.
Rule.Section 182: Governs providing false information to public servants to induce them to act to someone's detriment.
Rule.Section 171C: Governs undue influence at elections through interference with the free exercise of voting rights.
Rule.Section 191: Governs the giving of false evidence while under a legal obligation or oath to tell the truth.

Application.Section 171G: Governs directly because Salim's lie about the opponent's past was a false statement of fact intended to affect the election result.
Application.Section 499: Fails because although the act is defamatory, Section 171G is the lex specialis that specifically covers character attacks during elections.
Application.Section 182: Fails because Salim spoke to a public crowd rather than providing false information to a specific public servant in their official capacity.
Application.Section 171C: Fails because spreading a rumor, while dishonest, does not constitute the 'interference' or 'threat' required for undue influence.
Application.Section 191: Fails because Salim was not under a formal oath or legal obligation to state the truth while addressing a political crowd.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 171G, Salim is guilty because he published a false statement regarding a candidate's personal character with the intent to affect the result of an election.

Question: Scenario-based: Salman is accused of robbing a store. He offers to prove that he actually went to the police station right after the robbery happened to report that he saw the thief running away. Can he tell this to the court?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Any fact is relevant which shows or constitutes a motive or preparation for any fact in issue or relevant fact. The conduct of any party, or of any agent to any party, to any suit or proceeding, in reference to such suit or proceeding, or in reference to any fact in issue therein or relevant thereto, and the conduct of any person an offence against whom is the subject of any proceeding, is relevant, if such conduct influences or is influenced by any fact, in issue or relevant fact, and whether it was previous subsequent thereto.

Facts showing the existence of any state of mind, such as intention, knowledge, good faith, negligence, rashness, ill-will, or good-will towards any particular person, or showing the existence of any state of body or bodily feeling, are relevant, when the existence of any such state of mind or body or bodily feeling is in issue or relevant.

Admissions are relevant and may be proved as against the person who makes them, or his representative in interest; but they can not be proved by or on behalf of the person who makes them or by his representative in interest, except in the following cases:–

Statements, written or verbal, of relevant facts made by a person who is dead, or who cannot be found, or who has become incapable of giving evidence, or whose attendance cannot be procured without an amount of delay or expense which under the circumstances of the case appears to the Court unreasonable, are themselves relevant facts in the following cases:–

Facts which are the occasions, cause or effect, immediate or otherwise, of relevant facts, or facts in issue, or which constitute the state of things under which they happened, or which afforded an opportunity for their occurrence or transaction, are relevant.

Section Text: Any fact is relevant which shows or constitutes a motive or preparation for any fact in issue or relevant fact. The conduct of any party, or of any agent to any party, to any suit or proceeding, in reference to such suit or proceeding, or in reference to any fact in issue therein or relevant thereto, and the conduct of any person an offence against whom is the subject of any proceeding, is relevant, if such conduct influences or is influenced by any fact, in issue or relevant fact, and whether it was previous subsequent thereto.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is a prompt report of a crime by a party relevant conduct in their favor?

Rule.Section 8: Governs the relevancy of a party's conduct influenced by facts in issue, including making a complaint.
Rule.Section 14: Governs relevancy of facts showing state of mind like intent or good faith.
Rule.Section 21: Bars parties from proving their own admissions except in specific rare cases.
Rule.Section 32: Governs out-of-court statements from deceased or unavailable persons.
Rule.Section 7: Dictates that facts providing the cause, effect, or occasion for a crime are relevant.

Application.Section 8: Governs directly because reporting the crime to the police is 'conduct' influenced by the event, which is relevant under Illustration (k).
Application.Section 14: Fails because the report is a physical act of conduct rather than an internal state of mind itself.
Application.Section 21: Fails as the report is offered as relevant 'conduct' rather than a communicative 'admission' of guilt.
Application.Section 32: Fails since Salman is alive and the issue is his real-time behavior rather than a dying declaration.
Application.Section 7: Fails since the report happened after the event rather than being its prior occasion or physical effect.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 8, Illustration (k), the fact that you went to the police and made a report is relevant because it is conduct influenced by the incident.

Question: Scenario-based: Five people are participating in an illegal gathering to block a public road. One member gets frustrated and hits a passerby with a stick. Is every member of the group now guilty of 'rioting'?

Possible Sections.Full Text: An assembly of five or more persons is designated an "unlawful assembly," if the common object of the persons composing that assembly is: First.-To overawe by criminal force, or show of criminal force, Government or Legislature, or any public servant in the exercise of the lawful power of such public servant; or Second.-To resist the execution of any law, or of any legal process; or Third.-To commit any mischief or criminal trespass, or other offence; or Fourth.-By means of criminal force, or show of criminal force, to any person to take or obtain possession of any property, or to deprive any person of the enjoyment of a right of way, or of the use of water or other incorporeal right of which he is in possession or enjoyment, or to enforce any right or supposed right; or Fifth.-By means of criminal force, or show of criminal force, to compel any person to do what he is not legally bound to do, or to omit to do what he is legally entitled to do.
Whoever is a member of an unlawful assembly, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to six months, or with fine, or with both.

Whenever force or violence is used by an unlawful assembly, or by any member thereof, in prosecution of the common object of such assembly, every member of such assembly is guilty of the offence of rioting.

If an offence is committed by any member of an unlawful assembly in prosecution of the common object of that assembly, or such as the members of that assembly knew to be likely to be committed in prosecution of that object, every person who, at the time of the committing of that offence, is a member of the same assembly, is guilty of that offence.

Whoever, except in the case provided for by section 334, voluntarily causes hurt, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to one year, or with fine which may extend to one thousand taka, or with both.

Section Text: Whenever force or violence is used by an unlawful assembly, or by any member thereof, in prosecution of the common object of such assembly, every member of such assembly is guilty of the offence of rioting.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Does the use of force by one member of an unlawful group make all members liable for rioting?

Rule.Section 141: Governs the definition of an unlawful assembly based on its common illegal objective.
Rule.Section 143: Governs the punishment for simple membership in a peaceful but illegal gathering.
Rule.Section 146: Governs the specific definition of rioting, which is triggered as soon as any member of the assembly uses force.
Rule.Section 149: Governs joint liability for 'any other offence' committed by a member during the assembly's activities.
Rule.Section 323: Governs the substantive punishment for the individual act of causing physical hurt.

Application.Section 141: Fails because it defines the group's status rather than the specific offense of 'rioting' caused by violence.
Application.Section 143: Fails because once force is used, the charge elevates from simple membership to the more serious crime of rioting.
Application.Section 146: Governs directly because the use of the stick (force) by one member in prosecution of the goal makes everyone guilty of rioting.
Application.Section 149: Fails because rioting is the primary group offense defined by the act of force itself; Section 149 is usually for separate crimes like murder or theft.
Application.Section 323: Fails because it only targets the person who hit with the stick, while the question asks about the group's collective liability.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 146, every member is guilty of rioting because when any member of an unlawful assembly uses force or violence, the whole group is held responsible.

Question: Scenario-based: Salim is lighting large, professional-grade fireworks in a narrow alleyway surrounded by people. He doesn't want to hit anyone, but the fireworks are pashing very close to shoppers. Has he committed a crime?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Whoever causes hurt to any person by doing any act so rashly or negligently as to endanger human life, or the personal safety of others shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to six months, or with fine which may extend to five hundred taka, or with both.

Whoever does any act so rashly or negligently as to endanger human life or the personal safety of others, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three months, or with fine which may extend to two hundred and fifty taka, or with both.

Whoever does, with fire or any combustible matter, any act so rashly or negligently as to endanger human life, or to be likely to cause hurt or injury to any other person, or knowingly or negligently omits to take such order with any fire or any combustible matter in his possession as is sufficient to guard against any probable danger to human life from such fire or combustible matter, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to six months, or with fine which may extend to one thousand taka, or with both.

A person is guilty of a public nuisance who does any act or is guilty of an illegal omission which causes any common injury, danger or annoyance to the public or to the people in general who dwell or occupy property in the vicinity, or which must necessarily cause injury, obstruction, dangers or annoyance to persons who may have occasion to use any public right. A common nuisance is not excused on the ground that it causes some convenience or advantage.

Whoever makes any gesture, or any preparation intending or knowing it to be likely that such gesture or preparation will cause any person present to apprehend that he who makes that gesture or preparation is about to use criminal force to that person, is said to commit an assault.

Section Text: Whoever does any act so rashly or negligently as to endanger human life or the personal safety of others, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three months, or with fine which may extend to two hundred and fifty taka, or with both.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is performing a dangerous act that risks safety a crime if no one is actually hurt?

Rule.Section 337: Governs rash and negligent acts specifically when they result in an actual physical 'hurt'.
Rule.Section 336: Governs rash and negligent acts that 'endanger' safety or life, regardless of whether injury occurs.
Rule.Section 285: Governs negligence specifically with 'fire' or 'combustibles', carrying the same six-month penalty.
Rule.Section 268: Defines public nuisance broadly, focusing on community-wide annoyance or general danger.
Rule.Section 351: Governs assault, which requires a specific gesture making an individual fear immediate physical force.

Application.Section 337: Fails because the scenario does not state that any shopper was actually hit or physically injured by the fireworks.
Application.Section 336: Governs directly because lighting heavy fireworks in a crowded alley is a 'rash act' that 'endangers safety' even without a successful hit.
Application.Section 285: Governs partly, but Section 336 is the standard broader charge for the danger posed by the 'act' of fireworks in a crowd.
Application.Section 268: Fails as a primary charge because the Code has more specific provisions for rash acts that risk life than the general nuisance rule.
Application.Section 351: Fails because Salim's recklessness was general rather than a specific physical gesture intended to threaten a single person.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 336, Salim is guilty because he performed a rash act that endangered the personal safety of others.

Question: Scenario-based: Salim borrows an expensive camera from his friend for a weekend trip. While on the trip, he sells the camera to a tourist and keeps the money for himself. What is the correct charge?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Whoever, intending to take dishonestly any moveable property out of the possession of any person without that person's consent, moves that property in order to such taking, is said to commit theft.

Whoever dishonestly misappropriates or converts to his own use any moveable property, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both.

Whoever, being in any manner entrusted with property, or with any dominion over property, dishonestly misappropriates or converts to his own use that property, or dishonestly uses or disposes of that property in violation of any direction of law prescribing the mode in which such trust is to be discharged, or of any legal contract, express or implied, which he has made touching the discharge of such trust, or wilfully suffers any other person so to do, commits "criminal breach of trust".

Whoever, by deceiving any person, fraudulently or dishonestly induces the person so deceived to deliver any property to any person, or to consent that any person shall retain any property, or intentionally induces the person so deceived to do or omit to do anything which he would not do or omit if he were not so deceived, and which act or omission causes or is likely to cause damage or harm to that person in body, mind, reputation or property, is said to "cheat".

Whoever dishonestly receives or retains any stolen property, knowing or having reason to believe the same to be stolen property, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, or with fine, or with both.

Section Text: Whoever, being in any manner entrusted with property, or with any dominion over property, dishonestly misappropriates or converts to his own use that property, or dishonestly uses or disposes of that property in violation of any direction of law prescribing the mode in which such trust is to be discharged, or of any legal contract, express or implied, which he has made touching the discharge of such trust, or wilfully suffers any other person so to do, commits "criminal breach of trust".

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is selling property that was voluntarily given to you for a specific purpose considered theft or breach of trust?

Rule.Section 378: Governs theft, requiring a taking 'out of the possession' of the owner without consent.
Rule.Section 403: Governs dishonest misappropriation of items found or acquired without a formal trust relationship.
Rule.Section 405: Governs criminal breach of trust, triggered when property is 'entrusted' to someone who then misappropriates it.
Rule.Section 415: Governs cheating, where the owner is tricked into handing over property through deception from the start.
Rule.Section 411: Governs the receipt of goods that have already been stolen by someone else.

Application.Section 378: Fails because the friend gave the camera to Salim voluntarily, so there was no taking 'out of possession' without consent.
Application.Section 403: Fails because Section 405 is the more specific rule for property that is 'entrusted' to a friend rather than just found on the street.
Application.Section 405: Governs directly because the camera was 'entrusted' to Salim for a trip, and he dishonestly sold it (converted it to his own use).
Application.Section 415: Fails because Salim didn't use a trick to get the camera; he borrowed it honestly and only later decided to sell it.
Application.Section 411: Fails because Salim was the original borrower, not a third party buying a stolen camera from someone else.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 405, Salim is guilty of criminal breach of trust because he was entrusted with the camera and dishonestly sold it for his own benefit.

Question: Scenario-based: A man is legally required by an official to sign a statement he just gave. He refuses to sign it because he wants to be able to deny it later. Can he be punished for this refusal?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Whoever refuses to bind himself by an oath or affirmation to state the truth, when required so to bind himself by a public servant legally competent to require that he shall so bind himself, shall be punished with simple imprisonment for a term which may extend to six months, or with fine which may extend to one thousand taka, or with both.

Whoever, being legally bound to state the truth on any subject to any public servant, refuses to answer any question demanded of him touching that subject by such public servant in the exercise of the legal powers of such public servant, shall be punished with simple imprisonment for a term which may extend to six months, or with fine which may extend to one thousand taka, or with both.

Whoever refuses to sign any statement made by him, when required to sign that statement by a public servant legally competent to require that he shall sign that statement, shall be punished with simple imprisonment for a term which may extend to three months, or with fine which may extend to five hundred taka, or with both.

Whoever voluntarily obstructs any public servant in the discharge of his public functions, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three months, or with fine which may extend to five hundred taka, or with both.

Whoever, being legally bound to furnish information on any subject to any public servant, as such, furnishes, as true, information on the subject which he knows or has reason to believe to be false, shall be punished with simple imprisonment for a term which may extend to six months, or with fine which may extend to one thousand taka, or with both; or, if the information which he is legally bound to give respects the commission of an offence, or is required for the purpose of preventing the commission of an offence, or in order to the apprehension of an offender, with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both.

Section Text: Whoever refuses to sign any statement made by him, when required to sign that statement by a public servant legally competent to require that he shall sign that statement, shall be punished with simple imprisonment for a term which may extend to three months, or with fine which may extend to five hundred taka, or with both.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is refusing to sign one's own official statement a criminal act?

Rule.Section 178: Governs the refusal to take the initial oath to tell the truth.
Rule.Section 179: Governs the refusal to answer specific questions while under a legal duty to do so.
Rule.Section 180: Governs the refusal to sign a statement made to a competent public official when required to do so.
Rule.Section 186: Governs the general physical or verbal obstruction of a public servant's duties.
Rule.Section 177: Governs the act of providing false information in a required report.

Application.Section 178: Fails because the issue is about signing a finished statement, not taking the oath that starts the process.
Application.Section 179: Fails because the man already answered the questions (made the statement); he is now only refusing the signature.
Application.Section 180: Governs directly because the refusal to sign a statement as required by an official is the specific act prohibited by this section.
Application.Section 186: Fails because the law provides a more specific rule for signing statements compared to general obstruction of officials.
Application.Section 177: Fails because the man's refusal to sign does not mean the information inside the statement was necessarily false.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 180, the man is guilty because he refused to sign his own statement after being legally required to do so by a public servant.

Question: Scenario-based: A man is convicted of an offense where the law allows for a fine but does not state a maximum amount. Can the judge impose a fine so large that it bankrupts the offender?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Where no sum is expressed to which a fine may extend, the amount of fine to which the offender is liable is unlimited, but shall not be excessive.

In every case of an offence punishable with imprisonment as well as fine, in which the offender is sentenced to a fine, whether with or without imprisonment, and in every case of an offence punishable with imprisonment or fine, or with fine only, in which the offender is sentenced to a fine, it shall be competent to the Court which sentences such offender to direct by the sentence that, in default of payment of the fine, the offender shall suffer imprisonment for a certain term, which imprisonment shall be in excess of any other imprisonment to which he may have been sentenced or to which he may be liable under a commutation of a sentence.

If the offence be punishable with fine only, the imprisonment which the Court imposes in default of payment of the fine shall be simple, and the term for which the Court directs the offender to be imprisoned, in default of payment of fine, shall not exceed the following scale, that is to say, for any term not exceeding two months when the amount of the fine shall not exceed fifty taka, and for any term not exceeding four months when the amount shall not exceed one hundred taka, and for any term not exceeding six months in any other case.

The fine, or any part thereof which remains unpaid, may be levied at any time within six years after the passing of the sentence, and if, under the sentence, the offender be liable to imprisonment for a longer period than six years, then at any time previous to the expiration of that period; and the death of the offender does not discharge from the liability of any property which would, after his death, be legally liable for his debts.

The punishments to which offenders are liable under the provisions of this Code are,- Firstly,- Death; Secondly,- [Imprisonment for life]; Thirdly,-[Omitted by the Criminal Law (Extinction of Discriminatory Privileges) Act 1949 (Act No. II of 1950]. Fourthly,-Imprisonment, which is of two descriptions, namely:-

Section Text: Where no sum is expressed to which a fine may extend, the amount of fine to which the offender is liable is unlimited, but shall not be excessive.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is there a limit on a fine when the statute does not specify a maximum sum?

Rule.Section 63: Governs the amount of fine when unspecified, allowing it to be unlimited but prohibiting it from being excessive.
Rule.Section 64: Governs the court's authority to impose jail time if an offender fails to pay a court-ordered fine.
Rule.Section 67: Governs the scale of imprisonment for non-payment specifically for offenses that only carry a fine.
Rule.Section 70: Governs the time limit within which the state must collect an unpaid fine from an offender.
Rule.Section 53: Governs the categorization of fines as one of the six primary legal punishments.

Application.Section 63: Governs directly because it explicitly restricts 'unlimited' fines from becoming 'excessive', preventing the judge from bankrupting the offender.
Application.Section 64: Fails because it deals with the consequence of not paying rather than the rules for determining the fine's amount.
Application.Section 67: Fails because it regulates the jail sentence in default of payment, not the initial financial assessment.
Application.Section 70: Fails because it concerns the collection process over time rather than the initial judicial determination of the amount.
Application.Section 53: Fails because it is a general definition and lacks the specific restrictive language regarding the quantum of the fine.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 63, the fine can be unlimited if no sum is expressed, but the law strictly mandates that the amount shall not be excessive.

Question: Scenario-based: Minutes after a shop was robbed, a bystander pointed at Salman and yelled, 'The police are coming for the thief!' Salman immediately dropped his bag and ran away. Is this behavior relevant?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Facts which are the occasions, cause or effect, immediate or otherwise, of relevant facts, or facts in issue, or which constitute the state of things under which they happened, or which afforded an opportunity for their occurrence or transaction, are relevant.

Any fact is relevant which shows or constitutes a motive or preparation for any fact in issue or relevant fact. The conduct of any party, or of any agent to any party, to any suit or proceeding, in reference to such suit or proceeding, or in reference to any fact in issue therein or relevant thereto, and the conduct of any person an offence against whom is the subject of any proceeding, is relevant, if such conduct influences or is influenced by any fact, in issue or relevant fact, and whether it was previous subsequent thereto.

Facts necessary to explain or introduce a fact in issue or relevant fact, or which support or rebut an inference suggested by a fact in issue or relevant fact, or which establish the identity of anything or person whose identity is relevant, or fix the time or place at which any fact in issue or relevant fact happened or which show the relation of parties by whom any such fact was transacted, are relevant in so far as they are necessary for that purpose.

Facts not otherwise relevant are relevant– (1) If they are inconsistent with any fact in issue or relevant fact; (2) If by themselves or in connection with other facts they make the existence or non-existence of any fact in issue or relevant fact highly probable or improbable.

Admissions are relevant and may be proved as against the person who makes them, or his representative in interest; but they can not be proved by or on behalf of the person who makes them or by his representative in interest, except in the following cases:–

Section Text: Any fact is relevant which shows or constitutes a motive or preparation for any fact in issue or relevant fact. The conduct of any party, or of any agent to any party, to any suit or proceeding, in reference to such suit or proceeding, or in reference to any fact in issue therein or relevant thereto, and the conduct of any person an offence against whom is the subject of any proceeding, is relevant, if such conduct influences or is influenced by any fact, in issue or relevant fact, and whether it was previous subsequent thereto.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is a suspect's flight after being warned of police arrival relevant subsequent conduct?

Rule.Section 7: Governs the relevancy of facts that are the cause or effect of an event.
Rule.Section 8: Governs the relevancy of a party's conduct when influenced by facts in issue or relevant facts.
Rule.Section 9: Governs facts used to explain behavior or establish the identity of persons or objects.
Rule.Section 11: Governs the relevancy of facts that make an issue highly probable or improbable.
Rule.Section 21: Governs the rules for proving admissions against the party that made them.

Application.Section 7: Fails because running away is an active reaction to a warning, not a physical effect of the robbery itself.
Application.Section 8: Governs directly as Salman's flight is subsequent conduct influenced by the robbery and the bystander's warning.
Application.Section 9: Fails since the flight is primary evidence of conduct rather than a detail needed to introduce another fact.
Application.Section 11: Fails because Section 8 provides the specific rule for conduct influenced by the crime.
Application.Section 21: Fails as the act of running away is conduct, not a communicative statement constituting an admission.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 8, Illustration (f), Salman's act of running away after being told the police were coming is relevant as conduct influenced by the fact in issue.

Question: Scenario-based: Salim is infected with a dangerous, contagious disease. He intentionally takes a crowded public bus to another city, knowing his presence will likely spread the infection to others. Is he guilty of a crime?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Whoever malignantly does any act which is, and which he knows or has reason to believe to be, likely to spread the infection of any disease dangerous to life, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both.

Whoever unlawfully or negligently does any act which is, and which he knows or has reason to believe to be, likely to spread the infection of any disease dangerous to life, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to six months, or with fine, or with both.

A person is guilty of a public nuisance who does any act or is guilty of an illegal omission which causes any common injury, danger or annoyance to the public or to the people in general who dwell or occupy property in the vicinity, or which must necessarily cause injury, obstruction, dangers or annoyance to persons who may have occasion to use any public right. A common nuisance is not excused on the ground that it causes some convenience or advantage.

Whoever knowingly disobeys any rule made and promulgated by the Government for putting any vessel into a state of quarantine, or for regulating the intercourse of vessels in a state of quarantine with the shore or with other vessels, or for regulating the intercourse between places where an infectious disease prevails and other places, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to six months, or with fine, or with both.

Whoever does any act so rashly or negligently as to endanger human life or the personal safety of others, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three months, or with fine which may extend to two hundred and fifty taka, or with both.

Section Text: Whoever unlawfully or negligently does any act which is, and which he knows or has reason to believe to be, likely to spread the infection of any disease dangerous to life, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to six months, or with fine, or with both.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is it a crime to act in a way that is likely to spread a dangerous disease through negligence?

Rule.Section 270: Governs 'malignant' acts (hateful intent) that spread disease, carrying a harsher two-year sentence.
Rule.Section 269: Governs 'unlawful or negligent' acts that are likely to spread a life-threatening infection, carrying a six-month sentence.
Rule.Section 268: Defines public nuisance broadly but does not provide specific language for disease transmission.
Rule.Section 271: Governs the specific violation of established quarantine rules for vessels and travel.
Rule.Section 336: Governs general rash acts that endanger safety but is less specific than the disease-focused sections.

Application.Section 270: Fails because Salim acted with 'intent' to travel (negligence) rather than with a 'malignant' or hateful plan to deliberately poison others.
Application.Section 269: Governs directly because Salim's act of taking a bus while infected was 'unlawful' or 'negligent' and likely to spread a life-threatening disease.
Application.Section 268: Fails as a primary charge because the Code has a more specialized rule for disease risk than the general nuisance provision.
Application.Section 271: Fails because there is no mention of a formal quarantine order or a vessel in the scenario.
Application.Section 336: Fails because Section 269 is the specialized provision for public health risks involving infection, overriding the general safety rule.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 269, Salim is guilty because he performed a negligent act that he knew was likely to spread a life-threatening infection to others.

Question: Scenario-based: An employee admitted to stealing cash from a drawer. However, he only spoke because his boss promised, 'If you confess now, I won't report this to the police and you can keep working here.' Is this confession admissible?

Possible Sections.Full Text: No confession made to a police-officer shall be proved as against a person accused of any offence.

A confession made by an accused person is irrelevant in a criminal proceeding, if the making of the confession appears to the Court to have been caused by any inducement, threat or promise having reference to the charge against the accused person, proceeding from a person in authority and sufficient, in the opinion of the Court, to give the accused person grounds which would appear to him reasonable for supposing that by making it he would gain any advantage or avoid any evil of a temporal nature in reference to the proceedings against him.
No confession made by any person whilst he is in the custody of a police-officer, unless it be made in the immediate presence of a Magistrate, shall be proved as against such person.

If such a confession as is referred to in section 24 is made after the impression caused by any such inducement, threat or promise has, in the opinion of the Court, been fully removed, it is relevant.

If such a confession is otherwise relevant, it does not become irrelevant merely because it was made under a promise of secrecy, or in consequence of a deception practised on the accused person for the purpose of obtaining it, or when he was drunk, or because it was made in answer to questions which he need not have answered, whatever may have been the form of those questions, or because he was not warned that he was not bound to make such confession, and that evidence of it might be given against him.

Section Text: A confession made by an accused person is irrelevant in a criminal proceeding, if the making of the confession appears to the Court to have been caused by any inducement, threat or promise having reference to the charge against the accused person, proceeding from a person in authority and sufficient, in the opinion of the Court, to give the accused person grounds which would appear to him reasonable for supposing that by making it he would gain any advantage or avoid any evil of a temporal nature in reference to the proceedings against him.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is a confession obtained through a promise of job security and immunity by an employer admissible in court?

Rule.Section 25: Bars all confessions made directly to police officers from being proved against an accused.
Rule.Section 24: Excludes confessions if obtained through a promise of temporal advantage from a person in authority.
Rule.Section 26: Bars confessions in custody unless a Magistrate is physically present during the statement.
Rule.Section 28: Permits confessions once the influence of a prior threat or inducement has been completely removed from the mind.
Rule.Section 29: Governs confessions made under social deception or secrecy, which remain relevant if otherwise legal.

Application.Section 25: Fails because the confession was made to a private employer in an office rather than a police officer.
Application.Section 24: Governs directly because the boss is a person in authority and the promise of continued employment is a clear temporal advantage.
Application.Section 26: Fails since the employee was at work speaking to his boss rather than being under arrest or in police custody.
Application.Section 28: Fails because the confession was given while the employer's promise was the active and ongoing motivation for the employee.
Application.Section 29: Fails as the scenario involves a coercive promise of benefit (saving the job) rather than a simple trick or a lack of legal warning.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 24, the confession is irrelevant because it was obtained through a promise of advantage made by the boss, who is a person in authority.

Question: Scenario-based: I have a contract with a seller, but I also have other legal rights to the property that aren't mentioned in the contract. Does this Act take away my other rights just because I can't sue for specific performance?

Possible Sections.Full Text: In this Act, unless there be something repugnant in the subject or context,- "obligation" includes every duty enforceable by law: "trust" includes every species of express, implied, or constructive fiduciary ownership: "trustee" includes every person holding, expressly, by implication, or constructively, a fiduciary character:

Except where it is herein otherwise expressly enacted, nothing in this Act shall be deemed- (a) to give any right to relief in respect of any agreement which is not a contract; (b) to deprive any person of any right to relief, other than specific performance, which he may have under any contract; or (c) to affect the operation of the [Registration Act, 1908] on documents.
Specific relief cannot be granted for the mere purpose of enforcing a penal law.

Except as otherwise provided in this Chapter, the specific performance of any contract may in the discretion of the Court be enforced- (a) when the act agreed to be done is in the performance, wholly or partly, of a trust; (b) when there exists no standard for ascertaining the actual damage caused by non-performance of the act agreed to be done; (c) when the act agreed to be done is such that pecuniary compensation for its non-performance would not afford adequate relief; or (d) when it is probable that pecuniary compensation cannot be got for the non-performance of the act agreed to be done. Explanation - Unless and until the contrary is proved, the Court shall presume that the breach of a contract to transfer immoveable property cannot be adequately relieved by compensation in money, and that the breach of a contract to transfer moveable property can be thus relieved.

The dismissal of a suit for specific performance of a contract or part thereof shall bar the plaintiff's right to sue for compensation for the breach after dismissal of such contract or part, as the case may be.

Section Text: Except where it is herein otherwise expressly enacted, nothing in this Act shall be deemed- (a) to give any right to relief in respect of any agreement which is not a contract; (b) to deprive any person of any right to relief, other than specific performance, which he may have under any contract; or (c) to affect the operation of the [Registration Act, 1908] on documents.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Does the Act remove non-performance related relief rights available under a contract?

Rule.Section 3: Defines core legal terms such as obligation and trust for interpretation purposes.
Rule.Section 4: Saves existing legal rights from being abolished by the Act, except for specific performance.
Rule.Section 7: Prohibits using civil specific relief solely to implement criminal or penal legislation.
Rule.Section 12: Outlines the judicial power to enforce contracts where money is an insufficient remedy.
Rule.Section 29: Prevents a subsequent suit for money if a performance suit is dismissed.

Application.Section 3: Fails as it provides definitions rather than protecting other existing legal remedies.
Application.Section 4: Governs directly because Clause (b) explicitly prevents the Act from depriving persons of non-performance relief.
Application.Section 7: Fails because the scenario concerns private civil rights rather than criminal law enforcement.
Application.Section 12: Fails because it addresses when performance 'can' be granted, not the survival of other rights.
Application.Section 29: Fails as it limits future lawsuits rather than protecting current alternative rights under contract.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Specific Relief Act, 1877, section 4, Clause (b), the law does not take away your other rights — it only regulates specific performance while leaving your other contractual remedies intact.

Question: Scenario-based: Rahim passed away last month. I found an old business letter he wrote where he admits to receiving Taka 10,000 for a land deal. Can I use this letter in court to prove the money was paid?

Possible Sections.Full Text: An admission is a statement, oral or documentary [or contained in digital record], which suggests any inference as to any fact in issue or relevant fact, and which is made by any of the persons, and under the circumstances, hereinafter mentioned.

Entries in books of account [or digital record], regularly kept in the course of business, are relevant whenever they refer to a matter into which the Court has to inquire, but such statements shall not alone be sufficient evidence to charge any person with liability.

Statements, written or verbal, of relevant facts made by a person who is dead, or who cannot be found, or who has become incapable of giving evidence, or whose attendance cannot be procured without an amount of delay or expense which under the circumstances of the case appears to the Court unreasonable, are themselves relevant facts in the following cases:–
When there is a question whether a particular act was done, the existence of any course of business, according to which it naturally would have been done, is a relevant fact.

Admissions are relevant and may be proved as against the person who makes them, or his representative in interest; but they can not be proved by or on behalf of the person who makes them or by his representative in interest, except in the following cases:–

Section Text: Statements, written or verbal, of relevant facts made by a person who is dead, or who cannot be found, or who has become incapable of giving evidence, or whose attendance cannot be procured without an amount of delay or expense which under the circumstances of the case appears to the Court unreasonable, are themselves relevant facts in the following cases:– (2) When the statement was made by such person in the ordinary course of business, and in particular when it consists of any entry or memorandum made by him in books kept in the ordinary course of business, or in the discharge of professional duty; or of an acknowledgement written or signed by him of the receipt of money, goods, securities or property of any kind; or of a document used in commerce written or signed by him; or of the date of a letter or other document usually dated, written or signed by him.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is a deceased person's business letter admissible to prove receipt of money?

Rule.Section 17: Defines admissions as statements or records suggesting an inference about a fact in issue.
Rule.Section 34: Determines that account book entries are relevant but insufficient on their own to establish liability.
Rule.Section 32: Governs the relevancy of out-of-court statements made by deceased persons, including those made for business.
Rule.Section 16: Specifies that the existence of a routine business process is a relevant fact.
Rule.Section 21: Sets the rules for when a person can prove their own admissions or those of others.

Application.Section 17: Fails because it defines the nature of an admission but does not provide the hearsay exception for deceased individuals.
Application.Section 34: Fails because the issue concerns the admissibility of a specific letter rather than the weight of a formal account ledger.
Application.Section 32: Governs directly because the author is dead and the statement is a signed business acknowledgement of money received.
Application.Section 16: Fails since the goal is to prove a specific payment via a statement rather than proving a general routine occurred.
Application.Section 21: Fails because it regulates the parties who can prove admissions rather than creating the exception for the deceased.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 32, Subsection (2), Rahim's letter is relevant because it was a written acknowledgement of money received, made by a person who is now deceased.

Question: Scenario-based: Rahim, a factory clerk, passed away. I found a notebook where he recorded daily raw material deliveries. Can I use these notes in court to prove a shipment arrived on a specific date?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Entries in books of account [or digital record], regularly kept in the course of business, are relevant whenever they refer to a matter into which the Court has to inquire, but such statements shall not alone be sufficient evidence to charge any person with liability.

Statements, written or verbal, of relevant facts made by a person who is dead, or who cannot be found, or who has become incapable of giving evidence, or whose attendance cannot be procured without an amount of delay or expense which under the circumstances of the case appears to the Court unreasonable, are themselves relevant facts in the following cases:–
When there is a question whether a particular act was done, the existence of any course of business, according to which it naturally would have been done, is a relevant fact.

An entry in any public or other official book, register or record [,or digital record], stating a fact in issue or relevant fact, and made by a public servant in the discharge of his official duty, or by any other person in performance of a duty specially enjoined by the law of the country in which such book, register [, record or digital record] is kept, is itself a relevant fact.

Admissions are relevant and may be proved as against the person who makes them, or his representative in interest; but they can not be proved by or on behalf of the person who makes them or by his representative in interest, except in the following cases:–

Section Text: Statements, written or verbal, of relevant facts made by a person who is dead, or who cannot be found, or who has become incapable of giving evidence, or whose attendance cannot be procured without an amount of delay or expense which under the circumstances of the case appears to the Court unreasonable, are themselves relevant facts in the following cases:– (2) When the statement was made by such person in the ordinary course of business, and in particular when it consists of any entry or memorandum made by him in books kept in the ordinary course of business, or in the discharge of professional duty; or of an acknowledgement written or signed by him of the receipt of money, goods, securities or property of any kind; or of a document used in commerce written or signed by him; or of the date of a letter or other document usually dated, written or signed by him.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Are a deceased clerk's professional notes admissible as evidence?

Rule.Section 34: Governs the relevancy and weight of commercial account books kept by live parties.
Rule.Section 32: Permits out-of-court business entries as evidence when the person who wrote them is deceased.
Rule.Section 16: Relates to the existence of a routine to infer that an act was performed.
Rule.Section 35: Governs official entries made by government officers in public registers during their duty.
Rule.Section 21: Determines which parties are permitted to prove an admission in a legal trial.

Application.Section 34: Fails because it regulates the sufficiency of accounting evidence rather than the hearsay exception for the dead.
Application.Section 32: Governs directly as the clerk is deceased and the entry was a memorandum made in his professional duty.
Application.Section 16: Fails since the goal is to admit the actual written note rather than proving a general routine.
Application.Section 35: Fails as a private factory notebook is not an official government register kept by a public servant.
Application.Section 21: Fails because the clerk is a third party whose unavailability triggers the specific rules of Section 32.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 32, Subsection (2), Rahim's notes are relevant because they are entries made in the ordinary course of professional duty by a person who has since died.

Question: Scenario-based: Salim does an act that is illegal just to provoke a group of men, knowing that his provocation is very likely to cause them to start a riot. If the riot actually happens, what is the maximum jail term Salim faces?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Whoever knowingly joins or continues in any assembly of five or more persons likely to cause a disturbance of the public peace, after such assembly has been lawfully commanded to disperse, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to six months, or with fine, or with both.

Whoever malignantly, or wantonly, by doing anything which is illegal, gives provocation to any person intending or knowing it to be likely that such provocation will cause the offence of rioting to be committed, shall, if the offence of rioting be committed in consequence of such provocation, be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to one year, or with fine, or with both; and if the offence of rioting be not committed, with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to six months, or with fine, or with both.
Whoever is guilty of rioting, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both.

Whoever by words, either spoken or written, or by signs, or by visible representations, or otherwise, promotes or attempts to promote feelings of enmity or hatred between different classes of the citizens of Bangladesh, shall be punished with imprisonment which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both.

Whoever abets the commission of an offence by the public generally or by any number or class of persons exceeding ten, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, or with fine, or with both.

Section Text: Whoever malignantly, or wantonly, by doing anything which is illegal, gives provocation to any person intending or knowing it to be likely that such provocation will cause the offence of rioting to be committed, shall, if the offence of rioting be committed in consequence of such provocation, be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to one year, or with fine, or with both; and if the offence of rioting be not committed, with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to six months, or with fine, or with both.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: What is the penalty for illegally provoking a person into committing a riot if the riot occurs?

Rule.Section 151: Governs the refusal to leave a group after being ordered by police to break up.
Rule.Section 153: Governs illegal provocation intended to cause rioting, providing a one-year limit if the riot succeeds.
Rule.Section 147: Governs the punishment for the rioters themselves who actually use force.
Rule.Section 153A: Governs the promotion of communal hatred between different social or religious classes.
Rule.Section 117: Governs the instigation of a large group of more than ten people to commit a crime.

Application.Section 151: Fails because it deals with dispersal orders rather than the initial act of provocation that starts a riot.
Application.Section 153: Governs directly because Salim's 'illegal act' was intended to provoke a riot, which was 'committed in consequence', triggering the one-year penalty.
Application.Section 147: Fails because it punishes the physical violence of the riot, while Section 153 is the specific rule for the 'provoker' who didn't necessarily fight.
Application.Section 153A: Fails because there is no mention of Salim targeting classes of people; he only provoked individuals to riot.
Application.Section 117: Fails because while similar to abetment, Section 153 is the precise provision for riots caused by illegal provocation.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 153, Salim can be punished with a maximum of one year in prison if a riot is actually committed because of his illegal provocation.

Question: Scenario-based: I am in a private arbitration meeting with a business partner to settle a land dispute. Can my lawyer cite the Evidence Act to prevent the other side from showing a fake receipt during the arbitration?

Possible Sections.Full Text: In this Act the following words and expressions are used in the following senses, unless a contrary intention appears from the context:- "Court" includes all Judges and Magistrates and all persons, except arbitrators, legally authorized to take evidence.

**"May presume"** Whenever it is provided by this Act that the Court may presume a fact, it may either regard such fact as proved, unless and until it is disproved, or may call for proof of it: **“Shall presume”** Whenever it is directed by this Act that the Court shall presume a fact, it shall regard such fact as proved, unless and until it is disproved: **“Conclusive proof”** When one fact is declared by this Act to be conclusive proof of another, the Court shall, on proof of the one fact, regard the other as proved, and shall not allow evidence to be given for the purpose of disproving it.
Evidence may be given in any suit or proceeding of the existence or non-existence of every fact in issue and of such other fact as are hereinafter declared to be relevant, and of no others.

This act may be called the Evidence Act, 1872. It extends to the whole of Bangladesh and applies to all judicial proceedings in or before any Court, including Courts-martial, other than Courts-martial convened under the Army Act, 1952, the Naval Discipline Ordinance, 1961 or the Air Force Act, 1953 but not to affidavits presented to any Court or officer, nor to proceedings before an arbitrator; And it shall come into force on the first day of September, 1872.

An admission is a statement, oral or documentary [or contained in digital record], which suggests any inference as to any fact in issue or relevant fact, and which is made by any of the persons, and under the circumstances, hereinafter mentioned.

Section Text: This act may be called the Evidence Act, 1872. It extends to the whole of Bangladesh and applies to all judicial proceedings in or before any Court, including Courts-martial, other than Courts-martial convened under the Army Act, 1952, the Naval Discipline Ordinance, 1961 or the Air Force Act, 1953 but not to affidavits presented to any Court or officer, nor to proceedings before an arbitrator; And it shall come into force on the first day of September, 1872.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Does the Evidence Act, 1872 apply to proceedings conducted before a private arbitrator?

Rule.Section 3: Defines 'Court' but explicitly excludes arbitrators from the list of authorized evidence-takers.
Rule.Section 4: Defines judicial presumptions but does not establish the Act's scope of application.
Rule.Section 5: Permits evidence of relevant facts in suits but depends on the Act actually being applicable to the forum.
Rule.Section 1: States the Act applies to judicial proceedings in court but specifically excludes proceedings before an arbitrator.
Rule.Section 17: Defines what constitutes an admission in a legal sense.

Application.Section 3: Fails because it only defines the term 'Court' rather than establishing the overall negative scope of the entire Act's application.
Application.Section 4: Fails as it governs the logic of proof within a trial rather than whether the Act applies to a specific forum like arbitration.
Application.Section 5: Fails because it assumes the Act is already in force for the proceeding, which is the exact question being debated.
Application.Section 1: Governs directly because it explicitly lists 'proceedings before an arbitrator' as an area where the Evidence Act does not apply.
Application.Section 17: Fails as the definition of admission is a rule of content, not a rule of jurisdictional coverage.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 1, the Act does not apply because the law specifically excludes proceedings before an arbitrator from its rules.

Question: Scenario-based: Salman is accused of stealing a car. Can the prosecutor show that Salman was spotted hiding a set of car-theft tools in a nearby ditch just an hour after the vehicle went missing?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Facts which are the occasions, cause or effect, immediate or otherwise, of relevant facts, or facts in issue, or which constitute the state of things under which they happened, or which afforded an opportunity for their occurrence or transaction, are relevant.

Any fact is relevant which shows or constitutes a motive or preparation for any fact in issue or relevant fact. The conduct of any party, or of any agent to any party, to any suit or proceeding, in reference to such suit or proceeding, or in reference to any fact in issue therein or relevant thereto, and the conduct of any person an offence against whom is the subject of any proceeding, is relevant, if such conduct influences or is influenced by any fact, in issue or relevant fact, and whether it was previous subsequent thereto.

Facts showing the existence of any state of mind, such as intention, knowledge, good faith, negligence, rashness, ill-will, or good-will towards any particular person, or showing the existence of any state of body or bodily feeling, are relevant, when the existence of any such state of mind or body or bodily feeling is in issue or relevant.

When there is a question whether an act was accidental or intentional, or done with a particular knowledge or intention, the fact that such act formed part of a series of similar occurrences, in each of which the person doing the act was concerned, is relevant.

Facts which, though not in issue, are so connected with a fact in issue as to form part of the same transaction, are relevant, whether they occurred at the same time and place or at different times and places.

Section Text: Any fact is relevant which shows or constitutes a motive or preparation for any fact in issue or relevant fact. The conduct of any party, or of any agent to any party, to any suit or proceeding, in reference to such suit or proceeding, or in reference to any fact in issue therein or relevant thereto, and the conduct of any person an offence against whom is the subject of any proceeding, is relevant, if such conduct influences or is influenced by any fact, in issue or relevant fact, and whether it was previous subsequent thereto.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is the subsequent conduct of concealing crime tools relevant evidence against an accused person?

Rule.Section 7: Governs facts that are the cause, effect, or occasion of a crime.
Rule.Section 8: Governs the relevancy of a party's conduct influenced by facts in issue, including concealing objects used in the crime.
Rule.Section 14: Governs the relevancy of facts demonstrating an internal state of mind such as intent or ill-will.
Rule.Section 15: Applies when a series of similar acts prove that an event was intentional rather than an accident.
Rule.Section 6: Makes facts that form part of the same transaction as a fact in issue legally relevant.

Application.Section 7: Fails because hiding tools is an active subsequent reaction rather than a passive 'occasion' or physical 'effect' of the theft.
Application.Section 8: Governs directly because hiding the theft tools is subsequent 'conduct' influenced by the theft, as explicitly stated in Illustration (i).
Application.Section 14: Fails because the physical act of concealing tools is conduct, even though it may suggest an underlying state of mind.
Application.Section 15: Fails as the scenario involves a single act of concealment rather than a repetitive pattern of crimes used to prove intent.
Application.Section 6: Fails since the act of hiding the tools occurred an hour later at a different location rather than being part of the immediate theft transaction.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 8, Illustration (i), hiding the tools is relevant because it is conduct influenced by the crime and involves attempting to conceal objects used to commit it.

Question: Scenario-based: Karim told a witness, 'I'll confess to the burglary if you promise to give me Tk. 10,000 for my family.' If the witness was a police officer in plain clothes who agreed to the deal, is the confession admissible?

Possible Sections.Full Text: No confession made to a police-officer shall be proved as against a person accused of any offence.

A confession made by an accused person is irrelevant in a criminal proceeding, if the making of the confession appears to the Court to have been caused by any inducement, threat or promise having reference to the charge against the accused person, proceeding from a person in authority and sufficient, in the opinion of the Court, to give the accused person grounds which would appear to him reasonable for supposing that by making it he would gain any advantage or avoid any evil of a temporal nature in reference to the proceedings against him.
No confession made by any person whilst he is in the custody of a police-officer, unless it be made in the immediate presence of a Magistrate, shall be proved as against such person.

If such a confession is otherwise relevant, it does not become irrelevant merely because it was made under a promise of secrecy, or in consequence of a deception practised on the accused person for the purpose of obtaining it, or when he was drunk, or because it was made in answer to questions which he need not have answered, whatever may have been the form of those questions, or because he was not warned that he was not bound to make such confession, and that evidence of it might be given against him.

Provided that, when any fact is deposed to as discovered in consequence of information received from a person accused of any offence, in the custody of a police officer, so much of such information, whether it amounts to a confession or not, as relates distinctly to the fact thereby discovered, may be proved.

Section Text: A confession made by an accused person is irrelevant in a criminal proceeding, if the making of the confession appears to the Court to have been caused by any inducement, threat or promise having reference to the charge against the accused person, proceeding from a person in authority and sufficient, in the opinion of the Court, to give the accused person grounds which would appear to him reasonable for supposing that by making it he would gain any advantage or avoid any evil of a temporal nature in reference to the proceedings against him.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is a confession obtained through a financial bribe by an undercover officer admissible?

Rule.Section 25: Creates an absolute bar for any confession made directly to a police officer.
Rule.Section 24: Makes confessions irrelevant if caused by an inducement or promise of advantage from a person in authority.
Rule.Section 26: Bars confessions in police custody unless a Magistrate is physically present during the statement.
Rule.Section 29: States that a relevant confession does not become irrelevant just because it was obtained through deception.
Rule.Section 27: Allows custodial information that leads to the physical discovery of evidence to be proved in court.

Application.Section 25: Fails because the suspect believed he was speaking to a private citizen rather than a formal police officer.
Application.Section 24: Governs directly as the officer is a person in authority and the cash bribe is a clear temporal advantage.
Application.Section 26: Fails because the primary legal defect is the coercive inducement rather than the physical status of custody.
Application.Section 29: Fails since a bribe is a coercive inducement of advantage, which is a stronger ground for exclusion than mere deception.
Application.Section 27: Fails because the scenario describes a verbal exchange for money rather than the discovery of physical objects.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 24, the confession is irrelevant because it was caused by a promise of advantage made by a person in authority (the undercover officer).

Question: Scenario-based: Jamil hits Rahim with a heavy stick, causing a deep bone fracture in Rahim's leg. Which specific kind of 'hurt' has Jamil committed under the law?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Whoever causes bodily pain, disease or infirmity to any person is said to cause hurt.

The following kinds of hurt only are designated as "grievous":— Firstly.—Emasculation. Secondly.—Permanent privation of the sight of either eye. Thirdly.—Permanent privation of the hearing of either ear. Fourthly.—Privation of any member or joint. Fifthly.—Destruction or permanent impairing of the powers of any member or joint. Sixthly.—Permanent disfiguration of the head or face. Seventhly.—Fracture or dislocation of a bone or tooth. Eighthly.—Any hurt which endangers life or which causes the sufferer to be during the space of twenty days in severe bodily pain, or unable to follow his ordinary pursuits.

Whoever, except in the case provided for by section 334, voluntarily causes hurt, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to one year, or with fine which may extend to one thousand taka, or with both.

Whoever, except in the case provided for by section 335, voluntarily causes grievous hurt, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine.

Whoever, except in the case provided for by section 334, voluntarily causes hurt by means of any instrument for shooting, stabbing or cutting, or any instrument which, used as a weapon of offence, is likely to cause death, or by means of fire or any heated substance, or by means of any poison or any corrosive substance, or by means of any explosive substance or by means of any substance which it is deleterious to the human body to inhale, to swallow, or to receive into the blood, or by means of any animal, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, or with fine, or with both.

Section Text: The following kinds of hurt only are designated as "grievous":— Firstly.—Emasculation. Secondly.—Permanent privation of the sight of either eye. Thirdly.—Permanent privation of the hearing of either ear. Fourthly.—Privation of any member or joint. Fifthly.—Destruction or permanent impairing of the powers of any member or joint. Sixthly.—Permanent disfiguration of the head or face. Seventhly.—Fracture or dislocation of a bone or tooth. Eighthly.—Any hurt which endangers life or which causes the sufferer to be during the space of twenty days in severe bodily pain, or unable to follow his ordinary pursuits.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Does a bone fracture qualify as simple hurt or the specialized category of 'grievous' hurt?

Rule.Section 319: Defines basic 'hurt' as any act causing bodily pain, disease, or infirmity.
Rule.Section 320: Lists eight specific types of injuries that are legally designated as 'grievous'.
Rule.Section 323: Prescribes the one-year penalty for voluntarily causing simple hurt.
Rule.Section 325: Prescribes the seven-year penalty for voluntarily causing grievous hurt.
Rule.Section 324: Governs hurt caused by dangerous weapons like guns or knives.

Application.Section 319: Fails because while pain was caused, the injury (fracture) is severe enough to trigger a more specific higher definition.
Application.Section 320: Governs directly because Clause Seventhly explicitly classifies a 'fracture of a bone' as a kind of grievous hurt.
Application.Section 323: Fails because it is a punishment section and doesn't provide the definition for the injury type.
Application.Section 325: Fails because it sets the punishment once the injury is defined, rather than providing the primary legal classification.
Application.Section 324: Fails because a heavy stick is not typically a shooting, stabbing, or cutting instrument as defined in this section.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 320, Clause Seventhly, Jamil has committed 'grievous hurt' because the injury resulted in a fracture of a bone.

Question: Scenario-based: Rahim and Karim are on trial together for the same robbery. Rahim confessed and said, 'Karim and I did it together.' Can the judge use this confession to help decide if Karim is guilty?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Where there is reasonable ground to believe that two or more persons have conspired together to commit an offence or an actionable wrong, anything said, done or written by any one of such persons in reference to their common intention, after the time when such intention was first entertained by any one of them, is a relevant fact as against each of the persons believed to be so conspiring, as well for the purpose of proving the existence of the conspiracy as for the purpose of showing that any such person was a party to it.

A confession made by an accused person is irrelevant in a criminal proceeding, if the making of the confession appears to the Court to have been caused by any inducement, threat or promise having reference to the charge against the accused person, proceeding from a person in authority and sufficient, in the opinion of the Court, to give the accused person grounds which would appear to him reasonable for supposing that by making it he would gain any advantage or avoid any evil of a temporal nature in reference to the proceedings against him.
No confession made to a police-officer shall be proved as against a person accused of any offence.

When more persons than one are being tried jointly for the same offence, and a confession made by one of such persons affecting himself and some other of such persons is proved, the Court may take into consideration such confession as against such other persons as well as against the person who makes such confession.

Statements made by a party to the proceeding, or by an agent to any such party, whom the Court regards, under the circumstances of the case, as expressly or impliedly authorized by him to make them, are admissions. Statements made by parties to suits suing or sued in a representative character, are not admissions, unless they were made while the party making them held that character. Statements made by–

Section Text: When more persons than one are being tried jointly for the same offence, and a confession made by one of such persons affecting himself and some other of such persons is proved, the Court may take into consideration such confession as against such other persons as well as against the person who makes such confession.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: May a confession by one co-accused be used against another in a joint trial?

Rule.Section 10: Governs acts and statements of conspirators made in furtherance of their common plan.
Rule.Section 24: Excludes confessions obtained through coercive promises or threats.
Rule.Section 25: Excludes all confessions made directly to police officers.
Rule.Section 30: Allows the court to consider a proved confession against both the maker and their co-accused in a joint trial.
Rule.Section 18: Governs admissions by parties and authorized agents in civil or general proceedings.

Application.Section 10: Fails because a post-arrest confession is a retrospective admission of guilt, not a statement made to further an ongoing conspiracy.
Application.Section 24: Fails as there is no evidence of a threat or inducement; the question is purely about the confession's reach to a co-defendant.
Application.Section 25: Fails since the scenario asks about the confession's use in court after it has been 'proved,' assuming it wasn't a barred police statement.
Application.Section 30: Governs directly because Rahim and Karim are being tried jointly for the same offence, fulfilling the precise trigger for Clause 30.
Application.Section 18: Fails because criminal confessions have specific, more restrictive rules than general civil party admissions.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 30, Illustration (a), the judge may consider the confession as evidence against both Rahim and Karim because they are being tried jointly.

Question: Scenario-based: Rahim obtained a temporary stay order stopping his neighbor from digging a trench. Later, Rahim admitted he actually had no right to that land. Can the neighbor ask the judge to cancel that stay order now?

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Order XXXIX, Rule 1
Possible Sections.Full Text:
Where in any suit it is proved by affidavit or otherwise— that any property in dispute in a suit is in danger of being wasted, damaged or alienated by any party to the suit, or wrongfully sold in execution of a decree, or that the defendant threatens, or intends, to remove or dispose of his property with a view to defraud his creditors,
the Court may by order grant a temporary injunction to restrain such act, or make such other order for the purpose of staying and preventing the wasting, damaging, alienation, sale, removal or disposition of the property as the Court thinks fit, until the disposal of the suit or until further orders.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Order XXXIX, Rule 2A
Possible Sections.Full Text: Any order for an injunction may be discharged, or varied, or set aside by the Court, on application made thereto by any party dissatisfied with such order.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Order XXXIX, Rule 4
Possible Sections.Full Text: Any order for an injunction may be discharged, or varied, or set aside by the Court, on application made thereto by any party dissatisfied with such order.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 114
