Possible Sections.Full Text: Subject as aforesaid, any person considering himself aggrieved- (a) by a decree or order from which an appeal is allowed by this Code, but from which no appeal has been preferred, (b) by a decree or order from which no appeal is allowed by this Code, or (c) by a decision on a reference from a Court of Small Causes may apply for a view of judgment to the Court which passed the decree or made the order, and the Court may make such order thereon as it thinks fit.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 152
Possible Sections.Full Text: Clerical or arithmetical mistakes in judgments, decrees or orders or errors arising therein from any accidental slip or omission may at any time be corrected by the Court either of its own motion or on the application of any of the parties.

Relevant Section: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Order XXXIX, Rule 4
Section Text: Any order for an injunction may be discharged, or varied, or set aside by the Court, on application made thereto by any party dissatisfied with such order.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Does a court have authority to cancel a temporary injunction if a party provides a valid reason?

Rule.Order XXXIX, Rule 1: Governs the initial grant of a temporary injunction when a property is in danger of being destroyed or sold during trial.
Rule.Order XXXIX, Rule 2A: Provides for the punishment, including civil jail time, for anyone who intentionally breaks an active court stay order.
Rule.Order XXXIX, Rule 4: Specifies that any party who is unhappy with an injunction can ask the court to cancel (set aside) or change (vary) that order.
Rule.Section 114: Allows a party to ask for a formal 'review' of a final judgment if there is a massive error or significant new evidence.
Rule.Section 152: Restricts the court to fixing minor date typos or math calculation errors in finished judgment documents.

Application.Order XXXIX, Rule 1: Fails because it handles the 'starting' of an injunction rather than the 'cancellation' of an existing one.
Application.Order XXXIX, Rule 2A: Is inapplicable as the neighbor is following the order and seeking to cancel it legally, not 'disobeying' it.
Application.Order XXXIX, Rule 4: Governs directly because the neighbor is a 'dissatisfied party' applying to have the injunction 'set aside' due to Rahim's admission.
Application.Section 114: Fails because it handles 'judgments' (final trial outcomes), while the stay order is a 'temporary injunction' during the trial.
Application.Section 152: Does not apply because the problem is a substantive disagreement about land rights, not a simple typing error on the order.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Order XXXIX, Rule 4, the judge can cancel the order — any party who is unhappy with a stay order can apply to the court to have it canceled or changed.

Cited Acts and Sections: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Order XXXIX, Rule 4

Question: Scenario-based: Salim knows he has been diagnosed with a highly contagious and life-threatening virus. Despite his doctor's orders to isolate, he boards a crowded train to visit relatives, almost certainly exposing other passengers. Is this a crime?

Possible Sections.Full Text: A person is guilty of a public nuisance who does any act or is guilty of an illegal omission which causes any common injury, danger or annoyance to the public or to the people in general who dwell or occupy property in the vicinity, or which must necessarily cause injury, obstruction, dangers or annoyance to persons who may have occasion to use any public right. A common nuisance is not excused on the ground that it causes some convenience or advantage.

Whoever malignantly does any act which is, and which he knows or has reason to believe to be, likely to spread the infection of any disease dangerous to life, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both.

Whoever unlawfully or negligently does any act which is, and which he knows or has reason to believe to be, likely to spread the infection of any disease dangerous to life, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to six months, or with fine, or with both.

Whoever knowingly disobeys any rule made and promulgated by the Government for putting any vessel into a state of quarantine, or for regulating the intercourse of vessels in a state of quarantine with the shore or with other vessels, or for regulating the intercourse between places where an infectious disease prevails and other places, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to six months, or with fine, or with both.

Whoever does any act so rashly or negligently as to endanger human life or the personal safety of others, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three months, or with fine which may extend to two hundred and fifty taka, or with both.

Section Text: Whoever unlawfully or negligently does any act which is, and which he knows or has reason to believe to be, likely to spread the infection of any disease dangerous to life, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to six months, or with fine, or with both.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is acting in a way that risks spreading a deadly infection through negligence a criminal offense?

Rule.Section 268: Governs the broad definition of public nuisance, focusing on general danger or annoyance to the community.
Rule.Section 270: Governs the malignant or malicious spread of disease, requiring a higher level of hateful intent.
Rule.Section 269: Governs negligent or unlawful acts that are likely to spread a dangerous infection to others.
Rule.Section 271: Governs the specific violation of established quarantine rules, particularly involving ships or vessels.
Rule.Section 336: Governs general rash behavior that endangers human life when no more specific section applies.

Application.Section 268: Fails because it is a generic definition that is superseded by the more specialized disease-related sections of the Code.
Application.Section 270: Fails because the facts suggest Salim was being reckless/negligent by traveling rather than acting with a 'malignant' desire to poison the public.
Application.Section 269: Governs directly because boarding a train while infected is a negligent act known to be likely to spread a life-threatening infection.
Application.Section 271: Fails because the scenario involves a domestic train journey rather than the violation of a vessel's maritime quarantine order.
Application.Section 336: Fails because Section 269 is the lex specialis for public health risks involving infection, overriding the general safety provision.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 269, Salim is guilty because he performed a negligent act that he knew was likely to spread a disease dangerous to life.

Question: Scenario-based: Salim helps organize a small armed group with the specific goal of attacking government buildings to overthrow the current administration. What is the most severe punishment the law allows for this act of rebellion?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Whoever wages war against Bangladesh, or attempts to wage such war, or abets the waging of such war, shall be punished with death, or imprisonment for life, and shall also be liable to fine.

Whoever within or without Bangladesh conspires to commit any of the offences punishable by section 121, or to deprive Bangladesh of the sovereignty of her territories or of any part thereof, or conspires to overawe, by means of criminal force or the show of criminal force, the Government, shall be punished with imprisonment for life or with imprisonment of either description which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine.]

Whoever collects men, arms or ammunition or otherwise prepares to wage war with the intention of either waging or being prepared to wage war against Bangladesh, shall be punished with imprisonment for life or imprisonment of either description for a term not exceeding ten years, and shall also be liable to fine.

Whoever, by any act, or by any illegal omission, conceals the existence of a design to wage war against Bangladesh, intending by such concealment to facilitate, or knowing it to be likely that such concealment will facilitate the waging of such war, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine.

Whoever, with the intention of inducing or compelling the President of Bangladesh, or [the Government], to exercise or refrain from exercising in any manner any of the lawful powers of the President, or [the Government], assaults, or wrongfully restrains, or attempts wrongfully to restrain, or overawes, by means of criminal force or the show of criminal force, or attempts so to overawe, the President, [\* \* \*], shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine.

Section Text: Whoever wages war against Bangladesh, or attempts to wage such war, or abets the waging of such war, shall be punished with death, or imprisonment for life, and shall also be liable to fine.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: What is the maximum penalty for actively organizing and engaging in an armed rebellion against the state?

Rule.Section 121: Governs the actual waging, attempting, or abetting of war against the state, providing the highest penalties including death.
Rule.Section 121A: Governs the conspiracy to wage war or the intent to deprive the state of its territorial sovereignty.
Rule.Section 122: Governs the preliminary acts of collecting weapons or men in preparation for a future war.
Rule.Section 123: Governs the illegal concealment of a known design to wage war to facilitate its success.
Rule.Section 124: Governs assaults or use of force specifically intended to influence the President's official actions.

Application.Section 121: Governs directly because organizing an armed group to overthrow the government constitutes the actual act of waging or abetting war.
Application.Section 121A: Fails because it addresses the planning stage (conspiracy) rather than the active organization and execution of armed rebellion described.
Application.Section 122: Fails because Salim's actions involve the active use of an organized group for war rather than just the initial collection of materials.
Application.Section 123: Fails because it punishes the failure to report a plan rather than the active participation in the rebellion itself.
Application.Section 124: Fails because the target is the entire administration and state infrastructure rather than a specific personal assault on the President.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 121, the most severe punishment is death, although the court may also choose life imprisonment and a fine for waging war against the state.

Question: Scenario-based: Karim won a case in a local court that was permanently closed by the government last year. He now needs to enforce that judgment. Which court should he go to for enforcement?

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 37
Possible Sections.Full Text: The expression "Court which passed a decree," or words to that effect, shall, in relation to the execution of decrees, unless there is anything repugnant in the subject or context, be deemed to include,- (a) where the decree to be executed has been passed in the exercise of appellate jurisdiction, the Court of first instance, and (b) where the Court of first instance has ceased to exist or to have jurisdiction to execute it, the Court which, if the suit wherein the decree was passed was instituted at the time of making the application for the execution of the decree, would have jurisdiction to try such suit.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 38
Possible Sections.Full Text: A decree may be executed either by the Court which passed it, or by the Court to which it is sent for execution.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 39
Possible Sections.Full Text: (1) The Court which passed a decree may, on the application of the decree-holder, send it for execution to another Court,- (2) The Court which passed a decree may of its own motion send it for execution to any subordinate Court of competent jurisdiction.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 15
Possible Sections.Full Text: Every suit shall be instituted in the Court of the lowest grade competent to try it.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Order XXI, Rule 10
Possible Sections.Full Text: Where the holder of a decree desires to execute it, he shall apply to the Court which passed the decree or to the officer (if any) appointed in this behalf, or if the decree has been sent under the provisions hereinbefore contained to another Court then to such Court or to the proper officer thereof.

Relevant Section: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 37
Section Text: The expression "Court which passed a decree," or words to that effect, shall, in relation to the execution of decrees, unless there is anything repugnant in the subject or context, be deemed to include,- (a) where the decree to be executed has been passed in the exercise of appellate jurisdiction, the Court of first instance, and (b) where the Court of first instance has ceased to exist or to have jurisdiction to execute it, the Court which, if the suit wherein the decree was passed was instituted at the time of making the application for the execution of the decree, would have jurisdiction to try such suit.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Which court is legally responsible for enforcement if the original trial court no longer exists?

Rule.Section 37: Defines the court that currently holds jurisdiction over the matter as the 'court which passed the decree' if the original is gone.
Rule.Section 38: States the general venue rule that a judgment is carried out by the original court or a transferee court.
Rule.Section 39: Governs the voluntary transfer of a case from an active court to a different district for collection.
Rule.Section 15: Directs that a new legal action must start at the most junior possible level of the court system.
Rule.Order XXI, Rule 10: Outlines the standard procedural steps for a winner to ask an active court to start the execution process.

Application.Section 37: Governs directly because Clause (b) specifically handles the situation where the original 'court of first instance' has 'ceased to exist'.
Application.Section 38: Fails because it assumes the original court still exists to either act or send the file away.
Application.Section 39: Does not apply as it requires an active original court to perform the transfer of the decree.
Application.Section 15: Is inapplicable as it concerns starting a 'new suit' rather than identifying the successor for an 'existing decree'.
Application.Order XXI, Rule 10: Fails because it doesn't solve the problem of where to file if the court mentioned in the rule is closed.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, section 37, Clause (b), Karim must go to the court that currently has authority over the area — the law treats the current competent court as the one that passed the decree if the original court is gone.

Cited Acts and Sections: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 37(b)

Question: Scenario-based: Salim enters a house at 11 PM by using a duplicated key he made without permission. He intends to steal a laptop. What is the correct legal name for entering a house this way at night?

Possible Sections.Full Text: A person is said to commit “house-breaking" who commits house-trespass if he effects his entrance into the house or any part of it in any of the six ways hereinafter described; or if, being in the house or any part of it for the purpose of committing an offence, or, having committed an offence therein, he quits the house or any part of it in any of such six ways, that is to say: Firstly.- If he enters or quits through a passage made by himself, or by any abettor of the house-trespass, in order to the committing of the house-trespass. Secondly.- If he enters or quits through any passage not intended by any person, other than himself or an abettor of the offence, for human entrance; or through any passage to which he has obtained access by scaling or climbing over any wall or building. Thirdly.- If he enters or quits through any passage which he or any abettor of the house-trespass has opened, in order to the committing of the house-trespass by any means by which that passage was not intended by the occupier of the house to be opened. Fourthly.- If he enters or quits by opening any lock in order to the committing of the house-trespass, or in order to the quitting of the house after a house-trespass. Fifthly.- If he effects his entrance or departure by using criminal force or committing an assault, or by threatening any person with assault. Sixthly.- If he enters or quits by any passage which he knows to have been fastened against such entrance or departure, and to have been unfastened by himself or by an abettor of the house-trespass.
Whoever commits house-breaking after sunset and before sunrise, is said to commit "house-breaking by night".
Whoever commits house-trespass after sunset and before sunrise, is said to commit "lurking house-trespass by night".

Whoever commits criminal trespass by entering into or remaining in any building, tent or vessel used as a human dwelling or any building used as a place for worship, or as a place for the custody of property, is said to commit "house-trespass".

Whoever commits house-trespass having taken precautions to conceal such house-trespass from some person who has a right to exclude or eject the trespasser from the building, tent or vessel which is the subject of the trespass, is said to commit 'lurking house-trespass'.

Section Text: Whoever commits house-breaking after sunset and before sunrise, is said to commit "house-breaking by night".

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: How is house-breaking using a duplicate key classified when it happens at 11 PM?

Rule.Section 445: Governs house-breaking, defining it to include entry by using any unauthorized or duplicated key.
Rule.Section 446: Governs house-breaking when the entry occurs specifically between sunset and sunrise.
Rule.Section 444: Governs lurking trespass at night, focused on remaining hidden.
Rule.Section 442: Governs the general act of house-trespass into a dwelling.
Rule.Section 443: Governs lurking trespass during the day involving active concealment.

Application.Section 445: Fails because it defines the general method of entry as house-breaking but lacks the specialized night-time label.
Application.Section 446: Governs directly because using a key (house-breaking) at 11 PM (after sunset) meets the specific definition of house-breaking by night.
Application.Section 444: Fails because house-breaking is a more serious and specific offense than simple lurking trespass.
Application.Section 442: Fails because it is the broad basic category and ignores the specific key method and night-time element.
Application.Section 443: Fails because it is a daytime category and doesn't account for the 11 PM time or the key method.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 446, Salim's act is legally called 'house-breaking by night' because he entered the dwelling using a duplicate key after sunset.

Question: Scenario-based: A person is caught making fake 1,000 Taka coins. What is the most severe punishment possible for this crime?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Coin is metal used for the time being as money, and stamped and issued by the authority of some State or Sovereign Power in order to be so used. Bangladesh coin is metal stamped and issued by the authority of the Government<sup>84</sup> [* * *] in order to be used as money; and metal which has been so stamped and issued shall continue to be Bangladesh coin for the purposes of this Chapter, notwithstanding that it may have ceased to be used as money.

Whoever counterfeits or knowingly performs any part of the process of counterfeiting coin, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine.

Whoever makes or mends, or performs any part of the process of making or mending, or buys, sells or disposes of, any die or instrument, for the purpose of being used, or knowing or having reason to believe that it is intended to be used, for the purpose of counterfeiting coin, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, and shall also be liable to fine.

Whoever is in possession of any instrument or material, for the purpose of using the same for counterfeiting coin, or knowing or having reason to believe that the same is intended to be used for that purpose, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, and shall also be liable to fine; and if the coin to be counterfeited is Bangladesh coin, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine.

Whoever counterfeits, or knowingly performs any part of the process of counterfeiting Bangladesh coin, shall be punished with imprisonment for life, or with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine.

Section Text: Whoever counterfeits, or knowingly performs any part of the process of counterfeiting Bangladesh coin, shall be punished with imprisonment for life, or with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: What is the maximum penalty for counterfeiting national currency?

Rule.Section 230: Provides the legal definition for 'coin' and the specific category of 'Bangladesh coin' issued by government authority.
Rule.Section 231: Assigns a seven-year maximum sentence for the general offense of counterfeiting any type of coin.
Rule.Section 232: Imposes a maximum penalty of life imprisonment for counterfeiting coins specifically issued by the Government of Bangladesh.
Rule.Section 233: Punishes the making or selling of instruments designed for the purpose of counterfeiting coins with up to three years in prison.
Rule.Section 235: Governs the possession of counterfeiting materials, with an increased ten-year penalty if they are intended for national coins.

Application.Section 230: Fails because it defines the object of the crime rather than specifying the penal consequences for the act of counterfeiting.
Application.Section 231: Fails because it applies to general coins, whereas the scenario involves Taka, which triggers the higher penalty for national currency.
Application.Section 232: Governs directly because making fake Taka (Bangladesh coin) allows the court to impose the maximum sentence of life imprisonment.
Application.Section 233: Fails because it targets the tools of the trade rather than the actual process of creating the counterfeit money.
Application.Section 235: Fails because it punishes the possession of materials rather than the act of counterfeiting itself, which carries a harsher sentence.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 232, the most severe punishment is imprisonment for life, as faking Bangladesh coins is a much more serious crime than faking general coins.

Question: Scenario-based: I was tricked into confessing because an undercover officer pretended to be my lawyer in a jail cell. Does the fact that I was deceived into speaking make the confession automatically useless in court?

Possible Sections.Full Text: A confession made by an accused person is irrelevant in a criminal proceeding, if the making of the confession appears to the Court to have been caused by any inducement, threat or promise having reference to the charge against the accused person, proceeding from a person in authority and sufficient, in the opinion of the Court, to give the accused person grounds which would appear to him reasonable for supposing that by making it he would gain any advantage or avoid any evil of a temporal nature in reference to the proceedings against him.
No confession made to a police-officer shall be proved as against a person accused of any offence.
No confession made by any person whilst he is in the custody of a police-officer, unless it be made in the immediate presence of a Magistrate, shall be proved as against such person.

If such a confession is otherwise relevant, it does not become irrelevant merely because it was made under a promise of secrecy, or in consequence of a deception practised on the accused person for the purpose of obtaining it, or when he was drunk, or because it was made in answer to questions which he need not have answered, whatever may have been the form of those questions, or because he was not warned that he was not bound to make such confession, and that evidence of it might be given against him.

Provided that, when any fact is deposed to as discovered in consequence of information received from a person accused of any offence, in the custody of a police officer, so much of such information, whether it amounts to a confession or not, as relates distinctly to the fact thereby discovered, may be proved.

Section Text: If such a confession is otherwise relevant, it does not become irrelevant merely because it was made under a promise of secrecy, or in consequence of a deception practised on the accused person for the purpose of obtaining it, or when he was drunk, or because it was made in answer to questions which he need not have answered, whatever may have been the form of those questions, or because he was not warned that he was not bound to make such confession, and that evidence of it might be given against him.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Does deception by an officer automatically disqualify an otherwise relevant confession?

Rule.Section 24: Excludes confessions motivated by threats or promises of benefit from persons in authority.
Rule.Section 25: Creates an absolute bar for any confession made directly to a police officer.
Rule.Section 26: Bars confessions made by persons in police custody without a Magistrate present.
Rule.Section 29: States that a relevant confession does not become irrelevant simply because it was obtained through deception or a lack of warning.
Rule.Section 27: Allows information from an accused that leads to the physical discovery of evidence to be proved.

Application.Section 24: Fails because the accused was tricked into feeling safe rather than being threatened or promised a lighter sentence.
Application.Section 25: Fails as the accused did not know the person was an officer, making it a statement to a 'lawyer' rather than a formal police confession.
Application.Section 26: Fails because the presence of a Magistrate is a requirement for custodial confessions, but Section 29 specifically addresses the effect of deception.
Application.Section 29: Governs directly because the use of deception (pretending to be a lawyer) is explicitly listed as a condition that does not invalidate a confession.
Application.Section 27: Fails since the scenario describes a verbal admission without any subsequent physical discovery of objects.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 29, the confession is admissible because the law says a confession does not become irrelevant just because it was obtained through a trick or deception.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: How is house-breaking by unfastening a latch classified when it happens at 11 PM?

Rule.Section 445: Governs house-breaking, defining it to include entry by unfastening any latch, bolt, or lock.
Rule.Section 446: Governs the classification of house-breaking performed between sunset and sunrise.
Rule.Section 444: Governs lurking trespass at night, which focuses on remaining concealed.
Rule.Section 442: Governs simple house-trespass involving entry into a human dwelling building.
Rule.Section 443: Governs lurking trespass during the day involving active concealment.

Application.Section 445: Fails because it defines the general method of entry as house-breaking but lacks the specialized night-time label.
Application.Section 446: Governs directly because unfastening a latch (house-breaking) at 11 PM (after sunset) meets the specific definition of house-breaking by night.
Application.Section 444: Fails because house-breaking is a more serious and specific offense than simple lurking trespass.
Application.Section 442: Fails because it is the broad basic category and ignores the specific latch method and night-time element.
Application.Section 443: Fails because it is a daytime category and doesn't account for the 11 PM time or the latch method.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 446, Salim's act is legally called 'house-breaking by night' because he entered the dwelling by unfastening a latch after sunset.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: How is house-breaking by unfastening a latch classified when it happens at 11 PM?

Rule.Section 445: Governs house-breaking, specifically defining unfastening any fastening as a method of entry.
Rule.Section 446: Governs the classification of house-breaking when it occurs between sunset and sunrise.
Rule.Section 444: Governs lurking trespass at night, focused on remaining hidden rather than the method of entry.
Rule.Section 442: Governs simple house-trespass into a building used as a human dwelling.
Rule.Section 443: Governs lurking trespass during the day involving active concealment.

Application.Section 445: Fails because it defines the general method of entry as house-breaking but lacks the specific night-time designation.

Question: Scenario-based: Salman was injured when a falling brick hit him at a building site. To prove the builder was negligent, can Salman show that three other workers had already complained to the builder about those bricks being loose that morning?

Possible Sections.Full Text: When there is a question whether an act was accidental or intentional, or done with a particular knowledge or intention, the fact that such act formed part of a series of similar occurrences, in each of which the person doing the act was concerned, is relevant.

Any fact is relevant which shows or constitutes a motive or preparation for any fact in issue or relevant fact. The conduct of any party, or of any agent to any party, to any suit or proceeding, in reference to such suit or proceeding, or in reference to any fact in issue therein or relevant thereto, and the conduct of any person an offence against whom is the subject of any proceeding, is relevant, if such conduct influences or is influenced by any fact, in issue or relevant fact, and whether it was previous subsequent thereto.

Facts which are the occasions, cause or effect, immediate or otherwise, of relevant facts, or facts in issue, or which constitute the state of things under which they happened, or which afforded an opportunity for their occurrence or transaction, are relevant.

Facts showing the existence of any state of mind, such as intention, knowledge, good faith, negligence, rashness, ill-will, or good-will towards any particular person, or showing the existence of any state of body or bodily feeling, are relevant, when the existence of any such state of mind or body or bodily feeling is in issue or relevant.

Statements, written or verbal, of relevant facts made by a person who is dead, or who cannot be found, or who has become incapable of giving evidence, or whose attendance cannot be procured without an amount of delay or expense which under the circumstances of the case appears to the Court unreasonable, are themselves relevant facts in the following cases:–

Section Text: Facts showing the existence of any state of mind, such as intention, knowledge, good faith, negligence, rashness, ill-will, or good-will towards any particular person, or showing the existence of any state of body or bodily feeling, are relevant, when the existence of any such state of mind or body or bodily feeling is in issue or relevant.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is a party's prior knowledge of a defect relevant to proving their negligence in a subsequent incident?

Rule.Section 15: Governs the use of patterns of similar occurrences to distinguish between intentional acts and accidents.
Rule.Section 8: Governs motive, preparation, and physical conduct influenced by a fact in issue during a crime.
Rule.Section 7: Outlines relevancy for facts providing the cause, effect, or occasion for an event to occur.
Rule.Section 14: Makes facts relevant if they demonstrate a state of mind like knowledge or negligence regarding a specific issue.
Rule.Section 32: Governs out-of-court statements from deceased or unavailable persons under specific thematic categories.

Application.Section 15: Fails because the issue is the builder's 'knowledge' of the danger rather than whether the brick fell 'by accident.'
Application.Section 8: Fails since the workers' complaints are evidence of internal state rather than a physical preparation for a crime.
Application.Section 7: Fails as the complaints are social interactions rather than the immediate physical cause or occasion of the falling brick.
Application.Section 14: Governs directly because the prior warnings prove the builder had 'knowledge' of the risk, satisfying the definition of negligence in Section 14.
Application.Section 32: Fails since there is no evidence the workers are deceased; their live testimony about the complaints is relevant under Section 14.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 14, the previous complaints are relevant because they show that the builder had knowledge of the danger, which is a necessary element of negligence.

Question: Scenario-based: Zahid was fined by a court two years ago but has refused to pay. He believes that since time has passed, the government has lost its right to collect the money. How many years does the state have to collect an unpaid fine?

Possible Sections.Full Text: If the offence be punishable with fine only, the imprisonment which the Court imposes in default of payment of the fine shall be simple, and the term for which the Court directs the offender to be imprisoned, in default of payment of fine, shall not exceed the following scale, that is to say, for any term not exceeding two months when the amount of the fine shall not exceed fifty taka, and for any term not exceeding four months when the amount shall not exceed one hundred taka, and for any term not exceeding six months in any other case.

In every case of an offence punishable with imprisonment as well as fine, in which the offender is sentenced to a fine, whether with or without imprisonment, and in every case of an offence punishable with imprisonment or fine, or with fine only, in which the offender is sentenced to a fine, it shall be competent to the Court which sentences such offender to direct by the sentence that, in default of payment of the fine, the offender shall suffer imprisonment for a certain term, which imprisonment shall be in excess of any other imprisonment to which he may have been sentenced or to which he may be liable under a commutation of a sentence.

The fine, or any part thereof which remains unpaid, may be levied at any time within six years after the passing of the sentence, and if, under the sentence, the offender be liable to imprisonment for a longer period than six years, then at any time previous to the expiration of that period; and the death of the offender does not discharge from the liability of any property which would, after his death, be legally liable for his debts.
The imprisonment which is imposed in default of payment of a fine shall terminate whenever that fine is either paid or levied by process of law.

The term for which the Court directs the offender to be imprisoned in default of payment of a fine shall not exceed one-fourth of the term of imprisonment which is the maximum fixed for the offence, if the offence be punishable with imprisonment as well as fine.

Section Text: The fine, or any part thereof which remains unpaid, may be levied at any time within six years after the passing of the sentence, and if, under the sentence, the offender be liable to imprisonment for a longer period than six years, then at any time previous to the expiration of that period; and the death of the offender does not discharge from the liability any property which would, after his death, be legally liable for his debts.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: What is the limitation period for the recovery of a court-imposed fine?

Rule.Section 67: Provides the scale of jail time for non-payment of fines in fine-only offenses.
Rule.Section 64: Empowers the court to impose imprisonment as an alternative for unpaid fines.
Rule.Section 70: Fixes a six-year limit for fine recovery, or the duration of the sentence if longer.
Rule.Section 68: States that jail time for non-payment ends the moment the money is paid.
Rule.Section 65: Limits default jail time to one-fourth of the maximum possible prison term.

Application.Section 67: Fails because it regulates the length of a jail stay rather than the deadline for money collection.
Application.Section 64: Fails because it concerns the type of penalty rather than the time-bar for financial recovery.
Application.Section 70: Governs directly because it explicitly sets the six-year window for the government to levy an unpaid fine.
Application.Section 68: Fails because it addresses when a prisoner is released rather than when the debt expires.
Application.Section 65: Fails because it is a mathematical rule for sentencing rather than a limitation period for debt.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 70, the government has six years to collect the fine after the sentence is passed — or longer if the prison term itself exceeds six years.

Question: Scenario-based: Jamil enters into an argument with Salim on the street. Jamil pulls out a small knife and says, 'If you ever come near this shop again, I will cut your throat!' Jamil walks away immediately. Has he committed a crime?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Whoever makes any gesture, or any preparation intending or knowing it to be likely that such gesture or preparation will cause any person present to apprehend that he who makes that gesture or preparation is about to use criminal force to that person, is said to commit an assault.

Whoever threatens another with any injury to his person, reputation or property, or to the person or reputation of any one in whom that person is interested, with intent to cause to alarm to that person, or to cause that person to do any act which he is not legally bound to do, or to omit to do any act which that person is legally entitled to do, as the means of avoiding the execution of such threat, commits criminal intimidation.

Whoever intentionally insults, and thereby gives provocation to any person, intending or knowing it to be likely that such provocation will cause him to break the public peace, or to commit any other offence, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both.

Whoever intentionally puts any person in fear of any injury to that person, or to any other, and thereby dishonestly induces the person so put in fear to give donation or subscription of any kind or to deliver to any person any property or valuable security or anything signed or sealed which may be converted into a valuable security, commits 'extortion'.

Whoever holds out any threat of injury to any public servant, or to any person in whom he believes that public servant to be interested, for the purpose of inducing that public servant to do any act, or to forbear or delay to do any act, connected with the exercise of the public functions of such public servant, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both.

Section Text: Whoever threatens another with any injury to his person, reputation or property, or to the person or reputation of any one in whom that person is interested, with intent to cause to alarm to that person, or to cause that person to do any act which he is not legally bound to do, or to omit to do any act which that person is legally entitled to do, as the means of avoiding the execution of such threat, commits criminal intimidation.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Does a threat of future physical harm intended to stop someone from using a public area constitute criminal intimidation?

Rule.Section 351: Governs the immediate apprehension of force caused by gestures (assault).
Rule.Section 503: Defines criminal intimidation as threatening injury to cause alarm or force an omission of a legal act.
Rule.Section 504: Governs insults intended to provoke a breach of the public peace.
Rule.Section 383: Governs extortion, which requires the actual delivery of property by the victim.
Rule.Section 189: Governs threats of injury specifically directed at public servants in their duties.

Application.Section 351: Fails because Jamil threatened future action if Salim returned, rather than creating a fear of 'immediate' force before walking away.
Application.Section 503: Governs directly because Jamil threatened physical injury to 'cause alarm' and to force Salim to 'omit' his legal right to visit the shop.
Application.Section 504: Fails because the primary intent was to terrorize and control Salim's movements rather than to provoke a riot or public fight.
Application.Section 383: Fails because no property was demanded or delivered as a result of the threat.
Application.Section 189: Fails because Salim is a private citizen and the threat was unrelated to any government official's public function.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 503, Jamil is guilty of criminal intimidation because he threatened injury to Salim to alarm him and prevent him from visiting the shop.

Question: Scenario-based: A woman gives her diamond necklace to a jeweler to be polished. The jeweler instead sells the necklace and uses the money to pay his rent. What crime has the jeweler committed?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Whoever, intending to take dishonestly any moveable property out of the possession of any person without that person's consent, moves that property in order to such taking, is said to commit theft.

Whoever dishonestly misappropriates or converts to his own use any moveable property, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both.

Whoever, being in any manner entrusted with property, or with any dominion over property, dishonestly misappropriates or converts to his own use that property, or dishonestly uses or disposes of that property in violation of any direction of law prescribing the mode in which such trust is to be discharged, or of any legal contract, express or implied, which he has made touching the discharge of such trust, or wilfully suffers any other person so to do, commits "criminal breach of trust".

Whoever, by deceiving any person, fraudulently or dishonestly induces the person so deceived to deliver any property to any person, or to consent that any person shall retain any property, or intentionally induces the person so deceived to do or omit to do anything which he would not do or omit if he were not so deceived, and which act or omission causes or is likely to cause damage or harm to that person in body, mind, reputation or property, is said to "cheat".
Whoever commits criminal breach of trust shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, or with fine, or with both.

Section Text: Whoever, being in any manner entrusted with property, or with any dominion over property, dishonestly misappropriates or converts to his own use that property, or dishonestly uses or disposes of that property in violation of any direction of law prescribing the mode in which such trust is to be discharged, or of any legal contract, express or implied, which he has made touching the discharge of such trust, or wilfully suffers any other person so to do, commits "criminal breach of trust".

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is selling property that was given to you for a specific service considered criminal breach of trust?

Rule.Section 378: Defines theft as the dishonest taking of property out of the owner's possession without their consent.
Rule.Section 403: Punishes the dishonest misappropriation of property that a person came across without it being entrusted to them.
Rule.Section 405: Defines criminal breach of trust as the dishonest use of property that was specifically entrusted to a person for a purpose.
Rule.Section 406: Provides the three-year prison sentence or fine for individuals found guilty of committing criminal breach of trust.
Rule.Section 415: Defines cheating as using deception to trick someone into handing over their property or doing something they wouldn't otherwise do.

Application.Section 378: Fails because the necklace was legally given to the jeweler's possession for polishing, meaning he did not take it 'out of possession'.
Application.Section 403: Fails because it applies to general misappropriation, while the specific 'entrustment' of the necklace for polishing triggers a more specific section.
Application.Section 405: Governs directly because the jeweler was 'entrusted' with the necklace for a specific service but dishonestly sold it for his own use.
Application.Section 406: Fails because it provides the penalty for the offense rather than establishing the legal definition and criteria that qualify the jeweler's act.
Application.Section 415: Fails because the jeweler did not use a trick to get the necklace; he received it legally for work and only later decided to steal it.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 405, the jeweler is guilty of criminal breach of trust because he was entrusted with the necklace and dishonestly used it for himself.

Question: Scenario-based: Salim enters a house at 11 PM by quietly scaling a rain pipe to reach a window. He intends to steal a laptop. What is the correct legal name for entering a house this way at night?

Possible Sections.Full Text: A person is said to commit “house-breaking" who commits house-trespass if he effects his entrance into the house or any part of it in any of the six ways hereinafter described; or if, being in the house or any part of it for the purpose of committing an offence, or, having committed an offence therein, he quits the house or any part of it in any of such six ways, that is to say: Firstly.- If he enters or quits through a passage made by himself, or by any abettor of the house-trespass, in order to the committing of the house-trespass. Secondly.- If he enters or quits through any passage not intended by any person, other than himself or an abettor of the offence, for human entrance; or through any passage to which he has obtained access by scaling or climbing over any wall or building. Thirdly.- If he enters or quits through any passage which he or any abettor of the house-trespass has opened, in order to the committing of the house-trespass by any means by which that passage was not intended by the occupier of the house to be opened. Fourthly.- If he enters or quits by opening any lock in order to the committing of the house-trespass, or in order to the quitting of the house after a house-trespass. Fifthly.- If he effects his entrance or departure by using criminal force or committing an assault, or by threatening any person with assault. Sixthly.- If he enters or quits by any passage which he knows to have been fastened against such entrance or departure, and to have been unfastened by himself or by an abettor of the house-trespass.
Whoever commits house-breaking after sunset and before sunrise, is said to commit "house-breaking by night".
Whoever commits house-trespass after sunset and before sunrise, is said to commit "lurking house-trespass by night".

Whoever commits criminal trespass by entering into or remaining in any building, tent or vessel used as a human dwelling or any building used as a place for worship, or as a place for the custody of property, is said to commit "house-trespass".

Whoever commits house-trespass having taken precautions to conceal such house-trespass from some person who has a right to exclude or eject the trespasser from the building, tent or vessel which is the subject of the trespass, is said to commit 'lurking house-trespass'.

Section Text: Whoever commits house-breaking after sunset and before sunrise, is said to commit "house-breaking by night".

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: How is house-breaking by scaling a pipe classified when it happens at 11 PM?

Rule.Section 445: Governs house-breaking, specifically defining scaling a wall or structure as a method of entry.
Rule.Section 446: Governs the classification of house-breaking when the entry occurs between sunset and sunrise.
Rule.Section 444: Governs lurking trespass at night, which focuses on secrecy rather than the method of breaking entry.
Rule.Section 442: Governs general house-trespass into a human dwelling building.
Rule.Section 443: Governs lurking trespass during the day involving active concealment.

Application.Section 445: Fails because it defines the general method of entry as house-breaking but lacks the specialized night-time label.
Application.Section 446: Governs directly because scaling (house-breaking) at 11 PM (after sunset) meets the specific definition of house-breaking by night.
Application.Section 444: Fails because house-breaking is a more serious and specific offense than simple lurking trespass.
Application.Section 442: Fails because it is the broad basic category and ignores the specific scaling method and night-time element.
Application.Section 443: Fails because it is a daytime category and doesn't account for the 11 PM time or the scaling method.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 446, Salim's act is legally called 'house-breaking by night' because he entered the dwelling by scaling after sunset.

Rule.Section 445: Governs the definition of house-breaking, specifically unfastening any fastening to gain entry.
Rule.Section 446: Governs the classification of house-breaking when it occurs between sunset and sunrise.
Rule.Section 444: Governs lurking house-trespass during the night, focusing on secrecy.
Rule.Section 442: Governs general house-trespass without regarding the method or time of entry.
Rule.Section 443: Governs daytime lurking house-trespass where the intruder takes steps to remain hidden.

Application.Section 445: Fails because it defines the 'breaking' method but does not provide the specialized night-time designation.
Application.Section 446: Governs directly because unfastening a latch (house-breaking) at 11 PM (after sunset) triggers the night-time classification.
Application.Section 444: Fails because house-breaking is a more specific and aggravated form of trespass than simple lurking.

Application.Section 443: Fails because it is intended for daytime concealment and does not account for the method or 11 PM timing.

Question: Scenario-based: A judge realized he accidentally typed '2023' instead of '2024' in the final order. Can he fix this without a full new trial?

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 151
Possible Sections.Full Text: Nothing in this Code shall be deemed to limit or otherwise affect the inherent power of the Court to make such orders as may be necessary for the ends of justice or to prevent abuse of the process of the Court.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 152
Possible Sections.Full Text: Clerical or arithmetical mistakes in judgments, decrees or orders or errors arising therein from any accidental slip or omission may at any time be corrected by the Court either of its own motion or on the application of any of the parties.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Order VI, Rule 17
Possible Sections.Full Text:
The Court may at any stage of the proceedings allow either party to alter or amend his pleadings in such manner and on such terms as may be just, and all such amendments shall be made as may be necessary for the purpose of determining the real questions in controversy between the parties:

Provided that no application for amendment shall be allowed after the trial has commenced, unless the Court is of opinion that in spite of due diligence, the party could not have raised the matter before the commencement of trial:

Provided further that if an application for amendment is made after the trial has commenced and the Court is of opinion that the application is made to delay the proceedings, the Court shall make an order for the payment to the objector such cost by way of compensation as it thinks fit.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 153
Possible Sections.Full Text: The Court may at any time, and on such terms as to costs or otherwise as it thinks fit, amend any defect or error in any proceeding in a suit; and all necessary amendments shall be made for the purpose of determining the real question or issue raised by or depending on such proceeding.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 114
Possible Sections.Full Text: Subject as aforesaid, any person considering himself aggrieved- (a) by a decree or order from which an appeal is allowed by this Code, but from which no appeal has been preferred, (b) by a decree or order from which no appeal is allowed by this Code, or (c) by a decision on a reference from a Court of Small Causes may apply for a view of judgment to the Court which passed the decree or made the order, and the Court may make such order thereon as it thinks fit.

Relevant Section: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 152
Section Text: Clerical or arithmetical mistakes in judgments, decrees or orders or errors arising therein from any accidental slip or omission may at any time be corrected by the Court either of its own motion or on the application of any of the parties.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Can a court correct clerical errors in a judgment without formal litigation?

Rule.Section 151: Preserves the court's broad power to ensure fairness where no specific written rule applies.
Rule.Section 152: Specifically empowers a judge to fix typos, math errors, or accidental slips in judgments at any time.
Rule.Order VI, Rule 17: Allows parties to change or update their own written claims or defense statements.
Rule.Section 153: Grants a general power to the court to fix technical defects or formal errors in proceedings.
Rule.Section 114: Provides a process for a party to ask the judge to re-decide a case because of significant errors.

Application.Section 151: Fails because it is a backup power that only triggers when no specific rule already exists.
Application.Section 152: Governs directly because a date typo is the exact definition of a 'clerical mistake' or 'accidental slip'.
Application.Order VI, Rule 17: Does not govern because it addresses changes to 'pleadings' rather than the judge's final decision.
Application.Section 153: Is inapplicable as it concerns general defects rather than the specific final written judgment document.
Application.Section 114: Fails because it is a complex remedy for major errors, while a typo is handled by the faster Section 152 process.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, section 152, the judge can fix the typo immediately — the law allows courts to correct clerical errors or accidental slips in judgments at any time.

Cited Acts and Sections: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 152

Question: Scenario-based: Salim is caught in his own house holding a bag of fake currency notes that he made himself. He was about to go to the market to spend them. What is the correct charge?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Whoever counterfeits, or knowingly performs any part of the process of counterfeiting Bangladesh coin, shall be punished with imprisonment for life, or with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine.

Whoever, having any counterfeit coin which, at the time when he became possessed of it, he knew to be counterfeit, fraudulently or with intent that fraud may be committed, delivers the same to any person, or attempts to induce any person to receive it, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to five years, and shall also be liable to fine.

Whoever, fraudulently or with intent that fraud may be committed, is in possession of counterfeit coin, which is a counterfeit of Bangladesh coin, having known at the time when he became possessed of it that it was counterfeit, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine.

Whoever is in possession of any instrument or material, for the purpose of using the same for counterfeiting coin, or knowing or having reason to believe that the same is intended to be used for that purpose, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, and shall also be liable to fine; and if the coin to be counterfeited is Bangladesh coin, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine.

Whoever, having any counterfeit coin, which is a counterfeit of Bangladesh coin, and which, at the time when he became possessed of it, he knew to be a counterfeit of Bangladesh coin, fraudulently or with intent that fraud may be committed, delivers the same to any person, or attempts to induce any person to receive it, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine.

Section Text: Whoever, fraudulently or with intent that fraud may be committed, is in possession of counterfeit coin, which is a counterfeit of Bangladesh coin, having known at the time when he became possessed of it that it was counterfeit, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is possession of fake national currency with intent to defraud a crime even before spending it?

Rule.Section 232: Governs the actual process of counterfeiting or making the fake coins themselves.
Rule.Section 235: Governs the possession of tools and materials used to create counterfeit money.
Rule.Section 239: Governs the delivery or spending of general fake coins while knowing they are counterfeit.
Rule.Section 240: Governs the delivery or spending specifically of fake Bangladesh coins.
Rule.Section 243: Governs the simple possession of fake Bangladesh coins with a fraudulent intent.

Application.Section 232: Fails because the scenario focuses on Salim being caught 'holding' the finished bag of money rather than the act of manufacturing it.
Application.Section 235: Fails because it covers the possession of 'instruments or material' (like dyes or metal), not the possession of the completed fake money.
Application.Section 239: Fails because it requires the actual delivery to another person and applies only to general counterfeit coins.
Application.Section 240: Fails because Salim was caught before he could 'deliver' the money to anyone at the market.
Application.Section 243: Governs directly because it punishes the possession of counterfeit Bangladesh coins when the person intended to commit fraud with them.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 243, Salim is guilty because he was in possession of counterfeit Bangladesh coins with the intent to commit fraud.

Question: Scenario-based: Salim tells his friend to burn down an empty shed. While burning the shed, the fire accidentally spreads to a nearby house and destroys it too. Is Salim responsible for the burning of the house?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Whoever abets any offence shall, if the act abetted is committed in consequence of the abetment, and no express provision is made by this Code for the punishment of such abetment, be punished with the punishment provided for the offence. Punishment of abetment if the act abetted is committed in consequence and where no express provision is made for its punishment

When an act is abetted and a different act is done, the abettor is liable for the act done, in the same manner and to the same extent as if he had directly abetted it: Provided the act done was a probable consequence of the abetment, and was committed under the influence of the instigation, or with the aid or in pursuance of the conspiracy which constituted the abetment.

When an act is abetted with the intention on the part of the abettor of causing a particular effect, and an act for which the abettor is liable in consequence of the abetment, causes a different effect from that intended by the abettor, the abettor is liable for the effect caused, in the same manner and to the same extent as if he had abetted the act with the intention of causing that effect, provided he knew that the act abetted was likely to cause that effect.

Whenever any person, who if absent would be liable to be punished as an abettor, is present when the act or offence for which he would be punishable in consequence of the abetment is committed, he shall be deemed to have committed such act or offence.

Whoever commits mischief by fire or any explosive substance, intending to cause, or knowing it to be likely that he will thereby cause, damage to any property to the amount of one hundred taka or upwards or (where the property is agricultural produce) ten taka or upwards, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years and shall also be liable to fine.

Section Text: When an act is abetted and a different act is done, the abettor is liable for the act done, in the same manner and to the same extent as if he had directly abetted it: Provided the act done was a probable consequence of the abetment, and was committed under the influence of the instigation, or with the aid or in pursuance of the conspiracy which constituted the abetment.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is an abettor liable for a different, unintended crime committed as a result of their plan?

Rule.Section 109: Governs the standard punishment for the exact crime that was abetted.
Rule.Section 111: Governs liability for a 'different act' than the one abetted, provided it was a likely consequence of the original plan.
Rule.Section 113: Governs liability for a 'different effect' (like death instead of hurt) when the original act was committed as planned.
Rule.Section 114: Governs cases where the abettor is physically present at the scene and is thus treated as the principal offender.
Rule.Section 435: Governs the substantive crime and penalty for committing mischief by fire.

Application.Section 109: Fails because the crime actually committed (burning the house) was different from the shed burning Salim planned.
Application.Section 111: Governs directly because the house fire was a 'probable consequence' of starting a fire nearby, making Salim liable for that different act.
Application.Section 113: Fails because it addresses a different 'effect' of the same act, whereas here the destruction of the house was a 'different act' from burning the shed.
Application.Section 114: Fails because there is no mention of Salim being present while the fire was being set.
Application.Section 435: Fails because it defines the crime rather than the complex rule for determining Salim's vicarious liability.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 111, Salim is responsible for the burning of the house because it was a likely and natural consequence of the fire he told his friend to start.

Question: Scenario-based: Salim enters a house at 11 PM by quietly scaling a water pipe to reach a first-floor window. He intends to steal a laptop. What is the correct legal name for entering a house this way at night?

Possible Sections.Full Text: A person is said to commit “house-breaking" who commits house-trespass if he effects his entrance into the house or any part of it in any of the six ways hereinafter described; or if, being in the house or any part of it for the purpose of committing an offence, or, having committed an offence therein, he quits the house or any part of it in any of such six ways, that is to say: Firstly.- If he enters or quits through a passage made by himself, or by any abettor of the house-trespass, in order to the committing of the house-trespass. Secondly.- If he enters or quits through any passage not intended by any person, other than himself or an abettor of the offence, for human entrance; or through any passage to which he has obtained access by scaling or climbing over any wall or building. Thirdly.- If he enters or quits through any passage which he or any abettor of the house-trespass has opened, in order to the committing of the house-trespass by any means by which that passage was not intended by the occupier of the house to be opened. Fourthly.- If he enters or quits by opening any lock in order to the committing of the house-trespass, or in order to the quitting of the house after a house-trespass. Fifthly.- If he effects his entrance or departure by using criminal force or committing an assault, or by threatening any person with assault. Sixthly.- If he enters or quits by any passage which he knows to have been fastened against such entrance or departure, and to have been unfastened by himself or by an abettor of the house-trespass.
Whoever commits house-breaking after sunset and before sunrise, is said to commit "house-breaking by night".
Whoever commits house-trespass after sunset and before sunrise, is said to commit "lurking house-trespass by night".

Whoever commits criminal trespass by entering into or remaining in any building, tent or vessel used as a human dwelling or any building used as a place for worship, or as a place for the custody of property, is said to commit "house-trespass".

Whoever commits house-trespass having taken precautions to conceal such house-trespass from some person who has a right to exclude or eject the trespasser from the building, tent or vessel which is the subject of the trespass, is said to commit 'lurking house-trespass'.

Section Text: Whoever commits house-breaking after sunset and before sunrise, is said to commit "house-breaking by night".

Rule.Section 445: Governs house-breaking, specifically defining scaling a wall or structure as a method of entry.
Rule.Section 446: Governs house-breaking specifically when the entry occurs between sunset and sunrise.
Rule.Section 444: Governs lurking trespass at night, focusing on secrecy rather than the method of breaking entry.
Rule.Section 442: Governs general entry into a human dwelling with an illegal intent (house-trespass).
Rule.Section 443: Governs lurking trespass involving active concealment of the intruder during the daytime.

Application.Section 445: Fails because it defines the general act of scaling but doesn't include the time-specific night-time label.
Application.Section 446: Governs directly because scaling (house-breaking) at 11 PM (after sunset) meets the specific definition of house-breaking by night.
Application.Section 444: Fails because house-breaking is a more serious and specific offense than simple lurking trespass.
Application.Section 442: Fails because it is the broad basic category and ignores the specific scaling method and time.
Application.Section 443: Fails because it is a daytime category and doesn't account for the 11 PM time or the scaling method.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 446, Salim's act is legally called 'house-breaking by night' because he entered the dwelling by scaling after sunset.

Question: Scenario-based: A man puts on a fake police uniform and stops a driver, pretending to give him a ticket. He does this just as a prank to scare his friend. Has he committed a crime?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Whoever pretends to hold any particular office as a public servant, knowing that he does not hold such office or falsely personates any other person holding such office, and in such assumed character does or attempts to do any act under colour of such office, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description, for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both.

Whoever, not belonging to a certain class of public servants, wears any garb or carries any token resembling any garb or token used by that class of public servants, with the intention that it may be believed, or with the knowledge that it is likely to be believed, that he belongs to that class of public servants, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description, for a term which may extend to three months, or with fine which may extend to two hundred taka, or with both. # CHAPTER IXA: OF OFFENCES RELATING TO ELECTIONS

Whoever, by deceiving any person, fraudulently or dishonestly induces the person so deceived to deliver any property to any person, or to consent that any person shall retain any property, or intentionally induces the person so deceived to do or omit to do anything which he would not do or omit if he were not so deceived, and which act or omission causes or is likely to cause damage or harm to that person in body, mind, reputation or property, is said to "cheat".

Whoever voluntarily obstructs any public servant in the discharge of his public functions, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three months, or with fine which may extend to five hundred taka, or with both.

Nothing is an offence by reason that it causes, or that it is intended to cause, or that it is known to be likely to cause, any harm, if that harm is so slight that no person of ordinary sense and temper would complain of such harm.

Section Text: Whoever pretends to hold any particular office as a public servant, knowing that he does not hold such office or falsely personates any other person holding such office, and in such assumed character does or attempts to do any act under colour of such office, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description, for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is pretending to be a public official and performing an official act considered a crime even if done as a joke?

Rule.Section 170: Governs the false personation of a public servant and the performance of an act under that false authority.
Rule.Section 171: Governs the simple act of wearing a uniform or token of a public servant with the intent to be believed as such.
Rule.Section 415: Governs cheating, which requires inducing someone to part with property through deception.
Rule.Section 186: Governs the obstruction of a real public servant who is trying to do their official job.
Rule.Section 95: Governs trivial or slight harms that would not bother a person of ordinary temper.

Application.Section 170: Governs directly because the man both pretended to be an officer and performed an 'act' (giving a ticket) under that assumed character.
Application.Section 171: Fails because the man went beyond just wearing the 'garb' and actually performed an official-style act, triggering the more specific Section 170.
Application.Section 415: Fails because no property was taken from the friend, so the essential requirement of cheating is missing.
Application.Section 186: Fails because there was no real public servant involved whose work was being obstructed by the prankster.
Application.Section 95: Fails because impersonating a law enforcement officer and exercising police power is a serious legal violation, not a trivial matter.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 170, the man is guilty of personating a public servant because he performed an official act while pretending to hold office.

Question: Scenario-based: I was convicted by a first-class magistrate for theft and sentenced to three years in prison. Can I appeal this decision to the High Court Division?

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 407
Possible Sections.Full Text: Any person convicted on a trial held by any Magistrate of the second or third class may appeal to the chief Judicial Magistrate who may himself hear and dispose of the appeal or transfer it to any Additional Chief Judicial Magistrate for disposal, and may withdraw an appeal so transferred.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 410
Possible Sections.Full Text: Any person convicted on a trial held by a Sessions Judge or an Additional Sessions Judge may appeal to the High Court Division.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 408
Possible Sections.Full Text: Any person convicted on a trial held by a Metropolitan Magistrate or a Magistrate of the first class may appeal to the Court of Session.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 412
Possible Sections.Full Text: Notwithstanding anything hereinbefore contained where an accused person has pleaded guilty and has been convicted by a Court of Session [or any Metropolitan Magistrate] or Magistrate of the first class on such plea, there shall be no appeal except as to the extent or legality of the sentence.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 417
Possible Sections.Full Text: Subject to the provisions of sub-section (4), the Government may, in any case, direct the Public Prosecutor to present an appeal- to the High Court Division from an original or appellate Order of acquittal passed by any Court of Session; to the Court of Session from an original or appellate Order of acquittal passed by any Magistrate. Notwithstanding anything contained in section 418, if such an order is passed in any case instituted upon complaint, and if the order involves an error of law occasioning failure of justice, the complainant may present an appeal- to the High Court Division from an original order of acquittal passed by any Court of Session; to the Court of Session from an original order of acquittal passed by any Magistrate. No appeal by the complaint from an order of acquittal shall be entertained by the High Court Division or a Court of Session after the expiry of sixty days from the date of the order of acquittal. If, in any case, the admission of an appeal from an order of acquittal is refused, no appeal from that order of acquittal shall lie under sub-section (1).

Relevant Section: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 408
Section Text: Any person convicted on a trial held by a Metropolitan Magistrate or a Magistrate of the first class may appeal to the Court of Session.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: To which court must a person appeal a conviction issued by a first-class magistrate?

Rule.Section 407: Governs appeals from convictions by lower-tier second or third-class magistrates to the Chief Judicial Magistrate.
Rule.Section 410: Governs appeals from trials held by a Sessions Judge or Additional Sessions Judge to the High Court.
Rule.Section 408: Governs the legal pathway for appeals from Metropolitan or first-class magistrates to the Court of Session.
Rule.Section 412: Governs the restriction on the right to appeal when the conviction follows a voluntary plea of guilty.
Rule.Section 417: Governs the authority of the government to file an appeal specifically against a 'not guilty' verdict.

Application.Section 407: Fails because it applies only to lower magistrates rather than the first-class magistrate specified in the scenario.
Application.Section 410: Fails because the conviction was by a magistrate, whereas this section requires the trial judge to be a Sessions tier judge.
Application.Section 408: Directly and exclusively governs as it identifies the Court of Session as the correct appellate venue for a first-class magistrate's judgment.
Application.Section 412: Fails because it limits the scope of an appeal rather than defining the correct court in which to file it.
Application.Section 417: Fails because it concerns the state's challenge to an acquittal rather than a defendant's challenge to a conviction.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, section 408, you cannot appeal to the High Court; your appeal must be filed in the Court of Session since you were convicted by a first-class magistrate.

Cited Acts and Sections: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 408

Possible Sections.Full Text: Whoever commits criminal trespass by entering into or remaining in any building, tent or vessel used as a human dwelling or any building used as a place for worship, or as a place for the custody of property, is said to commit "house-trespass".

Whoever commits house-trespass having taken precautions to conceal such house-trespass from some person who has a right to exclude or eject the trespasser from the building, tent or vessel which is the subject of the trespass, is said to commit 'lurking house-trespass'.
