Question: Scenario-based: Salman suddenly quit his job after a theft happened at his office. When his boss asked why, Salman said, 'I'm leaving because I was offered a better job at the bank.' Is this statement relevant?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Any fact is relevant which shows or constitutes a motive or preparation for any fact in issue or relevant fact. The conduct of any party, or of any agent to any party, to any suit or proceeding, in reference to such suit or proceeding, or in reference to any fact in issue therein or relevant thereto, and the conduct of any person an offence against whom is the subject of any proceeding, is relevant, if such conduct influences or is influenced by any fact, in issue or relevant fact, and whether it was previous subsequent thereto.

Facts necessary to explain or introduce a fact in issue or relevant fact, or which support or rebut an inference suggested by a fact in issue or relevant fact, or which establish the identity of anything or person whose identity is relevant, or fix the time or place at which any fact in issue or relevant fact happened or which show the relation of parties by whom any such fact was transacted, are relevant in so far as they are necessary for that purpose.

Facts showing the existence of any state of mind, such as intention, knowledge, good faith, negligence, rashness, ill-will, or good-will towards any particular person, or showing the existence of any state of body or bodily feeling, are relevant, when the existence of any such state of mind or body or bodily feeling is in issue or relevant.

An admission is a statement, oral or documentary [or contained in digital record], which suggests any inference as to any fact in issue or relevant fact, and which is made by any of the persons, and under the circumstances, hereinafter mentioned.

Admissions are relevant and may be proved as against the person who makes them, or his representative in interest; but they can not be proved by or on behalf of the person who makes them or by his representative in interest, except in the following cases:–

Sixthly.- If he enters or quits by any passage which he knows to have been fastened against such entrance or departure, and to have been unfastened by himself or by an abettor of the house-trespass.
Whoever commits house-breaking after sunset and before sunrise, is said to commit "house-breaking by night".
Whoever commits house-trespass after sunset and before sunrise, is said to commit "lurking house-trespass by night".

Whoever commits criminal trespass by entering into or remaining in any building, tent or vessel used as a human dwelling or any building used as a place for worship, or as a place for the custody of property, is said to commit "house-trespass".

Whoever commits house-trespass having taken precautions to conceal such house-trespass from some person who has a right to exclude or eject the trespasser from the building, tent or vessel which is the subject of the trespass, is said to commit 'lurking house-trespass'.

Section Text: Whoever commits house-breaking after sunset and before sunrise, is said to commit "house-breaking by night".

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: How is house-breaking by scaling classified when it happens at 11 PM?

Rule.Section 445: Governs 'house-breaking', specifically including scaling a wall or spout as a method of entry.
Rule.Section 446: Governs house-breaking specifically when the entry occurs between sunset and sunrise (night-time).
Rule.Section 444: Governs lurking trespass at night, which focuses on secrecy rather than the 'breaking' method of entry.
Rule.Section 442: Governs general entry into a human dwelling with an illegal intent (house-trespass).
Rule.Section 443: Governs lurking trespass, involving active concealment of the intruder during the day.

Application.Section 445: Fails because it defines the general act of scaling but doesn't include the 'night-time' element required by the 11 PM time.
Application.Section 446: Governs directly because scaling (house-breaking) at 11 PM (after sunset) meets the specific definition of house-breaking by night.
Application.Section 444: Fails because house-breaking is a more serious and specific offense than simple lurking trespass.
Application.Section 442: Fails because it is the broad, basic category and ignores the specific method (scaling) and time (night).
Application.Section 443: Fails because it is a daytime category and doesn't account for the 11 PM time or the scaling method.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 446, Salim's act is legally called 'house-breaking by night' because he entered the dwelling by scaling after sunset.

Question: Scenario-based: I have a temporary right to hold onto a friend's bike until he pays me back for some repairs. Someone stole it from my garage. Can I sue to get the bike back even though I'm not the permanent owner?

Possible Sections.Full Text: A person entitled to the possession of specific moveable property may recover the same in the manner prescribed by the Code of Civil Procedure. Explanation 1 - A trustee may sue under this section for the possession of property to the beneficial interest in which the person for whom he is trustee is entitled. Explanation 2 - A special or temporary right to the present possession of property is sufficient to support a suit under this section.

Any person having the possession or control of a particular article of moveable property, of which he is not the owner, may be compelled specifically to deliver it to the person entitled to its immediate possession, in any of the following cases:- (a) when the thing claimed is held by the defendant as the agent or trustee of the claimant; (b) when compensation in money would not afford the claimant adequate relief for the loss of the thing claimed; (c) when it would be extremely difficult to ascertain the actual damage caused by its loss; (d) when the possession of the thing claimed has been wrongfully transferred from the claimant.

Specific relief is given- (a) by taking possession of certain property and delivering it to a claimant; (b) by ordering a party to do the very act which he is under an obligation to do; (c) by preventing a party from doing that which he is under an obligation not to do; (d) by determining and declaring the rights of parties otherwise than by an award of compensation; or (e) by appointing a receiver.
A person entitled to the possession of specific immoveable property may recover it in the manner prescribed by the Code of Civil Procedure.

In this Act, unless there be something repugnant in the subject or context,- "obligation" includes every duty enforceable by law: "trust" includes every species of express, implied, or constructive fiduciary ownership: "trustee" includes every person holding, expressly, by implication, or constructively, a fiduciary character:

Section Text: A person entitled to the possession of specific moveable property may recover the same in the manner prescribed by the Code of Civil Procedure. Explanation 1 - A trustee may sue under this section for the possession of property to the beneficial interest in which the person for whom he is trustee is entitled. Explanation 2 - A special or temporary right to the present possession of property is sufficient to support a suit under this section.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Does a temporary possessory right support a suit for recovery of moveable property?

Rule.Section 10: Allows recovery of moveable items by anyone with a current legal right to possession.
Rule.Section 11: Compels a non-owner currently holding an item to return it to the rightful claimant.
Rule.Section 5: Lists general modes of relief, including the delivery of property to a claimant.
Rule.Section 8: Provides the procedure for recovering land and buildings rather than personal items.
Rule.Section 3: Defines the role of a trustee in situations involving fiduciary duties and ownership.

Application.Section 10: Governs directly because Explanation 2 explicitly states a temporary right to possession supports a recovery suit.
Application.Section 11: Fails because it focuses on the duty of the 'holder' to deliver rather than the 'claimant's' right to sue.
Application.Section 5: Fails as it is a general categorization rather than a specific rule for temporary possessors.
Application.Section 8: Fails because a bike is moveable property, whereas Section 8 applies only to immoveable land.
Application.Section 3: Fails because the bike holder is acting as a bailee with a lien, not a fiduciary trustee.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Specific Relief Act, 1877, section 10, Explanation 2, you can sue to get the bike back — the law allows anyone with a temporary right to possession to file for recovery.

Question: Scenario-based: A legal document uses the phrase 'Good Faith' regarding a property sale. Does the law consider someone who was honest but extremely careless to have acted in good faith?

Possible Sections.Full Text: “good faith”: nothing shall be deemed to be done in good faith which is not done with due care and attention:

In computing the period of limitation prescribed for any suit, the time during which the plantiff has been prosecuting with due diligence another civil proceeding, whether in a Court of first instance or in a Court of appeal, against the defendant, shall be excluded, where the proceeding is founded upon the same cause of action and is prosecuted in good faith in a Court which, from defect of jurisdiction, or other cause of a like nature, is unable to entertain it. In computing the period of limitation prescribed for any application, the time during which the applicant has been prosecuting with due diligence another civil proceeding, whether in a Court of first instance or in a Court of appeal, against the same party for the same relief shall be excluded, where such proceeding is prosecuted in good faith in a Court which, from defect of jurisdiction, or other cause of a like nature, is unable to entertain it. In excluding the time during which a former suit or application was pending, the day on which that suit or application was instituted or made, and the day on which the proceedings therein ended, shall both be counted. For the purposes of this section, a plaintiff or an applicant resisting an appeal shall be deemed to be prosecuting a proceeding. For the purposes of this section misjoinder of parties or of causes of action shall be deemed to be a cause of a like nature with defect of jurisdiction.

Where any person having a right to institute a suit or make an application has, by means of fraud, been kept from the knowledge of such right or of the title on which it is founded, or where any document necessary to establish such right has been fraudulently concealed from him, the time limited for instituting a suit or making an application-
All instruments shall, for the purposes of this Act, be deemed to be made with reference to the Gregorian calendar.
Suits instituted in Bangladesh on contracts entered into in a foreign country are subject to the rules of limitation contained in this Act.

Section Text: “good faith”: nothing shall be deemed to be done in good faith which is not done with due care and attention:

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: What is the statutory definition of 'good faith' under the Limitation Act?

Rule.Section 2: Defines 'good faith' as requiring both honesty and the exercise of 'due care and attention'.
Rule.Section 14: Allows for the deduction of time if a person was suing in the wrong court because of an honest mistake about jurisdiction.
Rule.Section 18: Pauses the limitation period if a party used fraud or intentional lies to hide a right to sue.
Rule.Section 25: Mandates that all legal time limits in documents must be interpreted using the Gregorian calendar.
Rule.Section 11: Requires lawsuits in Bangladesh regarding contracts signed abroad to follow local limitation laws.

Application.Section 2: Governs directly because Subsection (7) provides the exact test for good faith, which explicitly excludes acts done without 'due care and attention'.
Application.Section 14: Fails because while it uses the term 'good faith', it doesn't provide the fundamental definition needed to test the 'careless' person's status.
Application.Section 18: Fails as it addresses intentional fraud rather than the standard of care required for acts to be considered in 'good faith'.
Application.Section 25: Fails as the question is about the definition of a legal standard (good faith) rather than the calendar used for counting days.
Application.Section 11: Fails as it defines which country's law applies rather than defining what 'good faith' means within that law.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Limitation Act, 1908, section 2, Subsection (7), the person did not act in 'good faith' — the law requires that acts be done with due care and attention, not just honesty.

Question: Scenario-based: Salim is caught trying to steal a bicycle. He has a loaded pistol in his pocket, but he never actually pulls it out or uses it during the attempt. Does the law give him a harsher sentence because of the gun?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Whoever commits theft shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, or with fine, or with both.

Whoever commits theft, having made preparation for causing death, or hurt, or restraint, or fear of death, or of hurt, or of restraint, to any person, in order to the committing of such theft, or in order to the effecting of his escape after the committing of such theft, or in order to the retaining of property taken by such theft, shall be punished with rigorous imprisonment for a term which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine.

In all robbery there is either theft or extortion. When theft is robbery Theft is "robbery" if, in order to the committing of the theft, or in committing the theft, or in carrying away or attempting to carry away property obtained by the theft, the offender, for that end, voluntarily causes or attempts to cause to any person death or hurt or wrongful restraint, or fear of instant death or of instant hurt, or of instant wrongful restraint.

Whoever commits robbery shall be punished with rigorous imprisonment for a term which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine; and, if the robbery be committed on the highway between sunset and sunrise, the imprisonment may be extended to fourteen years.

Whoever attempts to commit an offence punishable by this Code with imprisonment for life or imprisonment, or to cause such an offence to be committed, and in such attempt does any act towards the commission of the offence, shall, where no express provision is made by this Code for the punishment of such attempt, be punished with imprisonment of any description provided for the offence, for a term which may extend to one-half of the longest term of imprisonment provided for that offence, or with such fine as is provided for the offence, or with both.

Section Text: Whoever commits theft, having made preparation for causing death, or hurt, or restraint, or fear of death, or of hurt, or of restraint, to any person, in order to the committing of such theft, or in order to the effecting of his escape after the committing of such theft, or in order to the retaining of property taken by such theft, shall be punished with rigorous imprisonment for a term which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is there an enhanced penalty for theft when the offender is prepared to use violence, even if they don't?

Rule.Section 379: Governs the standard three-year punishment for simple theft without any weapons or preparation for violence.
Rule.Section 382: Governs theft where the offender has prepared for violence (like carrying a gun) to commit the crime or escape.
Rule.Section 390: Governs robbery, which requires the 'actual' use of violence or force during the theft.
Rule.Section 392: Governs the specific ten-year punishment range for someone convicted of robbery.
Rule.Section 511: Governs the general punishment for 'attempts' to commit crimes that were not finished.

Application.Section 379: Fails because it doesn't account for the added danger of carrying a deadly weapon while committing the crime.
Application.Section 382: Governs directly because carrying a loaded pistol is a 'preparation for causing death' during a theft, which elevates the punishment.
Application.Section 390: Fails because Salim did not actually 'cause' hurt or use the gun; he only had it ready (preparation).
Application.Section 392: Fails because a conviction for robbery requires actual violence, which didn't happen here.
Application.Section 511: Fails because the law has a more specific rule for theft involving weapon preparation (Section 382) compared to a generic attempt.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 382, Salim faces a harsher sentence of up to ten years in prison because he was prepared to use a deadly weapon during the theft.
